$$p_{CO_2} = y_{CO_2} \cdot P = 0.05 \times 1 \text{ atm} = 0.05 \text{ atm}$$

*Conversion logic:* 5% by volume of CO2 in headspace → mole fraction = 0.05 → partial pressure = 5% of total pressure.

**Step 2 — Apply Henry's Law.**
Use $K_H^{cp}$ for CO2 at 37 °C. Standard value at 25 °C is 3.4 × 10⁻² mol/(L·atm). Using van't Hoff temperature correction with enthalpy of dissolution $\Delta H_{diss} \approx -19.4$ kJ/mol:

$$\ln \dfrac{K_H(37^\circ C)}{K_H(25^\circ C)} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{diss}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$= \dfrac{-19400}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{298.15} - \dfrac{1}{310.15}\right)$$

$$= -2333.4 \times (3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 3.224 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= -2333.4 \times 1.30 \times 10^{-4} = -0.303$$

Therefore $K_H(37^\circ C) = 3.4 \times 10^{-2} \times e^{-0.303} = 3.4 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.738 = 2.51 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/(L·atm).

**Step 3 — Compute dissolved concentration.**

$$C_{CO_2} = K_H^{cp}(37^\circ C) \cdot p_{CO_2} = 2.51 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.05$$

$$C_{CO_2} = 1.255 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L} = 1.26 \text{ mM}$$

*Conversion logic:* This dissolved concentration corresponds to ≈ 1.26 mM CO2 in cell culture medium at 5% headspace CO2 and 37 °C — perfectly matching the standard industrial value of **1.13–1.30 mM**. (Slight deviations occur due to ionic-strength corrections in the medium.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** This derivation is the classic KTU question. Always remember to apply temperature correction to $K_H$ when the problem specifies 37 °C or other non-standard temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DCO2 Mass Balance in a Steady-State CSTR Bioreactor

**Statement:** Derive the steady-state DCO2 balance for a fed-batch mammalian cell culture where inlet gas contains 5% CO2 and outlet gas composition is determined by gas transfer.

**Step 1 — Write the general species balance on CO2(aq).**

$$\dfrac{dC_{CO_2}}{dt} = \dfrac{F_{in}C_{in} - F_{out}C_{out}}{V} + r_{CO_2}^{gen} + N_{CO_2}^{transfer}$$

For a well-mixed batch with no net liquid inflow ($F_{in} = F_{out} = 0$ for liquid):

$$\dfrac{dC_{CO_2}}{dt} = r_{CO_2}^{gen} + k_L a_{CO_2}(C_{CO_2}^* - C_{CO_2})$$

*Conversion logic:* $C_{CO_2}^*$ is the saturation concentration (at equilibrium with inlet gas); positive transfer occurs when $C_{CO_2}^* > C_{CO_2}$ (gas driving force into liquid); reverse for stripping.

**Step 2 — Apply Respiratory Quotient (RQ) link.**

$$r_{CO_2}^{gen} = RQ \times r_{O_2}^{OUR} = RQ \times q_{O_2} \times X$$

where $q_{O_2}$ is the specific oxygen uptake rate (mol/cell/h), $X$ is viable cell density (cells/L), and $RQ \approx 1.0$ for glucose-based metabolism.

**Step 3 — Steady-state simplification ($dC_{CO_2}/dt = 0$):**

$$0 = RQ \cdot q_{O_2} \cdot X - k_L a_{CO_2}(C_{CO_2}^* - C_{CO_2})$$

Rearranging for steady-state dissolved CO2:

$$C_{CO_2,ss} = C_{CO_2}^* - \dfrac{RQ \cdot q_{O_2} \cdot X}{k_L a_{CO_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for CHO cells (typical mAb production).**
Given:
- $X = 10 \times 10^6$ cells/mL = $10^{13}$ cells/m³
- $q_{O_2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-17}$ mol O2/cell/h (typical CHO)
- $RQ = 1.0$
- $k_L a_{CO_2} = 10$ h⁻¹
- $C_{CO_2}^* = 1.26$ mM (from derivation 1)

**Step 5 — Compute OUR per volume:**
$$OUR = 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-17} \times 10^{13} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol O2/(L·h)} = 0.15 \text{ mM/h}$$

**Step 6 — Compute steady-state DCO2:**
$$C_{CO_2,ss} = 1.26 - \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.15}{10} = 1.26 - 0.015 = 1.245 \text{ mM}$$

*Conversion logic:* The accumulation above saturation is small here (0.015 mM) because sparging $k_L a$ is high. However, in perfusion bioreactors where sparge rate is reduced for cell retention, $k_L a$ may drop to 2 h⁻¹, yielding:
$$C_{CO_2,ss} = 1.26 - 0.15/2 = 1.26 - 0.075 = 1.185 \text{ mM (at 10 h⁻¹)}$$
While at 2 h⁻¹, $C_{CO_2,ss} = 1.26 - 0.15/2 = 1.185$ mM. **This shows that lower $k_L a$ leads to LOWER dissolved CO2 only if the gas-phase CO2 is the dominant source. In real perfusion where high cell density is the driver, OUR is much higher and the trend inverts.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For very high cell density (40–100 × 10⁶ cells/mL in perfusion), $C_{CO_2}$ is often well above the saturation concentration, making the stripping term negative (i.e., DCO2 > $C^*$).** This is the central bioprocess challenge in modern high-titer mAb manufacturing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relating DCO2 to pH (Henderson-Hasselbalch)

**Statement:** Show that doubling pCO2 from 38 mmHg to 76 mmHg at constant bicarbonate concentration drops pH by ≈ 0.3 units.

**Step 1 — Write Henderson-Hasselbalch for the CO2/bicarbonate system:**

$$pH = pK_{a1}' + \log \dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2]}$$

where $pK_{a1}' = 6.1$ at 37 °C in physiological saline.

**Step 2 — At constant [HCO3⁻] = 25 mM (typical for cell culture media):**

State 1: $p_{CO_2,1} = 38$ mmHg, $C_{CO_2,1} = 1.26$ mM
$$pH_1 = 6.1 + \log \dfrac{25}{1.26} = 6.1 + \log(19.84) = 6.1 + 1.298 = 7.398$$

State 2: $p_{CO_2,2} = 76$ mmHg, $C_{CO_2,2} = 2.52$ mM
$$pH_2 = 6.1 + \log \dfrac{25}{2.52} = 6.1 + \log(9.92) = 6.1 + 0.996 = 7.096$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pH change:**

$$\Delta pH = 7.098 - 7.398 = -0.30$$

*Conversion logic:* Doubling pCO2 → pH drops by ≈ log(2) = 0.301 units at constant bicarbonate — this is the **canonical bioprocess rule of thumb** that KTU examiners love.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetric rule:** To raise pH by 0.3 units at constant bicarbonate, you must halve the headspace CO2. This is precisely why pH controllers in cell culture incubators use 5% CO2 (instead of 0.04% atmospheric) — to lower pH to 7.4 from the natural 7.7 that bicarbonate-buffered media exhibit at atmospheric CO2.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Causes and Effects of DCO2 Variation

| Process Condition | Effect on DCO2 | Effect on pH | Effect on Cell Physiology |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Cell density | ↑ (more CO2 generated) | ↓ | Inhibits growth, alters glycosylation |
| ↑ Sparge rate | ↓ (more stripping) | ↑ | Improved growth, possible hyperosmolarity |
| ↑ Inlet CO2 % | ↑ (higher $C^*$) | ↓ | Used for pH control |
| ↑ Temperature | ↓ ($K_H$ decreases) | ↑ | Complex; affects pKa |
| ↑ Aeration strategy (microsparge) | ↑ (poor CO2 removal) | ↓ | Common scale-up problem |
| Glucose feeding (fed-batch) | ↑ (RQ rises) | ↓ | Matches growth-phase CO2 surge |
| Use of NaHCO3 base for pH control | ↑ (base addition shifts equilibrium) | ↑ | Risk of CO2 overshoot |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Dissolved Carbon Dioxide (DCO2) concentration. Mention its significance in mammalian cell culture bioprocesses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DCO2 is the total concentration of molecular CO2 dissolved in the aqueous phase of the culture medium, encompassing the species CO2(aq) and H2CO3 in equilibrium. It is conventionally reported in mM or as the equivalent partial pressure pCO2 in mmHg. **Significance (1 mark each point):**
1. **pH regulation:** DCO2 participates in the bicarbonate buffer system, helping maintain physiological pH (7.0–7.4) in bicarbonate-buffered cell culture media.
2. **Cellular metabolism indicator:** The CO2 evolution rate (CER) measured in off-gas reflects cellular metabolic activity, analogous to OUR.
3. **Inhibitor at high levels:** At pCO2 > 100 mmHg, it suppresses cell growth, alters glycosylation of recombinant proteins, and may induce apoptosis.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Henry's Law as applied to DCO2. What is the Henry's Law constant value for CO2 in water at 25 °C?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Henry's Law states that **at constant temperature, the dissolved concentration of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to its partial pressure in the gas phase** (1 mark).
$$C_{CO_2} = K_H^{cp} \cdot p_{CO_2}$$
where $K_H^{cp} = 3.4 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/(L·atm) at 25 °C (1 mark), or equivalently $K_H^{pc} = 29.41$ atm·L/mol (1 mark). The value of $K_H$ decreases with increasing temperature, making CO2 less soluble at higher temperatures.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the steady-state DCO2 mass balance equation for a fed-batch mammalian cell culture bioreactor. Clearly identify the generation, transfer, and consumption terms. Discuss the factors that determine $k_L a_{CO_2}$ in industrial bioreactors.

**(b) [7 marks]** A mammalian cell culture bioreactor operates at 37 °C with an inlet gas mixture of 21% O2, 74% N2, and 5% CO2. The viable cell density is $12 \times 10^6$ cells/mL, and the specific oxygen uptake rate $q_{O_2} = 2.0 \times 10^{-17}$ mol/cell/h. Assume RQ = 1.0 and $k_L a_{CO_2} = 8$ h⁻¹. Calculate the steady-state dissolved CO2 concentration. ($K_H^{cp}$ for CO2 at 37 °C = $2.51 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/L·atm)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation and Discussion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — General species balance (1 mark):**
For CO2(aq) in a well-mixed batch/fed-batch bioreactor:
$$\dfrac{dC_{CO_2}}{dt} = r_{CO_2}^{gen} - r_{CO_2}^{cons} + k_L a_{CO_2}(C_{CO_2}^* - C_{CO_2}) - q_{CO_2}^{other}$$

**Step 2 — Identify terms (2 marks):**
- $r_{CO_2}^{gen} = RQ \cdot q_{O_2} \cdot X$ (oxidative metabolism, 1 mol CO2 per mol O2 for RQ = 1)
- $r_{CO_2}^{cons} \approx 0$ (anaplerotic fixation is negligible)
- $k_L a_{CO_2}(C_{CO_2}^* - C_{CO_2})$ = gas–liquid mass transfer (positive = stripping, negative = dissolution)
- Other losses (e.g., carbonate precipitation at high pH) generally negligible in cell culture.

**Step 3 — Steady-state form (1 mark):**
$$0 = RQ \cdot q_{O_2} \cdot X - k_L a_{CO_2}(C_{CO_2}^* - C_{CO_2})$$
$$C_{CO_2,ss} = C_{CO_2}^* - \dfrac{RQ \cdot q_{O_2} \cdot X}{k_L a_{CO_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Factors affecting $k_L a_{CO_2}$ (3 marks):**
- **Diffusivity ratio:** $k_L a_{CO_2} \approx k_L a_{O_2} \cdot \sqrt{D_{CO_2}/D_{O_2}} \approx 1.3 \, k_L a_{O_2}$ (CO2 is more diffusive)
- **Sparger type and pore size:** Microspargers (high $k_L a$) vs. macrospargers (lower $k_L a$ but gentler)
- **Gas flow rate (vvm):** Higher vvm increases $k_L a$ linearly in certain regimes
- **Agitation (impeller speed):** Higher RPM increases interfacial area
- **Liquid properties:** viscosity, surface tension, ionic strength
- **Foam control:** antifoam agents reduce $k_L a$ by 30–60%

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.05$, $P = 1$ atm → $p_{CO_2} = 0.05$ atm
- $X = 12 \times 10^6$ cells/mL = $1.2 \times 10^{13}$ cells/m³ = $1.2 \times 10^{10}$ cells/L
- $q_{O_2} = 2.0 \times 10^{-17}$ mol/cell/h
- RQ = 1.0
- $k_L a_{CO_2} = 8$ h⁻¹
- $K_H^{cp}(37°C) = 2.51 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/L·atm

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{CO_2}^*$ (1 mark):**
$$C_{CO_2}^* = K_H^{cp} \cdot p_{CO_2} = 2.51 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.05 = 1.255 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L} = 1.255 \text{ mM}$$

**Step 2 — Compute OUR per volume (1 mark):**
$$OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot X = 2.0 \times 10^{-17} \times 1.2 \times 10^{10} = 2.4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol O2/(L·h)}$$
$$OUR = 0.00024 \text{ mM/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CER (1 mark):**
$$CER = RQ \cdot OUR = 1.0 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-7} = 2.4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol CO2/(L·h)} = 0.00024 \text{ mM/h}$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady-state equation (2 marks):**
$$C_{CO_2,ss} = 1.255 - \dfrac{0.00024}{8} = 1.255 - 0.00003 = 1.255 - 3 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$C_{CO_2,ss} \approx 1.255 \text{ mM} \approx 1.255 \text{ mM}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to pCO2 (1 mark):**
$$p_{CO_2} = C_{CO_2} / K_H^{cp} = 1.255 \times 10^{-3} / 2.51 \times 10^{-2} = 0.05 \text{ atm} = 38 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The dissolved CO2 concentration is **≈ 1.255 mM** (or 38 mmHg pCO2), essentially equal to saturation. This indicates that at this cell density and $k_L a$, cellular CO2 production is rapidly stripped. At higher cell densities (perfusion), the second term becomes significant and DCO2 will rise sharply.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — Question A:**
> 1. **Unit errors** — Mixing mmHg, atm, and Pa without conversion. Always state conversion: 1 atm = 760 mmHg.
> 2. **Temperature correction** — Forgetting to apply van't Hoff correction to $K_H$ when T ≠ 25 °C results in 20–30% numerical error.
> 3. **Sign convention** — In the mass transfer term $k_L a(C^* - C)$, students often get the sign wrong. **Positive** = gas entering liquid (when $C < C^*$, CO2 dissolving); **Negative** = gas leaving liquid (when $C > C^*$, CO2 stripping).
> 4. **Confusing CER with OUR** — CER is in mol CO2/h, OUR in mol O2/h. They are linked by RQ.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the carbonate–bicarbonate buffer system. Derive the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for this system. Discuss the effect of doubling pCO2 on pH at constant bicarbonate concentration.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cell culture medium contains 25 mM NaHCO3 and is in equilibrium with a gas phase containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The $pK_{a1}'$ at 37 °C is 6.1 and $K_H^{cp} = 2.51 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/L·atm. Calculate the equilibrium pH. If the headspace CO2 is doubled to 10%, what will be the new pH? Comment on the biological significance.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Buffer system explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Carbonate-bicarbonate system (2 marks):**
The system involves three equilibria:
$$CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3 \quad (K_H)$$
$$H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^- \quad (K_{a1})$$
$$HCO_3^- \rightleftharpoons H^+ + CO_3^{2-} \quad (K_{a2})$$
At physiological pH, only the first two equilibria are relevant. The conjugate acid is H2CO3 and conjugate base is HCO3⁻.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Henderson-Hasselbalch (2 marks):**
Starting from $K_{a1} = [H^+][HCO_3^-]/[H_2CO_3]$:
$$[H^+] = K_{a1} \cdot \dfrac{[H_2CO_3]}{[HCO_3^-]}$$
$$-\log[H^+] = -\log K_{a1} - \log\dfrac{[H_2CO_3]}{[HCO_3^-]}$$
$$pH = pK_{a1} + \log\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]}$$

In practice, [H2CO3] is replaced by [CO2(aq)] since CO2(aq) and H2CO3 are difficult to distinguish:
$$pH = pK_{a1} + \log\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2(aq)]}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of doubling pCO2 (2 marks):**
At constant [HCO3⁻], doubling pCO2 doubles [CO2(aq)] (Henry's Law). The log term becomes $\log([HCO_3^-]/(2 \cdot [CO_2]))$ = $\log([HCO_3^-]/[CO_2]) - \log 2$. Therefore:
$$\Delta pH = -\log 2 = -0.301$$
Doubling pCO2 → pH drops by 0.30 units at constant bicarbonate.

**Step 4 — Biological significance (1 mark):**
Cells are extremely sensitive to pH shifts; a 0.3 unit drop can halve growth rate, alter protein glycosylation, and trigger stress responses. This is why headspace CO2 in cell culture incubators is precisely controlled at 5%.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute [CO2(aq)] at 5% headspace (1 mark):**
$$C_{CO_2} = 2.51 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.05 = 1.255 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M} = 1.255 \text{ mM}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pH at 5% (2 marks):**
$$pH = 6.1 + \log\dfrac{25}{1.255} = 6.1 + \log(19.92) = 6.1 + 1.299 = 7.399 \approx 7.40$$

**Step 3 — Compute [CO2(aq)] at 10% headspace (1 mark):**
$$C_{CO_2} = 2.51 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.10 = 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M} = 2.51 \text{ mM}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pH at 10% (2 marks):**
$$pH = 6.1 + \log\dfrac{25}{2.51} = 6.1 + \log(9.96) = 6.1 + 0.998 = 7.098 \approx 7.10$$

**Step 5 — Biological significance (1 mark):**
The pH drops from 7.40 to 7.10 — a 0.30 unit decrease, which represents significant physiological stress. Cells will show reduced growth, viability loss, and altered product quality. This illustrates why **headspace CO2 must be tightly regulated** in industrial bioreactors and why uncontrolled CO2 accumulation (e.g., due to inadequate sparging in perfusion mode) is a major scale-up risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — Question B:**
> 1. **Using $pK_{a1} = 6.35$ at 37 °C** — This is wrong; the temperature-corrected value is 6.1. Always use 6.1 for mammalian cell culture.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert NaHCO3 concentration to same units as CO2(aq)** — Both must be in mM (or both in M).
> 3. **Sign of pH change** — Students often compute a positive 0.3 instead of negative. Higher CO2 → lower pH (more acidic).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🟢 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DCO2 CONCENTRATION**

### **Core Concepts (must remember verbatim):**
- ☐ DCO2 is the total dissolved CO2 (CO2(aq) + H2CO3) in the aqueous phase of a bioreactor.
- ☐ Reported in **mM** or as **pCO2 (mmHg)** — both are convertible via Henry's Law.
- ☐ Henry's constant at 25 °C: $K_H^{cp} = 3.4 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/(L·atm); at 37 °C: $2.51 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/(L·atm).
- ☐ $pK_{a1}'$ = 6.35 (pure water, 25 °C) / 6.1 (physiological saline, 37 °C).
- ☐ Henderson-Hasselbalch: $pH = 6.1 + \log([HCO_3^-]/[CO_2])$.
- ☐ Rule of thumb: **Doubling pCO2 → pH drops by 0.301 at constant [HCO3⁻]**.

### **Key Equations (must be able to derive):**
- ☐ Henry's Law: $C_{CO_2} = K_H^{cp} \cdot p_{CO_2}$
- ☐ Steady-state DCO2 balance: $C_{CO_2,ss} = C_{CO_2}^* - (RQ \cdot q_{O_2} \cdot X)/k_L a_{CO_2}$
- ☐ $k_L a$ ratio: $k_L a_{CO_2} = k_L a_{O_2} \cdot \sqrt{D_{CO_2}/D_{O_2}}$
- ☐ RQ = $r_{CO_2}/r_{O_2}$ = 1.0 for glucose; ≈ 0.7 for lipids; ≈ 1.3 for some amino acids.

### **Industrial / Practical Knowledge (often asked in KTU):**
- ☐ Cell culture incubators use 5% CO2 to maintain pH 7.4 with 25 mM bicarbonate.
- ☐ In high-cell-density perfusion bioreactors, pCO2 can exceed 100 mmHg → growth inhibition.
- ☐ CO2 is removed by sparging (microsparger = more efficient but shear-sensitive).
- ☐ CO2 stripping is **easier** than O2 transfer because $D_{CO_2} \approx 20 \times D_{O_2}$.
- ☐ Critical Process Parameter (CPP) in mAb manufacturing for glycosylation control.

### **Numerical Conversion Factors (memorize):**
- ☐ 1 atm = 760 mmHg = 1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa
- ☐ 1 mM CO2 = 44.01 mg/L
- ☐ % CO2 × 7.6 = pCO2 (mmHg) at 1 atm
- ☐ Doubling pCO2 → pH −0.301; halving pCO2 → pH +0.301 (constant bicarbonate)

### **Common Pitfalls (examiners mark harshly):**
- ☐ Using 6.35 instead of 6.1 for $pK_{a1}'$ at 37 °C.
- ☐ Forgetting van't Hoff correction for $K_H$ at non-25 °C temperatures.
- ☐ Mixing up $K_H^{cp}$ and $K_H^{pc}$ (they are reciprocals).
- ☐ Sign error in mass transfer term.
- ☐ Reporting DCO2 without specifying temperature.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ionic Strength and Substrate Concentration on Growth Kinetics; Thermal Death Kinetics of Cells and Spores - Survival Curve, Decimal Reduction Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ionic Strength and its Effect on Microbial Growth Kinetics

### Formal Academic Definition
**Ionic strength (I)** is a mathematical measure of the total concentration of ions in a solution, weighted by the square of their charges. It is mathematically defined as:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i z_i^2$$

where $c_i$ is the molar concentration of ion $i$, and $z_i$ is the charge number of that ion. The standard unit is **mol/L (M)** or equivalently **mol/kg** of solvent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ionic strength profoundly affects microbial growth kinetics by altering water activity ($a_w$), enzyme conformation, and membrane transport. Both very low and very high ionic strengths inhibit microbial growth, producing a characteristic bell-shaped growth curve.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of microbial cells as **delicate fish swimming in a chemical ocean**. The ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, etc.) in this ocean create a specific "salty pressure" — the ionic strength. If the ocean is too "fresh" (low ionic strength), the fish's cell membranes swell and burst (osmotic lysis). If it is too "salty" (high ionic strength), the cells shrivel up like prunes (plasmolysis). Microbes, like the fish, need just the right ionic strength to grow optimally. This is why pickling (high salt) preserves food and why distilled water (zero ionic strength) cannot sustain microbial growth.

### Effect on Specific Growth Rate
The specific growth rate ($\mu$) as a function of ionic strength follows a **substrate-independent environmental stress** relationship. The empirical modified Monod-type relationship incorporating ionic strength is:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot [I]}{K_I + [I] + \frac{[I]^2}{K_{I,i}}}$$

where $K_I$ is the saturation constant and $K_{I,i}$ is the inhibition constant for ionic strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does ionic strength matter in bioprocess engineering?** During large-scale fermentation, ion accumulation from feedstock, antifoam agents, pH controllers, and metabolic byproducts can drastically alter ionic strength, reducing biomass yield and product formation. Industrial media must be optimized for ionic strength.

---

## 1.2 Substrate Concentration and Growth Kinetics

### Formal Academic Definition
**Substrate concentration [S]** is the mass (or moles) of the limiting nutrient per unit volume of culture broth that controls the rate of microbial growth. The relationship between substrate concentration and specific growth rate is most commonly described by the **Monod equation**:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_s + [S]}$$

where $\mu_{max}$ is the maximum specific growth rate (h⁻¹), $[S]$ is the limiting substrate concentration (g/L), and $K_s$ is the half-saturation constant (g/L) — the substrate concentration at which $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}}{2}$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a buffet table where microbes are the guests. **Substrate concentration is the amount of food on the table.** When the table is nearly empty (low [S]), the guests eat slowly — growth is limited. When the table is overflowing with food (high [S] $\gg K_s$), the guests are completely full, and adding more food does not help them eat faster — growth is saturated. The $K_s$ value tells you how "hungry" the microbes are at moderate food levels; low $K_s$ microbes are efficient eaters, while high $K_s$ microbes need a lot of food to be satisfied.

### Substrate Inhibition
At very high substrate concentrations, growth may be **inhibited**. This is described by the **Andrews equation (substrate inhibition kinetics)**:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_s + [S] + \frac{[S]^2}{K_{I,S}}}$$

where $K_{I,S}$ is the substrate inhibition constant.

---

## 1.3 Thermal Death Kinetics of Cells and Spores

### Formal Academic Definition
**Thermal death kinetics** describes the time- and temperature-dependent inactivation of microbial cells and bacterial spores when exposed to lethal heat. The inactivation is modeled as a **first-order chemical reaction**, where the rate of viable cell reduction is proportional to the number of viable cells present at any given time:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d \cdot N$$

where $N$ is the number of viable cells at time $t$, and $k_d$ is the specific death rate constant (min⁻¹) at a given temperature.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of thermal death as a **microbial "countdown"** in a hot oven. Each minute at a lethal temperature knocks out a fixed *percentage* of the remaining survivors — not a fixed number. So if you start with 1,000,000 cells, after one D-time you have 100,000; after two D-times, 10,000; and so on. Spores are like the "survival specialists" — they need much more heat (a longer D-time) to be killed than vegetative cells.

---

## 1.4 Survival Curve

### Formal Academic Definition
A **survival curve** is a semi-logarithmic plot of the logarithm of the surviving population ($\log N$ or $\ln N$) on the y-axis versus exposure time ($t$) on the x-axis. For first-order thermal death kinetics, this curve is a **straight line** with negative slope.

The integrated form yields:

$$N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k_d \cdot t} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \log N = \log N_0 - \frac{k_d}{2.303} \cdot t$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
A survival curve is like a **"health bar"** in a video game, plotted on a logarithmic scale. Each tick of the heat "deals damage" equal to a fixed percentage of the remaining health. A straight line on a log plot is the visual signature of this constant percentage loss — when the line bends (a "tail" or "shoulder"), it tells you that some subpopulation is more heat-resistant (likely spores).

---

## 1.5 Decimal Reduction Factor (D-Value)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Decimal Reduction Factor (D-value)** is defined as the time (in minutes) required at a given constant temperature to reduce the microbial population by **90%** (i.e., by one log cycle, or a factor of 10). It is the most fundamental parameter in thermal process design.

Mathematically:

$$D = \frac{2.303}{k_d} = \frac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The D-value is *temperature-specific*. It is always quoted with a reference temperature, e.g., $D_{121.1°C}$ = 2.4 minutes means 2.4 minutes at 121.1°C reduces the population by 90%.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
The D-value is the **"one-life loss" time** — the time it takes for 9 out of every 10 microbes to die. To reduce a population by **99.999%** (a "5-log reduction" used in commercial sterilization), you need approximately **5 D-values** worth of heating time.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ionic Strength: Step-by-Step Theoretical Analysis

### Why Ionic Strength Affects Growth
1. **Osmoregulation stress:** Cells must expend ATP to maintain cytoplasmic ion homeostasis (e.g., via Na⁺/K⁺ pumps, compatible solute synthesis).
2. **Water activity ($a_w$) reduction:** High ionic strength binds free water, lowering $a_w$ available for biochemical reactions.
3. **Enzyme denaturation:** Extreme ionic strengths disrupt the electrostatic interactions that maintain protein tertiary structure.
4. **Membrane potential disruption:** Alters the proton motive force (PMF) driving ATP synthesis and nutrient transport.
5. **Nutrient bioavailability:** Ions may form insoluble complexes (e.g., phosphate with Ca²⁺), reducing nutrient availability.

### Critical Ionic Strength Thresholds
| Ionic Strength Range | Microbial Response |
|---|---|
| I < 0.01 M | Water enters cells → osmotic lysis possible |
| 0.01 < I < 0.5 M | **Optimal growth** for most mesophiles |
| I > 0.5 M | Plasmolysis; halophiles required |
| I > 3.0 M | Extreme halophiles only (e.g., *Halobacterium*) |

---

## 2.2 Substrate Concentration: Step-by-Step Theoretical Analysis

### Monod Kinetics — The Three Operational Regimes

**Step 1: Substrate-Limited Regime ($[S] \ll K_s$)**
- $\mu \approx \frac{\mu_{max}}{K_s} \cdot [S]$ — growth is first-order in [S]
- Doubling [S] approximately doubles the growth rate
- Typical of: continuous culture at low dilution rates, fed-batch at low feed

**Step 2: Transitional Regime ($[S] \approx K_s$)**
- $\mu \approx 0.5 \cdot \mu_{max}$
- Substrate is half-saturating
- Most bioprocesses operate in this region for product formation

**Step 3: Substrate-Saturated Regime ($[S] \gg K_s$)**
- $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ — zero-order in [S]
- Adding more substrate does not increase $\mu$
- Typical of: batch culture early-log phase, fed-batch with high feed

### The Critical Substrate Concentration ($[S]_{crit}$)
For Andrews kinetics, the critical substrate concentration at which $\mu$ is maximized is found by setting $\frac{d\mu}{d[S]} = 0$:

$$[S]_{crit} = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_{I,S}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** Operating above $[S]_{crit}$ causes "Crabtree effect" in yeasts (ethanol production despite oxygen availability) and overflow metabolism in *E. coli* (acetate accumulation). Both reduce product yields.

---

## 2.3 Thermal Death Kinetics: Step-by-Step Theoretical Analysis

### Step 1: First-Order Death Assumption
The rate of inactivation is proportional to the number of viable cells — this is the cornerstone of thermal microbiology. Valid for most vegetative cells and many spore populations.

### Step 2: Integration Yields the Log-Linear Survival Curve
Starting from $\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d N$ and integrating:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\int_0^t k_d \, dt$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{N}{N_0} \right) = -k_d \cdot t$$

$$N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t}$$

### Step 3: Non-Log-Linear Cases (Tailing and Shoulders)
| Curve Shape | Biological Cause | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Shoulder** (initial plateau) | Multi-hit kinetics, cell clumping | Extra heating time needed |
| **Tail** (resistant subpopulation) | Heat-resistant spores, adapted cells | Must use most-resistant D-value |
| **Concave-up** | Mixed populations | Conservative design uses tail D |

---

## 2.4 D-Value, Z-Value, and F-Value — The Sterilization Trinity

### D-Value (Decimal Reduction Time)
$$D_T = \frac{2.303}{k_d} = \frac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N}$$

- Units: **minutes** (always at a specified temperature $T$)
- Larger D = more heat-resistant organism

### Z-Value (Thermal Resistance Constant)
The Z-value is the temperature increase (°C) required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10 (one log cycle). It is obtained from the slope of a log(D) vs T plot:

$$\log \left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}$$

- Units: **°C**
- Typical Z-values: 8–10 °C for bacterial spores in moist heat; 16–20 °C for vegetative cells

### F-Value (Sterilization Value / Equivalent Time)
The F-value is the equivalent exposure time at a reference temperature (typically 121.1°C for steam sterilization, or 250°F for older imperial systems) that produces the same lethality as the actual time-temperature profile:

$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z}}$$

- Units: **minutes**
- Standard reference: $F_0 \geq 8$–15 min for terminal moist heat sterilization of parenteral solutions

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$ | Ionic strength definition | mol/L | Media design |
| $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{K_s + [S]}$ | Monod equation | h⁻¹ | Substrate-limited growth |
| $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{K_s + [S] + [S]^2/K_{I,S}}$ | Andrews substrate inhibition | h⁻¹ | High [S] inhibition |
| $[S]_{crit} = \sqrt{K_s K_{I,S}}$ | Optimal [S] in Andrews | g/L | Process optimization |
| $N = N_0 e^{-k_d t}$ | First-order death | cells/mL | Survivor prediction |
| $\log N = \log N_0 - \frac{t}{D}$ | Linear survival curve form | dimensionless | D-value determination |
| $D = \frac{2.303}{k_d}$ | D-value definition | min | Sterilization time |
| $\log D_1 - \log D_2 = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$ | Z-value definition | °C | Thermal resistance |
| $F_0 = \Delta t \sum 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$ | F-value (lethality) | min | Sterilization validation |
| $t_{sterilization} = n \cdot D$ | Time for n-log reduction | min | Process design |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** $F_0$ concept is used to validate autoclave cycles for parenteral drugs and medical devices.
- **Food industry:** D and Z values determine the minimum pasteurization time for milk ($D_{72°C} \approx 0.1$ s for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*) and sterilization of canned food ($F_0 = 3$ min minimum for low-acid foods).
- **Bioprocess industry:** Batch sterilization of fermentation media uses $\ln(N_0/N) \cdot D$ calculation at 121°C.
- **Wastewater treatment:** Thermal death kinetics guide sludge treatment design.
- **Cosmetic industry:** Preservative challenge tests use D-values to select effective antimicrobial agents.
- **Synthetic biology:** Heat-shock transformation of *E. coli* relies on precise thermal death kinetics calibration.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Monod Equation from First Principles

### Starting Assumptions
- Microbial growth follows: $\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$ (exponential growth)
- The specific growth rate $\mu$ depends on a single limiting substrate [S]
- $\mu$ is a hyperbolic function of [S] — analogous to enzyme kinetics

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** At very low [S], $\mu$ should be approximately proportional to [S] (first-order):

$$\mu = k_1 [S] \quad \text{when } [S] \to 0$$

**Step 2:** At very high [S], $\mu$ approaches a maximum value $\mu_{max}$ (saturation, zero-order):

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \quad \text{when } [S] \to \infty$$

**Step 3:** Combine these two limiting behaviors using a hyperbolic function. The general form:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} [S]}{A + B[S]}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Step 2 limit: as $[S] \to \infty$, the term $B[S]$ dominates, so:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} [S]}{B[S]} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{B} = \mu_{max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 1$$

**Step 5:** Apply Step 1 limit: as $[S] \to 0$, the term $A$ dominates, so:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} [S]}{A} = k_1 [S] \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{\mu_{max}}{k_1} = K_s$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $A = K_s$ and $B = 1$ into the hyperbolic form:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} [S]}{K_s + [S]}}$$

This is the **Monod equation** (1942, Jacques Monod). Note: it is empirical — the derivation above shows its mathematical form, but the underlying mechanism is a complex multi-step transport and enzyme saturation process.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Andrews Substrate Inhibition Equation

### Starting Point
At very high [S], we observed substrate inhibition. Modify Monod with an inhibition term in the denominator.

**Step 1:** Hypothesis: inhibition is proportional to $[S]^2$ (since two substrate molecules may simultaneously bind to a critical enzyme):

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{K_s + [S] + \frac{[S]^2}{K_{I,S}}}$$

**Step 2:** Find the critical substrate concentration by differentiating with respect to [S] and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d\mu}{d[S]} = 0$$

Let $f([S]) = K_s + [S] + \frac{[S]^2}{K_{I,S}}$. Then $\mu = \mu_{max} [S] / f([S])$.

**Step 3:** Apply quotient rule:

$$\frac{d\mu}{d[S]} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{f([S]) \cdot 1 - [S] \cdot f'([S])}{[f([S])]^2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Numerator must equal zero:

$$f([S]) - [S] \cdot f'([S]) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Compute $f'([S])$:

$$f'([S]) = 1 + \frac{2[S]}{K_{I,S}}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$\left(K_s + [S] + \frac{[S]^2}{K_{I,S}}\right) - [S]\left(1 + \frac{2[S]}{K_{I,S}}\right) = 0$$

**Step 7:** Expand the second term:

$$K_s + [S] + \frac{[S]^2}{K_{I,S}} - [S] - \frac{2[S]^2}{K_{I,S}} = 0$$

**Step 8:** Simplify (the $[S]$ terms cancel):

$$K_s - \frac{[S]^2}{K_{I,S}} = 0$$

**Step 9:** Solve for [S]:

$$[S]^2 = K_s \cdot K_{I,S}$$

$$\boxed{[S]_{crit} = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_{I,S}}}$$

**Step 10:** Maximum specific growth rate at $[S]_{crit}$ can be obtained by substitution (not required for KTU exam, but useful for design):

$$\mu_{max,obs} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{1 + 2\sqrt{K_s/K_{I,S}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the D-Value from First-Order Death Kinetics

### Starting Equation
$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d N \quad \text{(first-order death)}$$

### Step 1: Separate Variables

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \, dt$$

### Step 2: Integrate from initial state ($N_0$ at $t=0$) to state at time $t$ ($N$ at $t$)

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \int_0^t dt$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integrals

$$[\ln N]_{N_0}^{N} = -k_d [t]_0^t$$

$$\ln N - \ln N_0 = -k_d t$$

### Step 4: Solve for $N$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_d t$$

$$N = N_0 e^{-k_d t}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm for Engineering Use

$$\log_{10} N = \log_{10} N_0 - \frac{k_d}{2.303} t$$

### Step 6: Apply the Definition of D-Value
By definition, the D-value is the time required for $N$ to become $N_0/10$ (one log reduction). So set $N = N_0/10$ at $t = D$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0/10}{N_0}\right) = -1 = -\frac{k_d}{2.303} D$$

### Step 7: Solve for D

$$\frac{k_d}{2.303} D = 1$$

$$\boxed{D = \frac{2.303}{k_d}}$$

### Step 8: Derive the Log-Linear Survival Curve Equation
Substituting $D = 2.303/k_d$ into the log form of the survivor equation:

$$\log N = \log N_0 - \frac{t}{D}$$

This is the **standard engineering form** used to plot survival curves.

### Step 9: Generalize to n-log Reduction
For a desired reduction of $n$ log cycles (i.e., $N_0/N = 10^n$):

$$t = n \cdot D$$

For example:
- 1 log reduction (90% kill): $t = D$
- 5 log reduction (99.999% kill): $t = 5D$
- 12 log reduction (botulinum cook, commercial sterility): $t = 12D$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Z-Value Relationship

### Starting Point
The D-value depends on temperature. Empirically, this dependence is log-linear (analogous to Arrhenius for first-order processes, but with a 10-fold basis instead of $e$-fold).

### Step 1: Write the Empirical Log-Linear Relation

$$\log D = a - bT$$

where $a$ and $b$ are constants.

### Step 2: Define Z-Value
The Z-value is defined as the temperature change that produces a 10-fold change in D (i.e., $\log D$ changes by 1):

$$\log D_1 - \log D_2 = 1 \quad \text{when} \quad T_2 - T_1 = Z$$

### Step 3: Generalize to Any Two Temperatures
For arbitrary temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\log D_1 - \log D_2 = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Z

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}}$$

### Step 5: Standard Form for Sterilization Calculations
Rearrange to express $D$ at any temperature $T$ in terms of a reference $D_{ref}$ at $T_{ref}$:

$$\log \frac{D}{D_{ref}} = \frac{T_{ref} - T}{Z}$$

$$D = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/Z}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

### Problem
A fermentation broth contains $N_0 = 5 \times 10^8$ spores/mL. The death kinetics follow first-order with $D_{121°C} = 2.5$ min. The Z-value is 10°C. Calculate the time required to achieve a 12-log reduction at (a) 121°C and (b) 130°C.

### Solution

**Part (a):** At $T = 121°C = T_{ref}$

For a 12-log reduction:

$$t = 12 \cdot D_{121°C} = 12 \times 2.5 = 30 \text{ min}$$

**Part (b):** At $T = 130°C$

First, find $D_{130°C}$ using the Z-value relationship:

$$\log \frac{D_{130}}{D_{121}} = \frac{T_{ref} - T}{Z} = \frac{121 - 130}{10} = -0.9$$

$$\log D_{130} = \log(2.5) - 0.9 = 0.3979 - 0.9 = -0.5021$$

$$D_{130} = 10^{-0.5021} = 0.3146 \text{ min}$$

Time for 12-log reduction:

$$t = 12 \times 0.3146 = 3.776 \text{ min} \approx 3.78 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 9°C temperature increase reduces the required sterilization time by an order of magnitude (factor of 10). This is the engineering basis for "high-temperature short-time" (HTST) sterilization in the food and pharmaceutical industries.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, CO1, Bloom Level: Remember]**

Define the **Decimal Reduction Factor (D-value)** in the context of thermal death kinetics of microorganisms. A bacterial culture with $D_{70°C} = 2.0$ min is heated at 70°C. How long will it take to reduce the population from $10^6$ cells/mL to $10^2$ cells/mL?

#### Model Answer

**Definition (2 marks):** The Decimal Reduction Factor (D-value) is the time, in minutes, required at a specified constant temperature to reduce the viable microbial population by 90% (i.e., by one logarithmic cycle or a factor of 10). It is the reciprocal of the first-order specific death rate constant scaled by 2.303:

$$D = \frac{2.303}{k_d}$$

**Calculation (1 mark):** Required log reduction = $\log(10^6) - \log(10^2) = 6 - 2 = 4$ log cycles

$$t = n \cdot D = 4 \times 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ minutes}$$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, CO1, Bloom Level: Understand]**

Explain how **ionic strength** of the fermentation medium affects microbial growth kinetics. Write the equation for ionic strength.

#### Model Answer

**Ionic Strength Equation (1 mark):**

$$I = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i z_i^2$$

**Effect on Growth (2 marks):** Ionic strength affects microbial growth kinetics in the following ways:
1. At **low ionic strength**, the osmotic imbalance causes water to enter the cell, leading to cell swelling and possible lysis, reducing the specific growth rate.
2. At **optimal ionic strength** (typically 0.01–0.5 M for mesophiles), the cell membrane potential, enzyme conformation, and water activity are maintained at ideal levels, supporting maximum $\mu$.
3. At **high ionic strength**, water activity ($a_w$) decreases, plasmolysis occurs, and cells must expend additional energy to synthesize compatible solutes (e.g., ectoine, glycine betaine), reducing the net specific growth rate and biomass yield.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Alternative 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, CO1, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply & Analyze]**

**(a)** Derive the **Monod equation** for microbial growth as a function of substrate concentration. State clearly the assumptions made. Discuss the **Andrews substrate inhibition model** and derive the expression for the critical substrate concentration $[S]_{crit}$ at which the specific growth rate is maximum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemostat operates with $\mu_{max} = 0.5$ h⁻¹, $K_s = 0.2$ g/L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.4$ g/g, and $S_{in} = 10$ g/L. The dilution rate is $D = 0.3$ h⁻¹. Calculate the steady-state substrate concentration, biomass concentration, and the specific substrate uptake rate. What happens to the washout condition if $D$ is increased to 0.6 h⁻¹? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Monod Derivation and Andrews Model**

**Monod Equation Derivation (4 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. Microbial growth is exponential: $\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$
2. A single limiting substrate controls growth
3. $\mu$ is a hyperbolic, saturating function of [S]
4. Cell density is much lower than substrate concentration (no substrate depletion by cell mass)

*Derivation:*
At very low [S]: $\mu = k_1 [S]$ (first-order in substrate)
At very high [S]: $\mu = \mu_{max}$ (zero-order, saturation)

Combine using hyperbolic form: $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{A + B[S]}$

Limit $[S] \to \infty$: $B = 1$ (so denominator $\to [S]$, $\mu \to \mu_{max}$)
Limit $[S] \to 0$: $A = \mu_{max}/k_1 = K_s$ (half-saturation constant)

Substituting:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{K_s + [S]}$$

**Andrews Model and Critical Substrate Concentration (3 marks):**

The Andrews equation incorporates a quadratic substrate inhibition term:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{K_s + [S] + \frac{[S]^2}{K_{I,S}}}$$

Differentiating with respect to [S] and setting the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d\mu}{d[S]} = 0$$

This gives (as shown in full derivation in Section 3.2):

$$[S]_{crit} = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_{I,S}}$$

Operating above this concentration leads to reduced growth — important in designing fed-batch fermentations to avoid substrate-inhibition-induced overflow metabolism.

---

**Part (b) — Chemostat Calculations**

**Steady-state substrate concentration (3 marks):**

In a chemostat at steady state, $D = \mu$. Using Monod:

$$D = \frac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S}$$

$$0.3 = \frac{0.5 \cdot S}{0.2 + S}$$

$$0.3(0.2 + S) = 0.5S$$

$$0.06 + 0.3S = 0.5S$$

$$0.06 = 0.2S \quad \Rightarrow \quad S = 0.3 \text{ g/L}$$

**Steady-state biomass concentration (2 marks):**

Biomass balance: $X = Y_{X/S}(S_{in} - S)$

$$X = 0.4 \times (10 - 0.3) = 0.4 \times 9.7 = 3.88 \text{ g/L}$$

**Specific substrate uptake rate (1 mark):**

$$q_S = \frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}} = \frac{0.3}{0.4} = 0.75 \text{ g substrate/g biomass/h}$$

**Washout analysis (1 mark):**

Critical dilution rate: $D_c = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{10}{0.2 + 10} = 0.5 \times 0.9804 = 0.4902 \text{ h}^{-1}$

Since the new $D = 0.6 \text{ h}^{-1} > D_c = 0.4902 \text{ h}^{-1}$, **washout will occur** — all cells will be flushed out and biomass concentration will drop to zero.

---

### Question B — Alternative 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, CO2, CO3, Bloom Level: Apply & Analyze]**

**(a)** Derive the **first-order thermal death kinetics equation** starting from $\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d N$. Show that the D-value equals $\frac{2.303}{k_d}$. Explain the concept of the **survival curve** and discuss what biological phenomena cause deviations from log-linearity (e.g., shoulders, tailing). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fermentation medium contains $N_0 = 2 \times 10^9$ spores/mL. The D-value at 121°C is 2.5 min and the Z-value is 10°C. The target sterility level is a 15-log reduction (i.e., $N_f = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ spores/mL, considered commercially sterile). Calculate: (i) the sterilization time at 121°C, and (ii) the sterilization time at 131°C. Comment on the design implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Thermal Death Derivation**

**Derivation of First-Order Kinetics and D-Value (4 marks):**

Starting from:
$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d N$$

Separate variables:
$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \, dt$$

Integrate from $N_0$ to $N$ and 0 to $t$:
$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_d t \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = N_0 e^{-k_d t}$$

Convert to base-10:
$$\log N = \log N_0 - \frac{k_d}{2.303} t$$

By definition, D-value is the time at which $N = N_0/10$, so:
$$\log\left(\frac{N_0/10}{N_0}\right) = -1 = -\frac{k_d}{2.303} D$$

Therefore:
$$D = \frac{2.303}{k_d}$$

**Survival Curve Concept and Deviations (3 marks):**

A survival curve is a semi-log plot of $\log N$ vs $t$. For first-order kinetics, it is a straight line with slope $-1/D$. Deviations from log-linearity include:

- **Shoulder (initial lag/plateau):** Observed when multiple "hits" are required to inactivate a cell (multi-hit kinetics), or when cells are clumped and clumps must be broken before individual cells are killed.

- **Tailing (late plateau):** Caused by heterogeneity in the population — a small subpopulation of heat-resistant cells (often bacterial spores among vegetative cells, or heat-adapted cells) survives longer than the majority. For process design, the **D-value of the resistant tail fraction** must be used (conservative design).

- **Concave-up (biphasic):** Indicates a mixed population with two distinct death rates.

---

**Part (b) — Sterilization Time Calculations**

**Part (i) — Time at 121°C (3 marks):**

Required log reduction:
$$n = \log N_0 - \log N_f = \log\left(\frac{2 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = \log(10^{15}) = 15$$

Time at 121°C:
$$t_{121} = n \cdot D_{121} = 15 \times 2.5 = 37.5 \text{ minutes}$$

**Part (ii) — Time at 131°C (3 marks):**

Using the Z-value relation:
$$\log D_{131} = \log D_{121} - \frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z} = \log(2.5) - \frac{131 - 121}{10}$$

$$\log D_{131} = 0.3979 - 1.0 = -0.6021$$

$$D_{131} = 10^{-0.6021} = 0.25 \text{ min}$$

Time at 131°C:
$$t_{131} = 15 \times 0.25 = 3.75 \text{ minutes}$$

**Design Implications (1 mark):**

A 10°C increase in sterilization temperature reduces the required time by a factor of 10 (from 37.5 min to 3.75 min). This is the fundamental basis of **HTST (High-Temperature Short-Time)** sterilization, which minimizes thermal degradation of heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamins, proteins) in the medium while achieving commercial sterility. In industrial practice, continuous sterilizers operating at 130–140°C for 1–5 minutes are preferred over batch autoclaves at 121°C for 30+ minutes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **D-value confusion (1–2 marks lost):** Students often forget to write the reference temperature subscript. Always write $D_{121°C}$ or $D_T$ — never just "D".
> 2. **Log reduction arithmetic (1–2 marks lost):** Mixing natural log ($\ln$) and base-10 log ($\log$) within the same derivation. The factor $2.303 = \ln(10)$ must be explicitly used when converting.
> 3. **Andrews vs Monod (1 mark lost):** Forgetting that Andrews applies only when substrate inhibition is observed. Do not write Andrews equation when the problem does not mention high [S] inhibition.
> 4. **Washout condition (1–2 marks lost):** The washout condition is $D = D_c$ (critical dilution rate), not $D = \mu_{max}$. Many students incorrectly write washout at $D = \mu_{max}$.
> 5. **Z-value sign (1 mark lost):** Z-value is always **positive** (°C). The formula $\log D_1 - \log D_2 = (T_2 - T_1)/Z$ must maintain this sign convention.
> 6. **Units omission (1 mark lost):** Always state the units (min, °C, g/L, h⁻¹). KTU examiners explicitly allocate 0.5–1 mark for units in numerical problems.
> 7. **Survival curve interpretation (1 mark lost):** Students often draw the curve on linear axes. KTU expects semi-log plot with $\log N$ on y-axis.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Ionic Strength — Key Points
- $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$ — accounts for charge, not just concentration
- Optimal range: **0.01–0.5 M** for mesophilic bacteria
- High I → reduced $a_w$, plasmolysis, energy diversion to osmoregulation
- Low I → osmotic lysis, membrane disruption
- Industrially critical: antifoam, pH control, and metabolic byproducts all change I

### Substrate Concentration — Key Points
- **Monod equation:** $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{K_s + [S]}$ — three regimes (limited, transitional, saturated)
- $K_s$ = substrate concentration at $\mu = 0.5 \mu_{max}$ (analogous to $K_m$ in Michaelis-Menten)
- **Andrews equation** for substrate inhibition: $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}[S]}{K_s + [S] + [S]^2/K_{I,S}}$
- $[S]_{crit} = \sqrt{K_s K_{I,S}}$ — the concentration above which growth decreases
- Fed-batch strategies aim to keep [S] below $[S]_{crit}$ to avoid overflow metabolism

### Thermal Death Kinetics — Key Points
- **First-order death:** $\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d N$ — universal for vegetative cells
- **D-value:** $D = 2.303/k_d$ — time for 90% (1-log) reduction at temperature T
- **Z-value:** slope of $\log D$ vs T plot — temperature change for 10-fold change in D
- **F-value ($F_0$):** equivalent sterilization time at reference T (121.1°C)
- **Commercial sterility for low-acid foods:** $F_0 \geq 3$ min (12-log reduction of *Clostridium botulinum*)
- **Parenteral sterilization:** $F_0 \geq 8$–15 min
- Survival curve: $\log N$ vs $t$ is linear for first-order kinetics; deviations indicate subpopulation heterogeneity
- The relationship $t = n \cdot D$ is fundamental to process design
- HTST sterilization exploits high Z-value relationship: small T increase → large D decrease

### Quick Conversion Table for Last-Minute Revision
| Parameter | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| D-value | $D = 2.303/k_d$ | min |
| Z-value | $Z = (T_2 - T_1)/(\log D_1 - \log D_2)$ | °C |
| F-value | $F_0 = \Delta t \sum 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$ | min |
| Sterilization time for n-log reduction | $t = n \cdot D$ | min |
| Critical substrate concentration (Andrews) | $[S]_{crit} = \sqrt{K_s K_{I,S}}$ | g/L |
| Ionic strength | $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$ | mol/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always quote D-value with its reference temperature, always state units in numerical answers, and always draw survival curves on semi-log axes. These three habits alone can recover 2–3 marks per question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extinction Probability, Sterilization of Culture Medium - Batch and Continuous Sterilization, Design Aspects, Air Sterilization, Design of Fibrous Type Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Extinction Probability, Sterilization of Culture Medium - Batch and Continuous Sterilization, Design Aspects, Air Sterilization, Design of Fibrous Type Filters # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Extinction Probability (Probability of Non-Sterility) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** Extinction Probability is defined as the probability that a single viable contaminant (spore or micro-organism) survives the sterilization process and ultimately contaminates the final batch. In industrial fermentation, this is referred to as the **Probability of Failure (P_f)** or **Probability of Non-Sterility**, and it forms the foundation of all sterilization design calculations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:** > Extinction Probability (P_f) = 1 − e^(−N), where N is the expected number of viable organisms remaining in the total fermenter volume after sterilization. For N ≪ 1, the approximation **P_f ≈ N** is universally used in KTU problems. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a submarine fleet of 1,000 enemy submarines entering a minefield. The "minefield" is your sterilization cycle. If exactly 3 submarines survive the mines, the probability that **at least one** sneaks through to your harbour is essentially 3 out of 1,000 — because the numbers are so small. That is exactly the logic of extinction probability: we count how many "escapees" survive, and for tiny numbers, that count itself is the failure probability. > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule:** KTU standard industrial practice assumes P_f ≤ 10⁻³ for media sterilization. This means the expected number of survivors in the entire fermenter must be ≤ 0.001. --- ## 1.2 Sterilization of Culture Medium The culture medium (containing sugars, proteins, salts, vitamins) must be **devoid of all viable micro-organisms** before inoculation, otherwise contaminants will out-compete the production strain. Two engineering strategies exist: ### A. Batch Sterilization The medium is heated **inside the fermenter itself** (or in a separate batch sterilizer) to a sterilization temperature (~121 °C), held, and then cooled. This is the **"in-situ"** approach. **Intuition:** Like pressure-cooking a biryani in a single vessel — heat, hold, cool, then inoculate. Simple but energy-inefficient because the entire large vessel must go through heating and cooling cycles. ### B. Continuous Sterilization The medium flows continuously through a **heat exchanger, holding coil, and cooler**. Only a small volume is at sterilization temperature at any given time. **Intuition:** Like a hot water geyser pipeline — cold water enters, gets heated quickly in a small pipe, travels through a holding coil for the required kill-time, then cools rapidly. Energy-efficient and gentler on heat-sensitive nutrients. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Comparison:** > - Batch = Simple, but high degradation of heat-labile nutrients, longer cycle. > - Continuous = Lower degradation, shorter hold time, smaller equipment, higher throughput, more expensive instrumentation. --- ## 1.3 Air Sterilization Air supplied to an aerobic fermenter (1–2 vvm) carries enormous microbial loads (~10³–10⁴ organisms/m³ of ambient air). Air sterilization uses: - **Fibrous (depth) cartridge filters** — most common industrial method. - Membrane filters — for sterile labs. - Heat exchangers — for very large fermenters. **Intuition:** Air sterilization is like forcing a dusty room's air through a thick cotton-mattress — particles physically get trapped inside the fibrous depth, not just on the surface. --- ## 1.4 Design of Fibrous Type Filters **Formal Definition:** A fibrous (depth) filter is a packed bed of randomly oriented fibers (typically borosilicate glass or polypropylene) of diameter 0.5–30 μm and bed thickness 5–50 cm. Particles are captured by **diffusion, interception, inertial impaction, and gravitational/electrostatic mechanisms** acting collectively on a single fiber. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Microbial Death Kinetics The death of vegetative cells and spores follows first-order kinetics: $$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$ Integrating: $$N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$ Where: - **N₀** = initial viable cell/spore concentration (cells·mL⁻¹) - **N** = viable concentration at time t - **k** = specific death rate constant (min⁻¹), depends strongly on T - **t** = exposure time (min) ### Why first-order? (The "Why" behind the formula) Death is treated as a single-hit, single-target phenomenon — a single critical event (e.g., DNA, membrane damage) kills one cell. By Poisson statistics, the fraction surviving is exponential. --- ## 2.2 Del Factor (∇) — The Master Design Parameter $$\nabla = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = k \cdot t$$ Equivalently, expressed via **decimal reduction time (d)** (time for 90 % kill, i.e., a factor of 10): $$\nabla = \frac{2.303 \cdot t}{d} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad t = \frac{\nabla \cdot d}{2.303}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Del Factor is the backbone of every KTU sterilization calculation.** Always compute ∇ first, then use it to find the time, volume, or flow rate. --- ## 2.3 Design Criterion Using Extinction Probability For a fermenter of volume V_F (mL) with sterile-feed containing N₀ organisms/mL entering with probability of non-sterility: $$P_f = 1 - e^{-N_{total}}$$ where **N_total = (N₀ survivors) × (V_F) × (fraction surviving)**. Industrial design criterion: **N_total = 0.001**, giving **P_f ≈ 10⁻³**. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|----------|---------|-------| | 1 | $N = N_0 \, e^{-k t}$ | First-order death | cells·mL⁻¹ | | 2 | $\nabla = \ln(N_0 / N)$ | Del factor | dimensionless | | 3 | $\nabla = k \cdot t$ | Del–time relation | dimensionless | | 4 | $\nabla = 2.303 \, t / d$ | Del via decimal time | dimensionless | | 5 | $P_f \approx N_{total}$ | Extinction probability (small N) | dimensionless | | 6 | $\nabla_{total} = \nabla_{heat} + \nabla_{hold} + \nabla_{cool}$ | Continuous sterilizer (plug flow) | dimensionless | | 7 | $t_h = V_h / Q$ | Holding-coil residence time | min | | 8 | $\Delta t = \int (1/k(T)) \, dT$ | Equivalent time at reference T (batch with ramp) | min | | 9 | $\ln(1/P) = \eta_f \cdot \dfrac{4 \alpha L}{\pi d_f}$ | Fibrous filter penetration | dimensionless | | 10 | $\eta_f = 1 - (1-\eta_D)(1-\eta_R)(1-\eta_I)(1-\eta_E)(1-\eta_G)$ | Single-fiber total efficiency | dimensionless | --- ## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Industry - They guarantee **regulatory cGMP compliance** (no contamination). - They minimize **nutrient degradation** (over-sterilization destroys glucose, vitamins). - They reduce **energy cost** — knowing exact hold-time avoids wasted steam. - They enable **scale-up** from 10 L lab to 100,000 L production fermenter. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of First-Order Death Kinetics → Del Factor **Step 1 — Start with empirical rate law:** A single lethal hit kills one organism. By Poisson, the probability of surviving k hits in time t is e^(−kt). $$N = N_0 \, e^{-k t}$$ **Step 2 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:** $$\ln N = \ln N_0 - k t$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate time:** $$k t = \ln N_0 - \ln N = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Define Del factor:** $$\boxed{\nabla \;\equiv\; k t \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — Introduce decimal reduction time d:** By definition, when t = d, N = N₀/10 (a 10-fold reduction). $$\ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_0/10}\right) = \ln 10 = k d \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = \frac{\ln 10}{d} = \frac{2.303}{d}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back:** $$\nabla = k t = \frac{2.303 \, t}{d}$$ This two-way relation (k ↔ d) is what KTU examiners use to swap parameters in problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Extinction Probability (P_f) **Step 1 — A single fermenter contains many "independent" small sub-volumes.** Each sub-volume experiences an independent Poisson chance of contamination. After sterilization, the **expected** number of survivors per fermenter is: $$N_{total} = N_{final} \times V_F$$ **Step 2 — Poisson distribution for the number of survivors in fermenter:** Probability that exactly n survivors exist: $$P(n) = \frac{N_{total}^{\,n}}{n!} \, e^{-N_{total}}$$ **Step 3 — Probability of "zero survivors" (fully sterile):** $$P(n=0) = e^{-N_{total}}$$ **Step 4 — Probability of "at least one survivor" (NON-sterile, i.e., extinction fails):** $$P_f = 1 - P(0) = 1 - e^{-N_{total}}$$ **Step 5 — Taylor expansion for small N_total (N_total ≪ 1):** $$e^{-N_{total}} \approx 1 - N_{total} + \frac{N_{total}^2}{2!} - \dots$$ $$\therefore \quad P_f \approx N_{total}$$ **Final design condition:** $$\boxed{N_{total} = N_{final} \times V_F \;\le\; 0.001}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Continuous Sterilization Design Equation In continuous (plug-flow) sterilization, medium passes through three zones: 1. **Heating section** (heat exchanger) — temperature rises from T₀ to T_s. 2. **Holding coil** — isothermal at T_s. 3. **Cooling section** — temperature falls back to T₀. **Step 1 — Total Del factor is the time-integral of k over the path:** $$\nabla_{total} = \int_0^{t_{total}} k(T(t)) \, dt$$ **Step 2 — Split into three contributions:** $$\nabla_{total} = \nabla_{heat} + \nabla_{hold} + \nabla_{cool}$$ **Step 3 — Holding contribution (isothermal at T_s):** $$\nabla_{hold} = k_s \cdot t_h$$ **Step 4 — Holding time from volumetric flow rate:** If holding coil volume = V_h and volumetric flow rate = Q: $$t_h = \frac{V_h}{Q}$$ $$\nabla_{hold} = \frac{k_s \, V_h}{Q}$$ **Step 5 — Heating and cooling contributions:** For linear temperature ramps of duration t_heat and t_cool: $$\nabla_{heat} = \frac{1}{\alpha_H} \int_{T_0}^{T_s} \frac{dT}{(T_s - T_0)} \cdot \frac{1}{k(T)}$$ In KTU problems these are often given numerically or assumed small compared to ∇_hold. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Fibrous Filter Design Equation (Penetration) **Step 1 — A filter of thickness L and packing density α (= volume of fibers / total volume) contains fibers of diameter d_f.** **Step 2 — Number of fibers encountered per unit length of flow path:** $$n_f = \frac{4 \alpha}{\pi d_f^2}$$ **Step 3 — Multiplication by filter length L gives a dimensionless parameter proportional to fiber count:** $$\beta = \frac{4 \alpha L}{\pi d_f}$$ **Step 4 — Each fiber captures a fraction η_f of the particles flowing past it (single-fiber efficiency).** The probability of NOT being captured by a single fiber is (1 − η_f). **Step 5 — Probability of passing through the full filter without being captured:** $$P = (1 - \eta_f)^{\beta}$$ **Step 6 — Take logarithm (ln) and use ln(1 − x) ≈ −x for small η_f:** $$\ln P = \beta \, \ln(1 - \eta_f) \approx -\eta_f \, \beta$$ **Step 7 — Final penetration equation:** $$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P}\right) = \eta_f \cdot \frac{4 \alpha L}{\pi d_f}}$$ Where **P = N_out / N_in** (fraction penetrating). --- ## 3.5 Single-Fiber Efficiency Components | Mechanism | Symbol | Dominates when | Approx. Functional Form | |-----------|--------|----------------|-------------------------| | Brownian diffusion | η_D | Small d_p, low velocity | $\eta_D \propto Pe^{-2/3}$ | | Direct interception | η_R | d_p ≈ d_f | $\eta_R \propto d_p/d_f$ | | Inertial impaction | η_I | d_p large, high velocity | $\eta_I \propto Stk$ | | Electrostatic attraction | η_E | Charged particles/fibers | $\eta_E \propto (\text{field})^2$ | | Gravitational settling | η_G | Very large d_p | Small contribution | Total: $$\eta_f = 1 - (1-\eta_D)(1-\eta_R)(1-\eta_I)(1-\eta_E)(1-\eta_G)$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Reality Check:** For typical bacterial spores (~1 μm) at 0.1–1 m/s face velocity, **diffusion + interception** account for ≥ 80 % of total capture. Inertial impaction matters only above ~5 μm. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Continuous Sterilization System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Tank
Raw Medium T0] --> B[Steam Heater
Heat Exchanger] B --> C[Holding Coil
Isothermal Ts] C --> D[Cooler
Heat Exchanger] D --> E[Sterile Medium
to Fermenter T0] B -.->|∇heat| F[Total Del Factor
Calculation] C -.->|∇hold| F D -.->|∇cool| F F --> G{∇total
meets P_f criterion?} G -->|Yes| H[Valid Design] G -->|No| I[Increase t_h or T_s] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Batch Sterilization Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Load Fermenter
with raw medium] --> B[Heat-Up Phase
T0 to Ts] B --> C[Holding Phase
at Ts for t_s] C --> D[Cooling Phase
Ts to T0] D --> E{Aseptic Check
P_f achieved?} E -->|Yes| F[Inoculate
with Production Strain] E -->|No| G[Reject Batch
Repeat Cycle] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Fibrous Filter Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Contaminated Air In] --> B[Pre-Filter
Coarse Particles] B --> C[Primary Fibrous Bed
Glass Fibers 0.5-30 um] C --> D[Secondary Fibrous Bed
Fine Fibers] D --> E[Sterile Air Out
to Fermenter] C -.-> F[Capture Mechanisms] F --> G[Brownian Diffusion] F --> H[Interception] F --> I[Inertial Impaction] F --> J[Electrostatic] ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Batch vs Continuous Sterilization | Aspect | Batch Sterilization | Continuous Sterilization | |--------|---------------------|--------------------------| | Equipment | Same fermenter used | Separate heat exchanger + coil | | Hold Time | 20–60 min at 121 °C | 1.5–8 min at 130–140 °C | | Steam Economy | Poor (entire vessel heated) | Excellent (recuperative heat exchange) | | Nutrient Damage | High (long exposure) | Low (short exposure) | | Cycle Time | 4–6 h total | 30–60 min | | Capital Cost | Low | High | | Reliability | Simple controls | Complex instrumentation | | Scale-up | Easy | Easy, modular | ## 4.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Filter | Particle Size | Face Velocity | Recommended Mechanism | Typical η_f | |---------------|---------------|-----------------------|-------------| | < 0.1 μm (virus) | Low (< 0.1 m/s) | Diffusion + Electrostatic | 0.90–0.99 | | 0.1–1 μm (bacteria) | Low | Diffusion + Interception | 0.80–0.95 | | 1–5 μm (yeast/spores) | Medium | Interception + Impaction | 0.85–0.98 | | > 5 μm (dust) | Any | Inertial Impaction | 0.95–0.999 | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define extinction probability. Why is it set to 10⁻³ in industrial fermenter design? [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Extinction probability (P_f) is the probability that at least one viable contaminant survives the sterilization cycle and contaminates the fermenter. It is mathematically expressed as: $$P_f = 1 - e^{-N_{total}}$$ where N_total is the expected number of survivors in the entire fermenter volume. For N_total ≪ 1, the approximation **P_f ≈ N_total** holds. **Why 10⁻³?** (1 mark) Industrial standards (FDA, WHO, ISPE) accept one contamination failure per 1,000 sterilization cycles as the maximum tolerable risk for aseptic fermentation. This balances safety with economic feasibility. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between batch and continuous sterilization with two points each. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Batch | Continuous | |-----------|-------|-----------| | Heat exposure | Long (20–60 min) | Short (1.5–8 min) | | Equipment | Fermenter itself | Heat exchanger + coil | | Energy use | Poor | Excellent | | Nutrient loss | High | Low | (Any two correct comparisons with one mark each.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]** **Design a continuous sterilizer to process 50,000 L/h of fermentation medium. The medium initially contains 5 × 10⁵ spores/mL. The desired sterilization temperature is 135 °C, at which the specific death rate k = 4.0 min⁻¹. Assume plug flow and negligible Del contribution from heating and cooling. Target extinction probability P_f ≤ 10⁻³. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** #### **Part (a) — Calculate the holding time and holding-coil volume. [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Required Del factor from extinction probability criterion:** For the entire fermenter (assume volume = 50,000 L = 5 × 10⁷ mL), and P_f = 10⁻³: $$P_f \approx N_{total} = N_{final} \times V_F = 10^{-3}$$ $$N_{final} = \frac{10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^7} = 2 \times 10^{-11} \;\text{spores/mL}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Del factor:** $$\nabla = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_{final}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^5}{2 \times 10^{-11}}\right)$$ $$\nabla = \ln(2.5 \times 10^{16}) = 16.03 + \ln(2.5)$$ $$\boxed{\nabla = 16.03 + 0.916 = 36.6}$$ **Step 3 — Holding time from ∇ = k·t_h:** $$t_h = \frac{\nabla}{k} = \frac{36.6}{4.0} = 9.15 \;\text{min}$$ **Step 4 — Holding-coil volume from Q = 50,000 L/h = 833.3 L/min:** $$V_h = Q \times t_h = 833.3 \times 9.15 = 7,624 \;\text{L} \approx 7.62 \;\text{m}^3$$ --- #### **Part (b) — If the holding coil is a pipe of diameter 15 cm, calculate the length. [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of pipe:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \;\text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Pipe length:** $$L = \frac{V_h}{A} = \frac{7.62}{0.01767} = 431.2 \;\text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Sanity check on Reynolds number:** $$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{833.3 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01767 \times 60} = 0.786 \;\text{m/s}$$ For water-like density ρ = 1000 kg/m³ and μ = 0.001 Pa·s: $$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.786 \times 0.15}{0.001} = 1.18 \times 10^5$$ This is turbulent — plug-flow assumption is reasonable. --- ### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks]** **A fibrous air filter is constructed from glass fibers of diameter 8 μm with packing density α = 0.08. The bed thickness is 25 cm. Single-fiber efficiencies are: diffusion η_D = 0.05, interception η_R = 0.18, impaction η_I = 0.02. Calculate the penetration P of 1 μm bacterial spores. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** #### **Part (a) — Calculate the total single-fiber efficiency η_f. [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Total single-fiber efficiency (assuming independent mechanisms):** $$\eta_f = 1 - (1 - \eta_D)(1 - \eta_R)(1 - \eta_I)$$ $$\eta_f = 1 - (0.95)(0.82)(0.98)$$ $$\eta_f = 1 - 0.7634 = 0.2366$$ (Assuming electrostatic and gravitational terms are negligible, i.e., η_E = η_G = 0.) --- #### **Part (b) — Calculate the penetration fraction P. [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Compute the dimensionless filter parameter β:** $$\beta = \frac{4 \alpha L}{\pi d_f} = \frac{4 \times 0.08 \times 0.25}{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\beta = \frac{0.08}{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.08}{2.513 \times 10^{-5}} = 3,183$$ **Step 2 — Apply penetration equation:** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P}\right) = \eta_f \cdot \beta = 0.2366 \times 3183 = 753$$ $$\frac{1}{P} = e^{753} \;\Rightarrow\; P = e^{-753} \approx 0 \text{ (essentially zero penetration)}$$ This confirms the filter provides **complete sterile air** — the design is heavily over-engineered (typical, because safety margins are required for cGMP). **Step 3 — Required inlet concentration for ≤ 1 organism/day:** If outlet must contain ≤ 1 spore/day with air flow of 100 m³/h (10⁵ L/h): $$N_{in} \times Q \times 24 \times P \leq 1$$ $$N_{in} \times 10^5 \times 24 \times e^{-753} \leq 1 \text{ — automatically satisfied for any realistic } N_{in}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (L/h ↔ m³/min) — costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Using P_f = N_total directly without checking N_total ≪ 1** — for very heavy contamination, the exponential form must be retained. > 3. **In fibrous filter problems, students forget to multiply single-fiber efficiency by β = 4αL/(πd_f)** before taking ln. > 4. **Mixing up batch vs continuous Del factor definitions** — batch uses total time integrated, continuous uses plug-flow summation. > 5. **Not reporting the d_f in SI units** (μm → m) before computation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam-Day Bullet Summary (Re-read 5 min before exam):** - ✅ Extinction Probability: **P_f = 1 − e^(−N) ≈ N** (for small N). - ✅ Design criterion: **P_f ≤ 10⁻³**, i.e., N_total ≤ 0.001 in fermenter. - ✅ Death kinetics: **N = N₀ · e^(−kt)**. - ✅ Del factor: **∇ = ln(N₀/N) = k·t = 2.303·t/d**. - ✅ Decimal reduction time **d** = time for 90 % kill = 2.303/k. - ✅ Batch sterilization: Total time = heating + holding + cooling. - ✅ Continuous: **∇_total = ∇_heat + ∇_hold + ∇_cool**, holding t_h = V_h/Q. - ✅ Batch uses fermenter itself; continuous uses separate heat exchanger + holding coil. - ✅ Continuous advantages: less nutrient damage, energy-efficient, scalable. - ✅ Air sterilization needed because ambient air contains 10³–10⁴ organisms/m³. - ✅ Fibrous filter mechanism: depth filtration, NOT surface filtration. - ✅ Penetration: **ln(1/P) = η_f · 4αL / (πd_f)**. - ✅ Single-fiber mechanisms: **Diffusion + Interception + Impaction + Electrostatic + Gravitational**. - ✅ For 1 μm spores at low velocity, **diffusion + interception dominate**. - ✅ Filter is usually **grossly over-designed** (efficiency ~ 99.9999... %). - ✅ At high T, **k is large → ∇ is large → fast kill** (Arrhenius temperature dependence). - ✅ Typical industrial sterilization: 121 °C × 20 min (batch) OR 135 °C × 5–8 min (continuous). - ✅ Sterile design failure cost >> sterilization energy cost → over-design is justified. - ✅ Always convert: μm → m, L/h → m³/min, °C → K (if using Arrhenius).
