<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — CORE DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 🧪 PART A: MEDIUM DESIGN ENGINEERING IN BIOPROCESSING

### 1.1 What is a Bioprocess Medium?

A **bioprocess medium** (also called a *fermentation broth* or *nutrient medium*) is a carefully formulated aqueous solution containing all the nutrients required to support microbial growth, metabolism, and product formation. It is the **biological "fuel"** that determines the productivity, yield, and economic feasibility of any fermentation process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A bioprocess medium is a chemically defined or complex aqueous substrate formulated to supply carbon, nitrogen, minerals, vitamins, growth factors, and environmental conditions (pH, dissolved oxygen, temperature) necessary for the optimal physiological and metabolic performance of a microbial, animal, or plant cell culture in a bioreactor.

### 1.2 Growth Medium vs. Production Medium

| Feature | **Growth Medium** | **Production Medium** |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Maximize biomass formation | Maximize product (metabolite) formation |
| **Nutrient Profile** | Rich, balanced, fast-growing | Often **limited** in one key nutrient (e.g., nitrogen) |
| **Growth Phase Targeted** | Exponential / trophophase | Stationary / idiophase |
| **Examples** | Luria-Bertani (LB) broth | Penicillin production medium (limited N) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a growth medium as a "balanced buffet" given to a child for healthy growth — proteins, carbs, vitamins all in good measure. A production medium, in contrast, is like a "specialized diet" given to a worker to maximize a specific task — like a bodybuilder's high-protein, low-fat diet. You *starve* the cells in one nutrient so they divert energy into the product of interest.

### 1.3 Stoichiometric Design Approach

The **stoichiometric design approach** uses elemental mass balances (Carbon, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Hydrogen) to calculate the **theoretical minimum nutrient requirements** to produce a given quantity of biomass. It is based on:

- **Elemental composition of biomass** (typical: CH₁.₈₀₆N₀.₂₅O₀.₅₅)
- **Yield coefficients** (Yₓ/ₛ, Yₚ/ₛ)
- **Degree of reduction balance**

### 1.4 Bioorganic Reaction Medium Engineering

This is the modern approach where the medium is engineered **based on the specific biochemical reaction pathway** being exploited. It considers:
- **Precursor molecules** (e.g., phenylalanine for penicillin side-chain)
- **Inducer molecules** (e.g., IPTG for recombinant protein expression)
- **Metabolic regulators** (feedback inhibitors are removed)
- **Rate-limiting enzymes** are activated by adding specific substrates

### 1.5 Novel Media

> [!NOTE]
> **Novel media** are next-generation engineered nutrient systems designed to overcome the limitations of traditional complex media. They include:
> - **Defined (chemically pure) media** — for reproducibility
> - **Animal-component-free (ACF) media** — for pharmaceutical safety
> - **Plant-based protein hydrolysates** — replacing yeast extract
> - **Designer media for high-cell-density culture (HCDC)**
> - **Microfluidic / single-cell media formulations**

---

## 📈 PART B: KINETIC MODELLING OF CELL GROWTH

### 2.1 What is Kinetic Modelling?

Kinetic modelling is the **mathematical representation of microbial growth rate** as a function of environmental and physiological variables (substrate concentration, pH, temperature, inhibitors, etc.). It is the backbone of bioreactor design, scale-up, and process control.

### 2.2 Model Structure and Complexity — The Classification Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Models are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:
> 1. **Structure** — *Unstructured* vs *Structured*
> 2. **Segregation** — *Unsegregated* vs *Segregated*

This creates **4 classes of models**:

| Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unstructured – Unsegregated** | Cells treated as a black-box, average population | **Monod Equation** |
| **Structured – Unsegregated** | Cells have internal compartments/chemical pools | Cybernetic models |
| **Unstructured – Segregated** | Population distributed by age/size | Population balance models |
| **Structured – Segregated** | Most detailed; age + internal chemistry | Single-cell models |

### 2.3 The Monod Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Monod Equation (1949)** is the cornerstone unstructured-unsegregated kinetic model, proposed by **Jacques Monod** — analogous in form to the Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetics equation.

$$
\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}
$$

Where **μ** is specific growth rate (h⁻¹), **μ_max** is maximum specific growth rate, **S** is limiting substrate concentration, and **K_s** is the half-saturation constant (substrate concentration at which μ = ½ μ_max).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a buffet restaurant. As you add more tables (substrate S), the rate at which guests enter the dining room (growth rate μ) increases — but only up to a maximum, after which adding more tables doesn't help because the restaurant is already full (μ_max). The **K_s** is like a "softness" parameter — a low K_s means even a small amount of substrate is enough to reach near-maximum growth (eager eater), while a high K_s means the microbe is "picky" and needs lots of substrate.

### 2.4 Monod Chemostat Model

A **chemostat** is a continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) with sterile feed inflow and cell + spent medium outflow. The Monod chemostat model predicts steady-state concentrations of biomass and substrate as functions of the **dilution rate (D)**.

### 2.5 Models with Growth Inhibitors (Substrate Inhibition)

At high substrate concentrations, growth is often **inhibited**. Key models:

**Andrews (Haldane) Equation:**
$$
\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S + \dfrac{S^2}{K_i}}
$$

**Other inhibition models:**
- Aiba-Edwards: Exponential inhibition term
- Webb: Substrate inhibition with cooperativity
- Yano: Generalized model with multiple inhibition constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Substrate Inhibition):** Substrate inhibition is like over-watering a plant. A little water (substrate) helps growth, but too much water "drowns" the roots. The **K_i** parameter represents the plant's "tolerance threshold" — high K_i means the cell can handle lots of substrate before getting poisoned.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 🧪 PART A: MEDIUM DESIGN — STEP-BY-STEP THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK

### 2.1 The Design Procedure for Growth and Production Media (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a **systematic 6-step design procedure**:

**Step 1 — Define the Biological System**
- Identify the strain (bacteria, yeast, mold, plant, animal cells)
- Define the goal: biomass, primary metabolite, secondary metabolite, or recombinant protein

**Step 2 — Determine Stoichiometric Requirements**
- Use elemental balance equation
- Carbon source is calculated based on Yₓ/ₛ (yield of biomass on substrate)

**Step 3 — Identify Required Nutrients (Macronutrients + Micronutrients)**
- **Carbon source** (glucose, sucrose, glycerol, starch)
- **Nitrogen source** (ammonium salts, urea, yeast extract, peptone)
- **Phosphorus** (KH₂PO₄)
- **Sulfur** (MgSO₄)
- **Trace elements** (Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, Mo, B)
- **Vitamins** (thiamine, biotin, pantothenate)
- **Growth factors** (amino acids, purines, pyrimidines)

**Step 4 — Set Environmental Parameters**
- **pH** (typical: 6.5–7.5 for most bacteria; 4.5–5.5 for yeast)
- **Temperature** (optimal for the strain)
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** for aerobic cultures

**Step 5 — Optimize Through Iterative Experimentation**
- Use **Design of Experiments (DoE)** — Plackett-Burman, Response Surface Methodology (RSM)
- Vary concentrations to find optimum

**Step 6 — Scale-Up Validation**
- Test in lab → pilot → industrial scale

### 2.2 Stoichiometric Design Approach — Theoretical Framework

**Elemental Balance Equation (general form):**

$$
\text{Carbon Source} + \text{Nitrogen Source} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Biomass} + \text{Product} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**For a generic aerobic heterotrophic growth:**

$$
\text{C}_{\alpha}\text{H}_{\beta}\text{O}_{\gamma} + a\text{NH}_3 + b\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \delta\text{C}_{\text{biomass}} + \varepsilon\text{CO}_2 + \zeta\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Biomass elemental composition (typical):**

$$
\text{CH}_{1.806}\text{N}_{0.25}\text{O}_{0.55}
$$

### 2.3 Bioorganic Reaction Medium Engineering — Conceptual Layers

| Layer | Engineering Action | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Precursor Feeding** | Add biosynthetic precursors | Phenylacetic acid for penicillin G |
| **Inducer Addition** | Switch on gene expression | IPTG for *lac* operon |
| **Feedback Inhibition Removal** | Limit end-product accumulation | Remove accumulating ethanol |
| **Catabolite Repression Removal** | Use slow-release sugars | Glucose fed-batch instead of bolus |
| **Precursor Pools Enhancement** | Boost cofactors (NADH, ATP) | Add amino acid supplements |

### 2.4 Novel Media — Classification Tree

```
Novel Media
├── Defined (Synthetic) Media
│   ├── Chemically pure components
│   └── Reproducible batch-to-batch
├── Complex Media (Hydrolysates)
│   ├── Yeast extract (animal-free options)
│   ├── Plant peptones (soy, wheat)
│   └── Casein hydrolysates
├── HCDC (High Cell Density Culture) Media
│   ├── Concentrated nutrients
│   └── Fed-batch optimized
└── ACF (Animal Component Free) Media
    └── Regulatory-compliant (FDA/EMA)
```

---

## 📈 PART B: KINETIC MODELLING — THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

### 2.5 Classification of Kinetic Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The model classification is based on **two dichotomies**:
> 1. **Structured vs Unstructured** — Does the model describe internal cell biochemistry?
> 2. **Segregated vs Unsegregated** — Does the model distinguish individual cells in the population?

### 2.6 Unstructured Unsegregated Models — Theoretical Basis

These models treat cells as a **single homogeneous "black box"** characterized only by:
- Total biomass concentration (X)
- Limiting substrate concentration (S)
- Product concentration (P)

**Key Assumption:** Cell composition is **constant** throughout growth. There is no consideration of intracellular pools of RNA, protein, DNA, etc.

### 2.7 Monod Equation — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

The Monod equation is **phenomenological**, derived from the observation that specific growth rate increases hyperbolically with limiting substrate concentration:

**Three Limiting Cases:**
1. When **S << Kₛ**: μ ≈ (μ_max/Kₛ) · S (first-order in S)
2. When **S >> Kₛ**: μ ≈ μ_max (zero-order, saturated)
3. When **S = Kₛ**: μ = 0.5 · μ_max (definition of Kₛ)

### 2.8 Monod Chemostat Model — Theoretical Derivation Foundation

In a chemostat, the steady-state mass balances give:

**Biomass Balance:**
$$
D \cdot X = \mu \cdot X \Rightarrow \mu = D \text{ (at steady state)}
$$

**Substrate Balance:**
$$
D(S_0 - S) = \mu \cdot X / Y_{X/S}
$$

This couples **bioreactor operation** (D) with **kinetics** (μ, Yₓ/ₛ).

### 2.9 Substrate Inhibition Models — Comparative Analysis

| Model | Equation Form | Behavior at Low S | Behavior at High S | μ_max Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Monod** | μ = μ_max · S/(Kₛ+S) | First-order | Saturates at μ_max | No inhibition |
| **Andrews (Haldane)** | μ = μ_max · S/(Kₛ+S+S²/K_i) | First-order | Decreases after peak | Substrate inhibits |
| **Aiba-Edwards** | μ = μ_max · S/(Kₛ+S) · exp(-S/K_i) | First-order | Exponential decay | Strong inhibition |
| **Webb** | μ = μ_max · S · [1 + S/K]⁻¹ · [1 + S/K_i]⁻¹ | First-order | Decays | Mild inhibition |
| **Yano** | μ = μ_max · S/(Kₛ+S+S²/K_i · (1+S/K)) | First-order | Biphasic decay | Generalized |

---

## 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of numerical KTU questions on this module.**

| # | Equation / Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | **Monod Equation** | h⁻¹ | Specific growth rate prediction |
| 2 | $\mu = D$ (at steady state) | Chemostat equilibrium | h⁻¹ | Bioreactor design |
| 3 | $D_{max} = \mu_{max}\left(1-\sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{K_s+S_0}}\right)$ | Wash-out dilution rate | h⁻¹ | Critical dilution rate |
| 4 | $\bar{S} = \dfrac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}$ | Steady-state substrate | g/L | Steady-state analysis |
| 5 | $\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - \bar{S})$ | Steady-state biomass | g/L | Productivity analysis |
| 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S + S^2/K_i}$ | **Andrews (Haldane) Inhibition** | h⁻¹ | Substrate inhibition |
| 7 | $S^* = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}$ | Critical substrate concentration (Haldane) | g/L | Optimal substrate conc. |
| 8 | $\mu_{max,S^*} = \dfrac{\mu_{max}}{1 + 2\sqrt{K_s/K_i}}$ | Maximum μ in inhibited system | h⁻¹ | Peak growth rate |
| 9 | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot e^{-S/K_i}$ | **Aiba-Edwards Model** | h⁻¹ | Strong inhibition |
| 10 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | g/g | Yield calculations |
| 11 | $\text{C-mol balance: } Y_{SX} = \dfrac{\text{C-mol biomass}}{\text{C-mol substrate}}$ | Stoichiometric yield | C-mol/C-mol | Medium design |
| 12 | $\text{Carbon balance: } 1 = Y_{SX} + Y_{SC} + Y_{SG}$ | Carbon distribution | dimensionless | Stoichiometric medium design |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **Kₛ** (half-saturation constant for substrate) with **K_i** (inhibition constant). They have **opposite effects**: a high Kₛ means low affinity for substrate (slow growth at low S); a high K_i means high tolerance to substrate inhibition (growth continues at high S).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ✏️ STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 📐 DERIVATION 1: Monod Chemostat Steady-State Equations (FULL STEP-BY-STEP)

### Setting Up the System

Consider a chemostat (single CSTR) of volume **V** with:
- Feed substrate concentration: **S₀** (g/L)
- Feed flow rate: **F** (L/h)
- Biomass concentration in reactor: **X** (g/L)
- Substrate concentration in reactor: **S** (g/L)
- Product concentration: **P** (g/L)

### Step 1: Define the Dilution Rate

The dilution rate **D** is defined as the volumetric flow rate per unit volume:

$$
D = \frac{F}{V} \quad \left[\text{units: h}^{-1}\right]
$$

**Logic:** A higher flow rate (F) or smaller volume (V) means more frequent replacement of reactor contents. This is the inverse of the **residence time** (τ = V/F = 1/D).

### Step 2: General Mass Balance Equation

For any species with concentration **C** in a chemostat:

$$
V \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} = F \cdot C_{in} - F \cdot C + V \cdot r_{reaction}
$$

This is the general unsteady-state mass balance where:
- **F · C_in** = rate of inflow (g/h)
- **F · C** = rate of outflow (g/h)
- **V · r_reaction** = rate of generation/consumption inside (g/h)

### Step 3: Biomass Mass Balance

Let **r_X = μ · X** (rate of biomass production).

$$
V \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} = F \cdot X_0 - F \cdot X + V \cdot \mu \cdot X
$$

**Logic:** Cells multiply (μ · X) but also get washed out (F · X). If the feed is sterile (X₀ = 0):

$$
V \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} = -F \cdot X + V \cdot \mu \cdot X
$$

Dividing both sides by V:

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = -D \cdot X + \mu \cdot X = (\mu - D) \cdot X
$$

### Step 4: Apply Steady-State Condition (dX/dt = 0)

At steady state, biomass concentration is constant:

$$
0 = (\mu - D) \cdot \bar{X}
$$

Since $\bar{X} \neq 0$ (we want biomass in the reactor):

$$
\boxed{\bar{\mu} = D}
$$

**KEY RESULT #1:** At steady state, the **specific growth rate equals the dilution rate**. This is the cornerstone of chemostat theory — by controlling D, we control μ.

### Step 5: Substrate Mass Balance

Let **r_S = -(μ · X) / Y_X/S** (rate of substrate consumption, where Y_X/S is biomass yield on substrate).

$$
V \cdot \frac{dS}{dt} = F \cdot S_0 - F \cdot S + V \cdot \left(-\frac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}}\right)
$$

Dividing by V:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = D \cdot (S_0 - S) - \frac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

### Step 6: Apply Steady-State Condition (dS/dt = 0)

$$
0 = D(\bar{S_0} - \bar{S}) - \frac{\bar{\mu} \cdot \bar{X}}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Using $\bar{\mu} = D$:

$$
D \cdot (S_0 - \bar{S}) = \frac{D \cdot \bar{X}}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Canceling D from both sides:

$$
S_0 - \bar{S} = \frac{\bar{X}}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Solving for X̄:

$$
\boxed{\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - \bar{S})}
$$

**KEY RESULT #2:** Biomass at steady state depends on substrate consumed.

### Step 7: Apply the Monod Equation

From Key Result #1: $\bar{\mu} = D = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot \bar{S}}{K_s + \bar{S}}$

Solving for S̄:

$$
D(K_s + \bar{S}) = \mu_{max} \cdot \bar{S}
$$

$$
D \cdot K_s + D \cdot \bar{S} = \mu_{max} \cdot \bar{S}
$$

$$
D \cdot K_s = \mu_{max} \cdot \bar{S} - D \cdot \bar{S}
$$

$$
D \cdot K_s = \bar{S}(\mu_{max} - D)
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{S} = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}}
$$

**KEY RESULT #3:** Steady-state substrate concentration.

### Step 8: Steady-State Biomass

Substituting into Key Result #2:

$$
\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot \left(S_0 - \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot \left(\frac{S_0 \cdot (\mu_{max} - D) - D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}\right)}
$$

### Step 9: Wash-Out Condition

Wash-out occurs when **X̄ → 0**, meaning **S̄ → S₀**. This happens when:

$$
S_0 = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}
$$

Solving for D:

$$
S_0 \cdot (\mu_{max} - D) = D \cdot K_s
$$

$$
S_0 \cdot \mu_{max} - S_0 \cdot D = D \cdot K_s
$$

$$
S_0 \cdot \mu_{max} = D(K_s + S_0)
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{wash-out} = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S_0}{K_s + S_0} = \mu_{max} \cdot \left(\frac{S_0}{K_s + S_0}\right)}
$$

This is also expressed as:

$$
\boxed{D_{max} = \mu_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{K_s}{K_s + S_0}\right)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The wash-out dilution rate is the maximum flow rate a chemostat can sustain without losing the entire biomass population. Above this rate, cells are washed out faster than they can grow.

---

## 📐 DERIVATION 2: Andrews (Haldane) Substrate Inhibition — Critical Parameters

### Starting Equation

$$
\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S + \dfrac{S^2}{K_i}}
$$

### Step 1: Find the Critical Substrate Concentration (S*) Where μ is Maximum

Take derivative of μ with respect to S and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d\mu}{dS} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{(K_s + S + S^2/K_i) - S(1 + 2S/K_i)}{(K_s + S + S^2/K_i)^2} = 0
$$

Numerator simplifies to:

$$
K_s + S + \frac{S^2}{K_i} - S - \frac{2S^2}{K_i} = K_s - \frac{S^2}{K_i} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
K_s = \frac{S^{*2}}{K_i}
$$

$$
\boxed{S^* = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}}
$$

**Physical meaning:** S* is the substrate concentration at which growth rate is maximum, beyond which inhibition dominates.

### Step 2: Find the Maximum Specific Growth Rate (μ_max, inhibited)

Substitute S* back into the Andrews equation:

$$
\mu_{max,S^*} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}}{K_s + \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i} + \dfrac{K_s \cdot K_i}{K_i}}
$$

The denominator simplifies (since $S^{*2}/K_i = K_s$):

$$
K_s + \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i} + K_s = 2K_s + \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}
$$

Factor out $\sqrt{K_s}$:

$$
\mu_{max,S^*} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}}{\sqrt{K_s}(2\sqrt{K_s} + \sqrt{K_i})} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_i}}{2\sqrt{K_s} + \sqrt{K_i}}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{K_i}$:

$$
\boxed{\mu_{max,S^*} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{1 + 2\sqrt{K_s/K_i}}}
$$

**Physical meaning:** This is the **actual peak growth rate** in a substrate-inhibited system — always less than μ_max. The ratio $K_i/K_s$ determines how severely the inhibition reduces growth.

---

## 📐 DERIVATION 3: Stoichiometric Medium Design — Glucose Requirement for E. coli Biomass

### Given Data
- Glucose feed: C₆H₁₂O₆
- E. coli biomass formula: CH₁.₈₀₆N₀.₂₅O₀.₅₅ (per C-mol)
- Required biomass: 100 g
- Y_X/S (C-mol basis) ≈ 0.42

### Step 1: Convert Biomass to C-Moles

Molecular weight of biomass formula:
$$
MW_{biomass} = 12 + 1.806 + 14 \times 0.25 + 16 \times 0.55 = 25.66 \text{ g/C-mol}
$$

C-moles of biomass:
$$
n_C = \frac{100}{25.66} = 3.897 \text{ C-mol}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate C-Moles of Glucose Required

$$
n_{glucose} = \frac{n_C}{Y_{X/S}} = \frac{3.897}{0.42} = 9.28 \text{ C-mol}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Mass of Glucose

Each C-mol of glucose = (180 g/mol) / 6 = 30 g/C-mol
$$
m_{glucose} = 9.28 \times 30 = 278.4 \text{ g}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate NH₃ Required (N Source)

Per C-mol biomass, 0.25 mol N needed. So:
$$
n_{NH_3} = 3.897 \times 0.25 = 0.974 \text{ mol}
$$

Mass:
$$
m_{NH_3} = 0.974 \times 17 = 16.56 \text{ g}
$$

### Step 5: Carbon Balance Check

C-mol biomass + C-mol CO₂ = C-mol glucose supplied
$$
3.897 + (9.28 - 3.897) = 3.897 + 5.383 = 9.28 \text{ C-mol} \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** This stoichiometric calculation gives the **theoretical minimum**. Real media contain 2–3× excess to account for incomplete uptake, side reactions, and maintenance metabolism.

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 📚 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Stoichiometric Design Approach" in medium formulation. Mention the typical elemental composition of microbial biomass.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **stoichiometric design approach** is a theoretical method of designing a fermentation medium based on the **elemental mass balance** (carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen) between the nutrients supplied and the biomass produced. It uses **yield coefficients** (Y_X/S, Y_P/S) and the **degree of reduction** of substrates to calculate the minimum quantities of each nutrient required to produce a desired amount of biomass.

The typical elemental composition of microbial biomass is approximately:
$$
\text{CH}_{1.806}\text{N}_{0.25}\text{O}_{0.55}
$$

This formula serves as the basis for calculating carbon and nitrogen requirements during medium design. **Stoichiometric design is the foundation; experimental optimization is then layered on top.** (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between "Structured" and "Unstructured" kinetic models with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Unstructured Model** | **Structured Model** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Description** | Black-box; no internal detail | Includes intracellular chemical pools |
| **Variables** | Only X, S, P | X, S, P + internal pools (RNA, protein, DNA) |
| **Complexity** | Low; few parameters | High; many parameters |
| **Example** | **Monod Equation** | Cybernetic models, Pirt model |
| **Use Case** | Engineering design, scale-up | Physiological / metabolic research |

Unstructured models assume constant cell composition, making them suitable for **engineering applications** like bioreactor design where simplicity and accuracy are needed. Structured models are essential when **dynamic responses** (e.g., diauxic growth, lag phase) must be predicted. (3 marks)

---

## 📚 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state biomass and substrate concentration equations for a Monod chemostat. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemostat operates with feed substrate S₀ = 20 g/L, μ_max = 0.5 h⁻¹, K_s = 0.2 g/L, Y_X/S = 0.5 g/g. Calculate:
(i) The wash-out dilution rate
(ii) The steady-state substrate concentration at D = 0.3 h⁻¹
(iii) The steady-state biomass concentration at the same D
(iv) The productivity (g/L·h) of biomass. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Full Derivation

**Step 1: Dilution Rate Definition**

The dilution rate **D** characterizes the volumetric throughput of the chemostat:

$$
D = \frac{F}{V} \quad [\text{h}^{-1}]
$$

**Step 2: Biomass Mass Balance**

For sterile feed (X₀ = 0), the biomass balance gives:

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = -D \cdot X + \mu \cdot X = (\mu - D) \cdot X
$$

**Step 3: Steady-State Condition for Biomass**

At steady state, $\frac{dX}{dt} = 0$ and X̄ ≠ 0, therefore:

$$
\boxed{\mu = D}
$$

**Step 4: Substrate Mass Balance**

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = D \cdot (S_0 - S) - \frac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

**Step 5: Steady-State Condition for Substrate**

Setting dS/dt = 0 and using μ = D:

$$
0 = D(S_0 - \bar{S}) - \frac{D \cdot \bar{X}}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Cancelling D and solving:

$$
\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - \bar{S}) \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}
$$

**Step 6: Apply Monod Equation**

From μ = D and $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$:

$$
D = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot \bar{S}}{K_s + \bar{S}}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
D \cdot K_s + D \cdot \bar{S} = \mu_{max} \cdot \bar{S}
$$

$$
D \cdot K_s = \bar{S}(\mu_{max} - D)
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{S} = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}} \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}
$$

**Step 7: Steady-State Biomass**

Substituting Eq. 2 into Eq. 1:

$$
\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot \left(S_0 - \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot \left[\frac{S_0(\mu_{max} - D) - D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}\right]}
$$

**(7 marks — distributed as: 2 marks Step 1-3, 2 marks Step 4-5, 3 marks Step 6-7)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- S₀ = 20 g/L
- μ_max = 0.5 h⁻¹
- K_s = 0.2 g/L
- Y_X/S = 0.5 g/g
- D = 0.3 h⁻¹

**(i) Wash-out dilution rate:**

$$
D_{wash-out} = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S_0}{K_s + S_0} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{0.2 + 20} = \frac{10}{20.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{wash-out} = 0.495 \text{ h}^{-1}}
$$

**(ii) Steady-state substrate concentration:**

$$
\bar{S} = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.2}{0.5 - 0.3} = \frac{0.06}{0.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{S} = 0.3 \text{ g/L}}
$$

**(iii) Steady-state biomass concentration:**

$$
\bar{X} = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - \bar{S}) = 0.5 \times (20 - 0.3) = 0.5 \times 19.7
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{X} = 9.85 \text{ g/L}}
$$

**(iv) Biomass Productivity:**

Productivity (P_X) = D × X̄

$$
P_X = 0.3 \times 9.85 = 2.955
$$

$$
\boxed{P_X = 2.955 \text{ g/L·h}}
$$

**(7 marks — distributed as: 2 marks part (i), 2 marks part (ii), 2 marks part (iii), 1 mark part (iv))**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Andrews (Haldane) model** for substrate inhibition. Derive expressions for the critical substrate concentration (S*) and the maximum specific growth rate under inhibition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bacterial culture follows Andrews kinetics with μ_max = 0.6 h⁻¹, K_s = 1.0 g/L, K_i = 10 g/L.
(i) Calculate S* (the substrate concentration at which μ is maximum)
(ii) Calculate μ at S = S*
(iii) At what substrate concentration is the growth rate exactly half of μ_max (in the inhibited regime)?
(iv) Comment on the practical implications of substrate inhibition in industrial bioreactor design. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Derivation

The **Andrews (Haldane) equation** for substrate-inhibited growth is:

$$
\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S + \dfrac{S^2}{K_i}}
$$

**Step 1: Differentiate μ with respect to S**

Setting $\dfrac{d\mu}{dS} = 0$:

$$
\frac{d\mu}{dS} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{K_s + S + S^2/K_i - S(1 + 2S/K_i)}{(K_s + S + S^2/K_i)^2}
$$

**Step 2: Numerator Simplification**

The numerator becomes:

$$
K_s + S + S^2/K_i - S - 2S^2/K_i = K_s - S^2/K_i
$$

Setting to zero:

$$
K_s = \frac{S^{*2}}{K_i}
$$

$$
\boxed{S^* = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute S* into the Andrews Equation**

Using S*² / K_i = K_s, the denominator becomes:

$$
K_s + S^* + K_s = 2K_s + S^* = 2K_s + \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mu_{max,S^*} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}}{2K_s + \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}}
$$

Factoring $\sqrt{K_s}$:

$$
\mu_{max,S^*} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_i}}{2\sqrt{K_s} + \sqrt{K_i}}
$$

Dividing by $\sqrt{K_i}$:

$$
\boxed{\mu_{max,S^*} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{1 + 2\sqrt{K_s/K_i}}}
$$

**(7 marks — distributed as: 2 marks equation + setup, 2 marks S* derivation, 3 marks μ_max derivation)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** μ_max = 0.6 h⁻¹, K_s = 1.0 g/L, K_i = 10 g/L

**(i) Critical substrate concentration:**

$$
S^* = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i} = \sqrt{1.0 \times 10} = \sqrt{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{S^* = 3.162 \text{ g/L}}
$$

**(ii) Maximum specific growth rate under inhibition:**

$$
\mu_{max,S^*} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{1 + 2\sqrt{K_s/K_i}} = \frac{0.6}{1 + 2\sqrt{1.0/10}}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.6}{1 + 2 \times 0.3162} = \frac{0.6}{1.6325}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mu_{max,S^*} = 0.3676 \text{ h}^{-1}}
$$

**(iii) Substrate concentration at μ = 0.5 · μ_max = 0.3 h⁻¹ in inhibited regime:**

Using Andrews equation:

$$
0.3 = \frac{0.6 \cdot S}{1.0 + S + S^2/10}
$$

$$
\frac{0.5 \cdot S}{1.0 + S + 0.1 S^2} = 0.5
$$

$$
0.5 S = 0.5 + 0.5 S + 0.05 S^2
$$

$$
0 = 0.5 + 0.05 S^2
$$

This is a quadratic with positive solution:

$$
0.05 S^2 = -0.5 \Rightarrow S^2 = -10
$$

**Wait** — recalculating. We want μ = 0.5 × μ_max = 0.3 h⁻¹:

$$
0.3 = \frac{0.6 \cdot S}{1.0 + S + 0.1 S^2}
$$

$$
0.3(1.0 + S + 0.1 S^2) = 0.6 S
$$

$$
0.3 + 0.3 S + 0.03 S^2 = 0.6 S
$$

$$
0.03 S^2 + 0.3 S - 0.6 S + 0.3 = 0
$$

$$
0.03 S^2 - 0.3 S + 0.3 = 0
$$

Divide by 0.03:

$$
S^2 - 10 S + 10 = 0
$$

Using quadratic formula:

$$
S = \frac{10 \pm \sqrt{100 - 40}}{2} = \frac{10 \pm \sqrt{60}}{2} = \frac{10 \pm 7.746}{2}
$$

Two solutions: S = 8.873 g/L or S = 1.127 g/L

The **higher value (S = 8.873 g/L)** corresponds to the inhibited regime (substrate > S*).

$$
\boxed{S_{inhibited} = 8.873 \text{ g/L}}
$$

**(iv) Practical Implications:**

In industrial bioreactor design, substrate inhibition has critical implications:

1. **Fed-batch operation** is preferred over batch for substrates that inhibit growth at high concentrations (e.g., acetic acid in *E. coli*, phenol in bioremediation).
2. **Optimum substrate concentration** must be maintained around S* to maximize productivity.
3. **Continuous cultures** must be operated below S* (i.e., D should be high enough that S̄ < S*) to avoid inhibition.
4. **Dilution strategies** (gradual feeding, multi-stage reactors) help maintain S in the optimal range.
5. The **ratio K_i/K_s** determines the severity of inhibition: high K_i/K_s means mild inhibition (tolerant strain).

**(7 marks — distributed as: 1.5 marks each for (i) and (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 2.5 marks for (iv))**

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing μ and μ_max:** Students often write μ_max where μ should be used (or vice versa). Remember: μ is the **actual** specific growth rate; μ_max is the **maximum theoretical** rate.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** When using KTU's standard g/L units, ensure time units are consistent. If μ_max is in h⁻¹, then D must also be in h⁻¹.
>
> 3. **Incorrect wash-out interpretation:** Students often write "wash-out occurs when D = μ_max" — this is **wrong**. Wash-out occurs at D = μ_max × S₀/(K_s + S₀), which is **always less than μ_max**.
>
> 4. **Substrate inhibition oversight:** When a question gives only K_s and asks for growth rate at high S, students forget that the **Andrews model** must be used, not the Monod model.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in mass balances:** Remember the convention: **generation is positive, consumption is negative**. Substrate consumption has a negative sign.
>
> 6. **Quadratic equation mistakes:** In Andrews-type problems, students forget to take the **positive root** or to interpret the **physically meaningful** root (rejecting negative concentrations).
>
> 7. **Unit mismatch in Y_X/S:** Sometimes Y_X/S is given in C-mol basis; conversion to mass basis is needed before applying to standard equations.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST)

### 🧪 MEDIUM DESIGN ENGINEERING — Key Points

- ✅ **Growth medium** = rich, balanced, for biomass; **Production medium** = limited in one nutrient, for product
- ✅ The **6-step design procedure**: Strain → Stoichiometry → Nutrients → Environment → Optimization → Scale-up
- ✅ **Stoichiometric design** is based on **elemental balance**: C, H, O, N for biomass formula CH₁.₈₀₆N₀.₂₅O₀.₅₅
- ✅ **Bioorganic reaction medium engineering** = engineering the medium based on the biochemical pathway
- ✅ **Novel media**: Defined, Complex (hydrolysates), HCDC, ACF
- ✅ Macros = C, N, P, S; Micros = Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, Mo; Vitamins = thiamine, biotin
- ✅ DoE / RSM used for optimization

### 📈 KINETIC MODELLING — Key Points

- ✅ **2×2 model matrix**: Structured × Segregated
- ✅ **Monod model** is **unstructured + unsegregated** (most common in engineering)
- ✅ **Monod equation**: $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S/(K_s + S)$
- ✅ **Three cases**: S ≪ K_s (1st order), S = K_s (μ = 0.5 μ_max), S ≫ K_s (zero order)
- ✅ **Chemostat**: At steady state, **μ = D** (KEY EQUATION)
- ✅ **Wash-out condition**: $D_{max} = \mu_{max} \cdot S_0/(K_s + S_0)$
- ✅ **Andrews equation**: $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S/(K_s + S + S^2/K_i)$
- ✅ **Critical substrate**: $S^* = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}$
- ✅ **Inhibited μ_max**: $\mu_{max,S^*} = \mu_{max}/(1 + 2\sqrt{K_s/K_i})$
- ✅ **Aiba-Edwards**: $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S/(K_s + S) \cdot e^{-S/K_i}$
- ✅ Substrate inhibition → use **fed-batch** or **controlled D** in continuous operation

### 🔑 DEFINITE-TO-APPEAR EQUATIONS (KTU 2024 Priority)

| # | Equation | Question Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S/(K_s + S)$ | Direct, conceptual |
| 2 | $\mu = D$ at steady state | Derivation, problem-solving |
| 3 | $\bar{S} = D \cdot K_s/(\mu_{max} - D)$ | Problem-solving |
| 4 | $\bar{X} = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - \bar{S})$ | Problem-solving |
| 5 | $D_{wash-out} = \mu_{max} \cdot S_0/(K_s + S_0)$ | Problem-solving |
| 6 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S/(K_s + S + S^2/K_i)$ | Conceptual + problem |
| 7 | $S^* = \sqrt{K_s \cdot K_i}$ | Derivation + problem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL EXAM STRATEGY:** For 14-mark problems, ALWAYS show your work with **explicit step-by-step derivation**. KTU examiners reward **methodology, not just final answers**. A wrong answer with correct methodology often gets 50–60% marks; a correct answer with skipped steps gets 80% but missing units or reasoning can lose you 1–2 marks. **Box your final answers** for easy identification during valuation.

topics.title: Product Inhibition and Inhibition by Toxic Compounds), Logistic Equation, Growth Models for Filamentous Organisms, Structured Kinetic Models, Compartment Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Inhibition and Inhibition by Toxic Compounds), Logistic Equation, Growth Models for Filamentous Organisms, Structured Kinetic Models, Compartment Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Product Inhibition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Inhibition (KTU 2024 Definition):** A kinetic phenomenon wherein the accumulation of a product (often the desired metabolite, an organic acid, or a byproduct) in the fermentation broth decreases the specific growth rate ($\mu$) of the producing microorganism, thereby limiting final product concentration, productivity, and yield.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a chef in a tiny kitchen. As plates of finished food pile up on the counter, the chef has less space to move, chop, and cook. The "growth rate" (chefs output) slows down because the *product* (cooked food) is physically crowding the work environment. Similarly, microbial cells "suffocate" or get biochemically stressed as their own products accumulate, leading to feedback inhibition of metabolic pathways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Examples:**
> - **Ethanol inhibition** in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* fermentations.
> - **Lactic acid inhibition** in *Lactobacillus* cultures.
> - **Acetic acid inhibition** in *E. coli* recombinant protein production.

### 1.2 Inhibition by Toxic Compounds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toxic Compound Inhibition:** A specific case of inhibitory kinetics where the inhibitor is a toxic substance (e.g., phenol, formaldehyde, cyanide, heavy metals, solvents, or high substrate concentration itself) that damages cellular machinery — denatures proteins, disrupts membranes, or inhibits enzyme function — leading to a reduction in specific growth rate and eventually cell death.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of it like inhaling smoke in a closed room. Even a small amount makes breathing harder; too much is fatal. Toxic compounds "poison" the cellular environment, with the effect intensifying exponentially rather than linearly as their concentration rises.

### 1.3 The Logistic Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logistic Equation (Verhulst, 1838; applied to microbiology by Malthus-Pearl-Reed):** A sigmoidal growth model that describes self-limiting population growth where the specific growth rate decreases linearly as the population approaches a maximum carrying capacity ($X_{max}$). It is an unstructured, deterministic, single-variable model.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A petri dish has a finite area. Bacteria initially grow exponentially, but as colonies get crowded, nutrients deplete and waste builds up. Growth tapers off and finally halts at a "carrying capacity" — the maximum number of cells the environment can sustain.

### 1.4 Growth Models for Filamentous Organisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filamentous Growth Models:** Specialized unstructured kinetic models that account for the morphology of filamentous microorganisms (e.g., fungi like *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, and actinomycetes like *Streptomyces*) where growth occurs by *apical (tip) extension* and *branching*, leading to distinct morphological phases (germination, vegetative growth, fragmentation) and complex rheology.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Unlike a single yeast cell that just divides into two, a mold grows like a tree. New "leaves" (branches) sprout at the tips, and the total "biomass" depends on how many tips exist and how fast each tip extends. The model must track tip density, hyphal length, and branching frequency.

### 1.5 Structured Kinetic Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structured Kinetic Models:** Mathematical models that explicitly account for the *internal composition* and *physiological state* of the cell by dividing the biomass into chemically or functionally distinct components (e.g., DNA, RNA, protein, carbohydrate, lipid, internal metabolite pools). They resolve the cellular "black box" of unstructured models.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Instead of treating a factory as a single "black box" that converts inputs to outputs, a structured model opens the factory door and tracks raw materials, work-in-progress inventory, finished goods, and machinery status. The growth rate depends on the internal state, not just the outside environment.

### 1.6 Compartment Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compartment Models:** A class of structured models in which the cell (or bioreactor system) is divided into a finite number of homogeneous, well-mixed sub-systems ("compartments" or "pools") interconnected by mass transfer or biochemical conversion fluxes. Each compartment has its own mass balance and kinetic description.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A hotel building is one "structure," but a fire-evacuation plan divides it into compartments — each floor has its own occupancy, ventilation, and escape routes. Tracking movement between floors (compartments) gives a much more accurate safety model than treating the whole building as a single room.

---

### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used in This Module

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Maximum specific growth rate, $\mu_{max}$ | **0.1 – 1.0 h⁻¹** | Intrinsic kinetic ceiling |
| Half-saturation constant, $K_S$ | **1 – 100 mg/L** | Affinity of cells for substrate |
| Product inhibition constant, $K_P$ | **5 – 50 g/L** (ethanol) | Toxicity threshold |
| Critical product concentration, $P_{max}$ | **100 – 120 g/L** (ethanol) | Point of complete growth arrest |
| Carrying capacity, $X_{max}$ | **2 – 50 g/L** | Maximum sustainable biomass |
| Inhibition constant for toxicant, $K_i$ | **10 – 1000 mg/L** | Strength of toxin |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Inhibition Kinetics

Inhibition kinetics arise in enzyme-catalyzed intracellular reactions and propagate to bulk cell growth. The classical patterns (transferred from enzyme kinetics) are:

#### 2.1.1 Competitive Product Inhibition
- Product competes with substrate for the *active site* of a key enzyme.
- The product resembles the substrate structurally (e.g., ethanol vs. glucose in glycolysis).
- Apparent $K_S$ increases with product concentration $P$.
- Rate expression:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S \left(1 + \dfrac{P}{K_P}\right) + S}$$

#### 2.1.2 Non-Competitive Product Inhibition
- Product binds to a *different site* (allosteric site) on the enzyme than the substrate.
- Effective $\mu_{max}$ decreases with $P$ while $K_S$ is unchanged.
- Rate expression:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{\left(K_S + S\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{P}{K_P}\right)}$$

#### 2.1.3 Uncompetitive Product Inhibition
- Product binds *only* to the enzyme-substrate complex.
- Both $K_S$ (apparent) and $\mu_{max}$ (apparent) decrease with $P$.
- Rate expression:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S\left(1 + \dfrac{P}{K_P}\right)}$$

#### 2.1.4 Mixed (General) Product Inhibition
- Combines competitive and non-competitive effects; very common for organic-acid product inhibition.
- Rate expression:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S \left(1 + \dfrac{P}{K_{P,comp}}\right) + S\left(1 + \dfrac{P}{K_{P,noncomp}}\right)}$$

#### 2.1.5 Linear Product Inhibition (Aromatic Hydrocarbon Analogue)
- Simple empirical form used in industry:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)$$
- $P_{max}$ is the extrapolated product concentration at which $\mu \to 0$.

#### 2.1.6 Exponential / Aiba Model
- Strongly used for ethanol inhibition in yeast:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \left(\frac{S}{K_S + S}\right) \cdot e^{-P/K_P}$$

#### 2.1.7 Teissier Model
- Power-law form:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)^n\right]$$

#### 2.1.8 Levenspiel Model
- Most widely used in industrial scale-up (KTU-favoured):
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)^n$$
- The exponent $n$ controls the *sharpness* of inhibition (typically $0.5 \le n \le 1.5$).

#### 2.1.9 Luong Model
- Accounts for the possibility of complete inhibition at a finite $P^*$:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \left(\frac{S}{K_S + S}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{P}{P^*}\right)^n$$
- $P^*$ is the *critical* product concentration at which growth ceases entirely (often $P^* \approx 120$ g/L for ethanol).

### 2.2 Inhibition by Toxic Compounds

For soluble toxicants, Andrews (1968) extended the Monod framework with a substrate-inhibition term. For a generic toxicant $I$, the analogous Andrews equation is:

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S + \dfrac{S^2}{K_i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $S^2/K_i$ appears:** At low $S$, the inhibitor is in trace form; at high $S$, the inhibitor overwhelms the cell, *and* the substrate itself becomes the toxicant (substrate inhibition). The squared term captures the cooperative binding of inhibitor molecules to the enzyme.

For other toxicants (e.g., phenol, formaldehyde), the classic **Andrews-Haldane** model is used, and the *critical inhibitor concentration* $I_{crit}$ at which $\mu$ is maximum is given by:

$$I_{crit} = \sqrt{K_S \cdot K_i}$$

For simple non-competitive toxicants, an alternative empirical form is:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} \cdot \frac{K_i}{K_i + I}$$

### 2.3 The Logistic Equation — Rigorous Form

> [!NOTE]
> The Logistic equation is a *self-inhibitory* (population-density-inhibited) growth model. It does NOT need an external product or toxin to limit growth; the biomass itself acts as the inhibitor.

The Verhulst-Pearl-Reed equation is:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} \cdot X \cdot \left(1 - \frac{X}{X_{max}}\right)$$

- $X$ = biomass concentration (g/L)
- $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (h⁻¹)
- $X_{max}$ = carrying capacity (g/L)
- The factor $(1 - X/X_{max})$ is the *logistic inhibition function*.

**Key Insight:** When $X \ll X_{max}$, the equation collapses to $\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} X$ (exponential growth). When $X \to X_{max}$, $\frac{dX}{dt} \to 0$ (stationary phase). The transition is *smooth* (sigmoidal) rather than abrupt.

### 2.4 Growth Models for Filamentous Organisms

#### 2.4.1 Pirt's Model (1966)
Distinguishes between *tip growth* and *non-tip* (existing hyphal) growth:
$$\frac{1}{X}\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{tip} \cdot f_{tip} + \mu_{wall} \cdot (1 - f_{tip})$$
- $f_{tip}$ = fraction of biomass in active tips.
- $\mu_{tip}$ >> $\mu_{wall}$ (tip extension is dominant).

#### 2.4.2 Prosser & Tough (1991) — Tip Extension Model
$$\mu = \mu_{tip} \cdot \frac{n_{tips}}{X}$$
- $n_{tips}$ = number of growing hyphal tips per unit volume.
- The growth rate is *directly proportional* to tip density.

#### 2.4.3 Bergter-Model (1983)
Includes branching kinetics:
$$\frac{dn_{tips}}{dt} = \mu_{branch} \cdot n_{tips} \cdot f(extensions)$$

#### 2.4.4 Empirically Modified Monod for Fungi
- Substrate limitation + mycelial age distribution:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} \cdot e^{-k_{age} \cdot \tau_{age}}$$
- $\tau_{age}$ = mean hyphal age; older hyphae contribute less to growth.

#### 2.4.5 Logistic Adaptation for Filamentous Biomass
- Extends the classical logistic to morphological states (germinated, hyphal, fragmented):
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X \cdot \left(1 - \frac{X}{X_{max}}\right) - k_{frag} \cdot X$$
- $k_{frag}$ = fragmentation rate constant (s⁻¹).

### 2.5 Structured Kinetic Models — Hierarchy

Structured models are classified by *degree of resolution* of the cell's internal state:

| Type | Variables Tracked | Complexity | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unstructured** | $X$ only | Low | Ideal, balanced growth |
| **Simple Structured** | $X$, $P$, $S$ | Low-Med | Single bottleneck metabolite |
| **Compartment** | $X_1$, $X_2$, ... pools | Medium | Multi-pool intracellular state |
| **Cybernetic** | Enzyme levels + regulation | High | Dynamic metabolic regulation |
| **Metabolic Flux** (FBA) | All reaction fluxes | Very High | Genome-scale |

A canonical single-internal-variable structured model (Ramkrishna, 1979) tracks a *key component* $C$ (e.g., active enzyme or RNA):
$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \mu - \beta \cdot C$$
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu(S, C) \cdot X$$

### 2.6 Compartment Models

The most cited form is the **Harder-Klaassen** (2-compartment) model, which divides biomass into:
- **Compartment A** (active, growing, young cells)
- **Compartment B** (inactive, mature, non-growing cells)

Mass balances:
$$\frac{dX_A}{dt} = \mu_A \cdot X_A - k_{AB} \cdot X_A$$
$$\frac{dX_B}{dt} = k_{AB} \cdot X_A - \mu_B \cdot X_B$$
- $k_{AB}$ = transition rate from A to B.
- $X = X_A + X_B$ (total biomass).
- Effective $\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_A X_A + \mu_B X_B}{X_A + X_B}$.

The **Williams two-compartment model** (1967) divides cells into a *synthetic* ($G_1$) compartment and a *structural/genetic* ($G_2$) compartment, predicting diauxic growth patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Compartment Models Matter (KTU context):** They explain *transient* phenomena (lag phase, overshoot, hysteresis in $\mu$ vs. $S$) that unstructured models CANNOT capture.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation Name | Formula | Use / Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Monod | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S}$ | Substrate-limited growth |
| Competitive product inhibition | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S(1 + P/K_P) + S}$ | Product competes with $S$ |
| Non-competitive product inhibition | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{(K_S + S)(1 + P/K_P)}$ | Product binds allosteric site |
| Uncompetitive product inhibition | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S(1 + P/K_P)}$ | Product binds ES complex |
| Linear product inhibition | $\mu = \mu_{max}(1 - P/P_{max})$ | Simple industrial model |
| Aiba | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S} e^{-P/K_P}$ | Ethanol inhibition |
| Teissier | $\mu = \mu_{max}[1 - (P/P_{max})^n]$ | Power-law form |
| Levenspiel | $\mu = \mu_{max}(1 - P/P_{max})^n$ | Most-used KTU form |
| Luong | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S} (1 - P/P^*)^n$ | Finite $P^*$ for zero growth |
| Andrews (substrate inhibition) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S + S^2/K_i}$ | Toxic-substrate case |
| Toxic inhibition (non-competitive) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S} \cdot \dfrac{K_i}{K_i + I}$ | External toxicant |
| Critical toxicant conc. | $I_{crit} = \sqrt{K_S K_i}$ | Andrews inflection point |
| Logistic ODE | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} X (1 - X/X_{max})$ | Self-inhibited growth |
| Logistic solution | $X(t) = \dfrac{X_{max}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0}\right) e^{-\mu_{max} t}}$ | Integrated form |
| Logistic inflection time | $t^* = \dfrac{\ln[(X_{max} - X_0)/X_0]}{\mu_{max}}$ | Time of maximum $dX/dt$ |
| Pirt tip-extension | $\mu = \mu_{tip} \cdot f_{tip} + \mu_{wall}(1 - f_{tip})$ | Filamentous growth |
| Prosser-Tough | $\mu = \mu_{tip} \cdot n_{tips}/X$ | Tip-density limited growth |
| Harder-Klaassen A-pool | $dX_A/dt = \mu_A X_A - k_{AB} X_A$ | Active compartment |
| Harder-Klaassen B-pool | $dX_B/dt = k_{AB} X_A - \mu_B X_B$ | Inactive compartment |
| Ramkrishna single-var. | $dC/dt = \alpha \mu - \beta C$ | Internal pool dynamics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility (Why this matters):**
> - **Scale-up & Design:** Correct inhibition model predicts final $P_{max}$ and dictates fermentor volume.
> - **Process Control:** In-situ product removal (ISPR) and fed-batch strategies are designed *because* of product inhibition.
> - **Toxicant Biotreatment:** The Andrews model governs reactor sizing for phenol/cresol biodegradation in wastewater.
> - **Process Economics:** A 10% improvement in $P_{max}$ estimation can save crores in capex for large fermentors.
> - **Regulatory Submission:** KTU 2024 mandates structured models in the B.Tech project report for batch process validation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Logistic Equation Solution

**Starting Point (Verhulst, 1838):**
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} \cdot X \cdot \left(1 - \frac{X}{X_{max}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables.** Move all $X$-dependent terms to the LHS and $t$ to the RHS:
$$\frac{dX}{X \left(1 - \dfrac{X}{X_{max}}\right)} = \mu_{max} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Partial-fraction decomposition.** Note the identity:
$$\frac{1}{X \left(1 - \dfrac{X}{X_{max}}\right)} = \frac{1}{X} + \frac{1}{X_{max} - X}$$

*Proof:* Combine the RHS over a common denominator:
$$\frac{1}{X} + \frac{1}{X_{max} - X} = \frac{(X_{max} - X) + X}{X(X_{max} - X)} = \frac{X_{max}}{X(X_{max} - X)} = \frac{1}{X\left(1 - \dfrac{X}{X_{max}}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from $X = X_0$ at $t = 0$ to $X = X$ at $t = t$:**
$$\int_{X_0}^{X} \left(\frac{1}{X'} + \frac{1}{X_{max} - X'}\right) dX' = \mu_{max} \int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**
$$\left[ \ln X' - \ln(X_{max} - X') \right]_{X_0}^{X} = \mu_{max} t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{X}{X_{max} - X}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{X_0}{X_{max} - X_0}\right) = \mu_{max} t$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate:**
$$\frac{X}{X_{max} - X} \cdot \frac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0} = e^{\mu_{max} t}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $X(t)$:**
$$\frac{X}{X_{max} - X} = \frac{X_0}{X_{max} - X_0} \cdot e^{\mu_{max} t}$$

Let $A = \dfrac{X_0}{X_{max} - X_0} \cdot e^{\mu_{max} t}$. Then $X = A(X_{max} - X)$, i.e., $X(1 + A) = A X_{max}$, so:

$$\boxed{X(t) = \frac{X_{max}}{1 + \dfrac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0} \cdot e^{-\mu_{max} t}}}$$

**Step 7 — Inflection time $t^*$.** The maximum $\frac{dX}{dt}$ occurs when $\frac{d^2X}{dt^2} = 0$, which mathematically corresponds to $X = X_{max}/2$ (the symmetric point of the sigmoid). Setting $X = X_{max}/2$ in the solution:

$$\frac{X_{max}}{2} = \frac{X_{max}}{1 + \dfrac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0} e^{-\mu_{max} t^*}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + \frac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0} e^{-\mu_{max} t^*} = 2$$

$$\frac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0} e^{-\mu_{max} t^*} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-\mu_{max} t^*} = \frac{X_0}{X_{max} - X_0}$$

$$\boxed{t^* = \frac{1}{\mu_{max}} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** $t^*$ is the time of *maximum* specific growth (in terms of *absolute* biomass production rate). For fed-batch design, substrate addition should peak around $t^*$ to maximize productivity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Levenspiel's Product Inhibition Model

**Starting assumption:** $\mu$ decreases as a *power-law* of the *uninhibited* specific growth rate:
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \left(1 - \frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)^n$$

**Step 1 — Apply Levenspiel's general framework** (1980). The classic product-inhibition equation is built on two observations:
- $P = 0 \Rightarrow \mu = \mu_{max}$ (no inhibition).
- $P = P_{max} \Rightarrow \mu = 0$ (total inhibition).

The simplest *smooth* function satisfying these boundary conditions is a power law:
$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_{max}} = \left(1 - \frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)^n$$

**Step 2 — Determine the exponent $n$.** Differentiate w.r.t. $P$ to find the *sensitivity*:
$$\frac{d\mu}{dP} = -n \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{1}{P_{max}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)^{n-1}$$

At $P = 0$:
$$\left.\frac{d\mu}{dP}\right|_{P=0} = -\frac{n \, \mu_{max}}{P_{max}}$$

Higher $n$ implies steeper initial drop in $\mu$ with $P$. For ethanol/yeast, $n \approx 0.5$; for lactic acid, $n \approx 1$.

**Step 3 — Linearized form (n = 1).** The linear model is the most common industrial form:
$$\mu = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)$$

This is widely used because it requires only one fitting parameter ($P_{max}$), which is directly measurable from batch data as the $P$-intercept of $\mu$ vs. $P$ plot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The Luong model $\mu = \mu_{max}(1 - P/P^*)^n$ is *more conservative* than Levenspiel because $P^* < P_{max}$ in many real systems; for design calculations, Luong is *safer* (under-predicts final concentration).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Andrews Critical Inhibitor Concentration

**Starting equation (Andrews, 1968):**
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S + S^2/K_i}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\mu$ w.r.t. $S$ to find the maximum.** Apply $\frac{d\mu}{dS} = 0$:
$$\frac{d}{dS}\left[\frac{S}{K_S + S + S^2/K_i}\right] = \frac{(K_S + S + S^2/K_i) - S(1 + 2S/K_i)}{(K_S + S + S^2/K_i)^2} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Numerator = 0:**
$$K_S + S + \frac{S^2}{K_i} - S - \frac{2S^2}{K_i} = 0$$

$$K_S - \frac{S^2}{K_i} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S^2 = K_S \cdot K_i$$

**Step 3 — Critical concentration:**
$$\boxed{S_{crit} = I_{crit} = \sqrt{K_S \cdot K_i}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum $\mu_{crit}$ at $S_{crit}$:** Substitute $S_{crit}^2 = K_S K_i$ into the Andrews equation:
$$\mu_{crit} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_S K_i}}{K_S + \sqrt{K_S K_i} + K_S K_i / K_i} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{K_S K_i}}{2K_S + \sqrt{K_S K_i}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{K_S K_i}$:
$$\mu_{crit} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{1 + 2\sqrt{K_S / K_i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** If $K_S$ is very small (high substrate affinity) and $K_i$ is very large (weak toxin), the inhibitor has negligible effect. If $K_S$ is large and $K_i$ is small, the system is highly sensitive to substrate levels — common in *Pseudomonas* growing on toxic aromatics.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Harder-Klaassen Two-Compartment Model

**Compartment definition:**
- $X_A$ = "active" biomass (growing pool).
- $X_B$ = "inactive" / "storage" biomass (non-growing pool).
- $X = X_A + X_B$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on compartment A:**
$$\frac{dX_A}{dt} = \underbrace{\mu_A \cdot X_A}_{\text{net growth of A}} - \underbrace{k_{AB} \cdot X_A}_{\text{transition to B}}$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on compartment B:**
$$\frac{dX_B}{dt} = \underbrace{k_{AB} \cdot X_A}_{\text{influx from A}} - \underbrace{\mu_B \cdot X_B}_{\text{outflow (maintenance/decay)}}$$

**Step 3 — Total biomass ODE:**
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{dX_A}{dt} + \frac{dX_B}{dt} = (\mu_A - k_{AB}) X_A + (k_{AB} \cdot \frac{X_A}{X_B} - \mu_B) X_B$$

In matrix form (linear approximation, $\mu_A$, $\mu_B$ constants):
$$\frac{d}{dt} \begin{bmatrix} X_A \\ X_B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mu_A - k_{AB} & 0 \\ k_{AB} & -\mu_B \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_A \\ X_B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues of the system matrix (determine stability):**
$$\det\begin{pmatrix} \mu_A - k_{AB} - \lambda & 0 \\ k_{AB} & -\mu_B - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = 0$$

$$(\mu_A - k_{AB} - \lambda)(-\mu_B - \lambda) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = \mu_A - k_{AB}, \quad \lambda_2 = -\mu_B}$$

**Step 5 — Stability analysis:** The system is stable (no runaway growth) iff $\lambda_1 < 0$, i.e., $\mu_A < k_{AB}$. This is the *mathematical reason* cells slow down even when substrate is plentiful — active cells transition to inactive state faster than they can grow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question may ask to "set up the matrix form and discuss stability." Always explicitly compute eigenvalues and relate them to biological meaning (active cell washout, stationary phase, etc.).

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the Ramkrishna Single-Variable Structured Model

**State variables:** $X$ (biomass), $C$ (intracellular key component, e.g., active enzyme pool).

**Step 1 — Biomass growth** (depends on internal state $C$):
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu(C, S) \cdot X$$

**Step 2 — Internal component dynamics** (formed via growth, degraded via dilution/damage):
$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \underbrace{\alpha \cdot \mu}_{\text{synthesis coupled to growth}} - \underbrace{\beta \cdot C}_{\text{decay/maintenance}}$$

where $\alpha$ = synthesis coefficient (gC/gX) and $\beta$ = degradation coefficient (h⁻¹).

**Step 3 — Specific growth rate is a function of $C$:**
$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} \cdot \frac{C}{K_C + C}$$

**Step 4 — Steady state:** At $\frac{dC}{dt} = 0$:
$$C_{ss} = \frac{\alpha \mu_{ss}}{\beta}$$

Substituting into the $\mu$ expression yields a *self-consistent* steady-state $\mu_{ss}$ that depends on $S$. This is the central feature of structured models: they can predict *transient* lag-phase dynamics that unstructured Monod cannot.

**Step 5 — Linear stability:** Perturb $C = C_{ss} + \delta C$ and linearize the $C$-equation:
$$\frac{d(\delta C)}{dt} = \left(\alpha \frac{\partial \mu}{\partial C}\bigg|_{ss} - \beta\right) \delta C$$

For local stability: $\alpha \frac{\partial \mu}{\partial C}\bigg|_{ss} < \beta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Insight:** The Ramkrishna model predicts that if a substrate is *too rapidly* shifted up, $C$ lags behind, causing a transient growth-rate overshoot (and vice versa for substrate downshift). This is observed in real cultures during fed-batch and pulse-feeding experiments.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Models

| Model | Predicts Lag | Predicts $\mu$ Overshoot | Parameters | Data Required | KTU Difficulty |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Monod | No | No | 2 | $X(t)$, $S(t)$ | Easy |
| Logistic | No | No | 3 | $X(t)$ | Easy |
| Monod + Linear Product Inh. | No | No | 4 | $X, S, P$ | Medium |
| Levenspiel Product Inh. | No | No | 3 | $X, P$ | Medium |
| Andrews (Toxicant) | No | No | 3 | $X, S$ | Medium |
| Harder-Klaassen (2-Cpt) | Yes | No | 4 | $X_A, X_B$ | Hard |
| Ramkrishna (1-var) | Yes | Yes | 4 | $X, C$ | Hard |
| Cybernetic (multiple enzymes) | Yes | Yes | $\ge 6$ | Enzyme data | Very Hard |

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Kinetic Models in Bioprocess Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[Structured]

    B31 --> B314[Levenspiel]
    B31 --> B315[Luong]
    B31 --> B316[Aiba]
    C --> C1[Compartment Models]

    C --> C3[Cybernetic Models]

    C2 --> C22[Nielsen Villadsen]

```
