3. **Pharmaceutical sterilization (SIP):** Steam-in-place cycles use Cooper to estimate how fast the jacket can raise broth from 121 °C.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Working Form `h = f(N, D, T, properties)`

Starting from the defining equation for a **Paddle agitator**:

$$
\frac{h \, T}{k} \;=\; 0.36 \, Re^{2/3} \, Pr^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the definition of Re.**

$$
\frac{h \, T}{k} \;=\; 0.36\!\left(\frac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu}\right)^{2/3}\!\left(\frac{C_p \,\mu}{k}\right)^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}
$$

**Step 2 — Group the exponents of μ.**

Exponent of $\mu$ in the right-hand side:
$$
\underbrace{-\tfrac{2}{3}}_{\text{from }Re^{-2/3}} \;+\; \underbrace{+\tfrac{1}{3}}_{\text{from }Pr^{+1/3}} \;+\; \underbrace{-0.14}_{\text{from }\mu/\mu_w}
\;=\; -\tfrac{2}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}-0.14 \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{3}-0.14 \;=\; -0.4733
$$

So $\mu$ appears with exponent $-0.4733 \approx -0.47$.

**Step 3 — Group the exponents of k.**

Exponent of $k$ in the right-hand side (note $k$ is in denominator on LHS but inside $Pr$ on RHS):
$$
+\tfrac{1}{3} \;(\text{from }Pr^{1/3}) \;-\; 1 \;(\text{from }k \text{ on LHS})
$$
Solving for `h`:
$$
h \;=\; 0.36 \,\rho^{2/3} N^{2/3} D^{4/3} \, C_p^{1/3} \, \mu^{-0.4733} \, k^{2/3} \, T^{-1}\, \mu_w^{-0.14}
$$

(Detailed step below for clarity.)

**Step 4 — Solve for h.**

Multiply both sides by $k/T$:
$$
h \;=\; \frac{k}{T} \cdot 0.36 \cdot \rho^{2/3} N^{2/3} D^{4/3} \, C_p^{1/3} \, \mu^{-0.4733} \, k^{1/3} \, \mu_w^{-0.14}
$$

Combine powers of $k$:
$$
k^{1} \cdot k^{1/3} \;=\; k^{4/3}
$$

Wait — recheck: $Pr^{1/3} = (C_p\mu/k)^{1/3} = C_p^{1/3}\mu^{1/3}k^{-1/3}$. So overall:
$$
h \;=\; 0.36 \,\rho^{2/3} N^{2/3} D^{4/3} \, C_p^{1/3} \, \mu^{1/3-2/3-0.14} \, k^{1-1/3} \, \mu_w^{-0.14}\, T^{-1}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad h \;=\; 0.36 \,\rho^{2/3} N^{2/3} D^{4/3} \, C_p^{1/3} \, \mu^{-0.4733} \, k^{2/3}\, \mu_w^{-0.14}\, T^{-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the explicit **engineering working form**. In KTU exams, you may either solve directly from the dimensionless form (preferred) or from this explicit form when individual fluid properties are tabulated.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2023 type):**
> A **paddle-agitated** jacketed bioreactor of inner diameter $T = 1.5 \text{ m}$ is fitted with an impeller of diameter $D = 0.5 \text{ m}$ running at $N = 120 \text{ rpm}$. The broth has the following bulk properties:
> $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3},\; \mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s},\; \mu_w = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s},\; C_p = 4180 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1},\; k = 0.6 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$.
> Estimate the inside film coefficient $h_i$ using the **Cooper correlation**.

**Step 1 — Convert N to s⁻¹.**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{120}{60} \;=\; 2.0 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Re.**

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 2.0 \times (0.5)^{2}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
\;=\; \frac{1000 \times 2.0 \times 0.25}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
\;=\; \frac{500}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
\;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Pr.**

$$
Pr \;=\; \frac{C_p \,\mu}{k} \;=\; \frac{4180 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.6} \;=\; \frac{4.18}{0.6} \;=\; 6.967
$$

**Step 4 — Compute viscosity ratio.**

$$
\frac{\mu}{\mu_w} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.7143
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Cooper (Paddle, $a = 0.36,\; b = 2/3,\; c = 1/3,\; d = 0.14$).**

$$
Nu \;=\; 0.36 \times (5.0 \times 10^{5})^{2/3} \times (6.967)^{1/3} \times (0.7143)^{0.14}
$$

Compute each factor:
- $(5.0 \times 10^{5})^{2/3} = (5.0)^{2/3} \times (10^{5})^{2/3} = 2.924 \times 10^{3.333} = 2.924 \times 2154.4 \approx 6299$
- $(6.967)^{1/3} \approx 1.908$
- $(0.7143)^{0.14} \approx 0.9562$

$$
Nu \;=\; 0.36 \times 6299 \times 1.908 \times 0.9562 \;\approx\; 4134
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for h.**

$$
h \;=\; \frac{Nu \, k}{T} \;=\; \frac{4134 \times 0.6}{1.5} \;\approx\; 1654 \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For water-like broth in a turbulent stirred tank, $h_i \approx 1000 - 5000 \text{ W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-1}$ is expected. Our answer $\approx 1654$ lies comfortably inside this band → **physically realistic**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of `h` to Parameters

$$
h \;\propto\; N^{2/3},\quad h \;\propto\; D^{4/3},\quad h \;\propto\; T^{-1}
$$

A double speed ⇒ $h$ increases by factor $2^{2/3} \approx 1.587$ (i.e., **+59 %**).
A double impeller diameter ⇒ $h$ increases by factor $2^{4/3} \approx 2.520$ (i.e., **+152 %**).

This sensitivity matrix is **exactly the kind of conceptual sub-question** KTU examiners ask under "Discuss the effect of agitation speed on the heat-transfer coefficient."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Cooper Correlation Pipeline

The block diagram below traces the **engineering information flow** from raw physical inputs to the final heat-transfer coefficient `h`. This is the conceptual "pipeline" examiners expect students to be able to draw from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Inputs: rho, mu, mu_w, Cp, k] --> B[Compute Prandtl Number Pr]
    A --> C[Compute Reynolds Number Re]
    D[Operating Conditions: N, D, T] --> C
    E[Geometry Selector: Paddle / PBT / Disc / Anchor] --> F[Lookup Cooper Constants a, b, c, d]
    F --> G[Form Dimensionless Nu Equation]
    B --> G
    C --> G
    A --> G
    G --> H[Calculate Nusselt Number Nu]
    H --> I[Convert Nu to h using h = Nu k divided by T]
    I --> J[Film Coefficient h inside wall]
    J --> K[Combine with h outside and wall resistance]
    K --> L[Overall U for Energy Balance Q = U A deltaT LMTD]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing Cooper Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{What is the impeller geometry?}
    S -- Paddle 2 blade --> P1[a=0.36, b=2/3, c=1/3, d=0.14]
    S -- Pitched Blade 45deg --> P2[a=0.76, b=2/3, c=1/3, d=0.14]
    S -- Disc Turbine Rushton --> P3[a=0.76, b=2/3, c=1/3, d=0.14]
    S -- Anchor or Gate --> P4[a=1.00, b=1/2, c=1/3, d=0.18]
    S -- Helical Ribbon --> P5[a=0.633, b=1/2, c=1/3, d=0.18]
    P1 --> OUT[Apply Nu = a Re^b Pr^c mu over mu_w to power d]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Schematic Mapping of Heat-Transfer Resistances

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Steam in jacket] -->|h outside| R1[Metal wall resistance x over k]
    R1 -->|h inside via Cooper| L[Bulk broth]
    L --> T[Process fluid temperature]
    T -.feedback.-> CTRL[Temperature controller adjusts N or steam flow]
    CTRL -.modifies.-> J
```

This **closed-loop architecture** is exactly how a real bioreactor's **PID temperature controller** interacts with the Cooper-predicted $h_i$: as broth viscosity or agitation speed drifts, $h_i$ shifts, and the controller compensates by throttling the steam valve.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Cooper correlation. State its general dimensionless form and the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The Cooper correlation is an empirical relation used to estimate the inside film heat-transfer coefficient $h_i$ in a jacketed, agitated vessel containing Newtonian fluid. Its general form is:

$$
\frac{h T}{k} \;=\; a \, Re^{b} \, Pr^{c}\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{\!d}
$$

Here `T` = tank diameter, `k` = thermal conductivity, `Re` = impeller Reynolds number, `Pr` = Prandtl number of the bulk fluid, and $\mu/\mu_w$ corrects for the viscosity change near the wall. Constants `a, b, c, d` depend on impeller geometry. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
**Why is a viscosity-correction factor $\mu/\mu_w$ included in the Cooper correlation?**

**Model Answer:**
When the wall temperature differs significantly from the bulk temperature, the thin fluid film adjacent to the wall exhibits a viscosity different from the bulk. Since heat transfer depends strongly on this near-wall viscosity, the ratio $\mu/\mu_w$ corrects the predicted $h$ for non-isothermal conditions. The exponent (~0.14 for turbines, ~0.18 for anchors) is empirically fitted. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the general form of the **Cooper correlation** for an agitated jacketed vessel and list the values of the empirical constants for (i) a paddle impeller and (ii) a pitched-blade turbine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A broth with $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $C_p = 4000 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$, $k = 0.55 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$, $\mu_w = 7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$ is agitated in a tank of $T = 2.0 \text{ m}$ using a **6-blade disc turbine** of diameter $D = 0.7 \text{ m}$ at $N = 90 \text{ rpm}$. Using the Cooper correlation, estimate the inside film coefficient $h_i$. State any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

1. The heat-transfer coefficient on the inside wall of a jacketed, agitated tank carrying a Newtonian fluid is governed by forced convection created by the rotating impeller.
2. Dimensionless analysis (Buckingham-Pi) yields three groups: $Nu$, $Re$, $Pr$. A fourth correction $\mu/\mu_w$ is added empirically to handle non-isothermal wall effects.
3. Thus the general form is:

$$
Nu \;=\; a \, Re^{b} \, Pr^{c}\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{\!d}
$$

   with $Nu = hT/k$, $Re = \rho N D^{2}/\mu$, $Pr = C_p\mu/k$.

4. The empirical constants are:

| Impeller | `a` | `b` | `c` | `d` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paddle | 0.36 | 2/3 | 1/3 | 0.14 |
| Pitched-blade turbine | 0.76 | 2/3 | 1/3 | 0.14 |

*(7 marks distributed: 2 marks for derivation logic, 2 marks for dimensionless form, 3 marks for table.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Assumptions:** Newtonian broth, jacket wall area ≈ inside surface, single-phase heat transfer, steady state, $h_i$ dominant over outside $h_o$ only when stated otherwise.

**Step 1 — Convert speed.**

$$
N \;=\; 90/60 \;=\; 1.5 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Re.**

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{1050 \times 1.5 \times (0.7)^{2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{1050 \times 1.5 \times 0.49}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{771.75}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1.5435 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Pr.**

$$
Pr \;=\; \frac{C_p\,\mu}{k} \;=\; \frac{4000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.55} \;=\; \frac{20}{0.55} \;=\; 36.36
$$

**Step 4 — Viscosity ratio.**

$$
\mu/\mu_w \;=\; 5/7 \;=\; 0.7143
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Cooper (Disc turbine: a = 0.76, b = 2/3, c = 1/3, d = 0.14).**

$$
Nu \;=\; 0.76 \times (1.5435 \times 10^{5})^{2/3} \times (36.36)^{1/3} \times (0.7143)^{0.14}
$$

- $(1.5435 \times 10^{5})^{2/3} \approx 2867$
- $(36.36)^{1/3} \approx 3.318$
- $(0.7143)^{0.14} \approx 0.9562$

$$
Nu \;\approx\; 0.76 \times 2867 \times 3.318 \times 0.9562 \;\approx\; 6921
$$

**Step 6 — Compute h.**

$$
h \;=\; \frac{Nu \cdot k}{T} \;=\; \frac{6921 \times 0.55}{2.0} \;\approx\; 1903 \text{ W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Answer:** $h_i \approx 1.9 \text{ kW m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-1}$ *(7 marks: 1 unit conversion + 2 each for Re/Pr/viscosity ratio + 2 final calculation.)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 type]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain how the **Cooper correlation** is embedded within the overall **heat-transfer design** of a jacketed bioreactor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Cooper correlation for a **paddle**, a **disc (Rushton) turbine**, and an **anchor** impeller. For a fixed vessel and fluid, in which case would you obtain the highest $h_i$ at identical $N$ and $D$? Justify with numerical scaling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

The heat-transfer design proceeds through the following functional blocks (text-rendered diagram):

```
[Jacket steam supply]
        ↓
[Outside film coefficient h_o]   ← computed via Kern's or equivalent
        ↓
[Metal wall conductive resistance x_w / k_w]
        ↓
[Inside film coefficient h_i]    ← computed via Cooper correlation:
                                  Nu = a·Re^b·Pr^c·(μ/μ_w)^d
        ↓
[Overall U via 1/U = 1/h_i + x_w/k_w + 1/h_o]
        ↓
[Energy balance: Q = U·A·ΔT_LMTD]
        ↓
[Jacket duty → steam mass flow, m_dot = Q / λ_steam]
```

The Cooper correlation block alone requires inputs: geometry (constants a, b, c, d), agitator speed $N$, impeller diameter $D$, and fluid thermal properties. It outputs $h_i$ which is then combined with the other two resistances to give `U`. The `U·A·ΔT` block then sizes the cooling duty. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

For a fixed vessel, fluid, $N$, and $D$, the three correlations differ only in the constants and exponents:

| Impeller | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paddle | 0.36 | 2/3 | 1/3 | 0.14 |
| Disc (Rushton) | 0.76 | 2/3 | 1/3 | 0.14 |
| Anchor | 1.00 | 1/2 | 1/3 | 0.18 |

**Numerical scaling at identical Re and Pr (assume $\mu/\mu_w = 1$):**

$$
Nu_{\text{Paddle}} / Nu_{\text{Disc}} \;=\; 0.36/0.76 \;\approx\; 0.47
$$

$$
Nu_{\text{Anchor}} / Nu_{\text{Disc}} \;=\; 1.00/0.76 \;\approx\; 1.32
$$

But Re dependence differs! At $Re = 10^{5}$:

- Paddle: $0.36 \times (10^{5})^{2/3} = 0.36 \times 4642 \approx 1671$
- Disc:   $0.76 \times (10^{5})^{2/3} = 0.76 \times 4642 \approx 3528$
- Anchor: $1.00 \times (10^{5})^{1/2} = 1.00 \times 316 \approx 316$

Hence **the disc (Rushton) turbine gives the highest $h_i$** at identical $N$ and $D$ in the **turbulent regime**. However, anchors dominate in **viscous / laminar broths** (e.g., mycelial fermentations, polymer solutions) because their geometry scrapes the wall directly. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm → s⁻¹**: $N$ in the Reynolds formula must be in s⁻¹, not rpm. A common −1 mark error.
> 2. **Mixing up `T` (tank) and `D` (impeller)**: The Cooper Nu uses `T` in the numerator, but Re uses $D^{2}$. Students often swap them → catastrophic error.
> 3. **Choosing wrong constants**: Paddle vs Rushton have *very* different `a` values (0.36 vs 0.76). If the question says "disc turbine," do **not** use the paddle constants.
> 4. **Ignoring $\mu/\mu_w$**: Examiners specifically test this. Always compute the ratio and raise it to the correct exponent.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions**: 1 mark is reserved for "Newtonian fluid, steady state, single phase" — say it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ Cooper correlation predicts the **inside film coefficient $h_i$** in a **jacketed, agitated Newtonian-fluid vessel**.
- ✅ General form: $Nu = a\, Re^{b}\, Pr^{c}\,(\mu/\mu_w)^{d}$.
- ✅ Constants depend **only on impeller geometry**: paddle (0.36), disc/pitched-blade (0.76), anchor (1.00).
- ✅ Standard exponents: $b = 2/3$ (turbine) or $1/2$ (anchor); $c = 1/3$; $d = 0.14$ (turbine) or $0.18$ (anchor).
- ✅ $Nu$ uses **tank diameter `T`**; $Re$ uses **impeller diameter `D`** — don't confuse them.
- ✅ $\mu$ is bulk viscosity; $\mu_w$ is wall viscosity; ratio captures non-isothermal correction.
- ✅ Convert rotational speed $N$ from **rpm to s⁻¹** before substituting.
- ✅ Higher $N$ or larger $D$ increases $h_i$; $\mu$ increase decreases $h_i$.
- ✅ Combine $h_i$ with $h_o$ and wall resistance $x_w/k_w$ for **overall $U$**.
- ✅ Use $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ to size jacket steam demand.
- ✅ Valid range typically $Re > 200$ (laminar limit) up to fully turbulent.

> [!TIP]
> **Final memory trick:** *"In Cooper, `T` carries the heat, `D` drives the flow, and `μ/μ_w` saves the day when the wall is hot."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Richards Correlation), Oxygen Transfer Mechanism, Assessment of KLa, Chemical Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Richards Correlation), Oxygen Transfer Mechanism, Assessment of KLa, Chemical Method ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Richards Correlation **Richards Correlation** is a semi-empirical engineering correlation proposed by **Richards (1961)** that relates the **volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($K_La$)** of oxygen in a fermenter to the **gassed power input per unit volume ($P_g/V$)** and the **superficial gas velocity ($v_s$)**. It is widely used in the design and scale-up of stirred-tank bioreactors because it captures the two most dominant hydrodynamic variables that govern oxygen transfer in mechanically agitated gas-liquid contactors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > The Richards Correlation is an empirical relationship used to predict the **volumetric oxygen transfer coefficient ($K_La$)** in agitated and aerated fermenters as a function of power input per unit volume and superficial gas velocity. It is expressed as: > $$K_La = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.33} (v_s)^{0.56}$$ > where $K$ is a system-dependent constant, $P_g/V$ is the gassed power input per unit fermenter volume (**W/m³**), and $v_s$ is the superficial gas velocity (**m/s**). **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of Richards Correlation as a "kitchen blender" recipe for dissolving sugar in water. The faster you stir (more **power input**), the smaller and more numerous the bubbles you create, and the faster the sugar (oxygen) dissolves. The more air you blow into the system (higher **superficial gas velocity**), the more bubbles sweep through the liquid. Richards mathematically "married" these two effects into a single predictive equation that engineers can use to design fermenters without running costly pilot-scale experiments. --- ### 1.2 Oxygen Transfer Mechanism The **Oxygen Transfer Mechanism** in a submerged fermentation describes the **stepwise transport of molecular oxygen ($O_2$)** from a gas bubble, through the surrounding liquid medium, and finally into the microbial cell where it is consumed in aerobic respiration. The overall process is governed by a **series of mass transfer resistances**, with the **stagnant liquid film surrounding the gas bubble** typically being the **rate-controlling (largest) resistance**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Concept:** > The driving force for oxygen transfer is the **concentration gradient** between the dissolved oxygen concentration at the gas-liquid interface ($C^*$, saturation) and the dissolved oxygen concentration in the bulk liquid ($C_L$). The rate of transfer is mathematically expressed by the **Two-Film Theory**: > $$OTR = K_La \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$ > where $OTR$ is the **Oxygen Transfer Rate (mol/m³·h)**, $K_La$ is the **volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹)**, and $(C^* - C_L)$ is the **driving force (mol/m³)**. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine blowing up a balloon underwater and letting the air slowly escape. The air doesn't directly mix with water — it must first dissolve at the balloon's surface, then diffuse through a thin "sluggish" water layer hugging the balloon, then travel through the bulk water, then cross another stagnant layer around a fish's gill, and only then reach the fish's bloodstream. Each of these "stops" along the journey is a **mass transfer resistance**, and the slowest one determines how fast the fish gets its oxygen. In a fermenter, the slowest "stop" is the **liquid film around the rising air bubble**. --- ### 1.3 Assessment of $K_La$ The **Assessment of $K_La$** refers to the experimental and analytical techniques used to **quantify the volumetric mass transfer coefficient** in a fermenter under specific operating conditions. Since $K_La$ is a critical parameter that determines whether a fermentation will be oxygen-limited, accurate measurement is essential. Common methods include the **chemical method (sodium sulfite oxidation)**, the **biological/gassing-out method (dynamic method)**, the **physical gassing-out method (nitrogen method)**, and the **steady-state oxygen balance method**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is $K_La$ important?** > A high $K_La$ means efficient oxygen supply; a low $K_La$ means poor aeration leading to cell starvation, low biomass yields, and accumulation of inhibitory byproducts such as ethanol or lactic acid. --- ### 1.4 Chemical Method (Sodium Sulfite Oxidation) The **Chemical Method** for $K_La$ assessment uses the **catalytic oxidation of sodium sulfite ($Na_2SO_3$) to sodium sulfate ($Na_2SO_4$)** by dissolved oxygen, in the presence of a **cobalt ($Co^{2+}$) or copper ($Cu^{2+}$) catalyst**. Because the reaction is **fast and zero-order with respect to sulfite** (as long as sulfite is in excess), the rate of sulfite disappearance equals the rate of oxygen transfer from gas to liquid, allowing $K_La$ to be determined by chemical titration. > [!NOTE] > **Governing Reaction:** > $$2 Na_2SO_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{Co^{2+} \text{ catalyst}} 2 Na_2SO_4$$ > The rate of oxygen transfer is then computed as: > $$K_La = \frac{N_{O_2}}{C^* - C_L}$$ > where $N_{O_2}$ is the molar oxygen absorption rate (mol/m³·h). **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of it as a "molecular sponge" experiment. You add sodium sulfite to the fermenter, and it greedily "eats" any oxygen that comes in contact with it. By measuring how fast the sulfite is consumed, you indirectly measure how fast oxygen is dissolving from the bubbles. The faster the bubbles deliver oxygen, the faster the sulfite disappears, and the higher the $K_La$ of your system. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Richards Correlation — Theoretical Breakdown The Richards Correlation is derived from **dimensional analysis** and **empirical fitting** of oxygen transfer data in pilot-scale stirred-tank fermenters. The underlying logic is as follows: - **Step 1: Identify the dominant hydrodynamic variables.** In a mechanically agitated gas-liquid system, the two most influential parameters on bubble formation, size, and residence time are: - The **agitation intensity** (captured as $P_g/V$, gassed power per unit volume). - The **gas throughput** (captured as $v_s$, superficial gas velocity). - **Step 2: Apply Buckingham Pi theorem.** These variables, along with physical properties of the broth (density, viscosity, diffusivity), are non-dimensionalized to identify the key dimensionless groups. Empirical regression on a large experimental dataset yields the exponents **0.33** and **0.56**. - **Step 3: Physical interpretation of exponents:** - The exponent **0.33** on $P_g/V$ indicates a **moderate sensitivity** of $K_La$ to agitation. Higher agitation breaks bubbles into smaller ones, increasing the interfacial area ($a$), which boosts $K_La$. - The exponent **0.56** on $v_s$ indicates a **stronger sensitivity** to gas flow rate. Higher $v_s$ increases both bubble hold-up and interfacial area. - **Step 4: Modified form for non-Newtonian broths.** For highly viscous mycelial fermentations (e.g., antibiotic production), the correlation is extended by replacing the broth viscosity with the **apparent viscosity ($\mu_{ap}$)** evaluated at an average shear rate: $$K_La = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.33} (v_s)^{0.56} \left(\frac{1}{\mu_{ap}}\right)^{x}$$ where $x$ is an empirically determined exponent (often between 0.4 and 0.7). - **Step 5: Real-world engineering utility.** This correlation is used during **scale-up** to maintain equivalent $K_La$ between a pilot (e.g., 100 L) and production (e.g., 50,000 L) fermenter by adjusting $P/V$ and $v_s$ appropriately. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Limitation:** Richards Correlation is most accurate for **low-viscosity Newtonian and mildly non-Newtonian** broths in conventional Rushton-type impeller systems. It may fail for highly viscous, shear-thinning, or foaming systems. --- ### 2.2 Oxygen Transfer Mechanism — Stepwise Resistances The transfer of oxygen from a gas bubble to a microbial cell encounters **seven sequential resistances**: | Step | Resistance Location | Relative Magnitude | Remarks | |:----:|:-------------------|:------------------:|:--------| | 1 | Bulk gas phase | **Negligible** | Gaseous diffusion is fast. | | 2 | Gas-liquid interface | **Low** | Interfacial transfer is rapid. | | 3 | **Stagnant liquid film around bubble** | **High (Rate-controlling)** | Determines overall $K_La$. | | 4 | Bulk liquid phase | Moderate | Convective transport helps. | | 5 | Stagnant liquid film around cell | Moderate | Often ignored in design. | | 6 | Cell membrane | Low-Moderate | Depends on cell physiology. | | 7 | Intracellular transport | Low | Diffusion inside cytoplasm. | > [!NOTE] > The overall rate is governed by the **largest resistance** (the liquid film around the bubble), which is the foundation of the **Two-Film Theory** and gives rise to the simple equation: $OTR = K_La(C^* - C_L)$. --- ### 2.3 Chemical Method — Theoretical Logic The sodium sulfite oxidation technique relies on the following chain of logic: - **Step 1:** Dissolve sodium sulfite in the fermenter at a concentration of approximately **0.5 to 1.0 M** (well above the saturation concentration of $O_2$). - **Step 2:** Add a catalyst — **cobalt sulfate ($CoSO_4$)** at $10^{-3}$ M — to accelerate the reaction rate. - **Step 3:** Bubble air (or oxygen) into the system. The reaction $2SO_3^{2-} + O_2 \to 2SO_4^{2-}$ occurs in the bulk liquid as fast as oxygen dissolves. - **Step 4:** Since the reaction is **zero-order in sulfite** (sulfite is in vast excess), the rate of oxygen absorption is **constant** and **equal to the rate of oxygen transfer** from the gas bubbles. - **Step 5:** Withdraw samples at fixed time intervals and analyze the **residual sulfite** by **iodometric back-titration** with standard sodium thiosulfate using starch indicator. - **Step 6:** Plot residual $[SO_3^{2-}]$ vs. time. The slope gives the rate of sulfite consumption, from which the oxygen transfer rate is calculated stoichiometrically. - **Step 7:** Compute $K_La$ using the equation $K_La = N_{O_2} / (C^* - C_L)$. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:-----:|:------------| | 1 | $OTR = K_La (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate equation | mol/m³·h | Basic mass transfer design | | 2 | $K_La = K \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.33} (v_s)^{0.56}$ | Richards Correlation | h⁻¹ | Scale-up and design of STRs | | 3 | $v_s = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_c}$ | Superficial gas velocity | m/s | Richards input variable | | 4 | $C^* = \dfrac{y_{O_2} P}{H}$ | Equilibrium dissolved $O_2$ | mol/m³ | Driving force calculation | | 5 | $\Delta C = C^* - C_L$ | Concentration driving force | mol/m³ | OTR numerator term | | 6 | $N_{O_2} = \dfrac{\Delta [SO_3^{2-}]}{2 \cdot \Delta t}$ | $O_2$ absorption rate | mol/m³·h | Chemical method core | | 7 | $K_La = \dfrac{N_{O_2}}{C^* - C_L}$ | $K_La$ from chemical method | h⁻¹ | Chemical method output | | 8 | $\mu_{ap} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law apparent viscosity | Pa·s | Modified Richards for non-Newtonian | | 9 | $N_A = K_L (C^* - C_L)$ | Local flux across film | mol/m²·h | Two-film theory basis | | 10 | $K_G = \dfrac{D_{O_2}}{\delta_L}$ | Liquid-side mass transfer coefficient | m/s | Fundamental definition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exponent Memory Tip:** "Richards loves **0.33** (one-third) for power and **0.56** for gas." A common KTU board exam trick is to swap these exponents to test conceptual understanding. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use of $K_La$ / Richards Correlation | |:-------|:------------------------------------| | **Antibiotic (Penicillin) Fermentation** | Maintain $K_La > 200$ h⁻¹ for high-producing strains of *Penicillium chrysogenum*. | | **Recombinant Protein Production** | Ensure oxygen supply matches high metabolic demand of *E. coli* and *Pichia pastoris*. | | **Biopolymer (PHB, Xanthan) Production** | Scale-up of highly viscous xanthan gum broths using modified Richards with $\mu_{ap}$ correction. | | **Wastewater Treatment (Aerobic)** | Design of surface aerators and diffused-air systems using oxygen transfer principles. | | **Mammalian Cell Culture** | Avoid high $K_La$ (sparging) — use surface aeration to prevent shear damage to delicate cells. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of $K_La$ from Chemical Method Data **Given:** - Fermenter volume: $V = 5$ L (working volume) - Initial $[SO_3^{2-}] = 0.5$ M - Catalyst: $Co^{2+}$ at $10^{-3}$ M - Air flow rate: $Q_g = 1$ L/min = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s - Temperature: $T = 25°C$, $P = 1$ atm - Dissolved oxygen saturation in air at 25°C: $C^* = 0.26$ mol/m³ - Bulk liquid $C_L \approx 0$ (sulfite consumes $O_2$ instantly) **Step 1: Sample residual sulfite at two time points** | Time (s) | Residual $[SO_3^{2-}]$ (M) | |:--------:|:-------------------------:| | 0 | 0.500 | | 600 | 0.487 | | 1200 | 0.474 | | 1800 | 0.461 | | 2400 | 0.448 | **Step 2: Calculate the rate of sulfite consumption** The change in sulfite concentration per unit time (slope of the linear plot) is: $$\frac{\Delta [SO_3^{2-}]}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.500 - 0.448}{2400 - 0} = \frac{0.052}{2400} = 2.167 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M/s}$$ Converting to mol/m³·h (multiply by 1000 for M → mol/m³, and by 3600 for s → h): $$\frac{\Delta [SO_3^{2-}]}{\Delta t} = 2.167 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 78.0 \text{ mol/m³·h}$$ **Step 3: Convert to oxygen transfer rate using stoichiometry** From the balanced reaction: $$2 SO_3^{2-} + O_2 \to 2 SO_4^{2-}$$ For every **2 moles of sulfite consumed**, **1 mole of oxygen** is absorbed. Therefore: $$N_{O_2} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\Delta [SO_3^{2-}]}{\Delta t} = \frac{78.0}{2} = 39.0 \text{ mol/m³·h}$$ **Step 4: Apply the OTR equation to solve for $K_La$** Using $C_L \approx 0$ (sulfite scavenges all $O_2$): $$OTR = K_La (C^* - C_L)$$ $$K_La = \frac{N_{O_2}}{C^* - C_L} = \frac{39.0}{0.26 - 0} = \frac{39.0}{0.26} = 150.0 \text{ h}^{-1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The fermenter has a $K_La$ of **150 h⁻¹**, which is typical for a **laboratory-scale stirred-tank bioreactor** operating at moderate agitation and aeration. This value is sufficient for many bacterial and yeast fermentations but marginal for high-cell-density cultures. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Richards Correlation — Conceptual & Dimensional Basis While Richards Correlation is **empirical**, its form can be rationalized by dimensional reasoning: **Step 1: List the governing variables and their dimensions** | Variable | Symbol | Dimensions | |:---------|:------:|:----------:| | Volumetric mass transfer coefficient | $K_La$ | $T^{-1}$ | | Gassed power per unit volume | $P_g/V$ | $M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-3}$ | | Superficial gas velocity | $v_s$ | $L \cdot T^{-1}$ | | Liquid density | $\rho_L$ | $M \cdot L^{-3}$ | | Liquid viscosity | $\mu_L$ | $M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-1}$ | | Diffusivity of $O_2$ in liquid | $D_L$ | $L^2 \cdot T^{-1}$ | **Step 2: Apply Buckingham Pi theorem to obtain dimensionless groups** The dimensionless $\pi$-groups are: $$\pi_1 = \frac{K_La \cdot L_c}{v_s}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{P_g}{V \cdot \rho_L \cdot v_s^3}, \quad \pi_3 = \frac{\mu_L}{\rho_L \cdot D_L}$$ where $L_c$ is a characteristic length (e.g., impeller diameter). Since $K_La$ is the target variable, inverting $\pi_1$ gives: $$K_La \propto \frac{v_s}{L_c}$$ **Step 3: Re-express $K_La$ in terms of $P_g/V$ and $v_s$** Eliminating $L_c$ between $\pi_1$ and $\pi_2$ and fitting experimental data yields: $$K_La = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.33} (v_s)^{0.56}$$ where the constants 0.33 and 0.56 absorb the effects of $\rho_L$, $\mu_L$, $D_L$, and geometric factors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** You will NOT be asked to derive the exponents. You must, however, justify them qualitatively (e.g., "0.33 on $P_g/V$ reflects that agitation primarily increases interfacial area $a$, while 0.56 on $v_s$ reflects that gas velocity increases both area and driving force"). --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — $K_La$ Assessment Methods | Feature | Chemical Method ($Na_2SO_3$) | Biological (Dynamic) Method | Physical Gassing-Out ($N_2$) Method | Steady-State Oxygen Balance | |:--------|:----------------------------:|:---------------------------:|:----------------------------------:|:---------------------------:| | **Principle** | Sulfite scavenges $O_2$; titration gives OTR | Sudden air cut-off; $C_L$ decay measured | $N_2$ stripping; re-aeration $C_L$ rise | Mass balance on $O_2$ in/out of fermenter | | **Catalyst Required** | Yes ($Co^{2+}$ or $Cu^{2+}$) | No (uses real culture) | No (uses broth without cells) | No | | **Cell Presence** | No (cell-free) | Yes (live culture) | No (cell-free) | Yes (real culture) | | **Reagents Needed** | Sodium sulfite, catalyst, iodine, thiosulfate, starch | None | Pure nitrogen gas | Dissolved $O_2$ probe | | **Disrupts Fermentation** | Yes (must be done in separate vessel) | No (in situ) | No (in situ) | No (in situ) | | **Measures True $K_La$ at Process Conditions** | Approximate (broth is different) | Yes | Approximate | Yes | | **Speed** | Slow (titration, 1-2 h) | Fast (minutes) | Fast (minutes) | Fast (continuous) | | **KTU Typical Marks** | 7-10 | 3-5 | 3-5 | 3-5 | | **Disadvantage** | Alters broth composition; not real system | Affected by cell respiration dynamics | No respiration; not real condition | Requires accurate gas analysis | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Oxygen Transfer Mechanism — Seven-Step Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["GAS BULBLE
Bulk Gas Phase
O2 partial pressure high"] B["GAS-LIQUID
INTERFACE
Equilibrium at C-star"] C["STAGNANT LIQUID FILM
AROUND BUBBLE
RATE CONTROLLING
RESISTANCE"] D["BULK LIQUID
PHASE
Convective transport
Dissolved O2 equals C-L"] E["STAGNANT LIQUID FILM
AROUND CELL
Secondary resistance"] F["CELL MEMBRANE
AND CYTOPLASM
Intracellular diffusion"] G["MITOCHONDRIA /
RESPIRATORY ENZYMES
O2 consumed in ETC"] A -->|Step 1 Negligible resistance| B B -->|Step 2 Low resistance| C C -->|Step 3 HIGHEST RESISTANCE
Governs KLa| D D -->|Step 4 Moderate| E E -->|Step 5 Moderate| F F -->|Step 6 Low| G G -->|Step 7 Low| H["CO2 AND
WATER PRODUCED
Final products"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip:** Each block in the flowchart represents a **mass transfer resistance** in series. The thickest conceptual barrier (Step 3 — the liquid film around the gas bubble) dictates the overall rate, which is why the entire engineering discipline of bioreactor design focuses on **maximizing interfacial area and turbulence** to thin this film. --- ### 4.2 Chemical Method — Experimental Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([START: Prepare Fermenter]) --> Prep["Fill fermenter with
0.5 M Na2SO3 solution
and 10 power minus 3 M CoSO4"] Prep --> Equil["Equilibrate at
25 degree C
and 1 atm"] Equil --> AirOn["Turn ON air supply
at fixed Q-g and N"] AirOn --> T0["Withdraw sample at t = 0
Record initial sulfite"] T0 --> Loop["Sample at regular
time intervals
t = 5, 10, 15, 20 min"] Loop --> Titrate["Iodometric back-titration
with Na2S2O3
Starch indicator"] Titrate --> Calc["Calculate residual
SO3 two minus
at each time point"] Calc --> Plot["Plot residual SO3 two minus
versus time
Determine slope"] Plot --> ComputeRate["Convert slope to
O2 transfer rate
N O2 = slope / 2"] ComputeRate --> ComputeKLa["Compute K-La equals
N O2 divided by C-star minus C-L
Assume C-L equals zero"] ComputeKLa --> Report[/"REPORT: K-La in h to power minus 1
Compare with design target"/] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why C-L = 0?** Because the cobalt-catalyzed sulfite oxidation is **instantaneous** relative to oxygen dissolution, any oxygen that enters the bulk liquid is immediately consumed. This makes the **driving force maximum** ($C^* - 0 = C^*$), simplifying the calculation and ensuring **sensitivity to the true mass transfer rate**. --- ### 4.3 $K_La$ Assessment Methods — Master Overview ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["K-La ASSESSMENT
METHODS IN
BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING"] Root --> M1["CHEMICAL METHOD
Sodium sulfite
oxidation"] Root --> M2["BIOLOGICAL METHOD
Dynamic gassing-out
using live culture"] Root --> M3["PHYSICAL METHOD
Gassing-out with
pure nitrogen"] Root --> M4["STEADY-STATE METHOD
Oxygen mass balance
on fermenter"] M1 --> S1["Best for cell-free
design studies
offline"] M2 --> S2["Best for real-time
process monitoring
in situ"] M3 --> S3["Best for physical
hydrodynamics studies
no respiration"] M4 --> S4["Best for production
fermenters
requires gas analyzer"] ``` --- ### 4.4 Richards Correlation — Functional Influence Map ```mermaid flowchart LR In1["INPUT 1
Gassed Power
P-g / V
W per m cube"] In2["INPUT 2
Superficial Gas
Velocity v-s
m per s"] Sys["RICHARDS
CORRELATION
K-La equals K times
P-g over V to 0.33
times v-s to 0.56"] Out1["OUTPUT
K-La in h to minus 1
Volumetric mass
transfer coefficient"] Use1["USED FOR
Scale-up of STR"] Use2["USED FOR
Design of new
fermenters"] Use3["USED FOR
Optimization of
operating conditions"] In1 --> Sys In2 --> Sys Sys --> Out1 Out1 --> Use1 Out1 --> Use2 Out1 --> Use3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the **Richards Correlation** for the volumetric mass transfer coefficient in a stirred-tank fermenter and mention the significance of the exponents **0.33** and **0.56**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Richards Correlation is an empirical equation that estimates the **volumetric oxygen transfer coefficient ($K_La$)** in mechanically agitated gas-liquid fermenters. It is given by: $$K_La = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.33} (v_s)^{0.56}$$ where $K$ is a constant, $P_g/V$ is the gassed power input per unit volume, and $v_s$ is the superficial gas velocity. **Significance of exponents:** - **0.33 on $P_g/V$:** Indicates that increasing agitation power moderately increases $K_La$, primarily by breaking bubbles into smaller ones and increasing the interfacial area $a$. - **0.56 on $v_s$:** Indicates that increasing gas velocity has a **stronger effect** on $K_La$, as it enhances both bubble hold-up and interfacial area. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the exponents in reverse (0.56 on $P_g/V$ and 0.33 on $v_s$). This is a **direct 1-mark loss**. Memorize: "Power gets **0.33** (one-third), Gas gets **0.56**." --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the role of **cobalt ions ($Co^{2+}$)** in the chemical method of $K_La$ determination? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cobalt ions ($Co^{2+}$), added typically as cobalt sulfate ($CoSO_4$) at a concentration of $10^{-3}$ M, act as a **homogeneous catalyst** that accelerates the oxidation of sodium sulfite ($Na_2SO_3$) by dissolved oxygen. The catalyzed reaction is: $$2 Na_2SO_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{Co^{2+}} 2 Na_2SO_4$$ The catalyst ensures the reaction is **instantaneous and zero-order** with respect to sulfite (as long as sulfite is in excess). This guarantees that the rate-limiting step is the **dissolution of oxygen** (i.e., the actual mass transfer), not the chemical reaction. Therefore, the rate of sulfite disappearance directly equals the rate of oxygen transfer, allowing accurate computation of $K_La$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **stepwise mechanism of oxygen transfer** from a gas bubble to a microbial cell in a submerged fermentation. Identify the **rate-controlling resistance** and justify your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **chemical method (sodium sulfite oxidation technique)** for the experimental determination of $K_La$ in a fermenter. Include the governing reaction, role of catalyst, procedure, and the formula used. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q(a) — Oxygen Transfer Mechanism (7 marks):** The transfer of oxygen from a gas bubble to the site of consumption inside a microbial cell occurs through **seven sequential mass transfer steps**, each associated with a specific resistance: **Step 1: Bulk gas phase transport.** Oxygen molecules diffuse through the bulk gas inside the bubble. This resistance is **negligible** because gas-phase diffusion coefficients are $\sim 10^4$ times higher than liquid-phase values. (0.5 mark) **Step 2: Gas-liquid interface.** At the bubble surface, oxygen molecules cross from the gas phase into the liquid phase. The concentration of dissolved $O_2$ at this interface is $C^*$, in equilibrium with the gas-phase partial pressure as per **Henry's Law**: $$C^* = \frac{y_{O_2} P}{H}$$ This resistance is **low** due to the rapid equilibration. (0.5 mark) **Step 3: Stagnant liquid film around the bubble (Rate-Controlling).** The dissolved oxygen must diffuse through a thin, relatively stagnant layer of liquid that surrounds each rising bubble. According to the **Two-Film Theory**, this film offers the **largest single resistance** to mass transfer. The flux is: $$N_A = \frac{D_{O_2}}{\delta_L} (C^* - C_L) = K_L (C^* - C_L)$$ where $\delta_L$ is the film thickness, $D_{O_2}$ is the diffusivity, and $K_L$ is the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient. (1.5 marks) **Step 4: Bulk liquid phase transport.** Once past the film, oxygen is carried through the bulk liquid by **convective mixing** (induced by agitation). This resistance is **moderate** and is often neglected when designing well-mixed fermenters. (0.5 mark) **Step 5: Stagnant liquid film around the cell.** As the cell is a discrete particle, another stagnant liquid film surrounds it. This resistance is **moderate** and can be significant in dense cell suspensions. (0.5 mark) **Step 6: Cell membrane and cytoplasm.** Oxygen crosses the cell wall/membrane and diffuses through the cytoplasm. For **Gram-negative bacteria** and **yeasts**, this resistance is **low**; for **mycelial pellets**, it can be **high** due to diffusion limitations. (0.5 mark) **Step 7: Intracellular consumption.** Finally, oxygen is consumed in the **electron transport chain** in the mitochondria (eukaryotes) or on the cell membrane (prokaryotes). This is a **biochemical step**, not a mass transfer step, but it removes $O_2$ and maintains the concentration gradient. (0.5 mark) **Identification of the rate-controlling resistance:** The **stagnant liquid film around the gas bubble (Step 3)** is the rate-controlling step because the liquid-phase diffusion coefficient $D_{O_2}$ is much smaller than the gas-phase value, and the film thickness $\delta_L$, though small, creates a substantial resistance that dominates all other steps. This is the foundation of the **Two-Film Theory** and the simple OTR equation: $OTR = K_La(C^* - C_L)$. (2 marks) > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Loss:** Students often describe all seven steps but **fail to identify Step 3 as rate-controlling**, or fail to write the **Two-Film equation** explicitly. This leads to a **2-mark deduction** even when the description is correct. --- **Model Solution for Q(b) — Chemical Method for $K_La$ (7 marks):** **Principle:** The chemical method is based on the **catalytic oxidation of sodium sulfite** by dissolved oxygen in the presence of cobalt or copper ions. (0.5 mark) **Governing Reaction:** $$2 Na_2SO_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{Co^{2+} \text{ or } Cu^{2+}} 2 Na_2SO_4$$ **Key Assumption:** As long as sodium sulfite is present in **excess** (typically 0.5 to 1.0 M), the reaction is **zero-order** in sulfite and **instantaneous** in the bulk liquid. Therefore, the dissolved oxygen concentration in the bulk liquid remains effectively **zero** ($C_L \approx 0$), and the **rate-limiting step is the physical transfer of oxygen** from the gas bubble to the liquid. (1 mark) **Role of Catalyst:** Cobalt sulfate ($CoSO_4$) at $\sim 10^{-3}$ M accelerates the reaction rate so that all dissolved $O_2$ is consumed immediately upon entering the bulk liquid. Without the catalyst, the uncatalyzed reaction is too slow to keep pace with mass transfer, leading to underestimation of $K_La$. (1 mark) **Procedure:** 1. **Preparation:** Fill the fermenter (or a separate aeration vessel) with a sodium sulfite solution (0.5 M) and add cobalt sulfate catalyst. (0.5 mark) 2. **Equilibration:** Bring the system to the desired temperature (e.g., 25°C) and pressure (1 atm). (0.25 mark) 3. **Aeration:** Start agitation and air sparging at the chosen impeller speed and gas flow rate. (0.25 mark) 4. **Sampling:** Withdraw samples of the liquid at regular time intervals (e.g., every 5 minutes for 20-30 minutes). (0.5 mark) 5. **Titration:** Analyze residual sulfite by **iodometric back-titration**: excess standard iodine is added to oxidize the remaining sulfite, and the unreacted iodine is titrated with standard sodium thiosulfate using **starch indicator**. (1 mark) 6. **Plotting:** Plot residual $[SO_3^{2-}]$ vs. time; determine the slope. (0.5 mark) 7. **Calculation:** Compute $K_La$ using: $$N_{O_2} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\Delta [SO_3^{2-}]}{\Delta t}, \quad K_La = \frac{N_{O_2}}{C^* - C_L}$$ With $C_L \approx 0$: $$K_La = \frac{N_{O_2}}{C^*} \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$ **Advantages and Limitations:** - ✅ Simple, inexpensive, and requires no specialized sensors. - ❌ Must be done in a **cell-free system**, so it does not capture the effect of cells and surfactants on $K_La$. - ❌ Adds sulfite and cobalt to the broth, making it unsuitable for direct in-process monitoring. (0.5 mark) > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often **forget the factor of 2** in converting sulfite consumption to oxygen transfer. Every 2 moles of sulfite consumed corresponds to only 1 mole of $O_2$. Forgetting this gives a $K_La$ value that is **double the correct value** — a guaranteed 1-mark penalty. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Discuss the **various methods for the assessment of $K_La$** in fermenters, highlighting the principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Richards Correlation** for $K_La$ estimation in stirred-tank bioreactors. Explain its utility in **scale-up** of fermentation processes. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for B(a) — Methods of $K_La$ Assessment (7 marks):** **Method 1: Chemical Method (Sodium Sulfite Oxidation)** - **Principle:** Catalytic oxidation of sulfite by dissolved $O_2$; rate of $O_2$ transfer equals rate of sulfite disappearance. - **Procedure:** Aerate sulfite solution with $Co^{2+}$ catalyst; sample and titrate. - **Advantage:** Simple, accurate for design studies. - **Limitation:** Cell-free, off-line, not representative of real broth. (1.5 marks) **Method 2: Biological / Dynamic Gassing-Out Method** - **Principle:** During a real fermentation, the air supply is suddenly cut off. The dissolved oxygen concentration $C_L$ falls linearly as cells consume $O_2$. From the slope: $$- \frac{dC_L}{dt} = q_{O_2} X = OUR \text{ (oxygen uptake rate)}$$ After the $O_2$ falls to a low value, air is reintroduced. The **re-aeration curve** is governed by: $$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} X$$ By fitting this curve, both $K_La$ and $q_{O_2} X$ can be extracted. (1.5 marks) **Method 3: Physical Gassing-Out (Nitrogen) Method** - **Principle:** Dissolved $O_2$ is stripped from a cell-free broth by bubbling pure nitrogen. Once $C_L = 0$, air is reintroduced, and the rising $C_L$ is recorded by a DO probe. Since there is no respiration ($q_{O_2} X = 0$): $$C_L(t) = C^* (1 - e^{-K_La \cdot t})$$ Fitting the re-aeration curve to an exponential gives $K_La$. (1.5 marks) **Method 4: Steady-State Oxygen Balance Method** - **Principle:** At steady state, the rate of $O_2$ supply equals the rate of $O_2$ consumption. A mass balance on dissolved $O_2$ yields: $$K_La (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} X$$ By measuring $C_L$ (DO probe) and independently measuring $q_{O_2} X$ (off-gas analysis), $K_La$ can be calculated. (1.5 marks) **Comparative Summary:** (1 mark) | Method | In situ | Cells | Speed | Best For | |:-------|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|:--------| | Chemical | No | No | Slow | Design | | Biological | Yes | Yes | Fast | Real fermentation | | Physical | Yes | No | Fast | Hydrodynamics | | Steady-State | Yes | Yes | Continuous | Production monitoring | > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Tip:** Most students describe only **2 methods** in this question. A complete answer must cover **at least 3** to score full marks. The **biological and chemical methods are the most frequently asked** in KTU exams. --- **Model Solution for B(b) — Richards Correlation Derivation & Scale-up (7 marks):** **Theoretical Basis:** The Richards Correlation is an **empirical relationship** that predicts $K_La$ from the two most influential operating variables in a stirred-tank fermenter: - **Gassed power input per unit volume** ($P_g/V$) - **Superficial gas velocity** ($v_s$) The functional form is: $$K_La = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.33} (v_s)^{0.56}$$ **Justification of the Form via Dimensional Analysis:** Let $K_La$ have dimensions of $[T^{-1}]$, $P_g/V$ have dimensions of $[M L^{-1} T^{-3}]$, and $v_s$ have dimensions of $[L T^{-1}]$. The dimensions of the right-hand side are: $$[K] \cdot [M L^{-1} T^{-3}]^{0.33} \cdot [L T^{-1}]^{0.56}$$ $$= [K] \cdot M^{0.33} L^{-0.33 - 0.56} T^{-0.99 - 0.56}$$ $$= [K] \cdot M^{0.33} L^{-0.89} T^{-1.55}$$ For dimensional consistency, $K$ must carry compensating dimensions, confirming that this is an **empirical (not fundamental)** equation. (2 marks) **Why the Exponents Take These Values:** - The exponent **0.33 on $P_g/V$** reflects that agitation primarily **breaks bubbles** into smaller ones, increasing the interfacial area $a$. This is a moderate effect because too much agitation causes bubble coalescence in some systems. - The exponent **0.56 on $v_s$** reflects that higher gas flow rate increases both **bubble hold-up** (volume fraction of gas) and **interfacial area**, which together strongly influence $K_La$. (1.5 marks) **Application in Scale-Up:** During scale-up from a pilot (e.g., 100 L) to a production (e.g., 50,000 L) fermenter, the goal is often to **maintain $K_La$ constant** so that oxygen supply remains adequate. Using Richards: $$K_La^{pilot} = K_La^{production}$$ $$K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)_p^{0.33} (v_s)_p^{0.56} = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)_m^{0.33} (v_s)_m^{0.56}$$ Engineers use this to compute the **required agitator power** and **gas flow rate** at the larger scale. A typical scale-up rule is to keep $P_g/V$ **constant** (same power density) and adjust $v_s$ based on the gas dispersion pattern. (2 marks) **Limitations:** - Valid only for **low-to-moderate viscosity** broths. - Cannot be used for **highly shear-sensitive** mammalian cell cultures. - Does not account for **antifoam agents** or **surfactants** that alter bubble coalescence. (1.5 marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Trap:** A common exam question is: *"Can Richards Correlation be used to predict $K_La$ in a 50,000 L fermenter from data obtained in a 5 L fermenter?"* The correct answer is **"approximately yes, with careful geometric similarity, but with significant uncertainty"**. A naive "yes" loses marks for not acknowledging the **limitations**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Oxygen Transfer & $K_La$** - **$K_La$ Definition:** Volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹) combining liquid-side mass transfer coefficient ($K_L$) and specific interfacial area ($a$). It is the single most important parameter governing **oxygen supply** in aerobic fermentations. - **OTR Equation:** $OTR = K_La (C^* - C_L)$ — derived from the **Two-Film Theory**. The driving force is $(C^* - C_L)$. - **Rate-Controlling Resistance:** The **stagnant liquid film around the gas bubble** is the largest single resistance. Engineering efforts to **increase $K_La$** focus on: - ↑ Agitation speed (increases $a$ by breaking bubbles) - ↑ Air flow rate (increases $a$ and hold-up) - Use of **baffles** (prevents vortexing) - Use of **porous spargers** (produces finer bubbles) - Use of **low-foam impellers** (e.g., Rushton, Scaba) - **Richards Correlation (1961):** $$K_La = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.33} (v_s)^{0.56}$$ - Exponent on $P_g/V$ = **0.33** (one-third) - Exponent on $v_s$ = **0.56** - **Chemical Method Principle:** - Reaction: $2 Na_2SO_3 + O_2 \to 2 Na_2SO_4$ - Catalyst: $Co^{2+}$ at $10^{-3}$ M - Assumption: $C_L \approx 0$ (sulfite scavenges all $O_2$) - Titration: Iodometric with sodium thiosulfate - Stoichiometry: 2 mol sulfite = 1 mol $O_2$ - **Four Major $K_La$ Assessment Methods:** 1. **Chemical** (sodium sulfite, off-line, design studies) 2. **Biological/Dynamic** (in situ, real culture, fast) 3. **Physical Gassing-Out** (in situ, no cells, $N_2$ stripping) 4. **Steady-State Balance** (in situ, real culture, continuous) - **C* Calculation (Henry's Law):** $$C^* = \frac{y_{O_2} P}{H}$$ For air at 25°C, 1 atm: $C^* \approx 0.26$ mol/m³ ≈ **8 ppm (mg/L)**. - **Numerical Tip:** $K_La$ in h⁻¹ is a **pseudo-first-order rate constant**. A typical lab STR achieves $K_La \approx 100-300$ h⁻¹; industrial fermenters can exceed $1000$ h⁻¹. - **Scale-Up Rule of Thumb:** To maintain $K_La$ on scale-up, keep $P_g/V$ approximately **constant** between scales. Geometric similarity (same impeller-to-tank diameter ratio) is essential. - **Common Exam Mnemonic — "RICH-0.33, GAS-0.56"**: - **RICHards → 0.33** for agitation power - **GAS velocity → 0.56** - **Pitfall to Avoid:** Never confuse $K_La$ (volumetric, h⁻¹) with $K_L$ (film coefficient, m/s). They differ by a factor of $a$ (m²/m³).

topics.title: Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method** is an advanced, two-vessel experimental technique used in bioprocess engineering for the in-situ determination of the **Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)** and the **Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient ($K_La$)** of a microbial culture operating inside a fermenter. It is a refined variant of the classical dynamic gassing out method, where a second (reference) fermenter running in parallel acts as an electrical/optical baseline compensator. By mathematically differencing the dissolved oxygen (DO) signals of the test fermenter (containing live, respiring cells) and the reference fermenter (identical medium but devoid of biological oxygen consumption), the true biological oxygen demand of the microorganisms is isolated from any instrumental drift, probe response lag, or non-biological gas exchange. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBBT502 / Module 2):** > This method is a compulsory sub-topic under the unit *"Aeration, Agitation, and Mass Transfer in Bioprocesses"*. KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive the governing equation and draw the schematic of the air-off / air-on cycle. Students must commit the OUR and $K_La$ equations to memory verbatim. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Bucket" Intuition Imagine you are a **doctor measuring the heart rate of a sleeping patient** in a noisy hospital room. - You have **two stethoscopes**: one on the patient's chest, and one placed on the pillow next to them (to capture the room's ambient noise). - If you simply count the beats from the patient's stethoscope, the *background hospital noise* will trick you into miscounting. - However, if you **subtract the ambient sound** (pillow stethoscope) from the chest stethoscope signal, you obtain the **true heartbeat rate**. The **Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method works on exactly this principle**: | Component | Hospital Analogy | Bioprocess Setup | |---|---|---| | Patient's chest stethoscope | Actual biological respiration | **Test Fermenter** (with live cells) | | Pillow stethoscope | Ambient noise baseline | **Reference Fermenter** (no live cells) | | Subtraction circuit | Difference amplifier | **Differential Amplifier / Software** | | Cleaned heartbeat signal | True heart rate | **True Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Both fermenters are subjected to the *exact same* sudden interruption and re-establishment of the air supply. Because the reference fermenter has no living cells, its DO only drifts due to **non-biological** reasons (slow probe equilibration, minor leaks). The differential reading therefore mathematically cancels all non-biological effects, leaving the pure microbial oxygen demand. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants - **Dissolved Oxygen Concentration ($C_L$):** measured in **mg/L** or **mmol/L** of $O_2$. - **Saturated Dissolved Oxygen Concentration ($C^*$):** typically **~7–8 mg/L at 30 °C** in water-based media (bold for emphasis in KTU answers). - **Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient ($K_La$):** measured in **h⁻¹** or **s⁻¹**. - **OUR ($Q_{O_2} \cdot X$):** measured in **mg $O_2$ / (L · h)**. - **Henry's Law Constant for $O_2$ at 30 °C:** approximately **4.4 × 10⁴ atm** (often given as **$H_c = 1.3$ mmol/(L·atm)** in bioprocess textbooks). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Principle of Operation — Three Distinct Phases The experimental run is divided into **three sequential phases**. KTU board examiners expect students to label these phases explicitly in diagrams. ### Phase I — Initial Saturation (Steady Aeration) - Air is sparged continuously into **both** the test and reference fermenters. - The medium is fully saturated; $C_L = C^*$ in both vessels. - Microbial metabolism is in quasi-steady state; the OUR is balanced by the oxygen transfer rate (OTR). - This phase establishes the **$C^*$ baseline**. ### Phase II — The Gassing-Out (Air-Off) Phase - The air supply to **both** fermenters is suddenly cut off simultaneously. - Agitation is typically maintained (to ensure perfect mixing). - In the **test fermenter**, the DO drops **linearly** because the only oxygen sink is microbial respiration. - In the **reference fermenter**, the DO stays essentially constant (only probe drift is recorded). - The slope of the differential DO curve directly yields the OUR. ### Phase III — Re-aeration (Air-On) Phase - Air is restored to both fermenters. - The DO in the test fermenter rises back toward $C^*$, following first-order kinetics. - The shape of this re-aeration curve is used to determine $K_La$. ## 2.2 Governing Mass Balance Equations The general oxygen mass balance for a well-mixed fermenter is: $$ \frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - Q_{O_2} \cdot X $$ where: - $\frac{dC_L}{dt}$ = rate of change of dissolved oxygen (mg/L/h) - $K_La$ = volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹) - $C^*$ = saturation DO concentration (mg/L) - $C_L$ = instantaneous DO concentration (mg/L) - $Q_{O_2}$ = specific oxygen uptake rate (mg $O_2$ / g cells · h) - $X$ = biomass concentration (g/L) ## 2.3 Simplified Equations for Each Phase | Phase | Assumption | Simplified Equation | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Phase II (Air-Off) | No transfer ($K_La(C^* - C_L) \approx 0$); reference has no respiration | $\frac{dC_L}{dt} = - Q_{O_2} \cdot X = -OUR$ | **Calculate OUR from slope** | | Phase III (Air-On) | OUR remains constant during short re-aeration | $\frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - OUR$ | **Calculate $K_La$ from re-aeration curve** | | Differential reading | Drift is identical in both vessels | $\frac{d(C_{L,\text{test}} - C_{L,\text{ref}})}{dt} = -OUR_{\text{true}}$ | **Compensate for drift** | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Differential"?** The *differential* design eliminates two common experimental headaches: **(i)** slow probe response, and **(ii)** small non-biological oxygen consumption (e.g., oxidation of media components like $Na_2S$). Without the reference vessel, these artefacts would be falsely attributed to microbial respiration. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorize This Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The symbols below are presented using $\vert$ instead of the vertical pipe character to preserve the markdown table grid. | Equation Number | Formula | Use / Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **(1) General O₂ balance** | $\dfrac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La(C^* - C_L) - Q_{O_2} \cdot X$ | Master balance used in all three phases | | **(2) OUR (air-off slope)** | $OUR = -\dfrac{\Delta C_L}{\Delta t} = - \text{slope of } C_L \text{ vs } t$ | Determined from Phase II | | **(3) Re-aeration linear form** | $\ln(C^* - C_L) = \ln(C^* - C_{L,0}) - K_La \cdot t$ | Obtained by integrating (1) with constant OUR | | **(4) $K_La$ from slope** | $K_La = - \text{slope of } \ln(C^* - C_L) \text{ vs } t$ | Determined from Phase III plot | | **(5) Differential OUR** | $OUR = -\dfrac{d(\Delta C_L)}{dt}$ where $\Delta C_L = C_{L,\text{test}} - C_{L,\text{ref}}$ | The key differential method expression | | **(6) OTR at steady state** | $OTR = K_La (C^* - C_L) = Q_{O_2} \cdot X = OUR$ | True when $dC_L / dt = 0$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility In industrial fermentation (e.g., **recombinant protein production in *Pichia pastoris***, **antibiotic production by *Streptomyces***, or **single-cell protein cultivation**), the Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method allows engineers to: 1. **Quantify the true respiratory load** on the culture, independent of probe-specific drift that can last for hours after steam sterilization. 2. **Validate scale-up calculations** by comparing bench-scale $K_La$ values with pilot-plant data. 3. **Monitor metabolic shifts** — a sudden rise in OUR may signal the depletion of a repressing carbon source and the onset of secondary metabolism (crucial in antibiotic fermentations). 4. **Calibrate online OUR soft-sensors** used in modern **PAT (Process Analytical Technology)** frameworks mandated by regulatory agencies. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — OUR from the Air-Off (Gassing-Out) Phase **Starting Point:** The general oxygen mass balance: $$ \frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - Q_{O_2} \cdot X $$ **Step 1 — Apply the Air-Off Condition.** When air supply is cut off, the transfer term $K_La (C^* - C_L)$ effectively becomes zero because the gas-liquid interface is no longer replenished at the sparger. (Note: Strictly, surface aeration may still contribute, but it is usually small and is **mathematically cancelled** in the differential method.) $$ \frac{dC_L}{dt} = - Q_{O_2} \cdot X = -OUR $$ **Step 2 — Recognize that OUR is Approximately Constant over a Short Interval.** If the air-off period is short (typically **30 s to 2 min**), the biomass $X$ and the specific uptake rate $Q_{O_2}$ are essentially constant. Therefore, $OUR$ is treated as a constant during this phase. **Step 3 — Integrate the Equation.** Integrating both sides over a finite time interval $[t_1, t_2]$: $$ \int_{C_{L,1}}^{C_{L,2}} dC_L = -OUR \int_{t_1}^{t_2} dt $$ $$ C_{L,2} - C_{L,1} = -OUR (t_2 - t_1) $$ **Step 4 — Solve for OUR.** $$ \boxed{ OUR = - \frac{C_{L,2} - C_{L,1}}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{C_{L,1} - C_{L,2}}{t_2 - t_1} } $$ **Step 5 — Apply the Differential Compensation.** If the reference fermenter shows a tiny drift $\Delta C_{L,\text{ref}}$ over the same interval (e.g., 0.05 mg/L drop in 60 s), the true biological OUR becomes: $$ \boxed{ OUR_{\text{true}} = - \frac{(C_{L,\text{test},2} - C_{L,\text{test},1}) - (C_{L,\text{ref},2} - C_{L,\text{ref},1})}{t_2 - t_1} } $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — $K_La$ from the Re-Aeration Phase **Starting Point:** When air is restored, the mass balance becomes: $$ \frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - OUR $$ **Step 1 — Rearrange the Equation.** $$ \frac{dC_L}{dt} + K_La C_L = K_La C^* - OUR $$ **Step 2 — Define the Driving Force.** Let $y(t) = C^* - C_L$. Then $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = -\dfrac{dC_L}{dt}$. Substituting: $$ -\frac{dy}{dt} = K_La \cdot y - OUR $$ $$ \frac{dy}{dt} = -K_La \cdot y + OUR $$ **Step 3 — Solve the Linear First-Order ODE.** The integrating factor is $e^{K_La t}$. Multiplying through: $$ \frac{d}{dt}\left( y \cdot e^{K_La t} \right) = OUR \cdot e^{K_La t} $$ Integrating from $t = 0$ (the moment air is restored) to time $t$: $$ y(t) \cdot e^{K_La t} - y(0) = \frac{OUR}{K_La}\left( e^{K_La t} - 1 \right) $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $y(t)$.** $$ y(t) = y(0) e^{-K_La t} + \frac{OUR}{K_La}\left( 1 - e^{-K_La t} \right) $$ **Step 5 — Identify the Steady-State Offset.** As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-K_La t} \to 0$, so the steady-state driving force is: $$ y_{\infty} = C^* - C_{L,\text{ss}} = \frac{OUR}{K_La} $$ This is the famous **steady-state mass balance** that KTU loves to ask in Part A. **Step 6 — Rearrange to a Linear Form.** Substituting $y_{\infty} = OUR / K_La$ into the expression for $y(t)$ and rearranging: $$ y(t) - y_{\infty} = \left( y(0) - y_{\infty} \right) e^{-K_La t} $$ Taking the natural logarithm of both sides: $$ \boxed{ \ln\left( y(t) - y_{\infty} \right) = \ln\left( y(0) - y_{\infty} \right) - K_La \cdot t } $$ **Step 7 — Practical Evaluation.** Plot $\ln\left( C^* - C_L - \dfrac{OUR}{K_La} \right)$ versus $t$ on semi-log paper. The slope of the resulting straight line equals $-K_La$. > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Practical Approach:** Many KTU problems use a simpler approximation. If $OUR$ is small relative to the transfer rate (e.g., at the start of fermentation with low biomass), the term $OUR$ is ignored, and the linearization reduces to: > $$\ln(C^* - C_L) = \ln(C^* - C_{L,0}) - K_La t$$ > Plotting $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ versus $t$ directly gives slope $= -K_La$. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Given:** A differential gassing-out experiment yields the following data for a yeast culture at 30 °C. $C^* = 7.0$ mg/L. During the re-aeration phase, the following readings were obtained (our value of $OUR$ from the air-off phase was **22.0 mg/L/h**): | Time $t$ (s) | $C_L$ (mg/L) | |---|---| | 0 | 1.5 | | 15 | 3.4 | | 30 | 4.7 | | 45 | 5.5 | | 60 | 6.0 | | 75 | 6.3 | | 90 | 6.5 | **Find:** (a) $OUR$ from the air-off phase data, and (b) $K_La$ from the re-aeration data. ### Part (a) — Calculation of OUR From the air-off phase, suppose $C_L$ dropped from **6.8 mg/L to 5.0 mg/L in 295 s** in the test fermenter, while the reference dropped from **6.8 mg/L to 6.7 mg/L** in the same interval. $$ OUR = - \frac{(5.0 - 6.8) - (6.7 - 6.8)}{295 \, \text{s}} $$ $$ OUR = - \frac{(-1.8) - (-0.1)}{295} $$ $$ OUR = - \frac{-1.7 \, \text{mg/L}}{295 \, \text{s}} = 0.00576 \, \text{mg/L/s} $$ Converting to mg/L/h: $$ \boxed{ OUR = 0.00576 \times 3600 = 20.75 \, \text{mg/L/h} \approx 20.8 \, \text{mg/L/h} } $$ ### Part (b) — Calculation of $K_La$ **Step 1 — Compute the steady-state driving force $y_{\infty}$.** $$ y_{\infty} = \frac{OUR}{K_La} \quad \text{(this is unknown — we will use iteration)} $$ **Step 2 — First Approximation (ignoring OUR, as is common in KTU problems).** Compute $y(t) = C^* - C_L$ for each $t$: | $t$ (s) | $C_L$ | $y = 7.0 - C_L$ | $\ln(y)$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 1.5 | 5.5 | 1.705 | | 15 | 3.4 | 3.6 | 1.281 | | 30 | 4.7 | 2.3 | 0.833 | | 45 | 5.5 | 1.5 | 0.405 | | 60 | 6.0 | 1.0 | 0.000 | | 75 | 6.3 | 0.7 | -0.357 | | 90 | 6.5 | 0.5 | -0.693 | **Step 3 — Linear Regression (KTU students may use the graphical method).** The slope of $\ln(y)$ versus $t$ is approximately: $$ \text{slope} = \frac{-0.693 - 1.705}{90 - 0} = \frac{-2.398}{90} = -0.0266 \, \text{s}^{-1} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to $K_La$.** $$ \boxed{ K_La = 0.0266 \, \text{s}^{-1} \approx 95.6 \, \text{h}^{-1} } $$ This is a typical $K_La$ value for a well-agitated, well-sparged 5–10 L bench-top fermenter. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **units** explicitly in your final answer. Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing "$K_La = 0.0266$" without the units, and another mark by writing "per second" instead of the correct symbol "$\text{s}^{-1}$". # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Two-Fermenter Differential Setup The following block-level functional architecture maps the interactions between the test fermenter, reference fermenter, and the differential signal processing chain. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TEST["TEST FERMENTER - LIVE CELLS"] T1[Air Sparger] T2[Agitator Impeller] T3[Microbial Culture] T4[DO Probe 1] end subgraph REF["REFERENCE FERMENTER - NO CELLS"] R1[Air Sparger] R2[Agitator Impeller] R3[Sterile Medium Only] R4[DO Probe 2] end subgraph CTRL["CONTROL AND ACQUISITION"] V1[Three-Way Solenoid Valve] S1[Signal Conditioner 1] S2[Signal Conditioner 2] DA[Differential Amplifier] REC[Digital Recorder or DAQ] end A[Compressed Air Supply] --> V1 V1 -->|On Phase| T1 V1 -->|On Phase| R1 V1 -->|Off Phase| X1[Atmosphere Vented] T3 -->|Biological O2 Consumption| T4 R3 -->|No Consumption| R4 T4 --> S1 R4 --> S2 S1 --> DA S2 --> DA DA --> REC REC -->|Plot C_L vs t| P1[Phase II Linear Slope yields OUR] REC -->|Plot ln C minus C_L vs t| P2[Phase III Slope yields K_La] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Timeline — The Three Phases of the Experiment This timeline shows the three operational phases and the expected DO trajectory in each fermenter. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PHASE 1 - STEADY SATURATION
Air ON, DO equals C*
Duration: 5 to 10 minutes"] --> B["PHASE 2 - GASSING OUT
Air OFF sudden
Test DO drops linearly
Reference DO drifts only slightly
Duration: 30 s to 2 min"] B --> C["PHASE 3 - RE-AERATION
Air ON restored
Test DO rises toward C*
First order kinetics
Duration: 3 to 5 min"] C --> D["DATA ANALYSIS
Phase 2 slope gives OUR
Phase 3 semi-log slope gives K_La"] ``` ## 4.3 Equipment & Instrumentation List | Item | Function | Typical Specification | |---|---|---| | **Galvanic / Polarographic DO Probe** | Measures $C_L$ in real time | Response time < 30 s | | **Differential Amplifier** | Subtracts reference from test signal | Bandwidth > 0.1 Hz | | **Three-Way Solenoid Valve** | Synchronously switches air supply | Response time < 1 s | | **Data Acquisition System (DAQ)** | Records both signals vs. time | Sampling rate ≥ 1 Hz | | **Reference Fermenter** | Provides baseline | Identical geometry and medium | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a Three-Way Valve Instead of Two Separate Valves?** > A single three-way solenoid valve ensures that **both fermenters are switched simultaneously**. Any delay between the two air-off events would introduce an error in the differential signal. KTU students drawing the schematic should explicitly label the air lines as going through a *common* switching device. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method for the measurement of Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR) in a fermenter. State **one** major advantage of the "differential" configuration over the simple dynamic method.** **Model Answer:** The Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method is an experimental technique in which two geometrically identical fermenters — one containing the live, respiring microbial culture (test fermenter) and the other containing an identical medium but without live cells (reference fermenter) — are simultaneously subjected to a sudden interruption (gassing-out) and subsequent re-establishment of the air supply. The dissolved oxygen (DO) signals from both fermenters are continuously recorded, and a differential signal is computed by subtracting the reference from the test reading. During the air-off phase, the slope of the differential DO curve directly yields the oxygen uptake rate (OUR) of the microorganisms. **Advantage:** The differential configuration mathematically compensates for instrumental drift of the DO probe and for any non-biological oxygen consumption (e.g., slow oxidation of media components), yielding a more accurate OUR than the single-vessel dynamic method. (Any one valid advantage is acceptable.) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Write the general oxygen mass balance equation for a well-mixed fermenter and clearly identify the term that represents the Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) and the term that represents the Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR).** **Model Answer:** The general oxygen mass balance for a well-mixed aerobic fermenter is: $$ \frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La(C^* - C_L) - Q_{O_2} \cdot X $$ where: - $\dfrac{dC_L}{dt}$ is the rate of accumulation of dissolved oxygen in the broth (mg/L/h). - $K_La(C^* - C_L)$ is the **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR)** from the gas phase to the liquid phase, expressed in mg/L/h. - $Q_{O_2} \cdot X$ is the **Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)**, i.e., the rate at which the microorganisms consume dissolved oxygen, also in mg/L/h. - $K_La$ is the volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹), $C^*$ is the saturation DO concentration (mg/L), $C_L$ is the instantaneous DO concentration (mg/L), $Q_{O_2}$ is the specific oxygen uptake rate (mg $O_2$ / g cells · h), and $X$ is the biomass concentration (g/L). At steady state, $dC_L/dt = 0$, hence $K_La(C^* - C_L) = Q_{O_2} \cdot X$, i.e., OTR = OUR. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain, with the help of a neat schematic diagram, the **Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method** for the determination of the **Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient ($K_La$)** in a fermenter. Describe the three distinct phases of the experiment. (7 marks) **(b)** Starting from the general oxygen mass balance equation, **derive the expression** for $K_La$ using the data from the re-aeration phase of the experiment. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Schematic (3 marks):** Draw a labelled diagram showing: - A **test fermenter** with live microbial culture, fitted with a DO probe. - A **reference fermenter** with sterile medium (no cells), fitted with an identical DO probe. - A **common air supply line** passing through a three-way solenoid valve. - A **differential amplifier** receiving signals from both probes. - A **recorder / computer** for data logging. **Three Phases (4 marks):** 1. **Phase I — Steady Saturation:** With air sparging continuously, both fermenters reach saturation. $C_L = C^*$ in both. Duration ~ 5–10 min. Used to establish the $C^*$ baseline. 2. **Phase II — Gassing Out (Air-Off):** The air supply is suddenly cut off. The DO in the test fermenter drops linearly with time due to microbial respiration. The reference fermenter DO drifts only marginally (probe response, slow equilibration). The differential slope yields **OUR**: $$ OUR = -\frac{d(\Delta C_L)}{dt} $$ 3. **Phase III — Re-aeration (Air-On):** Air is restored. The DO in the test fermenter rises toward $C^*$ following first-order kinetics. A semi-log plot of $(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ yields a straight line with slope $-K_La$. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Write the general mass balance** (1 mark): $$ \frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La(C^* - C_L) - Q_{O_2} X = K_La(C^* - C_L) - OUR $$ **Step 2 — State the assumptions** (2 marks): - The fermenter is perfectly mixed (lumped parameter model). - $C^*$ is constant during the short re-aeration phase. - OUR remains constant during the re-aeration phase (true if $X$ and $Q_{O_2}$ do not change appreciably over the few minutes of measurement). - Surface aeration effects are negligible. **Step 3 — Define $y(t) = C^* - C_L$** (1 mark): $$ \frac{dy}{dt} = -K_La \cdot y + OUR $$ **Step 4 — Solve the first-order linear ODE** (2 marks). Using the integrating factor $e^{K_La t}$: $$ y(t) = y(0)\, e^{-K_La t} + \frac{OUR}{K_La}\left(1 - e^{-K_La t}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Linearize** (1 mark): $$ \ln\left(y(t) - y_{\infty}\right) = \ln\left(y(0) - y_{\infty}\right) - K_La t, \quad \text{where } y_{\infty} = \frac{OUR}{K_La} $$ The slope of $\ln(y - y_{\infty})$ vs. $t$ is $-K_La$. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice) **(a)** With the help of a **labelled diagram and DO vs. time curve**, explain how the Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR) of a microbial culture is determined by the Dynamic Differential Gassing Out Method. Clearly indicate the slope used and the equation employed. (7 marks) **(b)** A fermenter running a yeast culture yields the following re-aeration data after a dynamic gassing-out experiment: $C^* = 7.5$ mg/L; the OUR was separately measured to be **18 mg/L/h**. The DO values recorded are: $t = 0$ s, $C_L = 1.0$ mg/L; $t = 30$ s, $C_L = 2.8$ mg/L; $t = 60$ s, $C_L = 4.0$ mg/L; $t = 90$ s, $C_L = 4.9$ mg/L; $t = 120$ s, $C_L = 5.5$ mg/L. Estimate $K_La$ using the simplified linearised form (assume OUR correction is small in the first iteration). (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Diagram (3 marks):** Same as Question A — two fermenters, common three-way valve, differential amplifier, recorder. Show the DO vs. time curve with three distinct regions: a flat plateau at $C^*$ (Phase I), a steep linear descent (Phase II), and an exponential rise back to $C^*$ (Phase III). 2. **Calculation of OUR (3 marks):** From Phase II, $$ OUR = -\frac{\Delta C_L}{\Delta t} $$ For the differential method, the slope of the **difference signal** ($C_{L,\text{test}} - C_{L,\text{ref}}$) versus $t$ is computed, and the absolute value gives the OUR. 3. **Units and significance (1 mark):** State units (mg/L/h) and mention that this is the *in-situ* OUR, valid for the current operating conditions. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Compute $y(t) = C^* - C_L$ for each $t$** (2 marks): | $t$ (s) | $C_L$ (mg/L) | $y = 7.5 - C_L$ (mg/L) | $\ln(y)$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 1.0 | 6.5 | 1.872 | | 30 | 2.8 | 4.7 | 1.548 | | 60 | 4.0 | 3.5 | 1.253 | | 90 | 4.9 | 2.6 | 0.956 | | 120 | 5.5 | 2.0 | 0.693 | **Step 2 — Compute the slope of $\ln(y)$ vs. $t$** (3 marks): Using the first and last points (graphical method acceptable): $$ \text{slope} = \frac{0.693 - 1.872}{120 - 0} = \frac{-1.179}{120} = -0.00983 \, \text{s}^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Estimate $K_La$** (1 mark): $$ \boxed{ K_La = 0.00983 \, \text{s}^{-1} \approx 35.4 \, \text{h}^{-1} } $$ **Step 4 — Optional correction for OUR** (1 mark): The steady-state offset is $y_{\infty} = OUR / K_La = (18/3600) / 0.00983 \approx 0.51$ mg/L. A corrected plot of $\ln(y - 0.51)$ vs. $t$ would refine the estimate slightly, but the simplified form is sufficient for 7-mark KTU problems. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign:** Many students write $K_La = \text{slope}$ instead of $K_La = -\text{slope}$. This is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**. > 2. **Mixing units:** Computing the slope from $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ in seconds but then reporting $K_La$ in h⁻¹ without conversion (or vice-versa). Always do the **unit conversion as the final step**, and write it on the answer sheet. > 3. **Skipping the schematic in Part B:** Even if the question asks only for derivation, KTU examiners often award **1–2 marks** for a labelled diagram. Always include it. > 4. **Ignoring the reference fermenter:** If a student writes $OUR = -\Delta C_L / \Delta t$ using only the test fermenter, they lose 1 mark for not acknowledging the differential correction. > 5. **Confusing OTR and OUR:** A very common error is to swap $K_La(C^* - C_L)$ (OTR) and $Q_{O_2} \cdot X$ (OUR).
