**Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient (KLa)** is a lumped, empirical parameter that quantifies the *overall rate capability* of a gas-liquid interface to transport a sparingly soluble gas (most commonly **oxygen, O₂**) from the gas phase into the liquid bulk of a bioprocess system. In the context of KTU Module 2 (Mass Transfer in Bioreactors), it is treated as a **constant for a given set of operating conditions** — hence the terminology *Constant KLa* — even though its absolute value is highly sensitive to hydrodynamic parameters such as impeller speed, sparger design, gas flow rate, fluid rheology, and geometric configuration.

Mathematically, KLa is defined as the product of the **true liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (kL)** and the **specific interfacial area (a)**:

$$K_{L}a \;=\; k_{L} \cdot a$$

Where:
- $k_L$ has units of **m·s⁻¹**
- $a$ has units of **m²·m⁻³** (interfacial area per unit liquid volume)
- $K_La$ therefore has units of **s⁻¹** (or **h⁻¹**), representing a **first-order rate constant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 2, KLa is treated as a **measurable, lumped engineering constant**, not as two independent variables. Students must NOT attempt to separate kL and a individually during experimental determination.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to **dissolve a sugar cube in a cup of water**:

- If you **stir slowly** with a spoon, sugar dissolves over a long time → **low KLa**.
- If you **stir rapidly** with a whisk, creating many small whirlpools and bubbles, sugar dissolves in seconds → **high KLa**.

In this analogy:
- The **sugar** = oxygen (O₂) being transferred.
- The **stirring** = agitation by the impeller.
- The **bubbles** introduced = the gas-liquid interfacial area.
- The **rate at which sugar disappears** = the **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR)**.

**KLa is essentially the "dissolving speed knob" of the bioreactor.**

## 1.3 Why KLa is Critical in Bioprocess Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Microbial cells consume oxygen at a rate governed by their **Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)**. If the OTR (governed by KLa) falls below the OUR, the culture becomes **oxygen-limited**, leading to:
> - Shift to mixed acid/ethanol fermentation (e.g., in *E. coli*).
> - Reduced biomass yield (YX/O drops).
> - Accumulation of toxic metabolites.
> - Possible culture washout in continuous systems.

Hence, KLa becomes a **design and scale-up cornerstone parameter** in aerobic fermentations.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Lab Bioreactor) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Mass Transfer Coeff. | $K_La$ | **100 – 1000** | h⁻¹ |
| Saturation DO concentration | $C^*$ | **6 – 8** | mg·L⁻¹ |
| Critical DO concentration | $C_{crit}$ | **0.5 – 2.0** | mg·L⁻¹ |
| Gas-liquid interfacial tension | $\sigma$ | **0.07** | N·m⁻¹ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of KLa: The Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923)

The theoretical foundation of KLa rests on the **Lewis-Whitman two-film model**, which conceptualizes the gas-liquid interface as two stagnant films — a gas film and a liquid film — through which the solute diffuses in steady state.

For a **sparingly soluble gas like O₂**, the gas-side resistance is negligible compared to the liquid-side resistance. Therefore, the **overall mass transfer coefficient** reduces to the **liquid-side coefficient kL**.

**Key logic steps:**

1. **Driving Force**: Mass transfer occurs due to a concentration gradient between the gas-liquid interface (equilibrium concentration $C^*$) and the bulk liquid (concentration $C_L$).
2. **Flux Across Interface**: Per Fick's First Law, the molar flux $N_A$ across the stagnant liquid film is proportional to $(C^* - C_L)$.
3. **Volumetric Rate**: Multiplying flux by the specific interfacial area $a$ gives the volumetric mass transfer rate.
4. **Lumping**: Since $k_L$ and $a$ cannot be measured independently under industrial conditions, they are combined into the single lumped parameter $K_La$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **non-negotiable formulae** for solving any KTU Module 2 numerical on KLa.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) | $\text{OTR} = K_La (C^* - C_L)$ | mg·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ | Steady-state transfer rate |
| 2 | Definition of KLa | $K_La = k_L \cdot a$ | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ | Lumped parameter |
| 3 | Dynamic DO Balance (Batch) | $\dfrac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La(C^* - C_L) - q_O \cdot X$ | mg·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ | Used in gassing-out method |
| 4 | Steady-state OTR = OUR | $K_La(C^* - C_L) = q_O \cdot X$ | mg·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ | Operating point equation |
| 5 | Enhancement by pressure | $C^* = \dfrac{y}{H} \cdot P$ | mol·L⁻¹ | Pure O₂ sparging |
| 6 | Van't Riet Correlation (coalescing) | $K_La = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5}$ | s⁻¹ | Non-Newtonian / coalescing |
| 7 | Van't Riet Correlation (non-coalescing) | $K_La = K' \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{0.7} \cdot v_s^{0.2}$ | s⁻¹ | Electrolyte-rich broths |
| 8 | Specific power input | $\dfrac{P_g}{V_L} = \dfrac{N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N_i^3 \cdot D_i^5}{V_L}$ | W·m⁻³ | Agitation effect |
| 9 | Superficial gas velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_{cs}}$ | m·s⁻¹ | Aeration effect |
| 10 | Integrated DO profile (no respiration) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C^* - C_{L,0}}{C^* - C_L}\right) = K_La \cdot t$ | dimensionless | Gassing-out analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Part A (3-mark) KTU questions**, equations (1), (2), and (4) appear most frequently. **Part B (14-mark)** derivations usually involve equation (3) and its integration (equation 10).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Scale-up Criterion**: Maintaining *constant KLa* between lab (50 L) and production (50,000 L) scales is a standard — though imperfect — scale-up strategy.
- **Reactor Sizing**: Determines the **maximum viable cell density** before oxygen limitation.
- **Process Control**: KLa is adjusted online via **agitation speed (N)** and **air flow rate (Q)** cascades in DCS systems.
- **Cost Optimization**: Aeration and agitation account for **30–40% of total energy consumption** in an aerobic fermentation plant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Fick's First Law of Diffusion applied across the liquid film.

**Step 1 — Flux across the liquid film:**

$$N_A = -D_{O_2,L} \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

Where $D_{O_2,L}$ is the diffusivity of O₂ in the liquid film.

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state assumption** (no accumulation in the film):

The concentration profile is linear, so the gradient is:

$$\frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{C_L - C^*}{\delta_L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Fick's Law:**

$$N_A = D_{O_2,L} \cdot \frac{C^* - C_L}{\delta_L} = k_L \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

Where the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient** is defined as:

$$k_L = \frac{D_{O_2,L}}{\delta_L}$$

**Step 4 — Convert flux to volumetric rate** by multiplying with the specific interfacial area $a$:

$$\text{OTR} = N_A \cdot a = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the lumped KLa:**

$$\boxed{\text{OTR} = K_{L}a \, (C^* - C_L)}$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The derivation chains *Fick's Law → Steady-state film → Flux definition → Volumetric lumping*. The OTR is **first-order** in the driving force $(C^* - C_L)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Integrated DO Profile (Gassing-Out Method)

This is the most important derivation for KTU numericals.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on dissolved oxygen in batch operation (no cells, no respiration):**

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La \, (C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dC_L}{C^* - C_L} = K_La \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from initial concentration $C_{L,0}$ at $t = 0$ to $C_L$ at time $t$:**

$$\int_{C_{L,0}}^{C_L} \frac{dC_L}{C^* - C_L} = \int_{0}^{t} K_La \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the left integral** using standard substitution $u = C^* - C_L$, $du = -dC_L$:

$$-\ln(C^* - C_L) \Big|_{C_{L,0}}^{C_L} = K_La \, t$$

**Step 5 — Apply limits:**

$$-\ln(C^* - C_L) + \ln(C^* - C_{L,0}) = K_La \, t$$

**Step 6 — Final integrated form:**

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{C^* - C_{L,0}}{C^* - C_L}\right) = K_{L}a \cdot t}$$

> **Interpretation:** A plot of $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C^* - C_{L,0}}{C^* - C_L}\right)$ versus $t$ yields a **straight line through the origin** with slope $= K_La$. This is the **classical static gassing-out / dynamic gassing-out experimental procedure**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State OTR Equals OUR

**Step 1 — In a steady-state chemostat or fed-batch, $dC_L/dt = 0$:**

$$0 = K_La \, (C^* - C_L) - q_O \cdot X$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to identify the operating point:**

$$\boxed{K_{L}a \, (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X}$$

> **Engineering Meaning:** The KLa must be large enough that the OTR satisfies the **Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR = $q_O X$)** of the culture at every instant, otherwise $C_L$ will collapse to zero.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: KLa Determination Methods

| Method | Principle | When $C_L$ Reaches | KLa Equation Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Gassing-Out** | N₂ strip → Air re-saturate, no cells | $C_L \rightarrow C^*$ | Eq. (10) above |
| **Dynamic Gassing-Out** | Active culture, air shut off, then re-applied | $C_L \rightarrow C_{crit}$ | Eq. (3) with $q_O X$ term |
| **Oxygen Balance Method** | Inlet vs. outlet O₂ in gas phase | Steady state | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{Q_g}{V_L}(y_{in} - y_{out})$ |
| **Sulfite Oxidation** | Chemical absorption by Na₂SO₃ + Cu²⁺ catalyst | — | Historical; rarely used today |
| **Moment Method (Linek)** | Impulse tracer in gas phase | — | Advanced research |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: KLa as the Central Mass Transfer Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Phase: Air / O2 Supply] --> B[Sparger Ring]
    B --> C[Bubble Swarm]
    C --> D[Gas-Liquid Interface]
    D --> E[Liquid Film Stagnant Layer]
    E --> F[Liquid Bulk Culture]
    F --> G[Microbial Cell]
    G --> H[Oxygen Uptake Rate OUR]

    subgraph Mass_Transfer_Zone
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph Resistance_Components
        R1[Gas Film Resistance 1/kg] --> R2[Liquid Film Resistance 1/kl]
        R2 --> R3[Bulk Liquid Mixing]
    end

    I[Constant KLa kl * a] --> D
    J[Operating Variables: N, Qg, sigma] --> I
    K[Geometric Variables: Di, Vs] --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The *lumped constant* KLa encapsulates both the **interfacial area (a)** (between bubble and liquid) and the **liquid-side coefficient (kL)** (governed by film thickness). External operating levers (impeller speed N, gas flow Qg) modulate KLa in real time.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: KLa Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Setpoint: Required OUR] --> CTRL[PI / PID Controller]
    CTRL --> ACT1[Agitator Speed Actuator]
    CTRL --> ACT2[Air Flow Valve Actuator]
    ACT1 --> BR[Bioreactor Vessel]
    ACT2 --> BR
    BR --> DO[Dissolved Oxygen Probe]
    DO --> TR[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
    TR --> CTRL
    BR --> GAS[Off-gas O2 Analyzer]
    GAS --> CALC[OUR Calculator]
    CALC --> CTRL
```

> **Engineering Insight:** A KLa-aware control strategy uses both the **DO probe** (fast, noisy) and the **off-gas O₂ balance** (slow, accurate) to compute OUR and adjust KLa in a **cascade configuration**.

## 4.3 Schematic: DO Profile During a Dynamic Gassing-Out Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: N2 Stripping] -->|CL falls to 0| P2[Stage 2: Air Re-applied]
    P2 -->|Exponential rise| P3[CL approaches C*]
    P3 --> P4[Linearized plot: ln C* minus CL over C* minus CL0 versus t]
    P4 --> P5[Slope equals KLa]
```

> **Block-Level Note:** This flowchart is the **gold-standard experiment** KTU examiners expect students to draw in 14-mark derivations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "volumetric mass transfer coefficient (KLa)" and state its units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> KLa is the product of the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (kL, m·s⁻¹) and the specific gas-liquid interfacial area (a, m²·m⁻³). It represents the **overall lumped capability** of a gas-liquid contactor to transfer a sparingly soluble gas such as oxygen into the liquid phase. Its units are **reciprocal time (s⁻¹ or h⁻¹)**, since it is a first-order rate constant with respect to the driving force $(C^* - C_L)$. **[3 marks: definition 2 + units 1]**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between kL and KLa. Why are they clubbed together in bioprocess engineering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **kL** is a *fundamental* property representing the local liquid-film conductance (m·s⁻¹), dependent on diffusivity and hydrodynamics of the film.
> - **KLa** is a *lumped engineering parameter* that includes both kL and the specific interfacial area *a* (m²·m⁻³).
> - They are clubbed because under industrial conditions, **kL and a cannot be measured independently**; only their product KLa is experimentally accessible through oxygen-balancing techniques. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **rate of oxygen transfer (OTR)** into the liquid medium in a fermenter using the two-film theory. Clearly state all assumptions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 10 m³ fermenter is sparged with air at 0.5 vvm. The dissolved oxygen concentration is observed to be **2.0 mg/L** at steady state, while the saturation concentration is **7.5 mg/L**. If the KLa is **250 h⁻¹**, calculate:
  1. The **oxygen transfer rate (OTR)** in mg·L⁻¹·h⁻¹.
  2. The **maximum cell density** that can be supported if $q_{O_2} = 0.15$ mg O₂ per mg cell per h.

**[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A (a) — OTR Derivation [7 marks]**

**Assumptions** *(1 mark)*:
1. Steady-state mass transfer.
2. Two stagnant films at the gas-liquid interface.
3. Gas-side resistance negligible (O₂ sparingly soluble).
4. Constant KLa during the transfer process.

**Derivation** *(5 marks)*:

Fick's First Law across the liquid film of thickness $\delta_L$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{O_2,L}}{\delta_L}(C^* - C_L) = k_L(C^* - C_L)$$

Multiplying by interfacial area per unit volume $a$:

$$\text{OTR} = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = K_La (C^* - C_L)$$

**Conclusion** *(1 mark)*: The OTR is first-order in driving force, with KLa as the lumped proportionality constant.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $V = 10 \text{ m}^3$ (not directly used in OTR calculation)
- $C^* = 7.5 \text{ mg/L}$
- $C_L = 2.0 \text{ mg/L}$
- $K_La = 250 \text{ h}^{-1}$
- $q_{O_2} = 0.15 \text{ mg O}_2 / (\text{mg cell} \cdot \text{h})$

**Part (i) — OTR calculation** *(3 marks)*:

$$\text{OTR} = K_La \cdot (C^* - C_L) = 250 \times (7.5 - 2.0)$$

$$\text{OTR} = 250 \times 5.5 = \mathbf{1375 \; mg \cdot L^{-1} \cdot h^{-1}}$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum cell density** *(4 marks)*:

At steady state, OTR = OUR:

$$\text{OTR} = q_{O_2} \cdot X_{max}$$

$$1375 = 0.15 \times X_{max}$$

$$X_{max} = \frac{1375}{0.15}$$

$$\boxed{X_{max} = 9166.67 \;\text{mg/L} \;\approx\; 9.17 \;\text{g/L}}$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **dynamic gassing-out method** for the experimental determination of KLa. Derive the integrated equation used in this method. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** In a non-coalescing fermentation broth, the following correlation is proposed: $K_La = K' (P_g/V_L)^{0.7} \cdot v_s^{0.2}$. A pilot-scale reactor with $P_g/V_L = 1500$ W·m⁻³ and $v_s = 0.005$ m·s⁻¹ gives $K_La = 380$ h⁻¹. Estimate the KLa in a production-scale reactor operating at $P_g/V_L = 800$ W·m⁻³ and $v_s = 0.003$ m·s⁻¹. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B (a) [7 marks]**

**Procedure** *(3 marks)*:
1. Operate the fermenter at the desired agitation/aeration conditions with a *living, respiring culture*.
2. Switch off the air supply and switch on **nitrogen** to strip DO until $C_L \to 0$ (gassing-out stage).
3. Re-introduce air; record the rising DO concentration $C_L$ as a function of time.
4. Plot $\ln(C^* - C_{L,0})/(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$; the slope = KLa.

**Derivation** *(4 marks)*:

Mass balance on DO (no cells → OUR = 0):

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La(C^* - C_L)$$

Separating and integrating (as in §3.2):

$$\ln\left(\frac{C^* - C_{L,0}}{C^* - C_L}\right) = K_La \cdot t$$

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Solve for the constant $K'$** using pilot data *(3 marks)*:

$$380 = K' \times (1500)^{0.7} \times (0.005)^{0.2}$$

Compute each term:

$$1500^{0.7} \approx 165.96$$

$$0.005^{0.2} \approx 0.3469$$

$$K' = \frac{380}{165.96 \times 0.3469} = \frac{380}{57.57} \approx 6.60$$

**Step 2 — Compute production KLa** *(3 marks)*:

$$K_La^{prod} = 6.60 \times (800)^{0.7} \times (0.003)^{0.2}$$

$$800^{0.7} \approx 102.34$$

$$0.003^{0.2} \approx 0.3133$$

$$K_La^{prod} = 6.60 \times 102.34 \times 0.3133$$

$$\boxed{K_La^{prod} \approx 211.6 \;\text{h}^{-1}}$$

**Conclusion** *(1 mark)*: A lower KLa is predicted in the production reactor, indicating the **scale-up penalty** that must be compensated by additional aeration or agitation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing s⁻¹ and h⁻¹ in KLa numericals → **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting driving-force sign:** OTR requires $(C^* - C_L)$, **not** $(C_L - C^*)$; sign error → **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Assuming C_L → 0 prematurely:** In dynamic gassing-out, $C_L$ approaches but does not strictly reach zero; proper use of $C_{L,0}$ in the log term is mandatory.
> 4. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** Examiners allocate **1 mark specifically** for stating assumptions of the two-film model.
> 5. **Confusing KLa (h⁻¹) with kL (m·s⁻¹):** They are *not interchangeable*; OTR uses **KLa**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Constant KLa**

- **Definition:** $K_La = k_L \cdot a$ (lumped coefficient, units **s⁻¹ or h⁻¹**).
- **Governing Equation:** $\text{OTR} = K_La (C^* - C_L)$.
- **Steady-State Operating Equation:** $K_La (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X$.
- **Two-Film Theory:** O₂ is sparingly soluble → **gas-side resistance negligible** → liquid-film controls.
- **Driving Force:** Always $(C^* - C_L)$ — equilibrium minus bulk.
- **Experimental Determination:**
  - *Static gassing-out:* no cells, integrated form gives slope = KLa.
  - *Dynamic gassing-out:* with cells, requires simultaneous OUR measurement.
  - *Oxygen balance (gas phase):* OTR = $(Q_g/V_L)(y_{in} - y_{out})$.
- **Van't Riet Correlations:**
  - Coalescing broths: exponent on $P_g/V_L$ = **0.4**.
  - Non-coalescing broths (electrolyte-rich): exponent = **0.7**.
- **Scale-up Strategy:** Maintain *constant $K_La$* (or constant $P_g/V_L$ and $v_s$) between scales.
- **KLa Dependence:** Increases with **agitation (N)** ∝ $N^{2.4}$, with **aeration (vvm)** ∝ $v_s^{0.5}$, decreases with **broth viscosity (μ)**.
- **Critical DO:** $C_L$ must be kept above $C_{crit}$ (typically 10–30% of $C^*$) to avoid oxygen limitation.
- **KLa ≠ kL:** KTU students frequently lose marks by treating them as identical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Mixing Quality, Constant Impeller Tip Speed
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constant Mixing Quality, Constant Impeller Tip Speed

# 🎯 Constant Mixing Quality & Constant Impeller Tip Speed — Scale-Up Criteria

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Scale-Up" in Bioprocess Engineering?

Scale-up is the **engineering art and science of reproducing a process** that works successfully at a small laboratory scale (typically **1–10 L**) into a large commercial production scale (typically **10,000–1,00,000 L**), while preserving **product yield, quality, and productivity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 Focus):**
> *Scale-up* is the procedure of applying the same process conditions as in the laboratory, to larger volumes of culture broth, ensuring identical or comparable **transport phenomena** — i.e., **heat transfer, mass transfer (oxygen), and momentum transfer (mixing)**.

---

### 1.2 Constant Mixing Quality — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Mixing Quality (CMQ)** is a scale-up criterion that aims to **preserve the homogeneity of the broth** at all scales by keeping the **degree of mixing / mixing time** comparable between the laboratory and production bioreactors.

In quantitative terms, it means the **mixing time ($t_m$)**, i.e., the time required to achieve a defined level of homogeneity (typically **95% or 99% homogeneity**), is held **constant** across scales.

$$t_{m,\text{lab}} = t_{m,\text{industrial}}$$

Mixing time is governed by the following dimensionless correlation (typical Rushton-type):

$$\frac{t_m \cdot N}{1} = f\!\left(\text{Re},\, \text{geometry}\right)$$

where $N$ is impeller rotational speed (rev/s) and Re is the impeller Reynolds number.

---

### 1.3 Constant Impeller Tip Speed — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Impeller Tip Speed** (also called *constant tip velocity* or *constant peripheral velocity*) is a scale-up criterion that keeps the **linear velocity at the tip of the impeller blade** identical across scales.

The mathematical statement is:

$$V_{\text{tip}} = \pi N D = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_1 D_1 = N_2 D_2$$

where $N$ = impeller speed (rev/s) and $D$ = impeller diameter (m).

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍲

**Imagine making a cup of tea (small vessel) vs. a giant industrial tea kettle (huge vessel).**

- In your cup, stirring with a spoon for **5 seconds** blends sugar evenly.
- In the big kettle, if you stir with the *same spoon* for 5 seconds, the sugar at the bottom will NOT dissolve properly because the spoon's edge only sweeps a tiny zone.
- To make the kettle work *as well as* the cup, you either:
  - 🌀 **Stir faster** (more vigorous mixing → **constant mixing time**), or
  - 🌀 **Use a bigger spoon / stirrer moving at the same edge speed** (so the fluid near the tip experiences the same shearing → **constant tip speed**).

These are exactly the two competing design philosophies of scale-up.

| Criterion | Real-World Analogy | Bioreactor Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Constant Mixing Quality | Stir long enough so sugar is fully dissolved | Equal homogeneity |
| Constant Tip Speed | Spoon-edge moves at same speed | Equal shear at impeller blade |

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Homogeneity threshold:** typically **95%** or **99%** for bioprocess.
- **Standard geometry ratio:** $D/T = 0.33$ (impeller-to-tank diameter, Rushton turbine).
- **Turbulent Re threshold:** $Re \geq 10^4$.
- **Newtonian fluid reference viscosity:** water at 30 °C ≈ **0.8 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two criteria are **rarely both simultaneously satisfied** during scale-up. They often produce **conflicting impeller speeds**, which is why choosing the correct scale-up criterion is a critical engineering decision in bioprocess design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scale-Up Problem — Step-by-Step Logic

When we increase volume, the following geometric ratios **remain constant only if geometric similarity is preserved**:

$$\frac{D_1}{T_1} = \frac{D_2}{T_2},\quad \frac{W}{D} = \text{const},\quad \frac{C}{T} = \text{const}$$

where:
- $D$ = impeller diameter
- $T$ = tank diameter
- $W$ = impeller blade width
- $C$ = clearance from tank bottom to impeller

The variables that **change with scale** and must be controlled are:
1. Impeller speed ($N$)
2. Aeration rate / superficial gas velocity ($v_s$)
3. Power input per volume ($P/V$)
4. Mixing time ($t_m$)
5. Shear rate / tip speed ($V_{\text{tip}}$)

---

## 2.2 Constant Mixing Quality — Detailed Mechanism

### Why is mixing time so important in bioprocesses?

1. **pH control:** Addition of acid/base must reach all cells within seconds.
2. **Dissolved oxygen uniformity:** Cells far from the sparger must still see DO ≥ critical value.
3. **Substrate addition:** Fed-batch glucose pulses must be distributed rapidly to avoid local osmotic stress.
4. **Heat removal:** Local hotspots denature enzymes / harm cells.

### Correlation for Mixing Time (Norwood & Metzner, modified by Kramers)

For a **single Rushton impeller** in a baffled tank, the most widely used correlation is:

$$\frac{t_m \cdot N}{Re^{-1}} = \text{constant}$$

Or, more explicitly, by dimensional analysis:

$$\frac{t_m \cdot N}{(D/T)^{-2}} = f(\text{Re})$$

where the **impeller Reynolds number** is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho N D^2}{\mu}$$

### Constant Mixing Quality Scale-Up Rule

To maintain the **same mixing time** at the production scale:

$$\frac{N_{\text{lab}} \cdot D_{\text{lab}}^2}{Re_{\text{lab}}} \cdot f(Re_{\text{lab}}) = \frac{N_{\text{prod}} \cdot D_{\text{prod}}^2}{Re_{\text{prod}}} \cdot f(Re_{\text{prod}})$$

In the **turbulent regime** ($Re > 10^4$), the function $f(Re)$ becomes approximately constant, which simplifies the rule to:

$$\boxed{t_m = \text{const} \;\Rightarrow\; N \cdot D^{n} = \text{const}, \quad n \approx 0}$$

In practice, for geometrically similar tanks:

$$\boxed{t_{m,1} \approx t_{m,2} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{N_1}{N_2} \approx 1}$$

That is, **impeller speed remains approximately the same**, but the **tip speed and $P/V$ will increase dramatically**.

---

## 2.3 Constant Impeller Tip Speed — Detailed Mechanism

### Definition of Tip Speed

The tip speed is the linear velocity of the impeller blade's outermost edge:

$$V_{\text{tip}} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D \quad \left[\text{m s}^{-1}\right]$$

### Physical Meaning

The tip speed directly governs:
- **Maximum shear rate** experienced by fluid elements passing near the blade tip
- **Cavitation tendency** (especially important for animal cell culture)
- **Maximum shear stress on cells** — *critical for shear-sensitive mammalian cells, plant cells, and filamentous fungi*

### Scale-Up Rule

For geometrically similar tanks:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{tip,1}} = V_{\text{tip,2}} \;\Rightarrow\; N_1 D_1 = N_2 D_2}$$

Therefore, when scaling up by a factor of $R$ in linear dimension:

$$N_2 = \frac{N_1}{R}$$

### Consequence on Power Number

The **Power number** (Newton number) for a Rushton turbine in turbulent flow is approximately constant (≈ **6** for single Rushton). Power is given by:

$$P = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

Substituting $N \propto 1/D$ under constant tip speed:

$$P_2 = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot \left(\frac{N_1}{R}\right)^3 \cdot (R D_1)^5 = R^2 \cdot P_1$$

Power per unit volume:

$$\left(\frac{P}{V}\right)_2 = \frac{R^2 P_1}{R^3 V_1} = \frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{P}{V}\right)_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Under constant tip speed, $P/V$ *decreases* with scale. This means **less aggressive mixing per unit volume** at industrial scale — useful for shear-sensitive cultures but may cause **oxygen limitation**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Scale-Up Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Impeller Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho N D^2}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | often held constant |
| 2 | Power number | $P_o = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}$ | dimensionless | constant in fully turbulent region |
| 3 | Power input | $P = P_o \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5$ | W (J/s) | scales as $R^5$ (constant $N$), $R^2$ (constant $V_{\text{tip}}$) |
| 4 | Power per volume | $P/V$ | W/m³ | $R^2$ (const $N$), $R^{-1}$ (const $V_{\text{tip}}$), $R^{0}$ (const $P/V$) |
| 5 | Impeller tip speed | $V_{\text{tip}} = \pi N D$ | m/s | **constant ⇒ $N \propto 1/D$** |
| 6 | Mixing time (turbulent) | $t_m \cdot N = \text{const} \approx 3$–$5$ | s | scales weakly with $D$ |
| 7 | Volumetric scale factor | $R = (V_2 / V_1)^{1/3}$ | dimensionless | geometric linear ratio |
| 8 | Superficial gas velocity | $v_s = Q_g / A_c$ | m/s | usually held constant |
| 9 | Shear rate (avg) | $\dot{\gamma} \approx k_s N$ | s⁻¹ | scales with $N$ |
| 10 | Kolmogorov eddy scale | $\eta = (\nu^3 / \varepsilon)^{1/4}$ | m | critical for cell damage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the linear-dimension scale factor $R = (V_2/V_1)^{1/3}$**. It is the single most common conversion in KTU scale-up problems.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antibiotic production (e.g., penicillin by *Penicillium chrysogenum*)**: Shear-sensitive → use **constant tip speed** or **constant $P/V$**.
- **Recombinant *E. coli* high-cell-density fermentation**: Less shear-sensitive → **constant $k_L a$** is the dominant criterion.
- **Animal cell culture (CHO, hybridoma)**: Extremely shear-sensitive → **constant tip speed**, often $V_{\text{tip}} < 1.5$ m/s.
- **Shear damage prediction**: Compare cell diameter $d_c$ with **Kolmogorov length scale** $\eta$; damage occurs when $\eta < d_c$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Similarity and Linear Scale Factor $R$

**Given:** Laboratory vessel volume $V_1 = 10$ L; Industrial vessel volume $V_2 = 10{,}000$ L. Tanks are **geometrically similar** ($D/T$, $W/D$, $C/T$ all constant).

**Step 1.** For geometrically similar vessels, all linear dimensions scale by the same factor $R$.

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} = R$$

**Step 2.** Volume scales as the cube of the linear factor.

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = R^3$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $R$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3} \\
  &= \left(\frac{10{,}000}{10}\right)^{1/3} \\
  &= (1000)^{1/3} \\
  &= 10
\end{aligned}$$

> So the impeller diameter, tank diameter, and blade width all increase by a factor of **10**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant Tip Speed: Impeller Speed at Industrial Scale

**Given:** Lab impeller diameter $D_1 = 0.05$ m running at $N_1 = 600$ rpm. Industrial impeller diameter $D_2 = 0.50$ m (consistent with $R = 10$).

**Step 1.** Convert $N_1$ to rev/s for dimensional consistency:

$$N_1 = \frac{600}{60} = 10\ \text{rev/s}$$

**Step 2.** Apply the constant-tip-speed scale-up rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi N_1 D_1 &= \pi N_2 D_2 \\
N_2 &= N_1 \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_2} \\
    &= 10 \cdot \frac{0.05}{0.50} \\
    &= 10 \cdot 0.1 \\
    &= 1\ \text{rev/s} \\
    &= 60\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Verify tip speed equality:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{tip},1} &= \pi \cdot 10 \cdot 0.05 = 1.5708\ \text{m/s} \\
V_{\text{tip},2} &= \pi \cdot 1 \cdot 0.50 = 1.5708\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

✅ Both equal to **1.57 m/s**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Number Comparison Under Constant Tip Speed

**Given:** Rushton turbine, $P_o = 6$ (constant in turbulent region), broth density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $D_1 = 0.05$ m, $N_1 = 10$ rev/s, $D_2 = 0.50$ m, $N_2 = 1$ rev/s.

**Step 1.** Power at lab scale.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N_1^3 \cdot D_1^5 \\
    &= 6 \cdot 1000 \cdot (10)^3 \cdot (0.05)^5 \\
    &= 6000 \cdot 3.125 \times 10^{-7} \\
    &= 1.875 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} \\
    &= 1.875\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Power at industrial scale.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N_2^3 \cdot D_2^5 \\
    &= 6 \cdot 1000 \cdot (1)^3 \cdot (0.50)^5 \\
    &= 6000 \cdot 0.03125 \\
    &= 187.5\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Verify ratio $P_2/P_1 = R^2 = 100$.

$$\frac{187.5}{1.875 \times 10^{-3}} = 100{,}000 \;\;\text{(note: units of } P_1 \text{ in mW gave mismatch)}$$

Let us recompute carefully in consistent SI units:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 6 \cdot 1000 \cdot 1000 \cdot (0.05)^5 \\
    &= 6{,}000{,}000 \cdot 3.125 \times 10^{-7} \\
    &= 1.875\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{187.5}{1.875} = 100 = R^2 = 10^2\ \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power per Volume ($P/V$) Under Constant Tip Speed

**Step 1.** Volume scales as $R^3$: $V_2 = R^3 V_1$.

**Step 2.** With $N \propto 1/D \propto 1/R$ and $D \propto R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_2}{P_1} &= \frac{N_2^3 D_2^5}{N_1^3 D_1^5} = \left(\frac{1}{R}\right)^3 \cdot R^5 = R^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Power per volume ratio:

$$\frac{(P/V)_2}{(P/V)_1} = \frac{P_2/V_2}{P_1/V_1} = \frac{R^2}{R^3} = \frac{1}{R}$$

So $(P/V)$ at industrial scale is **1/R of the lab value** — i.e., **decreases** as scale increases.

For $R = 10$:

$$(P/V)_2 = 0.1 \cdot (P/V)_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a frequent KTU exam trap. Students often assume $P/V$ stays the same or increases under constant tip speed. **It actually decreases.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Mixing Time Scaling

For a Rushton turbine in the turbulent regime, the well-established correlation is:

$$t_m \cdot N = A \cdot (D/T)^{-2.3}$$

where $A \approx 3$–$5$ (geometry-dependent constant).

**Step 1.** For geometric similarity, $D/T$ is constant → $A(D/T)^{-2.3}$ is constant = $K$.

$$t_m = \frac{K}{N}$$

**Step 2.** Under constant tip speed, $N_2 = N_1 / R$:

$$t_{m,2} = \frac{K}{N_2} = \frac{K \cdot R}{N_1} = R \cdot t_{m,1}$$

So mixing time **increases linearly with scale** by factor $R$.

**Step 3.** Implication:

$$\boxed{t_{m,2} = R \cdot t_{m,1}}$$

For $R = 10$: $t_{m,2} = 10 \cdot t_{m,1}$.

> A 10× longer mixing time may be unacceptable for pH-sensitive cultures — this is why **constant tip speed** is often unsuitable for highly mixed-needs fermentations.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Two Criteria

| Property | Constant Mixing Quality | Constant Impeller Tip Speed |
|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | $t_m = \text{const}$ | $\pi N D = \text{const}$ |
| Effect on $N$ | $N \approx \text{const}$ | $N \propto 1/D$ |
| Effect on $V_{\text{tip}}$ | $V_{\text{tip}} \propto D$ (increases) | constant |
| Effect on $P$ | $P \propto R^5$ (huge increase) | $P \propto R^2$ (modest increase) |
| Effect on $P/V$ | $P/V \propto R^2$ (increases sharply) | $P/V \propto R^{-1}$ (decreases) |
| Effect on $t_m$ | constant by definition | $t_m \propto R$ (worsens) |
| Cell shear risk | High at industrial scale | Low / controlled |
| Oxygen transfer risk | Low / overpowered | High (low $P/V$) |
| Typical application | Recombinant bacteria in defined media | Shear-sensitive (mammalian cells, plant cells) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scale-Up Decision Flowchart (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bioreactor Scale-Up Initiated: Lab to Industrial"] --> B{"Is the culture shear-sensitive?"}
    B -- "YES (mammalian, plant, filamentous fungi)" --> C["Apply Constant Tip Speed: N1·D1 = N2·D2"]
    B -- "NO (bacteria, robust yeast)" --> D{"Is oxygen transfer the limiting factor?"}
    D -- "YES" --> E["Apply Constant kLa criterion"]
    D -- "NO" --> F{"Is mixing homogeneity the priority?"}
    F -- "YES" --> G["Apply Constant Mixing Quality: tm = const, N ≈ const"]
    F -- "NO" --> H["Apply Constant P/V: P1/V1 = P2/V2"]

    C --> I["Result: N2 = N1/R, P/V decreases"]
    G --> J["Result: N unchanged, Vtip rises, P/V rises sharply"]
    E --> K["Result: Higher aeration + agitation"]
    H --> L["Result: Balanced power density"]

    I --> M["Verify no oxygen limitation"]
    J --> N["Verify no shear damage to cells"]
    K --> O["Verify no foam / stripping issues"]
    L --> P["Validate with pilot trials"]
```

## 4.2 Scale-Up Geometry and Impeller Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAB["Laboratory Scale: V1 = 10 L"]
        D1["Impeller Diameter D1 = 0.05 m"]
        T1["Tank Diameter T1 = 0.15 m"]
        N1["Impeller Speed N1 = 600 rpm"]
        V1["Tip Speed = 1.57 m/s"]
    end

    subgraph PROD["Industrial Scale: V2 = 10,000 L  R = 10"]
        D2["Impeller Diameter D2 = 0.50 m"]
        T2["Tank Diameter T2 = 1.50 m"]
        N2["Impeller Speed N2 = 60 rpm"]
        V2["Tip Speed = 1.57 m/s"]
    end

    D1 --> D2
    T1 --> T2
    N1 -- "Constant Tip Speed rule N2 = N1/R" --> N2
    V1 -- "Identical Vtip" --> V2
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Mixing Phenomena

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR["Power Input P from Motor"] --> IMP["Impeller Rotation N"]
    IMP --> BULK["Bulk Flow: Pumping Capacity Q"]
    IMP --> SHEAR["Shear Generation at Blade Tip"]
    BULK --> CIRC["Circulation Time tc = V/Q"]
    SHEAR --> DISS["Energy Dissipation Rate epsilon = P/rho·V"]
    BULK --> MIX["Mixing Time tm: 95% homogeneity"]
    DISS --> KOLM["Kolmogorov Length Scale eta = (nu³/eps)^1/4"]
    MIX --> QUAL["Mixing Quality Index"]
    KOLM --> CELL["Cell Damage Probability: eta less than dc = damage"]
    QUAL --> SC1["Scale-Up Criterion A: Constant tm"]
    CELL --> SC2["Scale-Up Criterion B: Constant Vtip"]

    SC1 --> OUT1["Drives Constant N"]
    SC2 --> OUT2["Drives N proportional 1/D"]
```

## 4.4 Tip Speed Versus Mixing Time — Engineering Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"What is your bioprocess constraint?"} -->|Heat or mass transfer limited| R1["P/V or kLa is the controlling criterion"]
    Q -->|Shear-limited culture| R2["Constant Tip Speed is the controlling criterion"]
    Q -->|Substrate gradient critical| R3["Constant Mixing Time is the controlling criterion"]
    Q -->|Foam control critical| R4["Constant Superficial Gas Velocity is the controlling criterion"]

    R1 --> DEC1["Accept higher power, design motor accordingly"]
    R2 --> DEC2["Accept longer tm, increase aeration if O2 deficient"]
    R3 --> DEC3["Accept higher Vtip, check shear tolerance of cells"]
    R4 --> DEC4["Accept higher mixing energy, control foam mechanically"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **constant impeller tip speed** as a scale-up criterion. State the governing equation and indicate the type of bioprocess for which it is most suited.

**Model Answer:**

> Constant impeller tip speed is a scale-up criterion that maintains identical **linear velocity at the outer edge of the impeller blade** across scales. It is governed by:
>
> $$\boxed{\pi N_1 D_1 = \pi N_2 D_2 \quad \text{or} \quad N_1 D_1 = N_2 D_2}$$
>
> where $N$ = impeller rotational speed (rev/s), $D$ = impeller diameter (m).
>
> It is most suited for **shear-sensitive cultures** such as **animal cell culture (CHO, hybridoma), plant cell culture, and filamentous fungi (e.g., *Streptomyces*)**, where mechanical stress from the impeller must be controlled to prevent cell damage.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for governing equation, 1 for application)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **three** scale-up criteria used in bioprocess engineering. State the equation governing each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Criterion | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Constant Power per Volume | $P_1/V_1 = P_2/V_2$ |
| 2 | Constant Impeller Tip Speed | $N_1 D_1 = N_2 D_2$ |
| 3 | Constant Mixing Time | $t_{m,1} = t_{m,2}$ |
| 4 *(bonus)* | Constant $k_L a$ | $k_{L}a_1 = k_{L}a_2$ |
| 5 *(bonus)* | Constant Impeller Reynolds Number | $N_1 D_1^2 = N_2 D_2^2$ |

*(3 marks: 0.5 per criterion + 0.5 per equation × 3 ≈ 3 marks)*

---

## 📝 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Instruction:** *Attempt either full Question A or full Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### ❓ Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** A laboratory bioreactor of working volume **50 L** uses a single Rushton turbine of diameter **$D_1 = 0.10$ m**, rotating at **$N_1 = 300$ rpm**. The broth is a Newtonian fermentation medium (density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, viscosity $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s). The vessel is to be scaled up to **5000 L** using **geometric similarity**.

**For constant tip speed scaling:**

1. Calculate the impeller diameter at industrial scale $D_2$.
2. Calculate the impeller speed $N_2$ in rpm.
3. Calculate the impeller Reynolds numbers at both scales and comment.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mixing time** correlation for a Rushton turbine in a baffled tank. Discuss why **constant mixing quality** and **constant tip speed** often produce **conflicting impeller speeds**, and recommend which one should be chosen for an **antibiotic fermentation using a shear-sensitive filamentous fungus**. Justify your recommendation.

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Compute the linear scale factor $R$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3} \\
  &= \left(\frac{5000}{50}\right)^{1/3} \\
  &= (100)^{1/3} \\
  &\approx 4.6416
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Industrial impeller diameter $D_2$.**

$$D_2 = R \cdot D_1 = 4.6416 \times 0.10 = 0.4642\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Convert $N_1$ to rev/s.**

$$N_1 = \frac{300}{60} = 5\ \text{rev/s}$$

**Step 4: Apply constant tip speed rule.**

$$N_1 D_1 = N_2 D_2 \;\Rightarrow\; N_2 = \frac{N_1 D_1}{D_2} = \frac{5 \times 0.10}{0.4642} = 1.077\ \text{rev/s}$$

Convert to rpm:

$$N_2 = 1.077 \times 60 \approx 64.6\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 5: Impeller Reynolds numbers.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_1 &= \frac{\rho N_1 D_1^2}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 5 \times (0.10)^2}{1 \times 10^{-3}} \\
     &= \frac{1000 \times 5 \times 0.01}{0.001} = 50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_2 &= \frac{\rho N_2 D_2^2}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.077 \times (0.4642)^2}{1 \times 10^{-3}} \\
     &= \frac{1000 \times 1.077 \times 0.2155}{0.001} \\
     &\approx 232{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** Both Reynolds numbers are well above $10^4$, so **fully turbulent flow** is maintained at both scales. Under constant tip speed, the impeller Reynolds number actually **increases** (since $Re \propto N D^2 \propto D$), confirming enhanced mixing intensity even at lower $N$ — but **bulk mixing time worsens**.

*(Marks: 1 for $R$, 1 for $D_2$, 1.5 for $N_2$, 1.5 each for $Re_1$ and $Re_2$ + comment = 7)*

---

#### Part (b) — Theory Answer

**Mixing time correlation for Rushton turbine:**

The mixing time in a baffled tank with a single Rushton impeller follows:

$$t_m \cdot N = K \left(\frac{T}{D}\right)^{m}$$

with $K \approx 3$–$5$ and $m \approx 2.3$ in the turbulent regime. For geometrically similar tanks ($T/D$ constant), $t_m \propto 1/N$.

**Why constant mixing quality and constant tip speed conflict:**

| Criterion | Effect on $N$ |
|---|---|
| Constant mixing quality | $N \approx \text{const}$ (independent of $D$) |
| Constant tip speed | $N \propto 1/D$ (decreases with scale) |

If both were imposed, the only solution is $D = \text{const}$ (no scale-up), which is impossible. So **exactly one** criterion must be chosen based on biological priority.

**Recommendation for antibiotic fermentation with shear-sensitive filamentous fungus:**

✅ **Choose constant tip speed.**

**Justification:**

1. **Cell protection:** Filamentous fungi (e.g., *Penicillium*) form hyphal aggregates that fragment under high shear. Constant $V_{\text{tip}}$ ensures the maximum shear rate at the impeller blade tip remains identical, preventing excessive fragmentation.
2. **Morphology preservation:** Fragmented hyphae reduce specific productivity of secondary metabolites like penicillin.
3. **Trade-off acknowledgment:** Mixing time will increase by factor $R$, but antibiotic fermentations are typically long (120–200 h), so a slightly longer mixing time is acceptable.
4. **Oxygen mitigation:** To compensate for reduced $P/V$, use **enhanced aeration** (higher $v_s$) or **enriched-air sparging**.

*(Marks: 2 for correlation, 2 for conflict explanation, 3 for recommendation + justification = 7)*

---

### ❓ Question B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **power per volume ($P/V$)** at lab scale and industrial scale when scaling up under **constant impeller tip speed**. Use the result to show what happens to $(P/V)$ when the linear scale factor $R = 10$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **10 L Rushton-impeller pilot bioreactor** ($D_1 = 0.07$ m, $N_1 = 500$ rpm) achieves a **mixing time** of $t_{m,1} = 6$ s in the turbulent regime. The process is scaled up geometrically to **10,000 L** under **constant tip speed**. Estimate the mixing time at industrial scale and discuss whether pH control will be problematic for a fed-batch antibiotic process requiring $\Delta t_{\text{response}} \leq 60$ s.

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $P/V$ under Constant Tip Speed

**Step 1.** Write the power equation for a Rushton turbine:

$$P = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

**Step 2.** Apply constant tip speed: $N D = \text{const} = K$.

$$\Rightarrow N = \frac{K}{D}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the power equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= P_o \cdot \rho \cdot \left(\frac{K}{D}\right)^3 \cdot D^5 \\
  &= P_o \cdot \rho \cdot K^3 \cdot D^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Take the ratio $P_2/P_1$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = R^2$$

**Step 5.** Volume ratio: $V_2/V_1 = R^3$.

**Step 6.** Power per volume ratio:

$$\frac{(P/V)_2}{(P/V)_1} = \frac{P_2/P_1}{V_2/V_1} = \frac{R^2}{R^3} = \frac{1}{R}$$

$$\boxed{(P/V)_2 = \frac{1}{R}(P/V)_1}$$

**For $R = 10$:**

$$(P/V)_2 = 0.1 \cdot (P/V)_1$$

So $(P/V)$ **decreases to one-tenth** of the lab value. This is the key engineering consequence.

*(Marks: 1 for power equation, 2 for substitution, 1.5 for ratio derivation, 1 for numerical application, 1.5 for conclusion = 7)*

---

#### Part (b) — Mixing Time and pH Control Assessment

**Step 1: Compute linear scale factor.**

$$R = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{10{,}000}{10}\right)^{1/3} = 10$$

**Step 2: Compute industrial impeller diameter and speed.**

$$D_2 = R \cdot D_1 = 10 \times 0.07 = 0.70\ \text{m}$$

$$N_2 = \frac{N_1}{R} = \frac{500}{10} = 50\ \text{rpm} = 0.833\ \text{rev/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute new tip speed (verify).**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{tip},1} &= \pi \times 8.333 \times 0.07 = 1.8326\ \text{m/s} \\
V_{\text{tip},2} &= \pi \times 0.833 \times 0.70 = 1.8326\ \text{m/s} \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute industrial mixing time.**

For a turbulent Rushton turbine, $t_m \cdot N = \text{const} \approx 4$ (typical $K$ value):

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{m,1} \cdot N_1 &= 6 \times 8.333 = 50 \\
t_{m,2} &= \frac{50}{N_2} = \frac{50}{0.833} \approx 60\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, using the simpler $t_m \propto 1/N$ for $N$ in rev/s with same geometry:

$$t_{m,2} = t_{m,1} \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2} = 6 \times 10 = 60\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5: pH control assessment.**

- Industrial mixing time: **$t_{m,2} = 60$ s**
- Required response time: **$\Delta t_{\text{response}} \leq 60$ s**
- **Marginal / borderline case.** Any heterogeneity (viscosity rise due to biomass, antifoam effect) can push $t_m$ above 60 s.
- **Recommendation:** Add **multiple Rushton impellers** (2 or 3 on a common shaft) to reduce mixing time by factor ≈ number of impellers, or add a **separate rapid-mix loop** for pH correction. Otherwise, pH control will become problematic.

*(Marks: 1 for $R$, 1 for $D_2$ and $N_2$, 2 for $t_{m,2}$ derivation, 1.5 for numerical answer, 1.5 for engineering interpretation = 7)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make — and Where Marks are Lost:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion of $N$:** Students mix rpm and rev/s within the same equation. Always state $N$ in rev/s for tip-speed formula $V_{\text{tip}} = \pi N D$.
> 2. **Wrong direction of $P/V$ under constant tip speed:** Many students incorrectly say "$P/V$ increases at industrial scale." **It decreases** (by factor $1/R$).
> 3. **Confusing $P$ with $P/V$:** A common trap in MCQs. $P$ scales as $R^2$ (constant $V_{\text{tip}}$), but $P/V$ scales as $1/R$.
> 4. **Not verifying geometric similarity:** A scale-up problem is **only solvable** if geometric similarity holds. Students must explicitly state this assumption.
> 5. **Forgetting Kolmogorov length scale:** When justifying constant tip speed, mention that the **maximum local energy dissipation rate** near the impeller is the cell-damage parameter — not $V_{\text{tip}}$ alone.
> 6. **Mixing time scaling confusion:** $t_m \propto 1/N$ ONLY for geometrically similar tanks in fully turbulent flow. In transition or laminar regimes, the dependence is different.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Scale-up** preserves process performance across geometrically similar tanks by maintaining selected transport-phenomena criteria.
- ✅ **Linear scale factor** $R = (V_2/V_1)^{1/3}$ — used everywhere.
- ✅ **Constant Mixing Quality** ⇒ mixing time $t_m$ held constant ⇒ $N$ stays roughly the same ⇒ $V_{\text{tip}}$, $P$, and $P/V$ **increase** sharply.
- ✅ **Constant Impeller Tip Speed** ⇒ $V_{\text{tip}} = \pi N D = \text{const}$ ⇒ $N \propto 1/D$ ⇒ $P \propto R^2$, $P/V \propto 1/R$ (decreases), $t_m \propto R$ (worsens).
- ✅ **Power equation:** $P = P_o \, \rho \, N^3 D^5$; $P_o \approx 6$ for Rushton turbine in turbulent regime.
- ✅ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho N D^2 / \mu$; fully turbulent when $Re > 10^4$.
- ✅ **Mixing time correlation (turbulent):** $t_m \cdot N \approx$ const for $T/D = $ const.
- ✅ **Choose constant tip speed** for **shear-sensitive** cultures (animal, plant, filamentous fungi).
- ✅ **Choose constant mixing quality** when homogeneity is more critical than shear.
- ✅ **Real bioprocesses** often use a **compromise**: constant $P/V$ (for shear) **combined with** constant $k_L a$ (for oxygen).
- ✅ **Verification always required:** calculate $Re$, $P/V$, $t_m$, $V_{\text{tip}}$ at both scales and ensure biological compatibility.

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love to combine a *numerical sub-question* (compute $R$, $N_2$, $P/V$) with a *theory sub-question* (justify the choice). Always end with a one-line engineering justification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermentation Monitoring - Various Physical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fermentation Monitoring - Various Physical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fermentation Monitoring** refers to the systematic, real-time measurement, recording, and control of critical process parameters within a bioreactor (fermenter) to ensure optimal microbial growth, product formation, and operational safety. When restricted to **Physical parameters**, it encompasses the measurement of variables such as **temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), foam, agitation speed, broth viscosity, pressure, gas flow rates, and liquid level**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** *Physical parameters monitored in a fermenter include temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, foam, agitation, and pressure. These are measured using in-line, on-line, or off-line sensors interfaced to a Distributed Control System (DCS) for feedback control.*

### 1.2 Classification of Physical Parameters

| Class | Examples | Measurement Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal** | Temperature | In-line / On-line |
| **Electrochemical** | pH, Redox (ORP) | In-line |
| **Gas-phase** | DO, Exit gas O₂ / CO₂ | In-line / On-line |
| **Mechanical** | Foam, Pressure, Agitation speed | In-line |
| **Rheological** | Viscosity, Turbidity | On-line / Off-line |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why monitor physical parameters?** Because a *living cell* (microbe) is highly sensitive to its microenvironment. Even a 2 °C deviation in temperature or 5% drop in DO can shift metabolism from product formation to by-product formation, drastically reducing yield.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a fermenter as a **"pressure cooker for microbes"** — the same way you monitor the flame, whistle, and steam in your kitchen to cook rice perfectly, a bioprocess engineer monitors the fermenter's "vitals" (temperature, pH, DO) to "cook" the cells into producing the desired product (e.g., penicillin, ethanol, insulin).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — ICU Patient Monitoring:** A fermenter behaves exactly like an ICU patient in a hospital. Both are living systems requiring continuous, multi-parameter monitoring (heart rate, O₂ saturation, temperature ↔ DO, pH, temperature). The DCS is the "nurse station" — alarms trigger when parameters cross set-points.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Physical Monitoring

#### **2.1.1 Temperature**

- **Range:** Mesophilic 20–45 °C; Thermophilic 50–80 °C.
- **Sensor:** Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD, e.g., Pt-100) or Thermistor inserted in a thermowell.
- **Principle:** Electrical resistance of platinum varies predictably with temperature.
- **Control:** Cooling coil + electric heater with PID controller.
- **Why critical:** Microbial growth rate µ follows the Arrhenius relationship; metabolic heat $Q_{met}$ must be removed.

$$
\mu = \mu_{opt} \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{opt}}\right)}
$$

#### **2.1.2 pH (Potential of Hydrogen)**

- **Range:** Typically 4.0 – 8.0 depending on organism.
- **Sensor:** Glass-membrane combination electrode (reference + measuring).
- **Principle:** Nernst equation — potential $E$ developed across glass membrane is proportional to $\log[H^+]$.
- **Control:** Automated addition of acid (H₂SO₄) / base (NH₄OH) via peristaltic pumps.
- **Why critical:** Affects enzyme activity, ionization of nutrients, and product stability.

$$
E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}
$$

Where $F$ = Faraday constant (**96485 C mol⁻¹**), $R$ = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

#### **2.1.3 Dissolved Oxygen (DO)**

- **Sensor:** Polarographic (Clark-type) electrode — Pt cathode + Ag/AgCl anode, separated by gas-permeable PTFE membrane.
- **Principle:** O₂ diffuses through membrane → reduced at cathode → current proportional to DO tension.
- **Critical for:** Aerobic fermentations; often the **rate-limiting** substrate.
- **Control:** Cascaded loop — agitation (RPM) → air flow rate → pure O₂ enrichment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Conversion:** DO is reported as **% air saturation**, **mg/L**, or **mmHg pO₂**. At 25 °C and 1 atm, pure water saturated with air contains **≈ 8.26 mg/L O₂**.

#### **2.1.4 Foam**

- **Sensor:** Conductivity-based or capacitance-based probe mounted at the top of the vessel.
- **Working:** Probe touches foam → completes circuit → antifoam (e.g., silicone-based) or mechanical foam breaker activated.
- **Why critical:** Foam can block gas exit filters, cause contamination ("wet filters"), and reduce working volume.

#### **2.1.5 Agitation Speed (RPM)**

- **Sensor:** Tachogenerator or optical encoder on the motor shaft.
- **Why critical:** Affects $k_La$ (volumetric mass transfer coefficient), mixing time, and shear stress (which damages shear-sensitive mammalian cells).
- **Equation:**

$$
k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{0.56} \cdot v_s^{0.7}
$$

Where $P_g$ = gassed power input (W), $V_L$ = liquid volume (m³), $v_s$ = superficial gas velocity (m/s).

#### **2.1.6 Pressure**

- **Sensor:** Diaphragm-type strain gauge on the top plate.
- **Why critical:** Affects solubility of gases (Henry's Law), and must be controlled for aseptic operation. Sterile fermenters run at **0.2 – 0.5 bar gauge**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Governing Equation | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst (pH) | $E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 RT}{F}\text{pH}$ | Volts (V) | 4 – 8 (pH) |
| 2 | Arrhenius (µ) | $\mu = \mu_{opt} e^{-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}-\frac{1}{T_{opt}}\right)}$ | h⁻¹ | 0.1 – 1.0 h⁻¹ |
| 3 | Henry's Law (O₂) | $C^* = \frac{p_{O_2}}{H}$ | mol/m³ | 8.26 mg/L (air, 25 °C) |
| 4 | $k_La$ (Van't Riet) | $k_La = K \left(\frac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{0.56} v_s^{0.7}$ | s⁻¹ | 50 – 1000 h⁻¹ |
| 5 | Metabolic Heat | $Q_{met} = Y_Q \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} \cdot V_L$ | W | 5 – 50 kW (industrial) |
| 6 | OUR | $\text{OUR} = k_La (C^* - C_L)$ | mg O₂ / L·h | 50 – 500 mg/L·h |
| 7 | Power Number | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$ | W | $N_p$ ≈ 5–6 (Rushton) |
| 8 | Saturation Constant | $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot C_L}{K_s + C_L}$ | h⁻¹ | $K_s$ (O₂) ≈ 0.01 mg/L |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $C^*$ (equilibrium dissolved O₂ at saturation) with $C_L$ (actual DO in broth). Always state both explicitly in answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for pH Electrode (Board-Examiner Favourite)

**Step 1: Half-cell reaction at the glass membrane**
The glass electrode develops a potential difference $E$ across a thin glass membrane that is selectively permeable to $H^+$ ions.

$$
\text{H}^+ \text{(outside)} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ \text{(inside)}
$$

**Step 2: Free energy change for ion transfer**
At equilibrium, the electrical work equals the chemical potential difference.

$$
\Delta G = -nFE
$$

**Step 3: Equate to chemical potential gradient**
Using the Nernst framework, the potential $E$ across the membrane is given by:

$$
E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{H^+, \text{outside}}
$$

**Step 4: Convert $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ and substitute pH**
Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$ and the definition $\text{pH} = -\log_{10} a_{H^+}$:

$$
E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical slope at 25 °C (298 K)**

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V/pH unit}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1-unit pH change produces a **59.16 mV** signal shift at 25 °C. This is why pH meters must be calibrated with two buffer solutions (typically pH 4 and pH 7) to determine $E^0$ and the slope.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_La$) from Air-Balance Method

**Step 1: Steady-state oxygen balance in broth**
At dynamic equilibrium, oxygen transfer rate (OTR) = oxygen uptake rate (OUR).

$$
\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = \text{OUR}
$$

**Step 2: Dynamic form during batch (unsteady-state)**
The DO concentration changes as:

$$
\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} X
$$

**Step 3: Integrating the unsteady balance**
If aeration is suddenly stopped, $k_La = 0$:

$$
\frac{dC_L}{dt} = -q_{O_2} X \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{OUR} = -\frac{dC_L}{dt}
$$

**Step 4: Re-aeration step**
When air is re-introduced, the solution rises from $C_L$ back to $C^*$. Integrating:

$$
C^* - C_L(t) = (C^* - C_L(0)) \cdot e^{-k_La \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5: Linearization for $k_La$**
Taking $\ln$ of both sides:

$$
\ln(C^* - C_L(t)) = \ln(C^* - C_L(0)) - k_La \cdot t
$$

A plot of $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line with slope $= -k_La$.

> [!TIP]
> **This dynamic gassing-out method** is the standard laboratory protocol for $k_La$ measurement — frequently asked in KTU Part B questions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Removal Requirement for a 50,000 L Fermenter

**Given:** Industrial fed-batch Penicillin fermenter.
- Working volume: $V_L = 50 \text{ m}^3$
- Biomass concentration: $X = 30 \text{ g/L} = 30 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Specific growth rate: $\mu = 0.15 \text{ h}^{-1}$
- Yield of heat on biomass: $Y_Q = 20 \text{ kJ/g cells}$

**Step 1: Biomass production rate**

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} \cdot V_L = \mu \cdot X \cdot V_L = 0.15 \times 30 \times 50 = 225 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to per-second basis**

$$
225 \, \text{kg/h} = \frac{225}{3600} = 0.0625 \text{ kg/s} = 62.5 \text{ g/s}
$$

**Step 3: Metabolic heat generation rate**

$$
Q_{met} = Y_Q \times (\text{biomass production rate})
$$

$$
Q_{met} = 20 \text{ kJ/g} \times 62.5 \text{ g/s} = 1250 \text{ kJ/s} = 1250 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Cooling duty required**

$$
Q_{cool} = Q_{met} + Q_{sensible} + Q_{losses} \approx 1.2 \times Q_{met}
$$

$$
Q_{cool} \approx 1500 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This 1.5 MW heat load explains why industrial fermenters require chilled water loops (4 – 8 °C) and not ordinary cooling towers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Sensor

| Condition | Recommended Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| High cell density (CHO cells) | Optical DO patch (fluorescence) | No electrolyte consumption |
| Long-duration fermentation (> 7 days) | Steam-sterilizable pH probe | Drift compensation possible |
| Shear-sensitive cells (insect cells) | Capacitance foam probe + low-shear Rushton | Mechanical foam breaker only |
| GMP-grade production | In-line single-use sensors | Pre-calibrated, no contamination |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Fermenter Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fermenter Vessel] --> B[Sensor Array]
    B --> C[Temperature RTD]
    B --> D[pH Probe]
    B --> E[Dissolved O2 Clark Electrode]
    B --> F[Foam Conductivity Probe]
    B --> G[Pressure Diaphragm]
    B --> H[Agitation Tachometer]
    C --> I[Signal Conditioner]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[ADC Converter]
    J --> K[Distributed Control System DCS]
    K --> L[PID Controllers]
    L --> M[Cooling Water Valve]
    L --> N[Acid Base Pumps]
    L --> O[Agitator VFD]
    L --> P[Air Flow Control]
    K --> Q[SCADA HMI Display]
    K --> R[Alarm Logger]
    K --> S[Data Historian]
```

### 4.2 Sensor Placement Cross-Section (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph vessel[Fermenter Vessel]
        T1[Pt100 RTD Thermowell]
        P1[pH Electrode Top Port]
        D1[DO Probe Bottom Port]
        F1[Foam Probe Top Headspace]
        AG1[Agitator Shaft Encoder]
        PR1[Pressure Transducer Top Plate]
        SP1[Sparger Ring Aeration]
        CO1[Cooling Coil Jacket]
    end
    T1 --> DCS1[Data Acquisition]
    P1 --> DCS1
    D1 --> DCS1
    F1 --> DCS1
    AG1 --> DCS1
    PR1 --> DCS1
    SP1 --> CTRL1[Control Loop]
    CO1 --> CTRL1
    DCS1 --> CTRL1
```

### 4.3 Feedback Control Loop for pH (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Set Point pH 6.8] --> B[Comparator]
    B --> C[Error Signal e]
    C --> D[PID Controller]
    D --> E[Peristaltic Pump]
    E --> F[Acid Base Addition to Vessel]
    F --> G[Broth pH]
    G --> H[pH Electrode Feedback]
    H --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is a **closed-loop negative feedback** system. The PID gains ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) must be tuned using **Ziegler-Nichols** method to avoid oscillations — a common KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**List any three physical parameters monitored in a fermenter. Name the sensor used for dissolved oxygen measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three physical parameters:
1. **Temperature** (measured by Pt-100 RTD) — 1 mark
2. **pH** (measured by glass-membrane combination electrode) — 1 mark
3. **Dissolved Oxygen** — measured by **Clark-type polarographic electrode** consisting of a Pt cathode and Ag/AgCl anode separated by a gas-permeable (Teflon) membrane. Oxygen diffuses through the membrane and is reduced at the cathode, producing a current proportional to the dissolved O₂ concentration. — 1 mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Why is foam control essential in a fermentation process? Name one chemical antifoam.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Foam control is essential because:
1. Foam can **block the exit gas filter** leading to pressure build-up and filter wetting, which compromises sterility (contamination risk). — 1 mark
2. Foam reduces the **effective working volume** of the fermenter and can cause entrainment of cells, leading to product loss. — 1 mark

**Chemical antifoam examples:** *Silicone-based polyether (e.g., polydimethylsiloxane), or natural oils such as sunflower oil / soybean oil.* — 1 mark

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular]**

**Q3. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **dissolved oxygen (DO) probe** used in fermentation monitoring. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A Clark-type polarographic DO probe consists of:
  - **Platinum cathode** (working electrode) — small disc.
  - **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) anode** (reference electrode).
  - **KCl electrolyte solution** filling the space between electrodes.
  - **Oxygen-permeable PTFE (Teflon) membrane** stretched over the cathode, held by an O-ring.
  - A small applied polarizing voltage of **≈ 0.6 – 0.8 V**.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Dissolved O₂ diffuses through the PTFE membrane (proportional to partial pressure gradient).
- At the Pt cathode, O₂ is reduced: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
- At the Ag anode: $4Ag + 4Cl^- \rightarrow 4AgCl + 4e^-$
- The resulting **current (µA)** is directly proportional to the dissolved O₂ concentration in the broth.

**Key Features (1 mark):**
- Membrane must be replaced periodically (4–6 weeks).
- Probe response time ≈ 30–60 s; must be agitated to avoid stagnant layer errors.
- Output is linear with $pO_2$ — ideal for cascading with agitation control.

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes — the DO sensor block connected to DCS.)

---

**(b)** Explain the **dynamic gassing-out method** for the determination of $k_La$ in a laboratory fermenter. Derive the relevant equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The dynamic gassing-out method involves two steps:
1. **De-oxygenation:** Nitrogen gas is sparged to strip dissolved O₂ from the broth (DO → 0).
2. **Re-aeration:** Air sparging is resumed and the rise of DO back to saturation $C^*$ is monitored.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting with the unsteady DO balance:

$$
\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2}X
$$

If OUR is constant (low cell density), separation yields:

$$
(C^* - C_L)\frac{dC_L}{dt} \text{ integration leads to} \ln(C^* - C_L) = \ln(C^* - C_{L0}) - k_La t
$$

**Step-by-step integration (as shown in Section 3.2):**

$$
\int_{C_{L0}}^{C_L} \frac{dC_L}{C^* - C_L} = -\int_0^t k_La \, dt
$$

$$
-\ln(C^* - C_L) \Big|_{C_{L0}}^{C_L} = -k_La t
$$

$$
\ln(C^* - C_L) = \ln(C^* - C_{L0}) - k_La t
$$

**Application (1 mark):**
A semi-log plot of $(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line; the slope is $-k_La$. This method is widely used in KTU lab courses for impeller design validation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. (a)** Describe the **principle, construction, and working of a pH probe** used in fermentation. State the **Nernst equation** and its significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A combination pH electrode has **two half-cells** in a single body:
  - **Glass measuring electrode** — thin Na₂O-CaO-SiO₂ glass bulb sensitive to H⁺.
  - **Reference electrode** — Ag/AgCl wire in saturated KCl solution, connected via a **salt bridge** (ceramic frit/junction).
- The glass membrane develops a potential $E$ that depends on the H⁺ activity difference between the inner buffer (pH 7) and the outside broth.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Ions $\text{H}^+$ interact with the hydrated glass layer, creating a **phase-boundary potential**.
- The measured voltage $E$ is the difference between glass electrode and reference electrode potentials, given by the **Nernst equation**.

**Nernst Equation (1 mark):**

$$
E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
- Slope is **59.16 mV/pH unit at 25 °C**.
- This linear relationship allows direct conversion of millivolt signal to pH; calibration with two buffers (pH 4 and pH 7) eliminates $E^0$ offset and verifies slope.

---

**(b)** An industrial fermenter with working volume **50 m³** is producing a recombinant protein. The biomass concentration is **25 g/L**, specific growth rate **0.2 h⁻¹**, and heat yield coefficient **$Y_Q$ = 22 kJ/g cells**. Calculate:
  (i) Biomass production rate in **kg/h**
  (ii) Metabolic heat load in **kW**
  (iii) Justify why an external chiller is mandatory. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Biomass production rate (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} \cdot V_L = \mu \cdot X \cdot V_L
$$

$$
= 0.2 \, \text{h}^{-1} \times 25 \, \text{g/L} \times 50{,}000 \, \text{L}
$$

$$
= 250{,}000 \, \text{g/h} = 250 \, \text{kg/h}
$$

**(ii) Metabolic heat load (3 marks):**

Convert to g/s:

$$
250 \, \text{kg/h} = \frac{250 \times 1000}{3600} = 69.44 \, \text{g/s}
$$

Apply:

$$
Q_{met} = Y_Q \times \text{biomass rate}
$$

$$
Q_{met} = 22 \, \text{kJ/g} \times 69.44 \, \text{g/s} = 1527.8 \, \text{kJ/s} = 1527.8 \, \text{kW}
$$

Including 20% safety margin for sensible heat and losses:

$$
Q_{cool} \approx 1.2 \times 1527.8 \approx 1833 \, \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) Justification (2 marks):**
- The fermenter generates **~1.8 MW** of metabolic heat continuously.
- Ordinary cooling water (25 °C) cannot remove this load; a **chilled water loop (4–8 °C)** is required.
- Without it, broth temperature would rise to **> 40 °C** in <30 minutes, killing the recombinant E. coli host and ruining the batch.
- Hence, an **external chiller with PID control** on the cooling coil flow rate is mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** Forgetting to convert kg/h → g/s before applying $Y_Q$ (in kJ/g) costs 1 mark. Always state the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Sign convention in Nernst:** Many students write the equation with a `+` sign instead of `−`. The correct sign is **negative** because pH is defined as $-\log[H^+]$.
> 3. **DO sensor terminology:** Do not call Clark probe a "voltaic cell" — it is a **polarographic (electrolysis) cell** requiring external voltage.
> 4. **Dynamic gassing-out assumption:** Students often forget to state the assumption of **constant OUR** during re-aeration. Examiner will deduct 1 mark.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **Physical parameters** monitored: **T, pH, DO, foam, agitation (RPM), pressure, gas flow.**
- [x] **DO sensor** = **Clark-type polarographic** electrode; principle = O₂ reduction at Pt cathode; current ∝ DO.
- [x] **pH sensor** = combination glass electrode; **Nernst slope = 59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C.**
- [x] **Temperature sensor** = Pt-100 RTD; resistance increases linearly with temperature.
- [x] **Foam control** uses **conductivity probe + antifoam agent** (silicone-based) or mechanical breaker.
- [x] **$k_La$ measurement** by **dynamic gassing-out method**: $\ln(C^* - C_L) = \ln(C^* - C_{L0}) - k_La t$.
- [x] **Metabolic heat load** scales with $\mu X V_L$; industrial fermenters need **MW-scale chilling**.
- [x] **Henry's Law** governs O₂ solubility: $C^* = p_{O_2} / H$.
- [x] **Feedback control** uses **PID controllers** with **cascaded loops** (e.g., DO → agitation → air flow).
- [x] **KTU hot keywords:** Clark electrode, Nernst equation, $k_La$, PID tuning, dynamic gassing-out, OUR = OTR at steady state.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical and Biological Parameters Measured or Controlled in Bioreactors, Physical and Chemical Sensors for Fermentation Medium and Gases, Online Sensors for Cell Properties, Offline Analytical Methods. Process Control - Open Loop and Closed Loop Control, Direct Regulatory Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemical and Biological Parameters Measured or Controlled in Bioreactors, Physical and Chemical Sensors for Fermentation Medium and Gases, Online Sensors for Cell Properties, Offline Analytical Methods. Process Control - Open Loop and Closed Loop Control, Direct Regulatory Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Bioreactor Parameters and Process Control

In KTU 2024 Scheme Bioprocess Engineering terminology, the **parameters measured or controlled in bioreactors** are the physical, chemical, and biological state variables that govern cell growth, substrate consumption, product formation, and overall reactor performance. These parameters form the backbone of bioprocess monitoring because they allow the operator to maintain the microenvironment within the narrow physiological window required by the microorganism or cell culture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Examiner Expectation):**
> A **bioreactor parameter** is any measurable state variable whose deviation from its set-point alters specific productivity (\(q_p\)), volumetric productivity, yield coefficient (\(Y_{X/S}\)), or product quality attributes in a bioprocess system.

**The classification of parameters measured/controlled in a bioreactor is:**

- **Physical Parameters:** Temperature, pressure, foam level, agitator speed (RPM), aeration rate, broth viscosity.
- **Chemical Parameters:** pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), dissolved \(CO_2\), redox potential, ionic strength, substrate concentration.
- **Biological Parameters:** Biomass concentration (\(X\)), cell viability, product titre, intracellular metabolites, recombinant protein content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Physical and chemical sensors (like pH and DO probes) are considered *online and in-situ* because they sit directly inside the broth. Biological parameters (like biomass and product concentration) historically required *offline* sampling, but modern *online* sensors (capacitance, NIR, fluorescence) now allow real-time biological monitoring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for the First-Time Learner

Imagine you are **baking a sourdough bread**. The dough is your "bioreactor," the yeast are your "cells," and the oven conditions are your "controlled parameters."

- You constantly check the **temperature** (physical) — if too hot, the yeast die.
- You taste-test (sample) the dough periodically to know how the **flavor is developing** (biological parameter — *offline analysis*).
- If the dough becomes too sticky/runny, you sprinkle flour (this is your **control action** responding to an **error**).

In a bioreactor, instead of a human sensing, a **sensor (probe)** detects the variable, a **controller** compares it with a desired **set-point**, and an **actuator** (pump, valve) corrects the deviation automatically. The dough's "taste test" is analogous to drawing a sample for HPLC or dry cell weight — slow, manual, and *offline*. The oven's built-in thermostat is the *closed-loop feedback* controller.

---

### 1.3 Process Control — Formal KTU Definitions

- **Open-Loop Control:** A control architecture where the controller output is *pre-determined* by a programmed schedule or operator, with **no feedback** from the process variable.

- **Closed-Loop Control (Feedback Control):** A control architecture where the controller continuously receives a measured process variable, computes the *error* (\(e(t) = SP - PV\)), and adjusts the actuator to minimise the error.

- **Direct Regulatory Control (DRC):** The lowest level of control hierarchy in a bioreactor, where single loops (e.g., temperature, pH, DO, agitation) regulate individual physical and chemical variables directly without intermediate supervisory logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** When asked to differentiate *Open Loop* vs *Closed Loop*, always present it as a *block diagram with a feedback line*. The presence or absence of the feedback signal is the *single most important* distinguishing feature.

### 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Working Volume (\(V_w\))** ranges typically **1 L to 200 m³**.
- **Standard Set-Points:** Temperature **\(30-37\ °C\)**; pH **\(6.8-7.4\)**; DO **\(>30\%\) air saturation** for aerobic cultures.
- **RTD/PT-100 sensor accuracy:** \(\pm 0.1\ °C\).
- **Standard pH electrode slope:** **\(59.16\ mV/pH\)** unit at \(25\ °C\).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Sensors for Fermentation Medium and Gases

Physical sensors convert mechanical, thermal, or fluid-dynamic properties into electrical signals. In bioreactors, the following physical parameters and their sensors are commonly used:

#### 2.1.1 Temperature Measurement
- **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD — PT-100):** Platinum wire whose resistance \(R\) varies with temperature as \(R(t) = R_0 \cdot [1 + \alpha t + \beta t^2]\), where \(\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ K^{-1}\).
- **Thermocouple (Type J/K):** Generates a Seebeck EMF based on the temperature difference between a hot junction (in broth) and a cold junction (ice-bath reference).
- **Thermistor:** Semiconductor bead exhibiting a steep non-linear resistance-temperature curve; high sensitivity but limited range.

#### 2.1.2 Pressure and Foam Sensors
- **Pressure Transducer:** Strain-gauge type, measures headspace and jacket pressure.
- **Foam Sensors:**
  * **Conductivity probe** — uses two electrodes; foam bridges the gap and completes a circuit.
  * **Capacitance probe** — measures the dielectric change between parallel plates when foam contacts them.
  * **Thermal foam sensor** — detects the lower thermal conductivity of foam vs. air.

#### 2.1.3 Agitator Speed and Aeration
- **Tachometer (Hall-effect sensor):** Measures RPM of the impeller.
- **Rotameter / Mass Flow Controller (MFC):** Controls inlet air / \(O_2\) / \(CO_2\) flow rates.
- **Thermal mass flow meter:** Two heated tubes; gas flow alters the temperature differential proportional to flow rate.

### 2.2 Chemical Sensors for Fermentation Medium and Gases

#### 2.2.1 pH Sensor (Glass Electrode)
A combination electrode consists of a *glass sensing bulb*, an *internal reference* (Ag/AgCl), and an *external reference* (saturated KCl). The potential generated is governed by the **Nernst equation**:

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \ln(a_{H^+})$$

where:
- \(E\) = measured electrode potential (V)
- \(E^\circ\) = standard potential (V)
- \(R\) = universal gas constant (**\(8.314\ J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}\)**)
- \(T\) = absolute temperature (K)
- \(F\) = Faraday constant (**\(96485\ C \cdot mol^{-1}\)**)
- \(a_{H^+}\) = activity of hydrogen ions (M)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** At 25 °C (298 K), the slope becomes \(\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = 59.16\ mV/pH\). This number appears in nearly every KTU pH-related question. Memorise it.
