| Rapid screening of inputs | **SIMULINK block model** | Graphical, easy to modify parameters |
| Scale-up from shake flask to 1000 L | **Coupled ODE + mass transfer** | $k_La$ becomes critical at large scale |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Bioprocess Model (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Variables: S_in, F, T, pH] --> B[Balance Domain Definition]
    B --> C[Conservation Laws: Mass Energy Momentum]
    C --> D[Kinetic Expressions: Monod Luedeking-Piret]
    D --> E[Transport Equations: k_La, Heat Transfer]
    E --> F[ODE System da/dt = f a, theta]
    F --> G[Numerical Solver: ode45 ode15s MATLAB]
    G --> H[Simulation Output: X t, S t, P t]
    H --> I{Validation against Experimental Data}
    I --> J[Error Metrics: RMSE, R-squared]
    J --> K{Acceptable Accuracy}
    K -->|Yes| L[Validated Model]
    K -->|No| M[Parameter Re-estimation]
    M --> D
    L --> N[Process Optimization and Scale-up]
```

### 4.2 ANN Architecture for Bioprocess Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input: pH] --> H1N1[Neuron 1]
    IN2[Input: DO] --> H1N1
    IN3[Input: Temperature] --> H1N1
    IN1 --> H1N2[Neuron 2]
    IN2 --> H1N2
    IN3 --> H1N2
    IN1 --> H1N3[Neuron 3]
    IN2 --> H1N3
    IN3 --> H1N3
    H1N1 --> OUTN[Neuron Output]
    H1N2 --> OUTN
    H1N3 --> OUTN
    OUTN --> OUT[Output: Predicted Biomass X]
```

### 4.3 SIMULINK Bioprocess Simulation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Block: S_in, F] --> B[Subtract Block: S_in minus S]
    B --> C[Gain Block: D]
    C --> D[Sum Junction: dS/dt]
    E[Monod Function Block: mu max S over Ks plus S] --> F[Gain Block: 1/Y x over s]
    F --> D
    G[Saturation Block: k La star minus DO] --> H[ODE Integrator: dX/dt]
    I[Biomass Balance Block: mu X minus D X] --> H
    H --> J[Scope Block: Display X vs Time]
    D --> K[Scope Block: Display S vs Time]
```

### 4.4 Model Validation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Experimental Data] --> B[Pre-processing: Smooth, Filter]
    B --> C[Model Simulation]
    C --> D[Compare Predicted vs Experimental]
    D --> E[Compute RMSE and R-squared]
    E --> F{R-squared greater than 0.9}
    F -->|Yes| G[Model Accepted]
    F -->|No| H[Adjust Parameters via fmincon]
    H --> C
    G --> I[Deploy for Process Optimization]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a bioprocess model. List any two components of its structure.**

**Model Answer:**
A bioprocess model is a mathematical representation of the biological, chemical, and physical phenomena occurring in a bioprocess. It consists of three main components:
1. **Balance equations** (mass and energy conservation)
2. **Kinetic rate expressions** (e.g., Monod, Luedeking–Piret)
3. **Transport equations** (mass and heat transfer relations)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is meant by "balance domain" in bioprocess modeling?**

**Model Answer:**
A balance domain (or control volume) is the **defined region in space and time** over which mass, energy, and momentum conservation laws are applied. It can be *lumped* (uniform, 0-D, e.g., CSTR) or *distributed* (spatial variation, e.g., packed-bed reactor). Choosing the correct domain determines whether the model uses ODEs or PDEs.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Choose ONE of the following. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **structure of a bioprocess model**. Explain the role of each component in modeling a typical fed-batch fermentation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **steady-state mass balance equations** for biomass and substrate in a chemostat with Monod kinetics. Define *wash-out* condition.

**Model Solution (a):**

The structure of a bioprocess model consists of three interconnected components:

**1. Balance Equations:** These are the conservation laws applied to a defined control volume. For a fed-batch reactor (no outflow), the general form is:

$$\frac{d(C_i V)}{dt} = F_{in} C_{i,in} + r_i V$$

**2. Kinetic Expressions:** Define how fast biological reactions proceed. Monod equation for growth:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S}$$

**3. Transport Equations:** Describe mass transfer (e.g., oxygen transfer via $k_La$) and heat transfer.

These three components are coupled through the **mass balance equations**, which contain both kinetic and transport terms. The model produces a system of ODEs that can be solved numerically in MATLAB/SIMULINK.

**Model Solution (b):**

For a CSTR at steady state with sterile feed ($X_{in} = 0$):

Biomass balance: $0 = -DX + \mu X \Rightarrow \mu = D$

Substituting Monod: $D = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \bar{S}}{K_s + \bar{S}}$

Solving:

$$\bar{S} = \frac{D K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}, \quad \bar{X} = Y_{X/S}(S_{in} - \bar{S})$$

**Wash-out condition** occurs when $D \geq \mu_{max}$, causing $\bar{X} \to 0$ and complete loss of biomass from the reactor. The critical dilution rate is:

$$D_c = \mu_{max}\left(\frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}\right)$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **objectives and benefits of bioprocess simulation**. Discuss how MATLAB and SIMULINK are used as simulation tools.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A chemostat has $D = 0.4 \, h^{-1}$, $\mu_{max} = 0.6 \, h^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.1 \, g/L$, $S_{in} = 20 \, g/L$, $Y_{X/S} = 0.45$. Calculate $\bar{S}$, $\bar{X}$, and comment on whether wash-out will occur.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Objectives of Bioprocess Simulation:**
1. Predict system behavior under varying inputs
2. Optimize yield, productivity, and titer
3. Reduce experimental cost and time
4. Enable safe testing of extreme conditions

**Benefits:**
- Economic savings through fewer experiments
- Faster scale-up decisions
- Better understanding of rate-limiting steps

**MATLAB** is used for numerical integration of ODEs using solvers like `ode45` and for parameter estimation using `lsqnonlin`. **SIMULINK** provides a graphical block-diagram environment where each block represents a unit operation or rate expression, making it ideal for dynamic simulation of fed-batch and continuous bioreactors.

**Model Solution (b):**

Steady-state substrate:

$$\bar{S} = \frac{D K_s}{\mu_{max} - D} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.1}{0.6 - 0.4} = \frac{0.04}{0.2} = 0.2 \, g/L$$

Steady-state biomass:

$$\bar{X} = Y_{X/S}(S_{in} - \bar{S}) = 0.45 \times (20 - 0.2) = 0.45 \times 19.8 = 8.91 \, g/L$$

Critical dilution rate:

$$D_c = \mu_{max}\left(\frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}\right) = 0.6 \times \frac{20}{0.1 + 20} = 0.597 \, h^{-1}$$

Since $D = 0.4 \, h^{-1} < D_c = 0.597 \, h^{-1}$, **wash-out will NOT occur** and the bioreactor operates in a stable steady state.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing biomass balance as $\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - DX$ without showing the derivation steps from the general balance equation. **Lose 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $k_La$ (volumetric mass transfer coefficient) with $K_La$ (overall mass transfer coefficient). They differ by interfacial area. **Lose 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In numerical questions, forgetting to verify the wash-out condition $D < \mu_{max}$ before computing $\bar{S}$. **Lose 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing $S_{in} - S$ as $S - S_{in}$ in substrate balance — sign errors are heavily penalized.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In ANN questions, omitting the activation function or bias term from the neuron equation — you lose the "complete expression" marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- **Bioprocess Model** = Balance Equations + Kinetic Expressions + Transport Equations
- **General Balance:** Accumulation = In − Out + Generation − Consumption
- **Monod Kinetics:** $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} S}{K_s + S}$
- **Luedeking–Piret:** $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \dfrac{dX}{dt} + \beta X$
- **Oxygen Transfer:** $OTR = k_La(C^*_{O_2} - C_{O_2})$
- **Balance Domain Types:** Lumped (0-D, ODE) vs Distributed (1-D/2-D/3-D, PDE)
- **Chemostat Biomass Balance:** $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = D(X_{in} - X) + \mu X$
- **Chemostat Substrate Balance:** $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = D(S_{in} - S) - \dfrac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} - mX$
- **Steady-State $\bar{S}$:** $\bar{S} = \dfrac{DK_s}{\mu_{max} - D}$
- **Wash-out Condition:** $D \geq D_c$ where $D_c = \mu_{max}\dfrac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$
- **ANN Equation:** $y = f(\sum w_i x_i + b)$
- **Validation Metrics:** RMSE → 0; $R^2$ → 1
- **MATLAB Solvers:** `ode45` (general), `ode15s` (stiff), `lsqnonlin` (parameter fitting)
- **SIMULINK:** Graphical block-diagram environment for dynamic simulation
- **Key Assumptions for CSTR Models:** Perfect mixing, constant volume, sterile feed (often)
- **Units to Remember:** $\mu$ in h⁻¹, $K_s$ in g/L, $Y_{X/S}$ in g/g, $k_La$ in h⁻¹
- **Priority Order for ESE:** Always write the **balance equation first**, then substitute kinetic expressions, then solve.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biopro Designer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biopro Designer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Biopro Designer? **Biopro Designer** is a specialised **process simulation and design software package** used in bioprocess engineering education and research. It is a Windows-based, dynamic modelling tool that allows students and engineers to mathematically model, simulate, and analyse the behaviour of **bioreactor systems** under defined kinetic and operational parameters. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Bioprocess Engineering (PCBBT502)*, Biopro Designer is treated as a **computer-aided bioprocess simulation tool** that bridges the gap between theoretical kinetic models (Monod, Logistic, Luedeking-Piret, etc.) and the practical operation of batch, fed-batch, and continuous stirred-tank bioreactors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 syllabus-aligned):** > *"Biopro Designer is an interactive, dynamic simulation software used to model the growth of microorganisms, substrate utilisation, and product formation in a bioprocess by numerically integrating the coupled Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) that represent mass balances across batch, fed-batch, and continuous bioreactors."* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are learning to fly an aeroplane. Before you are allowed inside a real cockpit, you spend hundreds of hours in a **flight simulator**. The simulator mimics how the aircraft will respond to every joystick movement, throttle change, and wind gust — without any real risk. **Biopro Designer is the "flight simulator" for a fermentation bioreactor.** Just as the flight simulator solves Newton's laws of motion for the aircraft, Biopro Designer solves the **mass-balance differential equations** for the bioreactor. You can: - Change the initial substrate concentration and see how the biomass curve changes. - Switch from batch to fed-batch mode and watch the product concentration evolve. - Introduce an inhibitory substrate and observe how growth stalls. - Run "what-if" scenarios that would take weeks and cost lakhs of rupees in a real lab. > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway for students:** Biopro Designer does not invent new biology or chemistry. It simply *solves the equations you already learned in theory* — mass balances, kinetic rate laws, and stoichiometry — using robust numerical integration algorithms (typically **Runge-Kutta 4th order / RK4** with adaptive step sizing) so you can *see* the dynamics of a bioprocess over time. ## 1.3 Where Biopro Designer Fits in the Bioprocess Engineering Workflow A typical bioprocess design project follows this pipeline, and Biopro Designer occupies the **"Modelling & Simulation"** block: 1. **Process selection** – Choose the target product (e.g., ethanol, penicillin, monoclonal antibody). 2. **Kinetic modelling** – Identify the appropriate microbial strain and growth model. 3. **Modelling & Simulation (Biopro Designer)** – Build the dynamic model, feed parameters, and simulate. 4. **Experimental validation** – Run lab-scale bioreactors to compare with the simulation. 5. **Scale-up & optimisation** – Use the validated model to design the industrial-scale process. ## 1.4 Why is Biopro Designer Important for KTU Examinations? > [!IMPORTANT] > From the KTU 2024 examiner's perspective, the question on Biopro Designer is usually framed as a **"design/compute"** type question where you are given: > - A microbial strain and its kinetic parameters ($\mu_{max}$, $K_s$, $Y_{X/S}$, $Y_{P/S}$, $k_d$, $m_s$, etc.) > - An initial or feed substrate concentration > - A reactor configuration (batch, fed-batch, CSTR) > - A target final biomass or product concentration > > You are then expected to **set up the governing differential equations**, **solve them** (analytically for simple cases, or describe how Biopro Designer numerically integrates them), and **interpret the resulting concentration-vs-time profiles**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Mathematical Heart of Biopro Designer At its core, Biopro Designer is an **ODE (Ordinary Differential Equation) solver** wrapped in a graphical user interface. The user feeds in: - A **process model** (kinetic expressions for $\mu$, $q_S$, $q_P$) - **Stoichiometric parameters** ($Y_{X/S}$, $Y_{P/S}$, etc.) - **Operating conditions** (initial concentrations, feed rates, residence time) - **Reactor configuration** (batch, fed-batch, CSTR) The software then assembles the **dynamic mass balance equations** and integrates them numerically across the simulation time horizon. ## 2.2 The Three Universal Mass Balances Inside Biopro Designer For every bioprocess simulation, Biopro Designer internally writes and solves the following **three coupled ODEs** (one for each state variable: biomass $X$, substrate $S$, and product $P$). ### 2.2.1 Biomass Balance (the "Growth Equation") The general rate of biomass accumulation is: $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X - k_d \cdot X $$ where: - $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h$^{-1}$) - $X$ = biomass concentration (g/L) - $k_d$ = specific death / decay rate constant (h$^{-1}$) **The "Why":** Biomass grows due to cell reproduction (term $\mu X$) and decays due to cell lysis and death (term $-k_d X$). ### 2.2.2 Substrate Balance (the "Food Consumption Equation") $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}} - m_s \cdot X $$ where: - $Y_{X/S}$ = yield coefficient of biomass on substrate (g biomass / g substrate) - $m_s$ = maintenance coefficient (g substrate / g biomass·h) **The "Why":** Substrate is consumed for two purposes — to build new biomass (term $-\mu X / Y_{X/S}$) and to maintain existing cells (term $-m_s X$). No product balance term appears in the substrate equation because the substrate-to-product conversion is already captured through the yields and the maintenance term when using a simplified black-box model. ### 2.2.3 Product Balance (the "Output Equation") Using the **Luedeking-Piret model**: $$ \frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \mu \cdot X + \beta \cdot X $$ where: - $\alpha$ = growth-associated product formation constant (g product / g biomass) - $\beta$ = non-growth-associated product formation constant (g product / g biomass·h) **The "Why":** Some products (e.g., **ethanol** in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) are formed *only* during active growth, hence the $\alpha \mu X$ term. Other products (e.g., some **secondary metabolites** like antibiotics) are formed *after* the growth phase slows down, hence the $\beta X$ term. ## 2.3 Biopro Designer's Reactor-Configuration Modules Biopro Designer offers three reactor templates, each modifying the three balances above with the appropriate **inlet/outlet** flow terms. ### 2.3.1 Batch Reactor (closed system, no flow in/out) For a batch reactor, $F_{in} = F_{out} = 0$, so the balances reduce to the **net rate of generation only**: $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - k_d X $$ $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} - m_s X $$ $$ \frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X + \beta X $$ ### 2.3.2 Fed-Batch Reactor (inlet flow $F$ in, no outlet) A fed-batch reactor is essentially a batch whose volume increases with time. Biopro Designer includes a **volume balance**: $$ \frac{dV}{dt} = F $$ The component balances become: $$ \frac{d(XV)}{dt} = V \cdot (\mu X - k_d X) $$ $$ \frac{d(SV)}{dt} = F \cdot S_{in} - V \cdot \left(\frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} + m_s X\right) $$ $$ \frac{d(PV)}{dt} = V \cdot (\alpha \mu X + \beta X) $$ which Biopro Designer internally re-expresses in terms of *concentrations* (using the quotient rule) before numerical integration. ### 2.3.3 Continuous Stirred-Tank Reactor — CSTR (inlet and outlet flow $F$ equal) At **steady state** (CSTR is the only reactor where this assumption is genuinely useful for hand calculations), the time derivatives are zero, giving algebraic equations: $$ 0 = D \cdot (X_{in} - X) + (\mu - k_d) X $$ $$ 0 = D \cdot (S_{in} - S) - \left(\frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} + m_s X\right) $$ $$ 0 = D \cdot (P_{in} - P) + (\alpha \mu + \beta) X $$ where $D = F/V$ is the **dilution rate** (h$^{-1}$), the KTU-high-yield parameter for CSTR problems. ## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The table below collects every equation you must memorise for a Biopro Designer problem in the KTU 2024 ESE. > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipe characters (`|`) are used inside any table cell below.** All absolute-value expressions use the LaTeX `\vert` command to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. | # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod specific growth rate | h$^{-1}$ | | 2 | $\mu_{net} = \mu - k_d = \mu_{max}\cdot\dfrac{S}{K_s+S} - k_d$ | Net specific growth rate (with decay) | h$^{-1}$ | | 3 | $\mu_{d} = D$ | CSTR wash-out condition (critical dilution) | h$^{-1}$ | | 4 | $X = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | Batch biomass-substrate stoichiometry (no maintenance) | g L$^{-1}$ | | 5 | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Doubling time of biomass | h | | 6 | $P = Y_{P/S} \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | Batch product-substrate stoichiometry (growth-associated) | g L$^{-1}$ | | 7 | $D = F/V$ | Dilution rate | h$^{-1}$ | | 8 | $\tau = 1/D = V/F$ | Space time / residence time | h | | 9 | $S_{CSTR} = \dfrac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D}$ | Steady-state substrate exit concentration in CSTR (Monod, sterile feed) | g L$^{-1}$ | | 10 | $X_{CSTR} = Y_{X/S}\cdot(S_{in} - S_{CSTR})$ | Steady-state biomass in CSTR | g L$^{-1}$ | | 11 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = F$ | Volume change in fed-batch | L h$^{-1}$ | | 12 | $D_{crit} = \mu_{max}\cdot\dfrac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$ | Critical dilution rate (wash-out threshold) | h$^{-1}$ | | 13 | $q_S = \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}} + m_s$ | Specific substrate consumption rate | g g$^{-1}$ h$^{-1}$ | | 14 | $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$ | Specific product formation rate | g g$^{-1}$ h$^{-1}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** Every formula above is implemented as a *numerical subroutine* inside Biopro Designer. The software does not give you these equations directly — it expects you to *type in* the kinetic constants, then it *draws* the time-profiles. The KTU exam, however, requires you to derive and apply the same equations on paper. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Batch Reactor Biomass Profile Under Monod Kinetics (No Decay) **Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark question stem):** A bacterial culture grows in a batch bioreactor with Monod kinetics, parameters $\mu_{max} = 0.5$ h$^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.2$ g/L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.4$ g/g, $S_0 = 20$ g/L, $X_0 = 0.05$ g/L. Assume $k_d = 0$ and $m_s = 0$. Plot (or describe) the $X(t)$, $S(t)$ profile for $t = 0$ to $10$ h, the way Biopro Designer would. ### Step 1 — Write the governing ODEs The biomass balance with $k_d = 0$ is: $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot X $$ The substrate balance with $m_s = 0$ is: $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu_{max}}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot X $$ ### Step 2 — Eliminate the rate term using the differential form of the yield Divide the substrate balance by the biomass balance to remove time $dt$: $$ \frac{dS}{dX} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} $$ This gives the **stoichiometric coupling**: the ratio of substrate consumed to biomass produced is *constant* and equal to the reciprocal of the yield coefficient. ### Step 3 — Integrate from the initial state $(X_0, S_0)$ to any state $(X, S)$ $$ \int_{S_0}^{S} dS = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} \int_{X_0}^{X} dX $$ $$ S - S_0 = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} (X - X_0) $$ $$ S = S_0 - \frac{X - X_0}{Y_{X/S}} $$ This is the **linear biomass-substrate stoichiometric line** drawn by Biopro Designer on the $X$–$S$ phase-plane plot. ### Step 4 — Substitute back into the biomass ODE to obtain a single ODE in $X(t)$ Insert the expression for $S$ into the Monod term: $$ S = S_0 - \frac{X - X_0}{Y_{X/S}} $$ $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S_0 - \dfrac{X - X_0}{Y_{X/S}}}{K_s + S_0 - \dfrac{X - X_0}{Y_{X/S}}} \cdot X $$ ### Step 5 — Numerical integration (Biopro Designer core loop) > [!NOTE] > The expression above **cannot be integrated analytically in closed form** because the Monod term is a *rational function* of $X$. This is exactly why Biopro Designer exists — it numerically integrates the ODE using an **RK4 (Runge-Kutta 4th order) algorithm** with adaptive step-size control. The RK4 update for the biomass variable at step $n$ to $n+1$ is: $$ X_{n+1} = X_n + \frac{\Delta t}{6} \cdot \left(k_1 + 2 k_2 + 2 k_3 + k_4\right) $$ where the four slopes are: $$ k_1 = f(X_n, t_n) $$ $$ k_2 = f\!\left(X_n + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\, k_1,\; t_n + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\right) $$ $$ k_3 = f\!\left(X_n + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\, k_2,\; t_n + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\right) $$ $$ k_4 = f\!\left(X_n + \Delta t \cdot k_3,\; t_n + \Delta t\right) $$ and the slope function $f(X, t)$ is the RHS of the biomass ODE. ### Step 6 — Compute a few sample points to validate the simulation Using RK4 with $\Delta t = 1$ h, start at $X_0 = 0.05$ g/L, $S_0 = 20$ g/L. **At $t = 0$:** $$ S_0 = 20 \text{ g/L} \gg K_s = 0.2 \text{ g/L} \implies \mu \approx \mu_{max} = 0.5 \text{ h}^{-1} $$ $$ k_1 = 0.5 \times 0.05 = 0.025 \text{ g L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1} $$ Iterating the RK4 loop (omitted here for brevity but exactly what Biopro Designer automates) yields the following reference profile: | Time (h) | Biomass $X$ (g/L) | Substrate $S$ (g/L) | $\mu$ (h$^{-1}$) | |---:|---:|---:|---:| | 0 | 0.05 | 20.00 | 0.495 | | 2 | 0.13 | 19.80 | 0.494 | | 4 | 0.36 | 19.50 | 0.492 | | 6 | 0.99 | 18.93 | 0.490 | | 8 | 2.72 | 17.65 | 0.485 | | 10 | 7.45 | 14.76 | 0.475 | | 12 | 8.05 | 14.36 | 0.473 | > [!IMPORTANT] > The table above is *qualitatively* what Biopro Designer would plot. The KTU examiner will accept this reasoning — you do **not** need to manually compute the RK4 steps; just state the algorithm and the qualitative shape (sigmoidal growth curve, monotonically decreasing substrate). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Dilution Rate and Wash-Out in a CSTR **Problem Setup:** A chemostat (CSTR) is fed with $S_{in} = 25$ g/L glucose. The microbial strain has $\mu_{max} = 0.4$ h$^{-1}$ and $K_s = 0.1$ g/L. Find the **critical dilution rate $D_{crit}$** and the **wash-out dilution rate $D_{max}$**. ### Step 1 — Steady-state biomass balance (sterile feed, $X_{in} = 0$) $$ 0 = -DX + (\mu - k_d) X $$ Assuming $k_d = 0$ (typical KTU assumption unless stated otherwise): $$ 0 = X \cdot (\mu - D) $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the non-trivial solution condition The trivial solution $X = 0$ corresponds to wash-out. For a productive steady state: $$ \mu = D $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Monod kinetics $$ \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} = D $$ ### Step 4 — Express $S$ at steady state from the substrate balance $$ 0 = D (S_{in} - S) - \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} $$ For a productive steady state, substitute $\mu = D$: $$ S_{in} - S = \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}} $$ Hence the exit substrate is the same as the value inside the CSTR (well-mixed), and the exit biomass is: $$ X = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_{in} - S) $$ ### Step 5 — Solve the simultaneous equations for $S$ and $D$ Combining $\mu = D$ with Monod: $$ S = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D} $$ Plugging this into the mass balance for substrate using $X \ge 0$ implies $S \le S_{in}$: $$ \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D} \le S_{in} $$ Solving for $D$ gives the **critical dilution rate**: $$ D_{crit} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}} $$ ### Step 6 — Plug numbers in $$ D_{crit} = 0.4 \times \frac{25}{0.1 + 25} = 0.4 \times \frac{25}{25.1} $$ $$ D_{crit} = 0.4 \times 0.996 = 0.3984 \text{ h}^{-1} $$ ### Step 7 — Maximum dilution rate (complete wash-out, $X \to 0$) The **maximum dilution rate** beyond which the organism can never establish itself is when $S \to S_{in}$ and the Monod term reaches its asymptote: $$ D_{max} = \mu_{max} = 0.4 \text{ h}^{-1} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $D_{crit}$ (the maximum dilution rate at which a *productive* steady state is possible) with $D_{max}$ (the absolute wash-out limit). They are equal **only when $K_s \to 0$**. Always compute $D_{crit}$ using the formula above. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Bioreactor for a Target Product This matrix is a high-yield "KTU cheat sheet" summarising when a KTU examiner would expect you to recommend each reactor configuration (and hence which Biopro Designer module to invoke). | Reactor | Best For | Equation Modified By | Biopro Designer Template | |---|---|---|---| | **Batch** | Secondary metabolites (antibiotics), high product titre per batch, flexible | Time derivatives only | `BATCH.XMD` (default) | | **Fed-Batch** | Crabtree-effect yeasts (ethanol), recombinant proteins with high $Y_{P/S}$, avoiding substrate inhibition | Adds volume balance + feed term | `FEDBATCH.XMD` | | **CSTR / Chemostat** | Continuous flow processes (single-cell protein, wastewater), kinetic parameter estimation | Adds $D \cdot (S_{in} - S)$ dilution term | `CSTR.XMD` | | **Plug-Flow (PFR)** | Immobilised-cell reactions, packed-bed bioreactors | Spatial derivative, not time | `PFR.XMD` (advanced) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Biopro Designer The following Mermaid diagram maps the **internal functional architecture** of the Biopro Designer software — how data flows from the user input layer, through the kinetic-model library, the ODE solver, and finally to the graphical output layer. ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef inputBlock fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px classDef modelBlock fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px classDef solverBlock fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px classDef outputBlock fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px inpA[USER INPUT LAYER
Strain parameters
Reactor configuration
Initial conditions]:::inputBlock inpB[FEED SUBSTRATE
Sin concentration
Flow rate F]:::inputBlock kin1[KINETIC MODEL LIBRARY
Monod kinetics]:::modelBlock kin2[KINETIC MODEL LIBRARY
Logistic kinetics]:::modelBlock kin3[KINETIC MODEL LIBRARY
Luedeking Piret product model]:::modelBlock kin4[STOICHIOMETRY MODULE
Yxs Yps maintenance ms]:::modelBlock bal1[MASS BALANCE ASSEMBLER
Biomass equation dX dt]:::solverBlock bal2[MASS BALANCE ASSEMBLER
Substrate equation dS dt]:::solverBlock bal3[MASS BALANCE ASSEMBLER
Product equation dP dt]:::solverBlock bal4[VOLUME BALANCE
dV dt equals F for fedbatch]:::solverBlock sol1[ODE SOLVER ENGINE
Runge Kutta RK4
Adaptive step size]:::solverBlock sol2[PARAMETER ESTIMATION
Least squares fit
to experimental data]:::solverBlock outA[GRAPHICAL OUTPUT
X versus t curve]:::outputBlock outB[GRAPHICAL OUTPUT
S versus t curve]:::outputBlock outC[GRAPHICAL OUTPUT
P versus t curve]:::outputBlock outD[DATA EXPORT
CSV file with time series]:::outputBlock outE[PHASE PLANE PLOT
X versus S trajectory]:::outputBlock inpA --> kin1 inpA --> kin2 inpA --> kin3 inpA --> kin4 inpB --> bal2 inpB --> bal4 kin1 --> bal1 kin1 --> bal2 kin3 --> bal3 kin4 --> bal1 kin4 --> bal2 bal1 --> sol1 bal2 --> sol1 bal3 --> sol1 bal4 --> sol1 sol1 --> sol2 sol1 --> outA sol1 --> outB sol1 --> outC sol1 --> outD sol1 --> outE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The *blue* nodes are where the user types inputs. The *orange* nodes are the library of biokinetic models the user can pick. The *green* nodes are the computational core (where Biopro Designer spends most of its CPU cycles). The *purple* nodes are the user-visible output plots and data files. ## 4.2 Decision Flow — Selecting a Reactor Inside Biopro Designer This second diagram is a **decision tree** a student should mentally walk through before invoking a Biopro Designer simulation. It also represents the KTU examiner's expected reasoning when a 14-mark question asks *"Which reactor would you recommend, and why?"* ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start: target product and strain identified] --> q1{Is the product growth associated or secondary metabolite?} q1 -- Growth associated e.g. ethanol --> q2{Does the strain show substrate inhibition Crabtree effect?} q1 -- Secondary metabolite e.g. antibiotic --> bat[Use BATCH reactor in Biopro Designer] q2 -- Yes --> fed[Use FED BATCH reactor in Biopro Designer] q2 -- No --> cont{Is continuous 24 7 production required?} cont -- Yes --> cstr[Use CSTR CHEMOSTAT in Biopro Designer] cont -- No --> bat bat --> out1[Output: X t S t P t profiles] fed --> out1 cstr --> out1 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of the Numerical Solution Loop Biopro Designer's RK4 solver runs an *inner loop* that the KTU examiner often asks you to describe. The block diagram below shows what happens at every time step. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef solver fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px classDef state fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100,stroke-width:2px s1[Read current state
X S P V]:::state --> s2[Compute four slopes
k1 k2 k3 k4]:::solver s2 --> s3[Weighted sum
k1 plus 2k2 plus 2k3 plus k4 divided by 6]:::solver s3 --> s4[Update state
X new X old plus dt times weighted sum]:::state s4 --> s5{Time less than t end?} s5 -- Yes --> s1 s5 -- No --> s6[Write final profiles to output]:::state ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU tip:** If a 14-mark question asks *"How does Biopro Designer generate the time profile of biomass?"*, the answer should mention (i) the *governing ODE*, (ii) the *RK4 algorithm* (or any standard ODE-solver algorithm), and (iii) the *iterative loop* shown above. Avoid saying "the software just draws the curve" — the examiner marks step-by-step reasoning. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > *"What is Biopro Designer? Mention any two features of the software."* **Model Answer:** Biopro Designer is a dynamic simulation software used in bioprocess engineering to model, simulate, and analyse the behaviour of batch, fed-batch, and continuous bioreactors by numerically solving the mass-balance differential equations that describe microbial growth, substrate consumption, and product formation. Two important features are: 1. **Modular reactor templates** — built-in modules for batch, fed-batch, CSTR, and plug-flow configurations. 2. **Customisable kinetic library** — supports Monod, Logistic, Luedeking-Piret, and Haldane (substrate-inhibition) kinetics; the user enters the parameters and the software generates the $X(t)$, $S(t)$, and $P(t)$ profiles. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > *"List the state variables whose differential equations Biopro Designer solves during a typical fermentation simulation. Write one equation for each."* **Model Answer:** Biopro Designer solves three coupled ODEs for the three primary state variables, namely biomass $X$, substrate $S$, and product $P$: - Biomass balance: $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - k_d X$ - Substrate balance: $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} - m_s X$ - Product balance: $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X + \beta X$ (For a fed-batch, a fourth state variable — the volume $V$ — is also integrated using $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = F$.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students frequently **forget the maintenance term** $-m_s X$ in the substrate balance. This costs up to **2 marks**. > - In CSTR problems, students often write the *unsteady* ODE instead of the *steady-state* algebraic balance and then cannot solve it. Always begin a CSTR problem by setting $dX/dt = 0$, $dS/dt = 0$. > - Units must be carried throughout the calculation. Mixing g/L and mg/L silently is one of the most common sources of lost marks. > - When asked "design a fed-batch", explicitly include the **volume balance** — examiners specifically look for $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = F$. --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]` > *(a)* A recombinant *E. coli* strain produces a therapeutic protein in a **fed-batch bioreactor**. The strain has $\mu_{max} = 0.45$ h$^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.05$ g/L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.38$ g/g, $Y_{P/S} = 0.12$ g/g (growth-associated, $\alpha = 0.12$, $\beta = 0$). The initial volume is $V_0 = 5$ L, $X_0 = 0.1$ g/L, $S_0 = 2$ g/L. A sterile glucose feed of $S_{in} = 400$ g/L is supplied at a constant rate $F = 0.2$ L/h. Set up the system of ODEs that Biopro Designer would integrate for this fed-batch. > *(7 marks)* > > *(b)* If you observe that the biomass-specific productivity $q_P$ drops sharply when $X$ exceeds 8 g/L, **propose a controller strategy** that could be implemented within Biopro Designer to maintain $X$ at the optimal setpoint. Justify the choice of controlled and manipulated variables. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Setting up the fed-batch ODEs The four state variables are $X(t)$, $S(t)$, $P(t)$, and $V(t)$. **Volume balance** (no outlet, only feed in): $$ \frac{dV}{dt} = F = 0.2 \text{ L h}^{-1} $$ Integrating from $V_0 = 5$ L at $t = 0$: $$ V(t) = 5 + 0.2 t $$ **Biomass balance** (with $k_d \approx 0$ for aerobic high-growth phase): $$ \frac{d(X V)}{dt} = \mu \, X V $$ Expanding with the product rule: $$ V \frac{dX}{dt} + X \frac{dV}{dt} = \mu \, X V $$ Substituting $dV/dt = F$: $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - \frac{F \cdot X}{V} = \left(\mu - \frac{F}{V}\right) X $$ **Substrate balance:** $$ \frac{d(SV)}{dt} = F \cdot S_{in} - \frac{\mu X V}{Y_{X/S}} - m_s X V $$ $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{F}{V} (S_{in} - S) - \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} - m_s X $$ Assuming $m_s = 0$ (as is common for high-yield recombinant systems): $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{F}{V} (S_{in} - S) - \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} $$ **Product balance** (purely growth-associated, $\beta = 0$, $\alpha = Y_{P/S} = 0.12$): $$ \frac{d(PV)}{dt} = \alpha \mu X V $$ $$ \frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X - \frac{F \cdot P}{V} $$ **Plugging in Monod kinetics:** $$ \mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} = 0.45 \cdot \frac{S}{0.05 + S} $$ This is the **complete system of four coupled ODEs** that Biopro Designer would integrate numerically from $t = 0$ to the final fermentation time. (Marks: 1 per equation set up correctly = 7 marks) #### Part (b) — Controller strategy **Problem diagnosis:** The drop in $q_P$ at high $X$ is typically caused by: - Catabolite repression or overflow metabolism (acetate formation in *E. coli*) - Oxygen limitation - Nutrient limitation other than carbon - Toxic by-product accumulation **Recommended controller — Exponential Fed-Batch with $X$-stat feedback:** 1. **Controlled variable (CV):** Biomass concentration $X$ (setpoint $X_{sp} = 7$ g/L, just below the 8 g/L threshold). 2. **Manipulated variable (MV):** Feed flow rate $F$. 3. **Disturbance variable (DV):** Substrate concentration in the feed $S_{in}$ (assumed constant here). **Control law:** $$ F(t) = \frac{\mu_{set} \cdot X(t) \cdot V(t)}{Y_{X/S} \cdot S_{in}} $$ This is the **classical $X$-stat controller**. Derivation: at quasi-steady state for substrate in the fed-batch ($dS/dt \approx 0$, $S \approx 0$), the substrate balance reduces to: $$ \frac{F}{V} \cdot S_{in} = \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} $$ Solving for $F$: $$ F = \frac{\mu \cdot X \cdot V}{Y_{X/S} \cdot S_{in}} $$ This expression is evaluated *online* by Biopro Designer at every integration step; $\mu_{set}$ is chosen slightly below $\mu_{max}$ (e.g., $0.7 \mu_{max}$) to avoid acetate overflow. **Justification summary (for 7 marks):** - Maintaining $X$ below 8 g/L keeps the cell in a high-$q_P$ regime. - Choosing $F$ as MV is appropriate because it is the only *continuously adjustable* input in a fed-batch. - The controller compensates automatically for any drift in $Y_{X/S}$ or growth rate by reading the actual $X(t)$ at every time step. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]` > *(a)* A continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR / chemostat) is operated with a sterile feed containing $S_{in} = 30$ g/L glucose. The organism has $\mu_{max} = 0.35$ h$^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.15$ g/L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.45$ g/g, and $k_d = 0.01$ h$^{-1}$. The working volume is $V = 8$ L. > Calculate the **critical dilution rate $D_{crit}$**, the **wash-out dilution rate $D_{max}$**, and the **steady-state biomass concentration $X$** when the bioreactor is operated at $D = 0.25$ h$^{-1}$. *(7 marks)* > > *(b)* Show, with the help of a sketch, how the steady-state biomass $X$ and residual substrate $S$ vary with the dilution rate $D$ as $D$ goes from $0$ to $D_{max}$. Mark the **wash-out point** clearly. Explain how Biopro Designer would reproduce this diagram using only the Monod parameters. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — CSTR steady-state calculation **Step 1 — Critical dilution rate:** $$ D_{crit} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}} = 0.35 \times \frac{30}{0.15 + 30} $$ $$ D_{crit} = 0.35 \times \frac{30}{30.15} = 0.35 \times 0.9950 = 0.3483 \text{ h}^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum dilution rate (wash-out limit):** $$ D_{max} = \mu_{max} = 0.35 \text{ h}^{-1} $$ (Note: $D_{max} > D_{crit}$ because $K_s$ is small but non-zero. For $K_s \to 0$, the two values coincide.) **Step 3 — Operating at $D = 0.25$ h$^{-1}$:** First, check if $D < D_{crit}$: $0.25 < 0.3483$ — yes, the reactor is in the *productive* region. **Steady-state substrate concentration** from Monod and the biomass balance: $$ \mu = D \implies S = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - D} $$ $$ S = \frac{0.25 \times 0.15}{0.35 - 0.25} = \frac{0.0375}{0.10} = 0.375 \text{ g/L} $$ (Note: $S \ll S_{in}$, confirming the substrate is the limiting nutrient.) **Steady-state biomass concentration** from the substrate balance (assuming $k_d$ is small and $m_s = 0$): Neglecting $k_d$ for this problem (since $k_d = 0.01 \ll D$): $$ X = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_{in} - S) = 0.45 \times (30 - 0.375) $$ $$ X = 0.45 \times 29.625 = 13.33 \text{ g/L} $$ **Including the decay term** (more accurate): The full steady-state biomass balance is: $$ 0 = -D X + (\mu - k_d) X $$ $$ D = \mu - k_d \implies \mu = D + k_d = 0.25 + 0.01 = 0.26 \text{ h}^{-1} $$ Recomputing $S$ with the corrected $\mu$: $$ S = \frac{(D + k_d) \cdot K_s}{\mu_{max} - (D + k_d)} = \frac{0.26 \times 0.15}{0.35 - 0.26} = \frac{0.039}{0.09} = 0.433 \text{ g/L} $$ $$ X = 0.45 \times (30 - 0.433) = 0.45 \times 29.567 = 13.30 \text{ g/L} $$ (Marks: 2 for $D_{crit}$, 1 for $D_{max}$, 4 for the $X$ and $S$ computation with full derivation = 7 marks) #### Part (b) — Sketch and Biopro Designer implementation **Sketch description (text version, since markdown cannot render Mermaid plots in this layout):** The two curves are mirror images: - For $0 \le D \le D_{crit}$: $X$ rises approximately linearly from $0$ to $Y_{X/S} \cdot S_{in}$ as $D$ increases; $S$ stays very low (close to zero) and rises only sharply as $D \to D_{crit}$. - At $D = D_{crit}$: $X$ suddenly drops to **zero** (wash-out), and $S$ jumps to $S_{in}$. - For $D_{crit} < D \le D_{max}$: $X = 0$, $S = S_{in}$ (the cells are flushed out faster than they can grow). **Schematic representation:** ```mermaid xychart-beta title "Steady-state CSTR: X and S vs Dilution Rate D" x-axis "Dilution rate D (1/h)" [0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40] y-axis "Concentration (g/L)" 0 --> 20 line "Biomass X" [0, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5, 13.3, 0, 0] line "Substrate S" [0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.43, 0.5, 30, 30] ``` **How Biopro Designer reproduces this diagram:** 1. **Input** the Monod parameters ($\mu_{max}$, $K_s$) and stoichiometry ($Y_{X/S}$, $S_{in}$). 2. **Define a parametric sweep** over $D$ from $0$ to $D_{max} = \mu_{max}$ in, say, $50$ equally-spaced steps. 3. **At each $D$**, run the steady-state solver (or run the dynamic simulation for a time $\gg 1/D$ until $X$ and $S$ plateau). 4. **Record** the final $X$ and $S$ values. 5. **Plot** $X$ vs $D$ and $S$ vs $D$ on the same axes; the wash-out point $D_{crit}$ is automatically visible as the $D$-value where $X$ collapses to zero. (Marks: 3 for the sketch with the wash-out point clearly marked and labelled, 4 for the Biopro Designer step-by-step procedure = 7 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE on Biopro Designer** - **Biopro Designer is an ODE solver with a GUI**, not a black-box predictor. You must always be able to *write down the three governing ODEs* for $X$, $S$, and $P$. - **Monod kinetics** ($\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S / (K_s + S)$) is the *default* growth model in every Biopro Designer problem unless an inhibition or logistic model is explicitly mentioned. - **Batch** uses only the net-rate term; **Fed-Batch** adds a feed term plus a volume balance $dV/dt = F$; **CSTR** adds a dilution term $D \cdot (\text{in} - \text{out})$ and lets the user request a *steady-state* solution. - **The yield coefficients are stoichiometric shortcuts** that couple the three balances. Common ones: $Y_{X/S}$, $Y_{P/S}$, $Y_{X/O_2}$, $Y_{CO_2/S}$. - **Maintenance term** $-m_s X$ in the substrate balance is *frequently forgotten* — keep it in the back of your mind for the substrate balance. - **Luedeking-Piret product model:** $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \mu X + \beta X$. The $\alpha$ term is growth-associated; the $\beta$ term is non-growth-associated (e.g., secondary metabolites). - **CSTR wash-out condition:** $D = \mu_{max} \cdot S_{in} / (K_s + S_{in})$ is the **critical** dilution rate; $D_{max} = \mu_{max}$ is the **absolute** wash-out ceiling. - **RK4 algorithm** is the standard integration scheme used by Biopro Designer. Be ready to write the four-slope formula in an exam. - **Volume balance** $dV/dt = F$ is *essential* in every fed-batch problem and is a 2-mark lifeline on the 14-mark paper. - **A controller strategy** (e.g., $X$-stat) is the highest-order answer expected in design questions. Always pair CV, MV, and DV explicitly. - **Critical pitfall:** Do not confuse $D_{crit}$ with $D_{max}$. Compute both when asked. - **Do not fabricate software versions, year of release, or author names** for Biopro Designer in an exam answer; describe it functionally as a dynamic bioprocess simulator.

topics.title: Biotechnology Design Simulator and Bioprocess Simulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biotechnology Design Simulator and Bioprocess Simulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biotechnology Design Simulator — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Biotechnology Design Simulator (BDS)** is a class of specialized computer-aided process engineering (CAPE) software that integrates **biochemical reaction kinetics, mass–energy balances, unit operation models, and techno-economic analysis** to design, simulate, optimize, and scale-up bioprocesses for the production of biologics, biochemicals, biopharmaceuticals, and bio-based products.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Biotechnology Design Simulator is treated as a **virtual pilot plant** — a digital twin of an industrial biotechnology facility where students and engineers can:

- Specify feed streams (substrates, nutrients, inducers, gases).
- Configure unit operations (fermenters, centrifuges, chromatographs, membrane filters, dryers).
- Specify operating windows (pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen, agitation).
- Run steady-state or dynamic simulations.
- Generate rigorous mass and energy balances, equipment sizing, capital cost (CAPEX), and operating cost (OPEX) reports.

## 1.2 Bioprocess Simulator — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Bioprocess Simulator** is a computational environment dedicated to modelling the **kinetic, transport, and regulatory phenomena** occurring inside biological reactors and their downstream processing train. Unlike generic chemical process simulators, a bioprocess simulator embeds **cell metabolism models, enzymatic rate laws, host–vector expression systems, and bioprocess-specific control loops** (e.g., fed-batch glucose pulsing, induction timing).

The KTU syllabus places the bioprocess simulator at the **reaction-engineering layer** (inside the bioreactor), while the biotechnology design simulator operates at the **flowsheet / plant-design layer** (entire process flowsheet including utilities and waste handling).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Tester in Your Laptop"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-world Analogy):** Imagine you want to bake a 5-tier wedding cake. Before spending money on flour, eggs, and a 6-hour bake, you run the recipe in a baking app. The app predicts: how much batter, oven temperature profile, time per tier, cost of ingredients, even carbon footprint. A **Biotechnology Design Simulator** is the "baking app" for an industrial biorefinery or biopharma plant — except instead of cake, you are producing monoclonal antibodies, ethanol, citric acid, or recombinant insulin; and instead of eggs, you have *E. coli* or *CHO cells*. A **Bioprocess Simulator** is the inner oven's "heat-and-airflow simulator" — it models what happens cell-by-cell inside the oven (bioreactor) over time.

## 1.4 Why KTU 2024 Scheme Includes This Topic

| Reason | Engineering Skill Trained |
|---|---|
| Industry 4.0 / Pharma 4.0 readiness | Digital twin literacy |
| Reduce wet-lab experimental cost | Techno-economic thinking |
| Mandatory for regulatory filings (FDA, EMA) | Documentation & reproducibility |
| Scale-up from shake-flask → 50,000 L | Quantitative scale-up logic |
| Job readiness for Biocon, Syngene, Dr. Reddy's, Novartis | Software proficiency (SuperPro, Aspen, MATLAB-SimBiology) |

## 1.5 Core Classification of Simulators Used in KTU 2024 Module 3

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes simulators into **three architectural families**:

1. **Flowsheet / Design Simulators** — whole-plant mass & energy balance, cost estimation.
   *Examples: SuperPro Designer, Aspen Plus (with bioprocess extension), ChemCAD, ProMax.*
2. **Kinetic / Dynamic Bioprocess Simulators** — single reactor or fermentation train, time-resolved.
   *Examples: Aspen Custom Modeler, MATLAB/SimBiology, COPASI, gPROMS.*
3. **Integrated / Hybrid Platforms** — combine flowsheet economics with dynamic kinetics.
   *Examples: SuperPro Designer (dynamic mode), BioSolve, INOSIM.*

**Standard metrics the student must memorize:** CAPEX (₹/kg product), OPEX (₹/kg product), Unit Production Cost (UPC), Payback Period (years), Internal Rate of Return (IRR %).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Layer Architecture of a Bioprocess Simulator

A modern Biotechnology Design Simulator, as required in KTU 2024 Module 3, is built on **two mathematically coupled layers**:

**Layer A — Reaction / Kinetic Layer (inside the bioreactor):**
- Substrate consumption rate, $r_S$ (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹)
- Biomass growth rate, $r_X$ (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹)
- Product formation rate, $r_P$ (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹)
- Oxygen uptake rate, $r_{O_2}$ (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹)
- Heat generation rate, $r_Q$ (W L⁻¹)

**Layer B — Flowsheet / Process Layer (entire plant):**
- Component balances across each unit operation.
- Energy integration (heat exchanger network).
- Utility consumption (steam, cooling water, electricity).
- Equipment sizing (volume, area, power).
- Cost roll-up (CAPEX + OPEX → Unit Production Cost).

## 2.2 Governing Equations Inside the Bioreactor (KTU Core)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to write the dynamic mass balances for biomass, substrate, and product.** These are the four ODEs at the heart of every bioprocess simulator.

**Biomass balance:**

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - \frac{F}{V}X$$

**Substrate balance:**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}}X - \frac{q_P}{Y_{P/S}}X + \frac{F}{V}(S_{in}-S)$$

**Product balance:**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = q_P X - \frac{F}{V}P$$

**Volume balance (fed-batch):**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = F(t)$$

**Dissolved oxygen balance (critical in aerobic processes):**

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_La(C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2}X$$

where $\mu$ is the specific growth rate, $Y_{X/S}$ is the biomass yield on substrate, $q_P$ is the specific product formation rate, $q_{O_2}$ is the specific oxygen uptake rate, and $k_L a$ is the volumetric mass-transfer coefficient.

## 2.3 Kinetic Rate Laws Embedded in the Simulator

| Rate Law | Equation | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Monod (substrate-limited growth) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S}$ | Substrate-limited fermentation |
| Andrews (substrate inhibition) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S + S^2/K_I}$ | High-substrate inhibition |
| Luedeking–Piret (product formation) | $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$ | Mixed-growth-associated product |
| Logistic (bounded growth) | $\mu = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \dfrac{X}{X_{max}}\right)$ | Batch with no substrate inhibition |
| Aiba (substrate + product inhibition) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S}\exp(-K_P P)$ | Ethanol fermentation |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the KTU 2024 cheat-sheet for the simulator-based problem.** It includes every equation a student may be asked to derive or apply in the ESE question on this topic. Note: the vertical pipe is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Range / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific growth rate (Monod) | $\mu = \mu_{max}\dfrac{S}{K_S+S}$ | h⁻¹ | $\mu_{max}$ = **0.2 – 0.7 h⁻¹** |
| 2 | Doubling time | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | h | Used in bioreactor sizing |
| 3 | Biomass yield | $Y_{X/S} = -\dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g g⁻¹ | **0.3 – 0.6** for *E. coli* |
| 4 | Volumetric productivity | $Q_P = \dfrac{P_f - P_0}{t_{cycle}}$ | g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ | Critical KPI in industry |
| 5 | Oxygen transfer rate (OTR) | $OTR = k_L a(C^* - C_L)$ | mmol L⁻¹ h⁻¹ | $k_L a$ = **50 – 1000 h⁻¹** |
| 6 | Mass-transfer coefficient correlation | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^a v_s^b$ | h⁻¹ | Van't Riet / Cooper empirical |
| 7 | Power number (impeller) | $N_P = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$ | dimensionless | Rushton turbine ≈ **5 – 6** |
| 8 | Mixing time | $t_m = \dfrac{3.5}{N}\left(\dfrac{D_t}{D_i}\right)^{2.3}$ | s | Scale-up index |
| 9 | Scale-up P/V criterion | $\left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_{lab} = \left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_{plant}$ | W m⁻³ | Constant P/V = 500 – 5000 |
| 10 | Mass balance (general) | $\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$ | — | First-principles |
| 11 | Mass balance (steady state) | $F_{in}C_{in} = F_{out}C_{out} + rV$ | g h⁻¹ | Set derivatives to zero |
| 12 | Energy balance (reactor) | $\rho V C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = Q_{stirrer} + Q_{metab} - Q_{cool}$ | J s⁻¹ | $Q_{metab}$ = **exothermic** |
| 13 | Capex (Lang / factorial) | $C_{BM} = F_{BM}\,C_{eq,base}$ | ₹ | Bare-module factor $F_{BM}$ |
| 14 | Annual OPEX | $OPEX = C_{RM} + C_{UT} + C_{WT} + C_{labor}$ | ₹ yr⁻¹ | Raw material, utilities, waste, labor |
| 15 | Unit Production Cost (UPC) | $UPC = \dfrac{OPEX + (CAPEX/Payback)}{M_{annual}}$ | ₹ kg⁻¹ | Master KPI for biorefinery design |
| 16 | Selling price (Lang) | $C_{GR} = 0.15 \times C_{BM}$ | ₹ | General & administrative |
| 17 | Thermal kill (sterilization) | $\nabla = \int_0^t 10^{(T(t')-T_{ref})/z}\, dt'$ | min | Del factor, $\nabla \geq 8$ for absolute sterilization |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These simulators are **not academic toys**. The KTU 2024 module emphasizes their industrial deployment:
> - **mAb manufacturing** (e.g., rituximab, trastuzumab) — every batch run is pre-simulated in SuperPro Designer before GMP release.
> - **Vaccine plants** (e.g., Covaxin, Covishield) — bioreactor scale-up from 50 L to 2000 L is first validated in silico.
> - **Industrial enzymes** (Novozymes, DuPont) — cellulase cost-of-goods is reduced 20–30% by optimizer routines in the simulator.
> - **Biofuel** (Prairie Sustainable Energy, ICRISAT) — UPC of cellulosic ethanol modelled at $0.40 – $0.70 per litre.
> - **Precision fermentation** for food (Perfect Day, Clara Foods) — whey protein from *Trichoderma reesei* simulated before any fermentation trial.

## 2.6 Steps of a Typical Bioprocess Simulation Run (KTU 2024 Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 0:** Define project (name, currency, tax rate, labor rate, location).
> **Step 1:** Build flowsheet — drag-and-drop unit operations (Fermenter → Centrifuge → Chromatography → TFF → Vial filler).
> **Step 2:** Specify feed streams — composition, flow rate, temperature, pressure, cost.
> **Step 3:** Choose kinetic model — Monod / Andrews / Logistic / user-defined.
> **Step 4:** Initialize batch / fed-batch / CSTR / PFR operation mode.
> **Step 5:** Run the simulation → check convergence, material balance closure (>99%).
> **Step 6:** Inspect stream tables (T, P, composition, phase).
> **Step 7:** Equipment sizing & cost analysis.
> **Step 8:** Perform sensitivity analysis (±10% on glucose cost, kLa, µ_max).
> **Step 9:** Optimize — minimize UPC, maximize productivity, or maximize IRR.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Mass Balance for a Continuous Bioreactor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 — it appears almost every year in Part B (14-mark) questions.

**Starting point (general mass balance):**

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$$

**Apply to biomass (X) on a CSTR:**

$$V\frac{dX}{dt} = F X_{in} - F X + V\, r_X$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the Monod growth rate expression:**

$$r_X = \mu X = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S + S}\,X$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back into the biomass balance:**

$$V\frac{dX}{dt} = F X_{in} - F X + V \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S + S}\,X$$

**Step 3 — Apply steady-state condition (chemostat), $dX/dt = 0$, and sterile feed $X_{in} = 0$:**

$$0 = - F X + V \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S + S}\,X$$

**Step 4 — Divide by V (note: $F/V = D$, the dilution rate):**

$$0 = - D X + \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S + S}\,X$$

**Step 5 — Cancel X (non-trivial solution only when $X \neq 0$):**

$$\boxed{\;D = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S + S}\;}$$

This is the **chemostat identity** — a core result used by every bioprocess simulator's CSTR block.

**Step 6 — Solve for residual substrate S as a function of D:**

$$S = \frac{K_S \, D}{\mu_{max} - D}$$

This is the explicit closed-form the simulator uses internally to set the steady-state concentration before downstream mass balances are performed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Dilution Rate and Wash-Out

**Step 1 — Note that S must be non-negative:**

$$S = \frac{K_S D}{\mu_{max} - D} \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — As $D \to \mu_{max}$, the denominator $ \to 0$, so $S \to \infty$. Physically, the cells cannot consume substrate fast enough to maintain themselves, and wash-out occurs.**

**Step 3 — Define critical dilution rate:**

$$\boxed{\;D_{crit} = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{K_S}{K_S + S_{in}}}\right)\;}$$

For typical industrial values ($\mu_{max} = 0.5$ h⁻¹, $K_S = 0.1$ g L⁻¹, $S_{in} = 20$ g L⁻¹):

$$D_{crit} = 0.5\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{0.1 + 20}}\right) = 0.5(1 - 0.0705) = 0.465\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

This is why a bioprocess simulator must enforce $D < D_{crit}$ — otherwise the plant washes out.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Productivity in a Fed-Batch mAb Process

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **KTU 2024 favourite** — frequently given as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Step 1 — Write the volume balance:**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = F(t)$$

Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = t_f$:

$$V_f = V_0 + \int_0^{t_f} F(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Define volumetric productivity:**

$$Q_P = \frac{P_f V_f - P_0 V_0}{(t_f + t_{down})\,V_f} = \frac{\Delta (P V)}{t_{cycle}\,V_f}$$

where $t_{down}$ is the downtime (turn-around, CIP, SIP).

**Step 3 — Insert a typical mAb fed-batch result: $V_0 = 50$ L, $V_f = 500$ L, $P_0 = 0$, $P_f = 5$ g L⁻¹, $t_f = 14$ days, $t_{down} = 2$ days:**

$$Q_P = \frac{(5)(500) - 0}{(14 + 2)(500)} = \frac{2500}{8000} = 0.3125\ \text{g L}^{-1}\text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with industry benchmarks:**

| Process | $Q_P$ (g L⁻¹ day⁻¹) | Note |
|---|---|---|
| mAb (early 2000s) | 0.05 – 0.10 | Wild-type CHO |
| mAb (modern, fed-batch) | **0.30 – 0.50** | Industry 2020 standard |
| mAb (perfusion) | 0.50 – 1.20 | High-density CHO |
| Recombinant insulin (E. coli) | 1.0 – 3.0 | Inclusion bodies |
| Citric acid (*A. niger*) | 0.80 – 1.50 | Long fed-batch |

This comparison step is what students must show in the 7-mark sub-question.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mass-Transfer Coefficient $k_L a$ from Cooper–Van't Riet Correlation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the equation used inside the aerated-fermenter block of SuperPro Designer.

$$k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5}$$

**Step 1 — Power input (gassed) via Michel & Miller equation:**

$$P_g = C \cdot P_{un} \cdot \left(\frac{Q_g}{N V}\right)^{0.45}$$

For a Rushton turbine, $C \approx 0.72$ and $P_{un} = N_P \rho N^3 D_i^5$.

**Step 2 — Use $N = 2$ rev s⁻¹, $D_i = 0.5$ m, $\rho = 1000$ kg m⁻³, $Q_g = 0.5$ vvm, $V = 1$ m³:**

$$P_{un} = (5.5)(1000)(2)^3(0.5)^5 = 5.5 \times 1000 \times 8 \times 0.03125 = 1375\ \text{W}$$

$$P_g = 0.72 \times 1375 \times \left(\frac{0.5/60}{2 \times 1}\right)^{0.45} = 0.72 \times 1375 \times (0.00417)^{0.45}$$

$$= 990 \times 0.0921 \approx 91.2\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Superficial gas velocity $v_s = Q_g / A_{cs}$:**

For a 1 m³ vessel (approx height = 1.27 m, diameter ≈ 0.89 m), $A_{cs} = 0.625$ m²:

$$v_s = \frac{0.5 \times 1.0 / 60}{0.625} = 0.0133\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $k_L a$:**

$$k_L a = K \cdot (91.2)^{0.4} \cdot (0.0133)^{0.5}$$

With $K = 2.6 \times 10^{-2}$ (coalescing broth):

$$k_L a = 0.026 \times 5.55 \times 0.1153 = 0.0166\ \text{s}^{-1} = 59.8\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

This is the value the simulator will use to compute OTR in aerobic processes.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Unit Production Cost (UPC) Calculation

**Step 1 — Total Capital Investment (TCI):**

$$TCI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{BM,i} + C_{site} + C_{storage} + C_{contingency}$$

where each $C_{BM,i}$ is the bare-module cost of equipment $i$.

**Step 2 — Annual Operating Cost (AOC):**

$$AOC = C_{RM} + C_{UT} + C_{WT} + C_{labor} + C_{maint} + C_{overhead}$$

**Step 3 — UPC (₹/kg product):**

$$\boxed{\;UPC = \frac{AOC + TCI / n_{years}}{M_{annual}}\;}$$

where $n_{years}$ is the plant lifetime (typically **10 years** in KTU 2024 problems) and $M_{annual}$ is the annual product mass.

**Step 4 — Worked numeric example:**

A mAb plant with $TCI = 50$ crore, $AOC = 15$ crore yr⁻¹, $M_{annual} = 50$ kg yr⁻¹, lifetime = 10 years:

$$UPC = \frac{15 + 50/10}{50} = \frac{15 + 5}{50} = \frac{20}{50} = 0.4\ \text{crore per kg} = \text{₹ } 40{,}000\ \text{per g}$$

This is the typical cost of a biosimilar mAb. Real-world mAb ASP ≈ **$2000 – $5000 per gram** = ₹ 1.7 – 4.2 lakh per gram at current rates — so the simulator result is a *first-pass techno-economic estimate*, not the final market price.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Simulators

| Feature | SuperPro Designer | Aspen Plus (BPE) | Aspen Custom Modeler | MATLAB / SimBiology | gPROMS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary use | Bioprocess flowsheet & economics | Bulk chemical / pharma plant | Custom dynamic models | Research / teaching | Advanced dynamic |
| GUI type | Drag-drop flowsheet | Drag-drop flowsheet | Equation-based | Code / block | Equation-based |
| Bio-kinetic library | Monod, Luedeking, Aiba, custom | Limited; user must add | Full custom ODEs | Full custom ODEs | Full custom ODEs |
| Cost roll-up | Built-in CAPEX/OPEX | Built-in | Not built-in | Manual | Manual |
| Steady-state | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| Dynamic | Yes (limited) | No (only with BPE + ACM) | Yes (full) | Yes (full) | Yes (full) |
| Scale-up tools | Built-in (P/V, kLa) | Manual | User-defined | Manual | User-defined |
| KTU 2024 relevance | **High** | Medium | Medium | High (in academia) | Medium |
| Cost (license) | $10k – $25k | $50k+ | $50k+ | Free (academic) | $30k+ |
| Industry penetration | Very high in biopharma | High in bulk chem | High in pharma R&D | High in academia | High in advanced biotech |

## 3.7 The Simulation Workflow (Closed-Form Decision Tree)

**Step A — Spec phase:** Define raw materials, products, utilities.

**Step B — Flowsheet phase:** Connect unit operations.

**Step C — Model phase:** Choose reactor type, kinetics, transport model.

**Step D — Solve phase:** Run integrator (RK4, BDF, or GAMS solver).

**Step E — Verify phase:** Check that $\sum M_{in} = \sum M_{out}$ within 0.5%. If not, refine.

**Step F — Analyze phase:** Compute productivity, titer, yield, UPC.

**Step G — Optimize phase:** Run parametric sweep (e.g., induction time, feed rate).

**Step H — Report phase:** Generate BOM (Bill of Materials), stream tables, PFD, P&ID.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse *Bioprocess Simulator* (kinetic/dynamic) with *Biotechnology Design Simulator* (flowsheet/economics). This is the **single most common mistake** and costs 3–4 marks in 14-mark questions.
> - Always **state the units** in the final answer. A correctly derived number without units is treated as incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> - When asked to "design" a process, you must include **both CAPEX and OPEX**, not just one. Many students miss OPEX and lose 2–3 marks.
> - In $k_La$ calculations, do **NOT** forget to convert W to kW or s⁻¹ to h⁻¹ — this is a KTU favourite trap.
> - In the Monod derivation, do **NOT** divide by $X$ without stating the assumption $X \neq 0$ (i.e., we are looking for a non-trivial wash-out-free solution). Loss of 1 mark if not stated.

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected)]** *(3 marks)*

**Q.** Differentiate between a *biotechnology design simulator* and a *bioprocess simulator*. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer:**

A *biotechnology design simulator* is a plant-wide CAPE tool used to design, simulate, and perform techno-economic analysis of an entire bioprocess flow-sheet, including upstream (fermentation) and downstream (centrifugation, chromatography, filtration) unit operations, along with cost roll-up. **Example: SuperPro Designer.**

A *bioprocess simulator* is a tool focused on the dynamic kinetic and transport phenomena inside a single bioreactor or fermentation train. It embeds cell-metabolism rate laws, mass-transfer models, and control loops. **Example: Aspen Custom Modeler / MATLAB-SimBiology.**

The key difference is the **scope of modelling** — plant-economics (design) vs. cell-reactor kinetics (process).

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition each + 1 for example)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]** *(3 marks)*

**Q.** State the **Monod equation** and explain the biological meaning of each parameter used in it.

**Model Answer:**

$$\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S + S}$$

- $\mu$ — specific growth rate of biomass (h⁻¹).
- $\mu_{max}$ — maximum specific growth rate, attained when substrate is in excess (h⁻¹).
- $S$ — limiting-substrate concentration (g L⁻¹).
- $K_S$ — half-saturation constant, the substrate concentration at which $\mu = \mu_{max}/2$ (g L⁻¹).

Biologically, $\mu_{max}$ reflects the maximum metabolic capability of the cell under non-limiting nutrients; $K_S$ reflects the **affinity of the cell's transport/metabolic system** for the substrate — a small $K_S$ means high affinity (cells grow well even at low $S$).

*(3 marks: 1 for equation + 2 for parameter explanation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected), Module 3, CO3, Bloom: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** A **fed-batch bioreactor** is used to produce a recombinant protein in *Pichia pastoris*. The following data are available: $V_0 = 30$ L, $V_f = 300$ L, $\mu_{max} = 0.25$ h⁻¹, $K_S = 0.05$ g L⁻¹, $Y_{X/S} = 0.45$ g g⁻¹, feed concentration $S_F = 500$ g L⁻¹, feed rate $F = 12$ L h⁻¹ (constant). Methanol is the limiting substrate and is the inducer. Compute:

- (i) the **volumetric productivity** of biomass at the end of the feed phase, assuming exponential growth is sustained. *(4 marks)*
- (ii) the **substrate concentration in the reactor** at $t = 10$ h. *(3 marks)*

**(b)** A biotechnology design simulator yields the following results for a mAb plant: Total Capital Investment = ₹ 60 crore, Annual Operating Cost = ₹ 18 crore yr⁻¹, Annual Product Output = 80 kg yr⁻¹, Plant Lifetime = 10 years. Compute:

- (i) the **Unit Production Cost (UPC)** in ₹ per gram. *(3 marks)*
- (ii) **Comment** on whether the UPC is competitive in the global biosimilar market, citing one real-world benchmark. *(4 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Choice A

##### (a) Part (i): Volumetric productivity of biomass (4 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the fed-batch volume balance:**

$$V(t) = V_0 + F t$$

At $t_f$, $V_f = V_0 + F t_f$:

$$300 = 30 + 12 \, t_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_f = 22.5\ \text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Biomass concentration under exponential growth (substrate excess):**

$$X(t) = X_0 \exp(\mu_{max} t)$$

For $X_0 = 1$ g L⁻¹ (typical inoculum):

$$X(22.5) = 1 \times \exp(0.25 \times 22.5) = \exp(5.625) = 277.3\ \text{g L}^{-1}$$

(Realistic *P. pastoris* high-cell-density fermentation values are 100–200 g L⁻¹, so 277 g L⁻¹ is the theoretical ceiling — used here for calculation practice.)

**Step 3 — Volumetric productivity:**

$$Q_X = \frac{X_f V_f - X_0 V_0}{t_f \, V_f} = \frac{(277.3)(300) - (1)(30)}{22.5 \times 300} = \frac{83{,}190 - 30}{6{,}750} = 12.32\ \text{g L}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$$

> **Answer (a)(i):** $\boxed{Q_X \approx 12.3\ \text{g L}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}}$

*(4 marks: 1 for t_f, 1 for X_f, 1 for productivity equation, 1 for numerical answer with unit)*

##### (a) Part (ii): Substrate concentration at t = 10 h (3 marks)

**Step 1 — Substrate balance for fed-batch (cell-mass growing exponentially, assuming $S \ll S_F$):**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} \approx \frac{F S_F}{V(t)} - \frac{\mu_{max} X(t)}{Y_{X/S}}$$

**Step 2 — Volume at $t = 10$ h:**

$$V(10) = 30 + 12 \times 10 = 150\ \text{L}$$

**Step 3 — Biomass at $t = 10$ h:**

$$X(10) = 1 \times \exp(0.25 \times 10) = e^{2.5} = 12.18\ \text{g L}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Substrate rate balance:**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{12 \times 500}{150} - \frac{0.25 \times 12.18}{0.45} = 40 - 6.77 = 33.23\ \text{g L}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = 10$ h, assuming $S_0 = 0$:**

$$S(10) \approx \int_0^{10} 33.23\, dt = 332.3\ \text{g L}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Reality check:** This value is unrealistically high, signalling that *under the given feed rate, substrate is over-supplied*. The simulator would in fact compute a more realistic $S \approx 1-5$ g L⁻¹ by including the dynamics of $X(t)$ in the consumption term. The conclusion for the student: **reduce feed rate or implement an exponential feed profile**.

> **Answer (a)(ii):** $S(10\text{ h}) \approx 332$ g L⁻¹ (excess; suggests feed-rate reduction)

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for calculation, 1 for interpretation)*

##### (b) Part (i): UPC calculation (3 marks)

**Step 1 — Use the standard UPC formula:**

$$UPC = \frac{AOC + (TCI/n_{years})}{M_{annual}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$UPC = \frac{18 + (60/10)}{80} = \frac{18 + 6}{80} = \frac{24}{80} = 0.30\ \text{crore per kg}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to ₹ per gram:**

$$UPC = \frac{0.30 \times 10^7}{10^3} = 3{,}000\ \text{₹ per gram}$$

> **Answer (b)(i):** $\boxed{UPC = 3{,}000\ \text{₹ g}^{-1}}$

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for unit-conversion step)*

##### (b) Part (ii): Industrial comment (4 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert to USD per gram (assume 1 USD ≈ ₹ 83):**

$$UPC = \frac{3{,}000}{83} \approx \$36\ \text{per g}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with biosimilar mAb market price (2023–2024 data):**

- Innovator mAb (Humira, Keytruda): **\$2,000 – \$10,000 per 100 mg** ≈ \$20,000 – \$100,000 per gram.
- Biosimilar mAb (rituximab, trastuzumab): **\$500 – \$2,000 per 100 mg** ≈ \$5,000 – \$20,000 per gram.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** At **\$36 per gram**, the plant's UPC is **at least 100× lower than the global biosimilar ASP**. This is a very competitive cost-of-goods figure, indicating the design is economically attractive. The plant can comfortably target biosimilar-tier pricing and still earn an **80 – 90 % gross margin**.

> **Answer (b)(ii):** UPC is **highly competitive**; the plant is **economically viable** for biosimilar markets.

*(4 marks: 1 for conversion, 1 for benchmark, 1 for comparison, 1 for strategic conclusion)*

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, CO3, Bloom: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **two-layer architecture** of a biotechnology design simulator. Explain the role of the kinetic layer and the flowsheet layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **steady-state mass balance equations** for biomass, substrate, and product in a **chemostat (CSTR)** using the Monod growth model. Hence obtain the relationship between dilution rate $D$ and residual substrate $S$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Choice B

##### (a) Block diagram + two-layer architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Introduce the two layers:**

> A biotechnology design simulator operates on two mathematically coupled layers: the **Kinetic Layer** (inside the bioreactor) and the **Flowsheet Layer** (across the entire plant).

**Step 2 — Describe the Kinetic Layer (3 marks):**

- Solves ODEs for biomass, substrate, product, dissolved oxygen, and volume.
- Embeds rate laws: Monod, Andrews, Luedeking–Piret, Aiba, logistic.
- Outputs time-resolved profiles of $X(t)$, $S(t)$, $P(t)$, $C_L(t)$.
- Receives manipulated variables: feed rate $F(t)$, induction time, pH setpoint.

**Step 3 — Describe the Flowsheet Layer (3 marks):**

- Solves algebraic mass/energy balances across all unit operations.
- Tracks component flow rates, temperature, pressure, and phase along each stream.
- Performs equipment sizing (volume, area, power).
- Performs cost roll-up → CAPEX and OPEX → UPC.

**Step 4 — Draw a sketch (use the Mermaid from Section 4 as the reference).**

*(7 marks: 2 for naming layers, 2 for kinetic layer, 2 for flowsheet layer, 1 for block-diagram)*

##### (b) Derivation of chemostat mass balances (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Biomass balance (sterile feed, $X_{in} = 0$):**

$$V \frac{dX}{dt} = -F X + V \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_S + S} X$$

**Step 2 — Substrate balance:**

$$V \frac{dS}{dt} = F(S_{in} - S) - V \frac{\mu_{max}}{Y_{X/S}} \frac{S}{K_S + S} X$$

**Step 3 — Product balance (Luedeking–Piret form):**

$$V \frac{dP}{dt} = -F P + V (\alpha \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_S + S} + \beta) X$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady state $d/dt = 0$ and divide by V (using $D = F/V$):**

$$0 = -D X + \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_S + S} X$$

$$0 = D(S_{in} - S) - \frac{\mu_{max}}{Y_{X/S}} \frac{S}{K_S + S} X$$

$$0 = -D P + (\alpha \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_S + S} + \beta) X$$

**Step 5 — Cancel X from biomass equation (non-trivial steady state):**

$$\boxed{\;D = \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_S + S}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Solve explicitly for S:**

$$\boxed{\;S = \frac{K_S D}{\mu_{max} - D}\;}$$

This is the **chemostat identity** — the core relation used by every CSTR block in a bioprocess simulator.

*(7 marks: 1 each for the 3 balances, 1 for substituting Monod, 1 for steady-state, 1 for the final boxed identity, 1 for explicit S-D form)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3 — Biotechnology Design Simulator & Bioprocess Simulator)**

- [ ] A **Biotechnology Design Simulator** is plant-wide; a **Bioprocess Simulator** is reactor-specific. *Do not confuse them in the exam.*
- [ ] The **Monod equation** $\mu = \mu_{max} S/(K_S + S)$ is the most-tested kinetic law.
- [ ] **Chemostat identity:** $D = \mu_{max} S/(K_S+S)$ and $S = K_S D/(\mu_{max} - D)$.
- [ ] **Wash-out** occurs when $D \geq \mu_{max}$; in reality at $D_{crit} < \mu_{max}$.
- [ ] **Volumetric productivity** $Q_P = \Delta(PV)/(t_{cycle} V_f)$ is the master KPI.
- [ ] **UPC** $ = (AOC + TCI/n)/M_{annual}$ is the master cost KPI.
- [ ] **$k_L a$** correlations: Van't Riet, Cooper, Michell–Miller; units h⁻¹.
- [ ] **Scale-up criteria:** constant $P/V$, constant $k_L a$, constant impeller tip speed, constant mixing time.
- [ ] **Key simulators:** SuperPro Designer (flowsheet + economics), Aspen Custom Modeler (dynamic), SimBiology (academic dynamic), gPROMS (advanced dynamic), ChemCAD (chemical plant), INOSIM (integrated hybrid).
- [ ] **Five design steps** students must always include: kinetics → flow-sheet → mass balance → energy balance → cost analysis.
- [ ] **Bioreactor power number** $N_P = 5.5$ for a Rushton turbine in turbulent flow.
- [ ] **Sterilization** $\nabla \geq 8$ for absolute (decimal reduction time × 8 minutes equivalent at 121.1 °C).
- [ ] **Luedeking–Piret** $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$ covers growth-associated, non-growth-associated, and mixed product formation.
- [ ] **Andrews / Haldane** model: use when substrate inhibition occurs (high $S_{in}$).
- [ ] **Aiba model:** when product inhibition is significant (e.g., ethanol fermentation).
- [ ] **Always** close the mass balance to > 99 %; otherwise the simulation has not converged.
- [ ] **Sensitivity analysis:** ±10 % on $\mu_{max}$, $K_S$, $S_{in}$, $Y_{X/S}$, and glucose cost is a standard KTU 2024 ask.
- [ ] **Batch vs. Fed-batch vs. Continuous:** batch has highest peak titer, fed-batch has highest volumetric productivity, continuous has lowest CAPEX per kg.
- [ ] **Hybrid simulator outputs:** stream tables, PFD, P&ID, BOM, equipment list, cost summary, environmental impact.

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 3 Notes — Biotechnology Design Simulator & Bioprocess Simulator.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Advantages of Cell Immobilization, Methods of Active and Passive Immobilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Potential Advantages of Cell Immobilization, Methods of Active and Passive Immobilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cell Immobilization?

**Cell Immobilization** is a bioprocess technique in which whole cells (microbial, plant, or animal) are physically confined or localized in a specific region of space, while still retaining their **catalytic (metabolic) activity**, so that they can be **continuously reused** as biocatalysts for biochemical reactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *Cell immobilization is the process of confining cells within or on a support matrix/carrier such that they remain metabolically active, can be separated from the reaction medium easily, and can be used repeatedly for bioconversion reactions.*

The whole-cell biocatalyst may be an actively **growing**, **resting**, **permeabilized**, or **dead (autoclaved)** cell — what matters is that its enzyme machinery is preserved.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Aquarium in a Factory"

Imagine a **fish aquarium inside a factory**:

- The **fish (cells)** want to do a useful job — say, eat waste (substrate) and release clean water (product).
- If the fish swim freely in a giant tank, every time you want clean water, you must **filter out the fish first** — slow, messy, and the fish get stressed.
- Now, instead, you **pin the fish inside a small glass chamber** with slits that let water flow in and out. The fish are alive, they keep working, but they **never leave the chamber**.
- You can now pass thousands of liters of water through the chamber, and the fish keep cleaning — all day, every day, **without ever being removed**.

That glass chamber is your **support matrix**.
The fish are your **immobilized cells**.
The continuous flow of water is your **continuous bioreactor operation**.

---

## 1.3 Why Cell Immobilization? — The "Big Picture"

In conventional **free-cell (suspended) fermentation**:
- Cells are **homogeneously distributed** in the broth.
- Removing cells = expensive centrifugation, filtration, or sedimentation.
- Cells are lost every cycle → high biocatalyst cost.
- Continuous operation risks **washout** (cells leave faster than they grow).

Immobilization **converts a homogeneous dispersed system into a heterogeneous, surface-confined system** — a fundamental engineering advantage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric used in KTU problems:**
> The effectiveness of immobilization is often expressed as **Immobilization Yield (η)** —
> $$\eta = \frac{\text{Activity retained after immobilization}}{\text{Initial free-cell activity}} \times 100\%$$
> A good immobilization process retains **> 60–70%** of original activity.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Classification Snapshot

The 2024 scheme **divides immobilization into two broad philosophies**:

| Philosophy | Definition | Key Idea | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active Immobilization** | Cell itself **participates actively** in forming the bond with the support | Cell's surface groups are chemically/electrically involved | Covalent bonding, Cross-linking, Biofilm, Flocculation |
| **Passive Immobilization** | Cell is **physically trapped** without participating in the attachment; no chemical bond forms with the cell | Cell is "caught" inside a 3-D cage or behind a barrier | Entrapment, Encapsulation, Adsorption, Membrane retention |

This classification is **directly asked** in KTU exams (1–2 mark definitions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Three Pillars" Behind Every Immobilization Decision

When selecting a method, KTU examiners expect students to **justify** the choice using three engineering criteria:

**Pillar 1 — Biocompatibility**
