| 10 % FBS | Growth factors, attachment proteins, lipids | G₀ arrest, rounded morphology |
| Antibiotics | Prevents bacterial contamination | Culture loss within 24–48 h |

---

## 3.5 Trypsinisation — The "Molecular Scissors" Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** Trypsinisation is the enzymatic dissociation of adherent cells from the culture substrate and from each other using the protease trypsin, typically at **0.25 % w/v in PBS + 0.02 % EDTA**, enabling subculture (passaging).

**Mechanism (Step-by-Step):**
1. **PBS wash** removes serum (which contains anti-trypsin α₂-macroglobulin).
2. **EDTA** chelates Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ — these divalent cations are essential for cadherin-mediated cell–cell adhesion and integrin-mediated attachment. Removing them weakens both junctions.
3. **Trypsin** cleaves peptide bonds at the C-terminal side of lysine and arginine residues, degrading the extracellular matrix proteins (fibronectin, collagen) and cadherins anchoring cells to the dish.
4. **Incubation time:** 2–5 min at 37 °C.
5. **Inactivation:** Add serum-containing medium (FBS contains trypsin inhibitors) or add specific inhibitor (e.g., soybean trypsin inhibitor).
6. **Mechanical tapping** dislodges cells → centrifugation → reseeding.

> [!WARNING]
> **Over-trypsinisation damages cells** by cleaving surface receptors (e.g., EGF receptor). KTU students often lose marks by failing to mention the **EDTA–trypsin synergy** and the **serum-based inactivation** step.

---

## 3.6 Saturation Density and Contact Inhibition

**Mathematical formulation of growth phases:**

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} = \mu N \left(1 - \frac{N}{\rho_{sat}}\right)
$$

This is the **logistic growth equation**. Solutions:
- When $N \ll \rho_{sat}$ → exponential growth (μ = max).
- When $N \rightarrow \rho_{sat}$ → growth rate → 0 (**contact inhibition**).

**Engineering significance:**
- **Normal cells** exhibit strong contact inhibition → form monolayer → low saturation density.
- **Transformed cells** (e.g., HeLa) **lose contact inhibition** → pile up → much higher saturation density → anchorage-independence → tumourigenic phenotype.
- This is the **classical in vitro assay for transformation** — a high-yield KTU topic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is 5 % CO₂ supplied to mammalian cell culture incubators?

**Model Answer:**
Mammalian cell culture media use the bicarbonate buffer system (Earle's salts). At 37 °C, the pKₐ of carbonic acid is 6.1. By the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation, maintaining pH **7.4** requires:

$$
\log \frac{[\text{HCO}_3^{-}]}{[\text{CO}_2]} = 1.3 \Rightarrow \frac{[\text{HCO}_3^{-}]}{[\text{CO}_2]} \approx 20
$$

At standard atmospheric pressure and 26 mM bicarbonate concentration, this ratio corresponds to a CO₂ partial pressure of ~40 mmHg, which is supplied by maintaining incubator CO₂ at **5 %**. This counteracts the metabolic production of CO₂ by cells and prevents alkaline drift of the medium, ensuring optimal enzyme function and protein synthesis. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is serum used in animal cell culture media? List its four main functions.

**Model Answer:**
Serum (typically **5–20 % Foetal Bovine Serum, FBS**) is added because it provides growth and attachment factors not yet fully replaced in chemically defined media. Its four primary functions are:

1. **Provides growth factors** (PDGF, EGF, FGF, IGF) that stimulate mitosis via receptor tyrosine kinase pathways.
2. **Provides adhesion factors** (fibronectin, laminin, vitronectin) that bind to plastic surfaces and present RGD sequences for integrin attachment.
3. **Provides transport proteins** (albumin, transferrin) carrying hormones, lipids, and iron.
4. **Provides protective agents** (antiproteases such as α₁-antitrypsin, and buffering against pH shock, toxins, and mechanical stress). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice — Select and Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the substrate and surface requirements for animal cell culture. Explain why tissue-culture treated plastic is preferred over glass.

**Model Answer:**

**Substrate requirement definition (1 mark):**
A solid, biologically inert, clean, surface-modified support that permits cell attachment, growth, division, and microscopic observation.

**Glass as substrate (2 marks):**
- Original substrate (Carrel, 1912 onward).
- Composition: borosilicate — chemically inert, optically clear, mechanically stable.
- Disadvantages:
  - Expensive and breakable.
  - Requires extensive cleaning (chromic acid, then extensive rinsing with deionised water).
  - Used today only for specialised applications (roller bottles, chamber slides).

**Plastic (polystyrene) — modern standard (3 marks):**
- Composition: transparent amorphous polystyrene, ideally suited to optical microscopy.
- Native polystyrene is hydrophobic (contact angle ≈ 90°) — cells cannot attach.
- **Tissue culture treatment** modifies the surface:
  - **Corona discharge / gas plasma** introduces polar groups (–OH, –COOH, –C=O) → hydrophilic (contact angle < 50°).
  - Negative surface charge promotes adsorption of serum proteins (fibronectin, vitronectin).
  - Some applications require **chemical coating** with **poly-D-lysine, collagen, fibronectin, or laminin** (e.g., neurons, hepatocytes, stem cells).

**Why plastic is preferred over glass (1 mark):**
Cheaper, disposable (no cross-contamination), already pre-treated by manufacturer, optically clear, mouldable into flasks/plates/dishes of any geometry, gamma-sterile off the shelf.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the role of serum, antibiotics, and pH in animal cell culture requirements.

**Model Answer:**

**Serum (3 marks):**
- Provides growth factors (PDGF, EGF), hormones (insulin, hydrocortisone), attachment proteins (fibronectin), transport proteins (transferrin), and protective agents.
- Standard use: **5–20 % FBS** in basal medium.
- Disadvantages: undefined composition, batch variability, risk of mycoplasma/viral contamination, xeno-problem for therapeutic cell products.
- Trend: replacement with **chemically defined, serum-free media** (e.g., for monoclonal antibody production in CHO).

**Antibiotics (2 marks):**
- Standard cocktail: **Penicillin (100 U/mL)** against Gram-positive bacteria, **Streptomycin (100 µg/mL)** against Gram-negative bacteria, **Amphotericin B (0.25 µg/mL)** against fungi.
- Heat-labile → must be added by sterile 0.22 µm filtration after autoclaving.
- **Limitation:** Masks mycoplasma contamination; routine screening required.

**pH (2 marks):**
- Required range: **7.2–7.4** (optimum **7.4**).
- Maintained by bicarbonate buffer (Earle's salts) coupled with **5 % CO₂** incubator atmosphere.
- Alternative/supplementary buffer: **HEPES (25 mM)** — effective independent of CO₂.
- Phenol red (10 mg/L) is the visual pH indicator: yellow → acidic (CO₂ excess), pink → optimum, purple → alkaline.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** — Alternative choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the physico-chemical requirements for animal cell culture. Why are temperature and osmolality critical?

**Model Answer:**

**Temperature (3 marks):**
- Most mammalian cell lines (originating from homeothermic mammals) require **37 °C ± 0.5 °C**.
- Maintained by **water-jacketed CO₂ incubators** with feedback thermostats.
- Biochemical basis: at 37 °C, all mammalian enzymes (DNA polymerase, RNA polymerase, ATP synthase, ribosomal peptidyl transferase) display optimal V_max.
- Deviation consequences:
  - Lower (room temp, 25 °C) → slowed metabolism, G₁ arrest, but reversible.
  - Higher (> 39 °C) → heat-shock protein induction, irreversible protein denaturation, apoptosis.

**Osmolality (2 marks):**
- Required range: **280–320 mOsm/kg** (isosmotic with plasma).
- Maintained by inorganic salt balance (NaCl ~ 6.8 g/L in DMEM).
- Deviation → plasmolysis (hyperosmotic) or cell swelling and lysis (hypoosmotic).
- Monitored with vapour pressure or freezing-point osmometers.

**Other physico-chemical parameters (2 marks):**
- **pH = 7.2–7.4** (bicarbonate–CO₂ system + HEPES).
- **CO₂ = 5 %** (for Earle's salts; lower for Hank's).
- **Humidity 95–98 %** — prevents evaporation → osmolality drift.
- **O₂ tension** — dissolved O₂ of ~20 % saturation adequate for static cultures; reduced (~ 30–60 %) preferred for stem cell cultures to mimic physiological hypoxia.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Differentiate between primary culture, cell line, and continuous cell line. Explain the role of trypsinisation in subculturing adherent cells.

**Model Answer:**

**Primary culture (2 marks):**
- Cells obtained directly from tissue by disaggregation (mechanical, enzymatic with trypsin/collagenase).
- Retain original tissue characteristics.
- **Finite lifespan** — undergo **Hayflick limit** (~ 20–80 population doublings for human fibroblasts) before senescence.

**Cell line (finite/secondary) (2 marks):**
- Primary cells subcultured (passaged) beyond the original culture vessel.
- Diploid, anchorage-dependent, retain contact inhibition.
- Limited passage number (e.g., WI-38 human fibroblasts).

**Continuous / immortalised cell line (2 marks):**
- Spontaneous or induced transformation (by SV40 large T-antigen, EBV, hTERT telomerase activation, or chemical carcinogens).
- **Loss of contact inhibition**, **anchorage independence**, **higher saturation density**, **tumourigenic in nude mice**.
- Unlimited proliferation (e.g., HeLa — cervical carcinoma, 1951; CHO — Chinese hamster ovary; Vero — African green monkey kidney; BHK-21; MDCK).

**Trypsinisation in subculturing (1 mark):**
- Purpose: dissociate adherent monolayer into single-cell suspension for transfer to new vessels.
- Steps: PBS wash → **0.25 % trypsin + 0.02 % EDTA** for 2–5 min at 37 °C → inactivate with serum → centrifuge → resuspend → reseed at **1:2 to 1:20 split ratio**.
- EDTA chelates Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ required for integrin and cadherin function; trypsin cleaves adhesion proteins.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (costing 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. Confusing **Hank's salts** (low bicarbonate, for ambient CO₂) with **Earle's salts** (high bicarbonate, requires 5 % CO₂).
> 2. Forgetting that **EDTA** is used **along with** trypsin (synergistic action on Ca²⁺-dependent adhesion).
> 3. Writing "serum provides nutrients" only — examiners want **growth factors, attachment factors, transport proteins, protective agents** (4 classes).
> 4. Failing to mention **phenol red as pH indicator** in culture media.
> 5. Writing "37 °C" without justification — must state **enzyme kinetics / V_max**.
> 6. Confusing **Hayflick limit** (for primary/finite lines) with **transformation** (immortalisation).
> 7. Not differentiating **anchorage-dependent** from **anchorage-independent** growth.
> 8. Forgetting that **contact inhibition** is **lost in transformed** cells → focus formation → in vitro transformation assay.
> 9. Stating FBS concentration as "10 %" without qualifying that this is **volume/volume (v/v)**.
> 10. Missing that **mycoplasma contamination** is **not visible** under standard light microscopy → requires **Hoechst DNA stain or PCR** for detection.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorise Before Exam**

- ☐ **Substrate:** Glass or **tissue-culture treated polystyrene**; native plastic hydrophobic → plasma/corona treatment → hydrophilic; coating with collagen/poly-D-lysine for fastidious cells.
- ☐ **Medium classes:** **Inorganic salts + amino acids + vitamins + glucose + hormones/growth factors + buffer system**.
- ☐ **Bicarbonate buffer:** pH = 6.1 + log([HCO₃⁻]/[CO₂]); requires **5 % CO₂** incubator for Earle's; HEPES as supplemental buffer.
- ☐ **FBS at 5–20 % v/v** provides 4 function classes: growth factors, attachment factors, transport proteins, protective agents.
- ☐ **Temperature = 37 °C ± 0.5** (enzyme V_max optimum).
- ☐ **pH = 7.2–7.4**, **Osmolality = 280–320 mOsm/kg**, **Humidity = 95–98 %**.
- ☐ **Phenol red = pH indicator**; yellow = acidic, pink = optimum, purple = alkaline.
- ☐ **Antibiotic cocktail:** Penicillin + Streptomycin + Amphotericin B.
- ☐ **Sterilisation methods:** Autoclave (121 °C, 15 psi, 20 min) for heat-stable; **0.22 µm filter** for heat-labile (serum, vitamins, antibiotics).
- ☐ **Primary culture = finite lifespan (Hayflick limit ~50 doublings)**; **Continuous = transformed, indefinite**.
- ☐ **Anchorage-dependent vs Anchorage-independent** (transformed).
- ☐ **Contact inhibition** = monolayer, G₀ arrest in normal cells; **LOST in transformed cells** → focus formation.
- ☐ **Trypsinisation:** 0.25 % trypsin + 0.02 % EDTA; PBS wash removes anti-trypsin serum proteins; inactivate with FBS.
- ☐ **Population Doubling Time:** $TD = \dfrac{0.301 \cdot t}{\log N_f - \log N_i}$.
- ☐ **Plating Efficiency:** $PE = \dfrac{\text{colonies formed}}{\text{cells inoculated}} \times 100$ %.
- ☐ **Laminar Airflow Cabinet Class II** with HEPA filter = sterile workspace (ISO 5).
- ☐ **CO₂ incubator** maintains T, CO₂, humidity simultaneously.
- ☐ **Mycoplasma** = invisible, insidious contaminant — test by **Hoechst stain or PCR**.
- ☐ **Serum-free media** = trend for therapeutic bioprocesses (avoid xeno-proteins).
- ☐ **Microcarriers** = scale-up solution for adherent cells in stirred bioreactors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bioreactor Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bioreactor Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioreactor Design Considerations** refer to the integrated set of engineering principles, geometric specifications, mechanical configurations, and operational criteria that govern the construction and selection of a vessel in which a controlled biological or biochemical process is carried out. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBBT502, Module 4), these considerations include vessel geometry, material of construction, agitation–aeration system, heat and mass transfer provisions, aseptic sealing, sterilization ports, foam control, sensor integration, and scale-up comparability.

A bioreactor (also called a *fermentor*) is essentially a **closed, sterile vessel** equipped with provisions for:
- Inoculation of the biological catalyst (cells/enzymes)
- Nutrient addition and pH/temperature control
- Agitation for uniform mixing
- Aeration (for aerobic processes)
- Heat removal (for exothermic bioconversions)
- Sampling, sensing, and product recovery

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of a bioreactor as a **"factory kitchen"** for microbes.

| Component | Analogy |
|---|---|
| Vessel body | A pressure cooker that never leaks |
| Impeller (agitator) | The ladle/stirrer that keeps the curry uniform |
| Sparger | The pipe blowing air bubbles into a fish tank |
| Baffles | The ridges inside a washing machine that prevent clothes from spinning in one lump |
| Heat exchanger (jacket/coil) | The double boiler that keeps the dish at the right temperature |
| Sampling port | The small window in an oven to check how the cake is baking |
| Exhaust line with condenser | The steam vent in the pressure cooker lid |

> [!NOTE]
> A bioreactor is *not* a chemical reactor with a biology sticker on it. Biological systems are **fragile** — they die at high temperatures, shear stress breaks their cell walls, contamination ruins the whole batch, and a 0.5 °C drift can halve the product yield. Hence, design considerations for bioreactors are **far more stringent** than for petrochemical or polymer reactors.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- Module 4 of PCBBT502 carries a significant weight in the End Semester Examination (ESE) and is frequently tested in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** formats.
- Topics like **kLa determination, power number, scale-up criteria, and aseptic design** are perennial KTU favorites.
- Understanding design considerations is essential because it bridges **reaction engineering (Module 2/3)** with **downstream processing and scale-up (Module 5)**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Bioreactor Design Considerations

A well-designed bioreactor must satisfy **eight interlocking design objectives**. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies them as follows:

### A. Geometric / Mechanical Design
1. **Vessel shape**: Cylindrical with hemispherical or torispherical bottom (preferred for stress distribution and full drainage).
2. **Aspect ratio (H/D)**: Typically **2:1 to 3:1** for stirred-tank reactors; **4:1 to 6:1** for airlift reactors.
3. **Working volume**: Generally **70–80%** of total volume (head space left for foam and gas disengagement).
4. **Material of construction**: Stainless steel **SS-316L** (preferred); borosilicate glass for small lab-scale units.
5. **Surface finish**: Internally polished to **Ra ≤ 0.4 µm** to prevent microbial adhesion and ease Cleaning-in-Place (CIP).

### B. Agitation System
- **Impeller types**: Rushton turbine (radial flow), pitched-blade turbine (axial/mixed flow), marine propeller, Scaba, hydrofoil.
- **Impeller diameter (Di)** is usually **D/3** of vessel diameter.
- **Number of impellers**: One per **2–3 vessel diameters** of liquid height.
- **Baffles**: Four vertical baffles of width **D/10** each, mounted off the wall by a small gap (T/72) to prevent Karman vortex formation.

### C. Aeration / Mass Transfer
- **Sparger design**: Porous (sintered) sparger for lab scale; ring sparger with drilled holes for industrial scale.
- **Gas dispersion**: Superficial gas velocity **0.004–0.008 m/s** for airlift; higher for STR.
- **Oxygen demand**: For aerobic cultures, dissolved oxygen (DO) must be maintained above the **critical dissolved oxygen concentration** (typically 10–30% air saturation).

### D. Heat Transfer
- **Jacket** (for vessels up to ~2 m³) or **external half-coil** (for larger vessels).
- Cooling requirements can reach **10–50 W/L** for highly aerobic fermentations.
- **q = U·A·ΔT** governs the heat removal rate.

### E. Sterilization & Aseptic Design
- **In situ sterilization (SIP)**: Steam at **121 °C, 15 psig, 20–30 min**.
- All penetrations (probes, ports) use **O-ring or fermentor collar seals** to maintain sterility.
- **Sterile boundary principle**: Every line entering the vessel must have a steam-block or sterile barrier.

### F. Foam Control
- **Mechanical foam breakers**: Conical discs or centrifugal foam breakers mounted on the agitator shaft.
- **Chemical antifoam**: Polypropylene glycol (PPG), silicone-based antifoams (sparingly, as they can affect mass transfer).

### G. Instrumentation & Control
- **Sensors**: pH, DO (dissolved oxygen), temperature, foam level, agitator speed, gas flow, weight.
- **Control loops**: PID controllers for pH (acid/base addition), DO (agitation speed / air flow cascade), temperature (cooling water flow / steam valve).

### H. Scale-Up / Scale-Down Compatibility
- Maintain similarity in:
  - Power input per unit volume (**P/V**)
  - Volumetric mass transfer coefficient (**kLa**)
  - Mixing time
  - Impeller tip speed
  - Shear (impeller tip speed / Kolmogorov eddy length)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — at least 4 of these equations appear in every KTU ESE paper for Module 4.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Number (Newtonian fluid) | $P_o = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5}$ | Dimensionless; depends on Re and impeller geometry |
| 2 | Reynolds Number (impeller) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D_i^2}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{N^2 \cdot D_i}{g}$ | Important when surface aeration/ vortexing occurs |
| 4 | Ungassed Power (Rushton) | $P_{g=0} = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$ | W (Watts) |
| 5 | Gassed Power (correction) | $\dfrac{P_g}{P_{g=0}} \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$ for Rushton under high aeration | Hughmark correlation |
| 6 | Volumetric mass transfer coefficient (Van't Riet) | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5}$ | $K \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ for coalescing, $4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ for non-coalescing |
| 7 | Volumetric mass transfer coefficient (Cooper) | $k_L a = K \cdot v_s^{0.67}$ | Simplified correlation |
| 8 | Power input per unit volume (scale-up) | $(P/V)_{scale} = (P/V)_{bench}$ | Constant P/V is the most common scale-up criterion |
| 9 | Heat transfer rate | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W |
| 10 | Steam sterilization — D value | $D_T = D_{121} \cdot 10^{(T - 121)/Z}$ | Minutes; Z = 10 °C for most vegetative bacteria |
| 11 | Del factor (sterilization) | $\nabla = \int_0^t 10^{(T(t) - 121)/Z} \, dt$ | log-cycle equivalent; for batch, $\nabla = t \cdot 10^{(T - 121)/Z}$ |
| 12 | Sterilization probability (Poisson) | $P_0 = e^{-N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}}$ | $N_0$ = initial spore count |
| 13 | Impeller tip speed | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D_i$ | m/s |
| 14 | Shear rate (Kolmogorov) | $\dot{\gamma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P/V}{\mu}}$ | s⁻¹ |
| 15 | Mixing time (impeller correlation) | $t_m \cdot N = \text{const}$ for geometrically similar vessels | Mixing time decreases with N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU rule:** The $k_L a$ correlation by **Van't Riet (1979)** is the one that KTU examiners set problems on. The form $k_L a = K \cdot (P_g/V)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5}$ with $K$ depending on medium coalescence is the standard.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial penicillin production** (100+ m³ STRs) uses Rushton turbines with multi-point spargers and 4 baffles.
- **Monoclonal antibody production** (mAb) uses **single-use wave bioreactors** or stirred tanks with low-shear marine impellers — shear sensitivity of mammalian cells is the design bottleneck.
- **Ethanol fermentation** uses **tower fermentors / airlift reactors** — low energy, no aeration, but high aspect ratio.
- **Wastewater treatment** uses **activated sludge tanks** with diffused aeration (kLa control is the design parameter).
- **Vaccine production (whole-cell)** uses rigidly controlled STRs with SIP/CIP automation, owing to pathogen risk.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Number, Reynolds Number, and Power Input

### 3.1.1 Power Drawn by an Impeller (Dimensional Analysis)

The power $P$ consumed by a rotating impeller in a Newtonian fluid depends on:

- Fluid density $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Fluid viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s)
- Impeller rotational speed $N$ (rev/s or 1/s)
- Impeller diameter $D_i$ (m)
- Gravitational acceleration $g$ (m/s²) — relevant when vortexing dominates

**Step 1: Form the functional relationship.**
$$ P = f(\rho,\ \mu,\ N,\ D_i,\ g) $$

**Step 2: List the fundamental dimensions.**

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensions (M, L, T) |
|---|---|---|
| Power | $P$ | $M L^2 T^{-3}$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |
| Speed | $N$ | $T^{-1}$ |
| Diameter | $D_i$ | $L$ |
| Gravity | $g$ | $L T^{-2}$ |

There are **3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T)** and **6 variables**, so by Buckingham's $\pi$ theorem we get $6 - 3 = 3$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 3: Choose repeating variables.** Pick $\rho,\ N,\ D_i$ (they together span all three dimensions).

**Step 4: Form the $\pi$ groups.**

Group 1 — involving $P$:
$$ \pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho^{a_1} N^{b_1} D_i^{c_1}} $$

Matching dimensions:
$$ M L^2 T^{-3} = (M L^{-3})^{a_1} (T^{-1})^{b_1} (L)^{c_1} $$

Equating exponents:
- M: $1 = a_1$ → $a_1 = 1$
- T: $-3 = -b_1$ → $b_1 = 3$
- L: $2 = -3 a_1 + c_1 = -3 + c_1$ → $c_1 = 5$

$$ \pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5} = P_o \quad \text{(Power Number)} $$

Group 2 — involving $\mu$:
$$ \pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho^{a_2} N^{b_2} D_i^{c_2}} = \rho^{-1} N^{-1} D_i^{-2} \mu $$

This is the inverse of the impeller Reynolds number:
$$ \pi_2 = \dfrac{1}{Re} \quad \text{where} \quad Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D_i^2}{\mu} $$

Group 3 — involving $g$:
$$ \pi_3 = \dfrac{g}{\rho^{a_3} N^{b_3} D_i^{c_3}} = N^2 D_i \cdot g^{-1} \cdot \rho^{0} $$

This is the inverse of the Froude number:
$$ \pi_3 = \dfrac{1}{Fr} \quad \text{where} \quad Fr = \dfrac{N^2 D_i}{g} $$

**Step 5: Final functional relationship.**
$$ P_o = f(Re, Fr) $$

**Step 6: For fully baffled vessels (Froude effects negligible):**
$$ P_o = f(Re) \quad \text{only} $$

This yields the classic $P_o$ vs. $Re$ curve, where:
- For $Re > 10^4$ (turbulent regime), $P_o = $ constant (e.g., **5.5** for Rushton 6-blade, **1.5** for pitched-blade 4-blade 45°).
- For $Re < 10$ (laminar), $P_o \propto 1/Re$ (slime-stream behavior).
- For $10 < Re < 10^4$ (transition), use published charts.

**Step 7: Solve for $P$:**
$$ P = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5 \quad \text{(W)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> In SI units, $N$ is in **rev/s (1/s)**. If given in rpm, convert: $N_{\text{rps}} = N_{\text{rpm}} / 60$. A common KTU error is forgetting this conversion, leading to answers off by a factor of 60³ = **216,000**.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A Rushton turbine ($D_i = 0.5$ m) rotates at 120 rpm in a baffled vessel containing a broth of density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and viscosity $\mu = 0.001$ Pa·s. The vessel diameter is $D = 1.5$ m. Find:
1. The Reynolds number
2. The power number (assume turbulent regime, $P_o = 5.5$)
3. The power input
4. The power input per unit volume (working volume = 10 m³)

**Step 1: Convert N to rps.**
$$ N = 120 / 60 = 2 \ \text{rev/s} $$

**Step 2: Calculate Re.**
$$ Re = \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 2 \cdot (0.5)^2}{0.001} = \dfrac{500}{0.001} = 5 \times 10^{5} $$

This is turbulent — $P_o = 5.5$ assumption is valid.

**Step 3: Calculate P.**
$$ P = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5 $$
$$ P = 5.5 \cdot 1000 \cdot (2)^3 \cdot (0.5)^5 $$
$$ P = 5.5 \cdot 1000 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.03125 = 5.5 \cdot 250 = 1375 \ \text{W} $$

**Step 4: Calculate P/V.**
$$ P/V = 1375 / 10 = 137.5 \ \text{W/m}^3 $$

This is within the typical industrial range (50–5000 W/m³), confirming validity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: kLa from Van't Riet Correlation

The **volumetric mass transfer coefficient** $k_L a$ represents the rate of oxygen transfer per unit driving force per unit volume. The Van't Riet (1979) empirical correlation (the most-cited in textbooks) is:

$$ k_L a = K \cdot \left( \dfrac{P_g}{V} \right)^{\alpha} \cdot v_s^{\beta} $$

where:
- $K \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ (SI) for coalescing broths
- $K \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ (SI) for non-coalescing broths
- $\alpha = 0.4$
- $\beta = 0.5$
- $P_g/V$ is gassed power per unit volume (W/m³)
- $v_s$ is superficial gas velocity (m/s)

**Derivation logic (semi-empirical):**

1. The overall mass transfer across the gas–liquid interface is governed by the **two-film theory**:
$$ N_A = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C_L) $$

where $C^*$ is the saturation DO concentration and $C_L$ is the actual DO.

2. The interfacial area $a$ increases with both **turbulence** (driven by $P/V$) and **gas holdup** (driven by $v_s$).

3. The mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ also increases with turbulence (smaller eddies renew the interface faster).

4. Empirically, $a \propto (P_g/V)^{0.4}$ and $k_L \propto (P_g/V)^{0}$ (only weakly dependent on turbulence in the $P/V$ range of interest, more on eddy size), and overall:
$$ k_L a \propto (P_g/V)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5} $$

5. The constant $K$ differs between coalescing (air–water) and non-coalescing (salt, antifoam-free broth) systems because coalescence reduces interfacial area.

**Worked example (KTU style):**

A 5 m³ fermentor receives air at a flow rate of 0.005 m³/s through a sparger. Vessel cross-section is 1 m². The gassed power input is 10 kW. The broth is non-coalescing. Calculate $k_L a$.

**Step 1: Superficial gas velocity.**
$$ v_s = Q / A = 0.005 / 1 = 0.005 \ \text{m/s} $$

**Step 2: Gassed P/V.**
$$ P_g / V = 10000 / 5 = 2000 \ \text{W/m}^3 $$

**Step 3: Apply Van't Riet correlation.**
$$ k_L a = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (2000)^{0.4} \cdot (0.005)^{0.5} $$

$$ (2000)^{0.4} = 10^{0.4 \cdot \log_{10}(2000)} = 10^{0.4 \cdot 3.301} = 10^{1.3204} \approx 20.92 $$

$$ (0.005)^{0.5} = 0.0707 $$

$$ k_L a = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 20.92 \cdot 0.0707 = 6.65 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}^{-1} $$

$$ k_L a \approx 6.65 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}^{-1} = 23.95 \ \text{h}^{-1} $$

This is a typical industrial value for aerobic fermentations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sterilization (D-Value, Z-Value, Del Factor)

### 3.3.1 Microbial Death Kinetics

Bacterial spore inactivation is **first-order**:
$$ \dfrac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N $$

Integrating:
$$ N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t} $$

**Step 1: Define D-value** (time to reduce spore population by 90% at temperature T):
$$ D_T = \dfrac{2.303}{k_T} $$

**Step 2: Define Z-value** (temperature change needed to change D by 10×):
$$ \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{(T_2 - T_1)/Z} $$

**Step 3: Combine to get D at any temperature T:**
$$ D_T = D_{121} \cdot 10^{(121 - T)/Z} $$

**Step 4: Del factor** (lethality integrated over a sterilization cycle):
$$ \nabla = \int_0^{t_{\text{ster}}} 10^{(T(t) - 121)/Z} \, dt $$

For an isothermal hold at $T$:
$$ \nabla = t \cdot 10^{(T - 121)/Z} $$

**Step 5: Probability of survival** (Poisson distribution, low counts):
$$ P_{\text{sterile}} = e^{-N_{\text{final}}} = e^{-N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}} = e^{-N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D_T}} $$

### 3.3.2 Worked Example

A fermentation medium contains $N_0 = 10^5$ spores/m³. $D_{121} = 1.5$ min, $Z = 10$ °C. The medium is held at $121$ °C for 20 min. Find the probability of a non-sterile unit.

**Step 1: Del factor.**
$$ \nabla = 20 \cdot 10^{(121 - 121)/10} = 20 \cdot 10^0 = 20 $$

**Step 2: Equivalent D at 121 °C**:
$$ D_{121} = 1.5 \ \text{min} $$

**Step 3: Equivalent time at 121 °C** = $\nabla \cdot D_{121}$? No — $\nabla$ is the number of log-cycles of reduction.

Each log-cycle of reduction takes 1 D-time.
Total log-reductions:
$$ \log_{10}(N_0 / N_{\text{final}}) = \nabla = 20 \ \text{log cycles} $$

Wait — this is not the correct interpretation of Del factor. Let me redo:

$$ \text{Log reduction} = t / D_T = 20 / 1.5 = 13.33 $$

**Step 4: Final spore count:**
$$ N_{\text{final}} = N_0 \cdot 10^{-13.33} = 10^5 \cdot 10^{-13.33} = 10^{-8.33} \approx 4.68 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{spores} $$

**Step 5: Probability of sterile unit:**
$$ P = e^{-4.68 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1 - 4.68 \times 10^{-9} \approx 1.0 $$

The probability of contamination is essentially **zero**.

> [!NOTE]
> For batch sterilization in fermentors, the typical design criterion is to achieve $\nabla \geq 45$ log-reductions to ensure acceptable sterility assurance level (SAL) of $10^{-3}$ even for the most resistant thermophilic spores.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Bioreactor Type

| Criterion | STR (Stirred Tank) | Airlift | Bubble Column | Packed Bed | Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aeration efficiency | High | Moderate–High | Low–Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Shear stress | High | Low | Low | Very Low | Moderate |
| Energy cost | High | Low | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Cell density | Medium | Medium | Low | Very High | High |
| Best for | Bacteria, yeast | Shear-sensitive (plant, animal) | Shear-sensitive, low-cost | Immobilized enzymes/cells | Immobilized cells with biofilm |
| Heat removal | Easy (jacket) | Moderate | Difficult | Excellent | Moderate |
| Scale-up | Well-defined (P/V) | Simple | Simple | Difficult | Moderate |
| Capital cost | High | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Moderate |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What are the key design considerations for an industrial bioreactor? List any six.**

**Model Answer (6 key points — pick any 6):**

1. **Vessel geometry** — Aspect ratio 2:1 to 3:1 for STR, working volume 70–80% of total.
2. **Material of construction** — SS-316L with internal surface finish Ra ≤ 0.4 µm for cleanability.
3. **Agitation system** — Rushton or pitched-blade turbines, Di = D/3, baffles D/10 wide.
4. **Aeration/mass transfer** — Adequate kLa to meet oxygen demand; sparger design (ring/porous).
5. **Aseptic design** — Steam-blocked penetrations, sterile boundary principle, SIP capability.
6. **Heat transfer** — Jacket or coil sized for q = U·A·ΔT, with cooling water circulation.
7. **Foam control** — Mechanical foam breaker + antifoam dosing.
8. **Instrumentation** — pH, DO, T, foam, agitation speed, gas flow, weight.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define Power Number (Po) and Reynolds Number (Re) for an agitated vessel. State their significance.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Power Number (Po):** A dimensionless number representing the ratio of drag force to inertia force on the impeller, given by:
$$ P_o = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5} $$

- **Reynolds Number (Re):** A dimensionless number indicating the flow regime in the impeller region, given by:
$$ Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D_i^2}{\mu} $$

**Significance:**
- Po vs. Re plot (Power curve) is used to determine power consumption for any impeller, fluid, and speed.
- Re > 10⁴ → turbulent; Re < 10 → laminar; in between → transition.
- For fully baffled vessels, Po is independent of Re in the turbulent regime, simplifying scale-up.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Power Number and Reynolds Number for an agitated tank using dimensional analysis. Discuss the Po–Re relationship in different flow regimes.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A Rushton turbine with diameter 0.6 m rotates at 90 rpm in a baffled vessel of diameter 1.8 m containing a broth of density 1050 kg/m³ and viscosity 0.002 Pa·s. The vessel working volume is 15 m³. Calculate the power input and the power input per unit volume. Comment on whether this matches typical industrial values.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Identify variables.** Power $P$ depends on $\rho,\ \mu,\ N,\ D_i,\ g$.

**Step 2: Buckingham π theorem.** With 6 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions, we get 3 π groups.

**Step 3: Form π groups** (using $\rho,\ N,\ D_i$ as repeating variables):
- $\pi_1 = P / (\rho N^3 D_i^5) = P_o$ (Power Number)
- $\pi_2 = \rho N D_i^2 / \mu = Re$ (Reynolds Number)
- $\pi_3 = N^2 D_i / g = Fr$ (Froude Number)

**Step 4: Functional relationship.**
$$ P_o = \phi(Re, Fr) $$

For fully baffled vessels, Froude effects vanish:
$$ P_o = \phi(Re) \ \text{only} $$

**Step 5: Flow regime discussion:**

| Re range | Regime | Po behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Re < 10 | Laminar | $P_o \propto 1/Re$ (viscous-dominated) |
| 10 < Re < 10⁴ | Transition | Po decreases monotonically |
| Re > 10⁴ | Turbulent | $P_o \approx$ const (geometry-dependent) |

**Step 6: Specific Po values (turbulent, fully baffled):**
- 6-blade Rushton disc turbine: $P_o \approx 5.5$
- 4-blade pitched-blade (45°): $P_o \approx 1.5$
- Marine propeller: $P_o \approx 0.35$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $D_i = 0.6$ m, $N = 90$ rpm = 1.5 rev/s
- $D = 1.8$ m, $V = 15$ m³
- $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.002$ Pa·s
- Impeller: Rushton turbine → $P_o = 5.5$ (assumed turbulent)

**Step 1: Verify turbulent regime by computing Re.**
$$ Re = \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{1050 \cdot 1.5 \cdot (0.6)^2}{0.002} = \dfrac{567}{0.002} = 2.835 \times 10^5 $$

Since $Re > 10^4$, the regime is turbulent and $P_o = 5.5$ is valid.

**Step 2: Compute power input.**
$$ P = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5 $$
$$ P = 5.5 \cdot 1050 \cdot (1.5)^3 \cdot (0.6)^5 $$
$$ P = 5.5 \cdot 1050 \cdot 3.375 \cdot 0.07776 $$
$$ P = 5.5 \cdot 1050 \cdot 0.2624 \approx 1515.5 \ \text{W} $$

**Step 3: Compute P/V.**
$$ P/V = 1515.5 / 15 = 101.0 \ \text{W/m}^3 $$

**Step 4: Comment.**
This $P/V$ value (~100 W/m³) is on the **lower end** of the typical industrial range (50–5000 W/m³) for bacterial cultures. It is appropriate for **low-cell-density, low-shear, oxygen-moderate** processes. For highly aerobic fermentations (e.g., *E. coli* high-density cultures), one would need P/V in the range 1000–5000 W/m³, which could be achieved by:
- Increasing N (e.g., to 180 rpm — $P \propto N^3$, so P/V becomes ~808 W/m³).
- Using multiple impellers (sums power).
- Improving gassing efficiency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Van't Riet correlation for volumetric mass transfer coefficient (kLa) in an agitated, aerated bioreactor. List the parameters and the standard constants for coalescing and non-coalescing media.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 20 m³ fermentor is fitted with a ring sparger and aerated with air at 0.025 m³/s. The vessel cross-section is 4 m². The gassed power input is 25 kW. The broth is non-coalescing. Using the Van't Riet correlation, calculate the volumetric mass transfer coefficient. State the assumptions.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** $k_L a$ is the volumetric mass transfer coefficient (s⁻¹), representing the rate of gas–liquid oxygen transfer per unit driving force per unit volume of broth.

**Two-film theory background:**
$$ N_{O_2} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) $$

**Van't Riet (1979) empirical correlation:**
$$ k_L a = K \cdot \left( \dfrac{P_g}{V} \right)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5} $$

**Parameters:**
- $P_g/V$ — gassed power input per unit volume (W/m³)
- $v_s$ — superficial gas velocity = Q/A (m/s)
- $K$ — empirical constant depending on medium coalescence

**Standard constants (SI units):**
| Medium | K value |
|---|---|
| Coalescing (air–water, salt-free, no antifoam) | $2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Non-coalescing (with salts, polymers, antifoam) | $4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Physical significance:** Higher P/V breaks gas into smaller bubbles (increases $a$); higher $v_s$ increases gas holdup (increases $a$); non-coalescing broths resist bubble merging (high $a$).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $V = 20$ m³, $Q = 0.025$ m³/s, $A = 4$ m²
- $P_g = 25{,}000$ W
- $K = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ (non-coalescing)
- $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.5$

**Step 1: Calculate $v_s$.**
$$ v_s = Q / A = 0.025 / 4 = 0.00625 \ \text{m/s} $$

**Step 2: Calculate $P_g / V$.**
$$ P_g / V = 25000 / 20 = 1250 \ \text{W/m}^3 $$

**Step 3: Apply Van't Riet correlation.**
$$ k_L a = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (1250)^{0.4} \cdot (0.00625)^{0.5} $$

**Step 4: Compute $(1250)^{0.4}$.**
$$ \log_{10}(1250) = 3.0969 $$
$$ 0.4 \times 3.0969 = 1.2388 $$
$$ (1250)^{0.4} = 10^{1.2388} = 17.33 $$

**Step 5: Compute $(0.00625)^{0.5}$.**
$$ (0.00625)^{0.5} = 0.0791 \ \text{m/s} $$

**Step 6: Final $k_L a$.**
$$ k_L a = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 17.33 \cdot 0.0791 $$
$$ k_L a = 6.17 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}^{-1} = 22.2 \ \text{h}^{-1} $$

**Assumptions:**
1. Newtonian broth behavior.
2. Spherical bubble regime.
3. Air–water interfacial analogy with given K.
4. Uniform gas dispersion.
5. Isothermal conditions (T constant at ~30 °C).
6. Surface aeration negligible (vessel is fully baffled).
7. No gas leakage through seals.

**Comment:** This is a moderate-to-high $k_L a$, suitable for high-demand aerobic fermentations. It falls in the industrial range of 100–1000 h⁻¹.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mark-loss zones for Module 4 — Bioreactor Design Considerations:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion of N (rpm → rps):** Many students forget to convert 90 rpm to 1.5 rev/s, leading to $P$ being off by a factor of $60^3 = 216{,}000$. Always convert first.
> 2. **Mixing up Po and P:** Power Number (Po) is dimensionless; Power (P) is in Watts. Do not write "Po = 1375 W" — that is P, not Po.
> 3. **Wrong exponents in Van't Riet:** The exponents are 0.4 and 0.5, NOT 0.5 and 0.5 or 0.4 and 0.4. This is a 1-mark loss per wrong exponent.
> 4. **Confusing kLa and kL:** $k_L a$ is the product, NOT the individual $k_L$ multiplied incorrectly.
> 5. **Sterile boundary violation:** Forgetting to mention **steam block, O-ring, or 0.2 µm filter** for each line crossing the vessel wall.
> 6. **Scale-up criterion confusion:** P/V is the **most commonly used**, not kLa. Some students quote kLa as the primary criterion, but kLa itself depends on P/V — so the underlying criterion is P/V.
> 7. **D-value vs. Z-value confusion:** D = time for 1 log reduction; Z = temperature for 1 log change in D. They are different physical quantities.
> 8. **Aspect ratio (H/D) mistakes:** STR uses 2:1 to 3:1; airlift uses 4:1 to 6:1. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks in design questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bioreactor Design Considerations (KTU 2024, Module 4):**

### Geometric & Mechanical
- Vessel: SS-316L, cylindrical, hemispherical bottom, Ra ≤ 0.4 µm
- Aspect ratio: STR 2:1–3:1; airlift 4:1–6:1
- Working volume: 70–80% of total
- 4 baffles, width D/10, off-wall gap T/72
- Impeller diameter: D/3; one impeller per 2–3 D of liquid height

### Agitation
- Power: $P = P_o \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$
- $P_o$ (Rushton, turbulent) ≈ 5.5
- $P_o$ (Pitched-blade 45°, turbulent) ≈ 1.5
- Re for transition to turbulence: ~10⁴
- Gassed power is typically 50–80% of ungassed

### Aeration / Mass Transfer
- $k_L a = K \cdot (P_g/V)^{0.4} \cdot v_s^{0.5}$
- K = 2.6 × 10⁻³ (coalescing); 4.5 × 10⁻³ (non-coalescing)
- Critical DO: 10–30% air saturation
- Superficial gas velocity: 0.004–0.008 m/s typical

### Heat Transfer
- $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$
- Cooling load: 10–50 W/L
- Jacket for small vessels, half-coil for large

### Sterilization
- D-value: time for 1 log reduction at T
- Z-value: 10 °C for most bacteria
- Del factor: $\nabla = t \cdot 10^{(T-121)/Z}$ for isothermal
- Design target: ∇ ≥ 45 for batch media sterilization

### Scale-Up Criteria
- Constant P/V (most common)
- Constant kLa
- Constant mixing time ($t_m \cdot N$ = const)
- Constant impeller tip speed
- Constant shear rate (Kolmogorov: $\dot{\gamma} = \sqrt{P/V / \mu}$)

### Aseptic / Sterile Boundary
- Steam block, O-ring, or 0.2 µm hydrophobic vent filter
- SIP at 121 °C, 15 psig, 20–30 min
- CIP with NaOH (1–2%) and HNO₃ (0.5–1%) rinse

### Foam Control
- Mechanical: centrifugal foam breaker on shaft
- Chemical: PPG or silicone antifoam (sparingly — affects kLa)

### Sensor Suite (mandatory)
- pH, DO, T, foam, agitation speed, gas flow, weight, level

### Key Take-Home Rule
> A bioreactor is a **closed, sterile, mixed, aerated, temperature-controlled, instrumented, scalable vessel**. If any one of these attributes is missing, the design is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perfusion Systems, Products of Animal Cell Cultures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Perfusion Systems, Products of Animal Cell Cultures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Perfusion Systems — Formal Definition

A **Perfusion Bioreactor System** is a continuous-mode animal cell culture configuration in which **fresh nutrient medium is continuously fed** into the culture vessel while **spent medium (containing the product and metabolites) is continuously withdrawn**, with the **cells retained inside the bioreactor** through a physical retention device (filter, membrane, or sedimentation device). Unlike batch or fed-batch, perfusion decouples the **residence time of the fluid** from the **residence time of the cells**, allowing the cells to remain in a productive, high-density state for extended durations ranging from **30 days to over 6 months**.

Mathematically, the system is characterized by a dilution rate (perfusion rate) $D$ defined in Equation 1 of Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Perfusion is the preferred industrial mode for **labile products** (e.g., monoclonal antibodies with non-human glycosylation, viral vaccines where continuous harvest is required) because the product is removed rapidly from the harsh bioreactor environment, minimizing degradation and proteolytic cleavage.

## 1.2 Products of Animal Cell Cultures — Formal Definition

Animal cell cultures are used to manufacture a class of complex, high-molecular-weight, post-translationally modified biopharmaceuticals — predominantly **glycoproteins** — that **cannot be correctly folded, glycosylated, or assembled by microbial or yeast systems**. These include monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), viral vaccines, recombinant therapeutic proteins, hormones, growth factors, interferons, and tissue plasminogen activator (tPA).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Aquarium Analogy for Perfusion**
>
> Imagine a **goldfish aquarium with an automatic filtration system**. Fresh water constantly drips in from a tap, and dirty water overflows out through a drain — **but the fish are kept inside by a mesh filter**. The fish (cells) get unlimited fresh water (nutrients) and their waste (ammonia, lactate) is constantly flushed away.
>
> Now imagine if instead you put the fish in a small bowl, fed them once, and waited — they would slowly suffocate in their own waste. **Perfusion = the aquarium; Batch = the bowl.**

For **animal cell products**, think of them as **luxury handbags** that only an artisan (mammalian cell) can stitch properly. Bacteria (cheap, fast workers) cannot sew in the right thread color (glycan structure), so even if the bag is "made," it does not look or function correctly when worn by the patient.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Typical perfusion rate (D):** 0.5 – 4.0 volume exchanges per day (1 – 8 reactor volumes per day)
- **Achievable viable cell density:** $(1\text{–}10) \times 10^7$ cells/mL (up to **$2 \times 10^8$ cells/mL** in advanced systems)
- **Run duration:** 30 – 180 days (vs. 7 – 14 days in fed-batch)
- **Productivity gain over fed-batch:** 5-fold to 25-fold for the same bioreactor footprint
- **Working volume of a typical industrial perfusion bioreactor:** 50 – 2000 L

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of Perfusion

The logical sequence of events in a perfusion system is:

1. **Cells are inoculated** into the bioreactor at low density (typically $0.2 \times 10^6$ to $0.5 \times 10^6$ cells/mL).
2. **Cells grow exponentially** to a high density, consuming glucose and glutamine, producing lactate and ammonia.
3. **At the target cell density, perfusion is initiated**: fresh medium enters at flow rate $F$, and spent medium leaves at the same flow rate $F$ (volume is constant).
4. **A cell retention device** (spin filter, hollow fiber, acoustic settler, centrifugal separator, or ATF — Alternating Tangential Flow) prevents cells from escaping in the harvest stream.
5. **Cells remain in a pseudo-steady state** — growth rate $\mu$ equals the perfusion dilution rate $D$, or is suppressed by anti-apoptotic strategies (e.g., temperature shift, growth factor withdrawal) to redirect carbon toward product synthesis.
6. **Harvest stream** — clarified, cell-free product — proceeds to downstream purification.

## 2.2 Classification of Perfusion Systems

| **S.No** | **Perfusion System Type** | **Retention Mechanism** | **Typical Operating Cell Density** | **Industrial Example** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Spin-Filter Perfusion | Internal rotating cylindrical mesh (10 – 25 µm pore) | $(1\text{–}5) \times 10^6$ cells/mL | Early mAb production (1980s) |
| 2 | Hollow-Fiber Perfusion | Ultrafiltration capillary membrane (MWCO 10 – 50 kDa) | $(1\text{–}5) \times 10^7$ cells/mL | Inoculum preparation, vaccine |
| 3 | Acoustic Perfusion | Ultrasonic standing-wave cell settler | $(1\text{–}5) \times 10^7$ cells/mL | mAb production (modern) |
| 4 | Centrifugal Perfusion (Centritech) | Continuous disc-stack centrifuge | $(1\text{–}10) \times 10^7$ cells/mL | High-density CHO cultures |
| 5 | ATF (Alternating Tangential Flow) | Hollow-fiber cartridge with bidirectional flow | $(5\text{–}20) \times 10^7$ cells/mL | Modern industrial CHO, PER.C6 |
| 6 | Microcarrier-Based Perfusion | Cells attached to microcarriers (Cytodex) retained by settling | $(1\text{–}3) \times 10^7$ cells/mL | Vero, HEK293 for vaccines |
| 7 | Microencapsulation | Cells trapped in alginate/poly-L-lysine beads | $(5\text{–}20) \times 10^7$ cells/mL | Hybridoma monoclonal antibodies |
| 8 | Membrane Bioreactor | Flat-sheet or hollow-fiber membrane on shell side | $(5\text{–}30) \times 10^7$ cells/mL | High-value mAb, virus |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Cell Retention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why retain cells instead of letting them wash out?**
>
> At dilution rates $D$ exceeding the maximum specific growth rate $\mu_{\max}$ of the cells (typically $\mu_{\max} = 0.02\text{–}0.04 \text{ h}^{-1}$ for CHO cells), a normal chemostat would wash out all cells. By retaining cells **physically**, perfusion overcomes the kinetic washout limit, achieving $D \gg \mu_{\max}$ while cells stay in the vessel at very high densities. This is the central engineering trick that makes perfusion powerful.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **Boundary / Operating Condition** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $D = \dfrac{F}{V_w}$ | Perfusion rate / Dilution rate | h⁻¹ or day⁻¹ | $F$ = inlet/outlet volumetric flow rate; $V_w$ = working volume |
| $Q_p = D \cdot P$ | Volumetric productivity | mg/(L·h) | $P$ = product concentration in harvest |
| $\text{CSPR} = \dfrac{F}{X \cdot V_w}$ | Cell-specific perfusion rate | nL/(cell·day) | Industry benchmark: 50 – 200 pL/(cell·day) |
| $\mu = D$ (for retained cells at SS) | Pseudo-steady state growth = dilution | h⁻¹ | Valid only if cells divide; suppressed in production phase |
| $\tau = \dfrac{V_w}{F} = \dfrac{1}{D}$ | Hydraulic residence time of fluid | h | Independent of cell residence time |
| $t_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{V_w \cdot X}{F \cdot X_{\text{out}}}$ | Mean cell residence time | days | If $X_{\text{out}} \to 0$, $t_{\text{cell}} \to \infty$ |
| $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Yield of cells on substrate | g/g | Glucose yield: $\sim 0.5\text{–}1.0 \times 10^9$ cells/g glucose |
| $\nu_{P/X} = q_P = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | Specific product formation rate | pg/(cell·day) | CHO mAb: 20 – 50 pg/(cell·day) |
| $\text{Fold gain} = \dfrac{Q_p^{\text{perf}}}{Q_p^{\text{batch}}}$ | Productivity advantage | dimensionless | Typical: 5× to 25× |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU TIP:** When exam questions ask "Why perfusion gives higher productivity than batch?", the answer must invoke the formula $Q_p = D \cdot P$. In batch, $D = 0$ after inoculation, so productivity is bounded by the final $P$ divided by total cycle time. In perfusion, even modest $P$ values, when multiplied by a continuous $D$, yield an integrated productivity that exceeds batch.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **mAb manufacturing:** Modern perfusion-ATF systems run continuously for 60 – 90 days, generating grams of mAb per liter of reactor per day.
- **Viral vaccine production:** Continuous harvest of virus from infected Vero or HEK293 cells reduces residence time of labile virus at the inactivation-prone 37 °C environment.
- **Cell-based meat & cellular agriculture:** Perfusion is being adopted for high-density muscle-cell expansion.
- **Stem cell expansion for cell therapy:** GMP-compliant perfusion bioreactors (e.g., PBS Biotech's Vertical-Wheel) expand mesenchymal stem cells to therapeutic doses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Mass Balance for Perfusion (Cells and Substrate)

### Step 1: Define the System and Variables

Consider a stirred-tank perfusion bioreactor with working volume $V_w$. 
- Fresh medium enters at volumetric flow rate $F$ with substrate concentration $S_0$ and **zero cells**.
- Harvest leaves at flow rate $F$ with substrate concentration $S$ and cell concentration $X_{\text{out}}$.
- Cells are retained such that $X_{\text{out}} = \eta \cdot X$ where $\eta$ is the leakage fraction ($\eta \ll 1$).

### Step 2: Cell Mass Balance

Accumulation = Inflow − Outflow + Growth

$$\frac{d(X V_w)}{dt} = F \cdot 0 - F \cdot X_{\text{out}} + \mu X V_w$$

Since $V_w$ is constant:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -\frac{F}{V_w} X_{\text{out}} + \mu X = -D \cdot \eta X + \mu X$$

### Step 3: Apply Perfect Retention Assumption

For an **ideal perfusion** with full retention, $\eta = 0$, so $X_{\text{out}} = 0$. The equation simplifies to:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$$

This is identical to a batch system — cells grow exponentially until nutrients deplete, inhibitors accumulate, or some artificial growth arrest is applied (e.g., temperature shift from 37 °C to 33 °C, or glutamine withdrawal).

### Step 4: Pseudo-Steady-State Operating Mode

In industrial perfusion, cells are often **chemostatically balanced** such that their growth rate equals the dilution rate created by the small leakage $\eta$:

$$\mu_{\text{net}} = D \cdot \eta$$

This means cells divide just fast enough to replace the ones leaking out — a "**pseudo-steady-state**" with constant $X$.

### Step 5: Substrate Mass Balance

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = D S_0 - D S - \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

### Step 6: Steady-State Solution for Substrate

At $dS/dt = 0$ and substituting $\mu = D \cdot \eta$:

$$D S_0 - D S = \frac{D \eta X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

$$S = S_0 - \frac{\eta X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The steady-state substrate concentration $S$ depends **only** on the **leakage fraction $\eta$** and the **cell density $X$**, not on the perfusion rate $D$ itself. This is the **key insight**: once $X$ is set by some control strategy (e.g., bleed of excess cells), $S$ adjusts automatically. This decoupling is why perfusion is so robust for industrial use.

### Step 8: Calculate Volumetric Productivity

$$Q_p = D \cdot P = D \cdot (q_P \cdot X)$$

Where $q_P$ is the specific production rate. Substituting:

$$Q_p = D \cdot q_P \cdot X$$

This shows that productivity scales **linearly with perfusion rate** and **linearly with cell density**. Doubling either parameter doubles productivity — a powerful design lever.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Comparison of Perfusion vs. Fed-Batch Productivity

Let us prove that perfusion outperforms fed-batch for the same reactor and final cell density.

**Assumptions:**
- Fed-batch: $V_w = V$, $X_{\text{final}} = X$, product titer $P_{\text{FB}}$, batch time $t_{\text{FB}} = 14$ days.
- Perfusion: $V_w = V$, $X = X$, steady-state titer $P_{\text{perf}}$, perfusion rate $D$.

**Fed-batch average productivity:**

$$\overline{Q}_{p,\text{FB}} = \frac{P_{\text{FB}}}{t_{\text{FB}}}$$

**Perfusion average productivity (over long run time):**

$$\overline{Q}_{p,\text{perf}} = D \cdot P_{\text{perf}}$$

**Ratio (fold gain):**

$$\text{Fold} = \frac{D \cdot P_{\text{perf}} \cdot t_{\text{FB}}}{P_{\text{FB}}}$$

### Numerical Example (Plug-in)

- $D = 1 \text{ day}^{-1}$, $P_{\text{perf}} = 0.5 \text{ g/L}$, $P_{\text{FB}} = 5 \text{ g/L}$, $t_{\text{FB}} = 14$ days:

$$\text{Fold} = \frac{1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 14}{5} = \frac{7}{5} = 1.4$$

With $D = 2 \text{ day}^{-1}$, $X = 5\times$ higher, $P_{\text{perf}} = 1 \text{ g/L}$:

$$\text{Fold} = \frac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 14}{5} = 5.6$$

This shows that even though the **perfusion titer is much lower** than the fed-batch titer, the **continuous operation** and **higher cell density** combine to give **multi-fold productivity gains** — typically 5× to 25× in real systems.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision: Perfusion vs. Fed-Batch vs. Batch

| **Parameter** | **Batch** | **Fed-Batch** | **Perfusion** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Volumetric productivity $Q_p$ | Lowest | High | **Highest** |
| Peak titer $P$ | Low | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Cell density achieved | $1\text{–}3 \times 10^6$/mL | $5\text{–}15 \times 10^6$/mL | **$10\text{–}100 \times 10^6$/mL** |
| Run time | 5 – 10 days | 10 – 18 days | **30 – 180 days** |
| Downstream load (volume processed) | Low | Moderate | **High** (continuous) |
| Product quality consistency | Variable | Variable | **Most consistent** |
| Capital cost | Lowest | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Operational complexity | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| Suitable for | Inoculum | Most mAbs, vaccines | **Labile mAbs, vaccines, stem cells** |
| Washout constraint | None | None | **Overcome by retention device** |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Major Products of Animal Cell Culture

| **Product Class** | **Specific Product** | **Cell Line Used** | **Clinical Indication** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs) | Rituximab, Trastuzumab, Bevacizumab, Adalimumab, Pembrolizumab | CHO, NS0, Sp2/0 | Cancer, autoimmune, inflammatory |
| Recombinant Coagulation Factors | Factor VIII, Factor IX | CHO, BHK | Hemophilia A and B |
| Recombinant Hormones | Erythropoietin (EPO), Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) | CHO | Anemia, infertility |
| Thrombolytics | Tissue Plasminogen Activator (tPA / Alteplase) | CHO | Acute myocardial infarction, stroke |
| Antiviral / Cytokines | Interferon-α, -β, -γ; Interleukin-2 | CHO, HeLa derivatives | Hepatitis, MS, cancer immunotherapy |
| Enzyme Replacement | Imiglucerase (glucocerebrosidase) | CHO | Gaucher's disease |
| Viral Vaccines | Polio (inactivated), Measles, Mumps, Rubella, Rabies, Influenza, Yellow fever, Hepatitis A, Varicella | Vero, MRC-5, WI-38, MDCK, Chick embryo fibroblasts | Prophylactic vaccination |
| Viral Vectors for Gene Therapy | AAV, lentiviral vectors | HEK293, HEK293T | Spinal muscular atrophy, CAR-T manufacture |
| Cell Therapy Products | Mesenchymal stem cells, iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes | MSC primary, iPSC | Regenerative medicine, heart failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these MUST be made in animal cells:**
> 1. **Glycosylation** — Human-compatible N-glycans (terminal sialic acid, core fucose patterns) are only correctly assembled by mammalian enzymes.
> 2. **Disulfide bond isomerization & protein folding** — Complex multi-domain proteins require chaperones (BiP, calnexin, calreticulin) present only in eukaryotes.
> 3. **γ-Carboxylation** — Vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X, Protein C) require this modification; done by CHO after vitamin K supplementation.
> 4. **Virus assembly & budding** — Replication-competent viral vaccines need host machinery for entry, uncoating, transcription, assembly, and egress.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Perfusion Bioreactor Functional Architecture

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    node1[Fresh Media Reservoir S0] --> node2[Feed Pump F]
    node2 --> node3[Bioreactor Working Volume Vw]
    node3 --> node4[Cells Retained by Retention Device]
    node4 --> node3
    node3 --> node5[Harvest Pump F]
    node5 --> node6[Cell-Free Harvest Stream P]
    node6 --> node7[Downstream Capture Chromatography]
    node3 --> node8[Sensor Array: DO pH T Glucose Lactate]
    node8 --> node9[Process Control System DCS]
    node9 --> node2
    node9 --> node5

    style node1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style node3 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style node4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style node6 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C
    style node7 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F
    style node9 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The control system (DCS) measures pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), temperature, glucose, and lactate via the sensor array, and adjusts the feed and harvest pump speeds to maintain the target perfusion rate. The retention device is the heart of the system — without it, all cells wash out.
