# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q1. Define perfusion culture. List any two cell retention devices used in perfusion bioreactors.**

**Model Answer:**

Perfusion culture is a continuous-mode animal cell cultivation method in which **fresh medium is continuously supplied** to the bioreactor and **spent medium is continuously withdrawn**, while **cells are retained inside** by a physical retention device. This decouples fluid residence time from cell residence time, enabling very high cell densities and long production runs.

**Two cell retention devices:**
1. **Spin filter** — a rotating cylindrical screen (10 – 25 µm pore) inside the vessel.
2. **Hollow-fiber cartridge / ATF system** — an external ultrafiltration module that returns retentate (cells) and lets permeate (cell-free product) pass.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q2. Why are monoclonal antibodies produced in CHO cells rather than in *E. coli*?**

**Model Answer:**

Monoclonal antibodies are large (~150 kDa) **glycoproteins** requiring:
1. **Correct disulfide bond formation** in both heavy and light chains — assisted by mammalian chaperones.
2. **Human-compatible N-glycosylation** (e.g., G0, G1, G2 glycoforms) on the Fc region — required for **FcRn receptor binding**, **effector function (ADCC)**, and **serum half-life**.
3. **Secretory pathway folding quality control** — only mammalian ER/Golgi performs this faithfully.

*E. coli* cannot glycosylate proteins and tends to form **inclusion bodies** with misfolded antibodies. Therefore CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary) cells are the industry standard.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question. Students must answer **one full question (with sub-parts a and b)** out of a choice of two. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total marks awarded: 14.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**Q.A. (a) Explain the principle and operation of a hollow-fiber perfusion bioreactor. Describe its advantages and limitations for animal cell culture. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A hollow-fiber bioreactor consists of a bundle of hollow fibers (made of polysulfone or polyethersulfone) housed inside a cylindrical cartridge. The cartridge has two compartments: the **intracapillary (lumen) space** and the **extracapillary (shell) space**. Animal cells are inoculated into the shell side, where they are trapped between the fibers. Fresh medium is pumped through the lumen; nutrients (low-MW solutes like glucose, amino acids, oxygen) diffuse **through the fiber membrane** (MWCO 10 – 50 kDa) into the cell compartment, while metabolic waste (lactate, ammonia) diffuses back. High-MW products (e.g., mAbs) are **retained on the shell side** because they are larger than the MWCO and accumulate at very high concentrations.

**Operation Steps:**
1. Sterilize cartridge (autoclave or SIP).
2. Inoculate cells into shell side via a port.
3. Circulate medium through lumen at low shear.
4. Maintain pH, DO, and temperature at set points.
5. Periodically harvest product from shell side (batch-wise) or operate as perfusion by continuously exchanging medium.

**Advantages:**
1. Achieves **cell densities of $10^8$ cells/mL** — far higher than stirred-tank.
2. **Low shear stress** — ideal for fragile anchorage-dependent cells (e.g., hybridomas, Vero).
3. Product concentration in shell side is **10 – 100× higher** than in stirred-tank — reduces downstream load.
4. Provides **3-D tissue-like architecture**, supporting differentiation and polarized secretion.

**Limitations:**
1. **Diffusion limitations** at high cell densities — nutrient gradients and waste accumulation in the cell-packed shell.
2. **Difficult to scale up** beyond ~5 L industrially due to membrane area constraints and gradient issues.
3. **Cell harvesting for counting** is problematic — cannot sample cells directly.
4. **Membrane fouling and clogging** over long runs.

**Diagram reference:** (See Section 4.2, External Filtration branch — Hollow Fiber Cartridge node)

---

**Q.A. (b) A perfusion bioreactor of 100 L working volume is operated at a perfusion rate of 1.5 reactor volumes per day. The steady-state viable cell density is $8 \times 10^6$ cells/mL. The steady-state antibody concentration in the harvest stream is 0.45 g/L. Calculate: (i) Volumetric productivity, (ii) Total antibody harvested per day, (iii) Cell-specific production rate $q_P$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_w = 100 \text{ L}$
- $D = 1.5 \text{ day}^{-1}$
- $X = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ cells/mL} = 8 \times 10^9 \text{ cells/L}$
- $P = 0.45 \text{ g/L}$

**(i) Volumetric Productivity $Q_p$:**

$$Q_p = D \cdot P = 1.5 \text{ day}^{-1} \times 0.45 \text{ g/L} = 0.675 \text{ g/(L·day)}$$

**(ii) Total Antibody Harvested Per Day:**

$$\text{Total} = Q_p \times V_w = 0.675 \text{ g/(L·day)} \times 100 \text{ L} = 67.5 \text{ g/day}$$

Alternatively:
$$F = D \cdot V_w = 1.5 \times 100 = 150 \text{ L/day}$$
$$\text{Total} = F \cdot P = 150 \text{ L/day} \times 0.45 \text{ g/L} = 67.5 \text{ g/day}$$

**(iii) Cell-Specific Production Rate $q_P$:**

$$q_P = \frac{Q_p}{X} = \frac{0.675 \text{ g/(L·day)}}{8 \times 10^9 \text{ cells/L}}$$

$$q_P = 8.44 \times 10^{-11} \text{ g/(cell·day)} = 84.4 \text{ pg/(cell·day)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Lose 2 marks):** Students often confuse $X$ in cells/mL with cells/L. Always convert to **consistent units** before plugging into equations. Here, $8 \times 10^6 \text{ cells/mL} = 8 \times 10^9 \text{ cells/L}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**Q.B. (a) Describe the major classes of products obtained from animal cell cultures, with at least two specific examples for each class. Explain why these products cannot be made in bacterial systems. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Class 1 — Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs):**
- *Examples:* Rituximab (anti-CD20, lymphoma), Trastuzumab (anti-HER2, breast cancer), Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF, colorectal cancer), Adalimumab (anti-TNFα, rheumatoid arthritis).
- *Why not in bacteria:* Requires glycosylation for Fc effector function; requires proper disulfide bond formation.

**Class 2 — Recombinant Coagulation Factors:**
- *Examples:* Factor VIII (Hemophilia A), Factor IX (Hemophilia B), Protein C (sepsis).
- *Why not in bacteria:* Requires **γ-carboxylation** of glutamic acid residues (vitamin K-dependent); only mammalian cells perform this post-translational modification.

**Class 3 — Recombinant Hormones:**
- *Examples:* Erythropoietin (EPO — anemia), Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH — infertility), Human Growth Hormone (hGH — though some are made in *E. coli*).
- *Why not in bacteria (for EPO):* EPO is heavily glycosylated; **deglycosylated EPO is biologically inactive** because sialic acid residues determine serum half-life via prevention of hepatic clearance.

**Class 4 — Thrombolytics:**
- *Examples:* Tissue Plasminogen Activator (tPA / Alteplase) — used in acute myocardial infarction and ischemic stroke.
- *Why not in bacteria:* Requires **N-glycosylation at Asn-117, Asn-184, and Asn-448** for fibrin-binding affinity and serum stability.

**Class 5 — Viral Vaccines:**
- *Examples:* Inactivated polio vaccine (Vero cells), Rabies vaccine (Vero or chick embryo fibroblasts), Influenza vaccine (MDCK cells), MMR vaccines (MRC-5, WI-38).
- *Why not in bacteria:* Viruses require **eukaryotic host machinery** for entry, replication, assembly, and egress; bacteria cannot support viral replication.

**Class 6 — Cytokines / Interferons / Interleukins:**
- *Examples:* Interferon-α (hepatitis B/C), Interferon-β (multiple sclerosis), Interleukin-2 (cancer immunotherapy).
- *Why not in bacteria:* Glycosylation affects pharmacokinetics and receptor binding affinity.

**Class 7 — Viral Vectors for Gene Therapy:**
- *Examples:* AAV vectors (Zolgensma for spinal muscular atrophy), Lentiviral vectors (CAR-T cell manufacturing).
- *Why not in bacteria:* Empty viral capsids cannot self-assemble in prokaryotes.

---

**Q.B. (b) Compare perfusion culture with fed-batch culture for mAb production in CHO cells. Use the productivity formula to justify why perfusion gives higher volumetric productivity. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **Fed-Batch** | **Perfusion** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Operating mode | Sequential bolus feeds | Continuous feed + harvest |
| Cell density | $5\text{–}15 \times 10^6$/mL | $10\text{–}100 \times 10^6$/mL |
| Peak mAb titer | $5\text{–}15 \text{ g/L}$ | $0.3\text{–}2 \text{ g/L}$ |
| Cycle time | $10\text{–}18$ days | $30\text{–}180$ days |
| Product stability | Variable (degrades over long batch) | Consistent (continuously removed) |
| Downstream volume | Single batch | Continuous (large total) |

**Productivity Formula Justification:**

For fed-batch, average volumetric productivity:

$$\overline{Q}_{p,\text{FB}} = \frac{P_{\text{final}}}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$$

For perfusion:

$$\overline{Q}_{p,\text{perf}} = D \cdot P_{\text{ss}}$$

For a typical mAb process:
- Fed-batch: $P_{\text{final}} = 6 \text{ g/L}$, $t_{\text{cycle}} = 14 \text{ days}$
$$\overline{Q}_{p,\text{FB}} = \frac{6}{14} \approx 0.43 \text{ g/(L·day)}$$

- Perfusion: $D = 1.5 \text{ day}^{-1}$, $P_{\text{ss}} = 1 \text{ g/L}$
$$\overline{Q}_{p,\text{perf}} = 1.5 \times 1 = 1.5 \text{ g/(L·day)}$$

**Fold gain:**
$$\frac{\overline{Q}_{p,\text{perf}}}{\overline{Q}_{p,\text{FB}}} = \frac{1.5}{0.43} \approx 3.5\times$$

By increasing $D$ and $X$, fold gains of **5× to 25×** are achievable in industrial settings, justifying the high capital cost of perfusion equipment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students write "perfusion is always better" — this is **incorrect**. For very stable products (e.g., mAbs with high thermal stability), fed-batch remains preferred because of **lower capital cost** and **simpler validation**. Perfusion's edge emerges primarily for **labile** products and **personalized cell-therapy** applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Perfusion Systems — Key Points
- Perfusion = **continuous feed + continuous harvest with cell retention**.
- Decouples fluid residence time ($\tau = 1/D$) from cell residence time.
- Retention devices: **spin filter, hollow fiber, ATF, acoustic settler, centrifuge, microcarriers, microencapsulation**.
- Operating perfusion rate: **0.5 – 4.0 reactor volumes/day** (industrial).
- Cell density: up to **$10^8$ cells/mL** in advanced systems.
- Run duration: **30 – 180 days** (vs. 7 – 14 in fed-batch).
- Productivity gain over fed-batch: **5× – 25×**.
- **Centrifugal (Centritech)** and **ATF** are modern industry favorites.

### Critical Formulas
- $D = F/V_w$
- $Q_p = D \cdot P$
- $\text{CSPR} = F/(X \cdot V_w)$
- $q_P = Q_p / X$
- $\overline{Q}_{p,\text{perf}} \gg \overline{Q}_{p,\text{FB}}$ for same $V_w$

### Products of Animal Cell Culture — Key Points
- **CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary)** cells produce >70% of approved therapeutic glycoproteins.
- mAbs (Rituximab, Trastuzumab, Pembrolizumab, etc.) require **CHO / NS0 / Sp2/0**.
- **Vero, MRC-5, WI-38, MDCK** are standard vaccine substrates.
- **HEK293** is dominant for **AAV and lentiviral vectors**.
- Critical PTMs: **N-glycosylation, γ-carboxylation, disulfide isomerization, sialylation**.
- Cannot be made in *E. coli* because bacteria lack: glycosylation machinery, γ-carboxylase, ER chaperones, secretory pathway.

### KTU 2024 Scheme Frequently-Tested Concepts
1. Definition of perfusion with diagram.
2. Comparison: Batch vs. Fed-batch vs. Perfusion.
3. Calculation of $Q_p$, $D$, $\text{CSPR}$, $q_P$ for any given problem.
4. Naming of cell retention devices with examples.
5. Listing of products (with at least 2 examples per class) and justification for mammalian production.

### Quick Mnemonic for Perfusion Devices — **"SHAKE-MAC"**
- **S**pin filter
- **H**ollow fiber
- **A**TF (Alternating Tangential Flow)
- **K** (no K — skip)
- **E**xternal centrifuge (Centritech)
- **M**icrocarrier
- **A**coustic settler
- **C**entrifugal settler

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bioreactor Considerations for Suspension Cultures, Immobilized Systems and Organ Cultures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bioreactor Considerations for Suspension Cultures, Immobilized Systems and Organ Cultures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Bioreactor Consideration" in Cell Culture Biotechnology?

In Bioprocess Engineering, the term **bioreactor configuration** refers to the engineered vessel and its hydrodynamic, mass-transfer, and mechanical environment in which biological cells (microbial, plant, animal, or insect) are propagated to produce biomass, metabolites, enzymes, monoclonal antibodies, vaccines, or even whole tissues. A *bioreactor consideration* is therefore the set of engineering design rules, constraints, and operational parameters that must be satisfied so that the **biological phase** (living cells) remains viable, metabolically active, and productive inside the **reactor phase** (the hardware).

For Module 4 of PCBBT502, the focus is narrowed to three distinct culture philosophies:

1. **Suspension Cultures** – Cells grow as free, dispersed individuals in a stirred or aerated liquid.
2. **Immobilized Cell Systems** – Cells are physically confined (entrapped, encapsulated, or surface-adsorbed) within or on a support matrix while the surrounding fluid flows past them.
3. **Organ Cultures / Tissue Cultures** – Three-dimensional tissue fragments or whole organs are maintained *ex vivo* with preserved histological architecture and differentiated function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCBBT502 / M4):**
> The student must be able to *compare and contrast* the design and operation of bioreactors used for animal/plant cell cultures (suspension vs. immobilized vs. organ). This is a **higher-order CO (CO4 – Analyze)** and appears regularly as a 14-mark structured question.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

| Culture System | Everyday Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Suspension Culture** | A swarm of bees in a hurricane-safe, well-ventilated greenhouse | Cells (bees) are free-floating; the air/medium (wind) must be controlled carefully because bees are fragile and easily damaged. |
| **Immobilized System** | Fish trapped in a floating net cage in a flowing river | Cells (fish) are held in a fixed lattice; the medium (river) flows past carrying nutrients and oxygen, but the fish are shielded from violent currents. |
| **Organ Culture** | A living flower kept fresh in a slow-drip hydroponic perfusion jar | The whole tissue (flower) is intact, delicate, and requires a gentle, continuous supply of nutrients and oxygen without mechanical disturbance. |

### 1.3 The Central Engineering Dilemma

The core trade-off that governs all three systems is captured by a single equation concept:

$$\text{Biological Productivity} = f\big(\text{Cell Density},\ \text{Specific Productivity},\ \text{Viability}\big)$$

To maximize this, the engineer must simultaneously satisfy:

- **Sufficient mass transfer** of $\text{O}_2$, nutrients, heat, and metabolites.
- **Minimal hydrodynamic shear** (because mammalian/insect/plant cells are **shear-sensitive** — typically tolerate shear rates $< 1\text{–}5\ \text{s}^{-1}$ whereas bacteria tolerate $> 1000\ \text{s}^{-1}$).
- **Sterility and sterility maintenance** in long-term perfusion.
- **Suitable microenvironment** (pH, $\text{pO}_2$, osmolality, $\text{pCO}_2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in the Industry:**
> The volumetric oxygen transfer coefficient, $\mathbf{k_La}$ (units: $\mathbf{h^{-1}}$ or $\mathbf{s^{-1}}$), is the **single most important parameter** for comparing bioreactor performance in cell culture. Typical target: $\mathbf{k_La \approx 1\text{–}15\ h^{-1}}$ for animal cell suspension; $\mathbf{k_La \approx 10\text{–}100\ h^{-1}}$ for microbial systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Suspension Culture Bioreactors

#### 2.1.1 Stirred-Tank Reactor (STR) – CSTR with Marine/Scaba Impellers
- Used most widely for **microbial, insect, and some robust animal cell lines** (e.g., CHO, hybridoma).
- Impeller type is **critical**: Rushton (high shear, used for bacteria) vs. **marine propeller / pitched-blade / Scaba (low shear, used for animal cells)**.
- Typical operating specific power input: $\mathbf{P/V \le 25\ W\ m^{-3}}$ for shear-sensitive cultures (vs. $> 1000\ \text{W m}^{-3}$ for bacteria).

#### 2.1.2 Airlift and Bubble-Column Reactors
- **No mechanical impeller** → low shear, ideal for fragile cells.
- Use **draft tube** to create internal circulation loop.
- Driving force: density difference between gassed and ungassed riser.
- $\mathbf{k_La}$ is lower than STR → acceptable for low $\text{O}_2$ demand of animal cells (~0.5–5 × 10⁻¹² g O₂/cell·h).

#### 2.1.3 Wave-Mixed / Orbitally Shaken Bioreactors
- Disposable, single-use bags rocked on a platform.
- Low shear, low $\text{k_La}$, used for seed-train and small-volume production.

**Operational Design Constraints (Why & How):**

| Parameter | Reason | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Low impeller speed (N) | Avoid Kolmogorov eddy length $< $ cell diameter | $N \approx 30\text{–}100\ \text{rpm}$ (animal cells) |
| Sparging with $\text{O}_2 / \text{N}_2$ | Pure $\text{O}_2$ at low flow avoids bubble rupture damage | $v_{sg} \approx 0.001\text{–}0.005\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ |
| Pluronic F-68 addition | Surfactant protects cells at bubble interface | $0.05\text{–}0.3\ \text{g L}^{-1}$ |
| pH control via $\text{CO}_2$ / $\text{NaHCO}_3$ | Maintain bicarbonate buffer | pH 7.0–7.4 |
| Temperature | Mammalian = 37 °C, Insect = 27–28 °C, Plant = 25 °C | tight control ±0.5 °C |

#### 2.1.4 Kolmogorov Eddy Length (Shear Criterion)

The critical shear theory for cell damage states that if the smallest turbulent eddy length scale $\eta$ is **smaller than the cell diameter** $d_c$, hydrodynamic damage will occur.

$$\eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$$

where $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of medium (m² s⁻¹) and $\varepsilon$ = local energy dissipation rate (W kg⁻¹).

> **Rule-of-thumb:** $\eta \ge 2 d_c$ ⇒ no shear damage.

---

### 2.2 Immobilized Cell Bioreactors

#### 2.2.1 Why Immobilize?
- **High cell density** (10⁷–10⁸ cells per mL of support vs. 10⁶ cells mL⁻¹ in suspension).
- **Cell reuse** → continuous operation possible.
- **Protection from shear** (cells shielded inside matrix).
- **Easier downstream separation** (cells ≠ product stream).
- **Reduced washout** at high dilution rates.

#### 2.2.2 Immobilization Methods

| Method | Mechanism | Example | Biomass Loading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Entrapment** | Cells physically trapped in polymer matrix | Ca-alginate, κ-carrageenan, agarose | High (≤ 30% w/v) |
| **Encapsulation** | Cells inside semi-permeable membrane | Polylysine–alginate microcapsules | High |
| **Adsorption** | Cells attach to surface | Microcarriers (Cytodex), DEAE-Sephadex | Surface-limited |
| **Covalent Bonding** | Chemical link to matrix | Glutaraldehyde-bound | Permanent |
| **Self-Aggregation** | Natural flocs/granules | *Zoogloea*, methanogenic granules | Variable |
| **Biofilm** | Surface-adhered consortium | Trickling filters, fluidized MBBR | Moderate |

#### 2.2.3 Bioreactor Types for Immobilized Cells

1. **Packed-Bed Reactor (PBR)**: Support particles stationary; liquid flows up/down.
   - Pros: simple, high cell density.
   - Cons: channelling, poor mass transfer at center, gas holdup issues.
2. **Fluidized-Bed Reactor (FBR)**: Particles suspended by upward liquid flow; high mass transfer.
3. **Hollow-Fiber Reactor**: Cells in shell side; medium in lumen; mimics capillary.
4. **Membrane Reactor**: Cells separated by UF/MF membrane.

#### 2.2.4 Mass-Transfer Limitation in Immobilized Systems (Key Concept)

Inside a spherical bead, the substrate (e.g., $\text{O}_2$ or glucose) diffuses inward and is consumed by reaction. The **Thiele modulus** $\phi$ and **effectiveness factor** $\eta_{eff}$ quantify this:

$$\phi = R \sqrt{\frac{\mu_{\max}}{D_S K_S}}$$

$$\eta_{eff} = \frac{\text{Actual rate}}{\text{Rate at surface conditions}}$$

For a first-order reaction in a sphere:

$$\eta_{eff} = \frac{3}{\phi}\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right]$$

If $\phi \gg 1$ → diffusion-limited (low effectiveness); if $\phi \ll 1$ → reaction-limited (effectiveness ≈ 1).

---

### 2.3 Organ / Tissue Culture Bioreactors

#### 2.3.1 Defining Feature
- Preserves **3-D architecture**, cell–cell and cell–matrix interactions, *in vivo*-like function.
- Used in **toxicology, drug testing, regenerative medicine, bio-artificial organs** (e.g., liver, pancreas).
- Nutrient transport occurs by **diffusion + perfusion**; if organ > 200–500 µm thick without vasculature, central necrosis occurs.

#### 2.3.2 Reactor Designs
- **Static organ culture** (e.g., Trowell chamber) – on a metal grid at gas–liquid interface.
- **Perfusion chamber** – continuous medium flow over tissue.
- **Rotating-wall bioreactor (RWB / HARV)** – low shear, simulated microgravity; used for tissue engineering scaffolds.
- **Hollow-fiber organ cartridges** – packed with parallel capillaries, cells in extracapillary space.

#### 2.3.3 Engineering Constraints
- **Diffusion limit** (Fick's 2nd law): $t_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{L^2}{4D_S}$.
  For $L = 1\ \text{mm}$ and $D_S = 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2\text{s}^{-1}$: $t_{\text{diff}} \approx 125\ \text{s}$.
  This is why **perfusion** is mandatory for organoids > 500 µm.
- **Controlled shear** (laminar flow, $\dot{\gamma} < 1\ \text{s}^{-1}$).
- **Physiological pulsatility** (e.g., vascular-mimetic flow) for vascular constructs.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in **SI** unless otherwise stated. Use $\vert$ or \mid for absolute values in tables.

| # | Quantity / Formula | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kolmogorov eddy length | $\eta = \left(\dfrac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$ | $1\text{–}100\ \mu\text{m}$ | Shear damage criterion |
| 2 | Mean specific power input | $\dfrac{\bar{\varepsilon}}{} = \dfrac{P}{\rho V}$ | $0.01\text{–}10^{3}\ \text{W kg}^{-1}$ | Reactor scale-up |
| 3 | Power number (impeller) | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^{3} D_i^{5}}$ | $0.3\text{–}6$ | Power draw |
| 4 | Tip speed | $u_{tip} = \pi N D_i$ | $0.2\text{–}2\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ | Shear proxy |
| 5 | Volumetric $\text{O}_2$ transfer coeff. | $k_La = k_L a$ | $1\text{–}100\ \text{h}^{-1}$ | Aeration capacity |
| 6 | OTR (oxygen transfer rate) | $OTR = k_La (C^{*}_{O_2} - C_L)$ | $\text{mol O}_2\ \text{L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$ | Oxygen balance |
| 7 | OUR (oxygen uptake rate) | $OUR = q_{O_2}\,X$ | $\text{mol O}_2\ \text{L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$ | Oxygen balance |
| 8 | Steady-state balance | $OTR = OUR$ | equality at design | Sizing aeration |
| 9 | Thiele modulus (sphere) | $\phi = R \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{D_S K_S}}$ | $0.01\text{–}10$ | Internal diffusion |
| 10 | Effectiveness factor | $\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{3}{\phi}\left(\coth\phi - \dfrac{1}{\phi}\right)$ | $0\text{–}1$ | Immobilized rate |
| 11 | Damköhler number | $Da = \dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{k_L a\, C^{*}_{O_2}/K_S}$ | dimensionless | External diffusion |
| 12 | Diffusion time in tissue | $t_{diff} = \dfrac{L^{2}}{4 D_S}$ | seconds–hours | Necrosis criterion |
| 13 | Carberry number | $Ca = \dfrac{R\,k_S}{D_S}$ | dimensionless | Internal/external mass-transfer comparison |
| 14 | Mixing time | $\theta_m \approx \dfrac{V}{Q_{loop}}$ | $10\text{–}1000\ \text{s}$ | Homogeneity |
| 15 | Cell density in carriers | $X_{im} = \dfrac{\text{cells}}{\text{g carrier}}$ | $10^{7}\text{–}10^{9}\ \text{cells g}^{-1}$ | Immobilized load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility in Industry:**
> - The combination $k_La$, $P/V$, and $\eta$ (Kolmogorov) is used as a **scale-up triad** in monoclonal antibody (mAb) manufacturing.
> - The effectiveness-factor plot $\eta_{eff}$ vs. $\phi$ is the single most used chart for designing immobilized-enzyme or immobilized-cell reactors (e.g., bioethanol fermentation with immobilized yeast in alginate).

---

### 2.5 Comparison Matrix — The KTU Examiner's Favourite

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a guaranteed 14-marker.** Memorise the *engineering rationale* column — examiners give full marks only when reasoning is shown.

| Feature | Suspension Culture | Immobilized System | Organ Culture |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell state | Free in medium | Trapped on/in support | Intact 3-D tissue |
| Typical reactor | STR, airlift, bubble column | Packed-bed, fluidized-bed, hollow-fibre | Perfusion chamber, RWB, hollow-fibre cartridge |
| Cell density | $10^{5}\text{–}10^{6}\ \text{cells mL}^{-1}$ | $10^{7}\text{–}10^{8}\ \text{cells mL}^{-1}$ of bed | Physiological |
| Shear tolerance required | Low–moderate (animal) | Shielded | Extremely low |
| Mass transfer | Bulk fluid controls | External film + internal pore diffusion | Diffusion + perfusion |
| Productivity | Moderate | High (continuous) | Function-specific |
| Product separation | Difficult | Easy (cell-free stream) | N/A (tissue is product) |
| Typical application | mAb, vaccines | Bioethanol, biosensors | Drug testing, organ implants |
| Key limiting step | $k_La$, shear | $\eta_{eff}$ (internal diffusion) | Diffusion depth |
| Engineering trade-off | Aeration vs. shear | Density vs. diffusion | Function vs. viability |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Numerical Mastery:** Three numerical patterns are tested. We will derive the *core math* for each, then work a typical 7-mark sub-question end-to-end.

---

### 3.1 Derivation A — Kolmogorov Eddy Length and Shear-Damage Criterion

**Starting point:** Turbulent kinetic energy cascades from large eddies (length $L$) down to small eddies (length $\eta$) where it is dissipated viscously.

At the Kolmogorov scale, viscous dissipation equals the local kinetic energy per unit mass:

$$\varepsilon \sim \frac{u_{\eta}^{2}}{\tau_{\eta}}$$

The characteristic velocity $u_{\eta}$ and time scale $\tau_{\eta}$ at this scale are:

$$u_{\eta} = \frac{\eta}{\tau_{\eta}}$$

Viscous force dominates ⇒ Reynolds number at this scale $\sim 1$:

$$\frac{u_{\eta}\,\eta}{\nu} \sim 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{\eta} = \frac{\eta^{2}}{\nu}$$

Substitute back into the dissipation equation:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{u_{\eta}^{2}}{\tau_{\eta}} = \frac{\eta^{2}/\tau_{\eta}^{2}}{\eta^{2}/\nu} = \frac{\nu}{\tau_{\eta}^{2}}$$

But $\tau_{\eta} = \eta^{2}/\nu$ so:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\nu}{(\eta^{2}/\nu)^{2}} = \frac{\nu^{3}}{\eta^{4}}$$

Rearranging for $\eta$:

$$\eta^{4} = \frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering rule of thumb:**

$$\eta \ge 2\, d_{\text{cell}}$$

Therefore the **maximum allowable local power dissipation** to keep animal cells (e.g., $d_c = 15\ \mu\text{m}$) undamaged in medium with $\nu = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2\text{s}^{-1}$ is:

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{\nu^{3}}{(2 d_c)^{4}} = \frac{(10^{-6})^{3}}{(30 \times 10^{-6})^{4}}$$

Compute numerator: $10^{-18}$. Compute denominator: $(3 \times 10^{-5})^{4} = 81 \times 10^{-20} = 8.1 \times 10^{-19}$.

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{10^{-18}}{8.1 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.23\ \text{W kg}^{-1}$$

Converting to $\text{W m}^{-3}$ (with $\rho \approx 1000\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$):

$$\frac{P}{V}_{\max} = \rho\, \varepsilon_{\max} \approx 1.23 \times 10^{3}\ \text{W m}^{-3} = 1.23\ \text{kW m}^{-3}$$

> **Conclusion:** For animal cells with $d_c = 15\ \mu\text{m}$, total power input must be kept **below ~ 1 kW m⁻³** — orders of magnitude lower than microbial reactors.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Effectiveness Factor for Immobilized Spherical Beads

**Assumptions:**
- First-order internal kinetics with respect to substrate S: $r_S = k_1\, C_S$.
- Fickian diffusion with constant $D_S$ inside the bead.
- Spherical geometry, no external mass-transfer resistance (then modify with Biot number if needed).

**Mass balance in a spherical shell of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:**

$$\text{Diffusion in} - \text{Diffusion out} - \text{Consumption} = 0$$

$$4\pi r^{2}D_S\frac{dC_S}{dr}\bigg|_{r} - 4\pi (r+dr)^{2}D_S\frac{dC_S}{dr}\bigg|_{r+dr} - 4\pi r^{2}dr \cdot k_1 C_S = 0$$

Expanding to the limit $dr \to 0$:

$$D_S \frac{d^{2}C_S}{dr^{2}} + \frac{2D_S}{r}\frac{dC_S}{dr} - k_1 C_S = 0$$

**Boundary conditions:**

$$C_S = C_{S,b} \quad \text{at}\ r = R \quad \text{(surface)}$$

$$\frac{dC_S}{dr} = 0 \quad \text{at}\ r = 0 \quad \text{(symmetry, finite flux)}$$

**Non-dimensionalisation:** Let $c = C_S/C_{S,b}$ and $x = r/R$. Equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^{2}c}{dx^{2}} + \frac{2}{x}\frac{dc}{dx} - \phi^{2} c = 0$$

where

$$\phi = R\sqrt{\frac{k_1}{D_S}}$$

**General solution:**

$$c(x) = \frac{1}{x}\left[A \sinh(\phi x) + B \cosh(\phi x)\right]$$

Apply symmetry at $x = 0$: $\sinh(0) = 0$, $\cosh(0) = 1$ ⇒ $B = 0$ (else $c \to \infty$).

$$c(x) = \frac{A \sinh(\phi x)}{x}$$

Apply surface condition $c(1) = 1$:

$$A = \frac{1}{\sinh(\phi)}$$

$$c(x) = \frac{\sinh(\phi x)}{x \sinh(\phi)}$$

**Rate of reaction inside bead:**

$$r_{\text{actual}} = \int_0^R k_1 C_S \cdot 4\pi r^{2}\, dr = 4\pi R D_S C_{S,b}\phi \left[\coth(\phi) - \frac{1}{\phi}\right]$$

**Rate if concentration were everywhere $C_{S,b}$:**

$$r_{\text{surface}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} k_1 C_{S,b}$$

**Effectiveness factor:**

$$\eta_{eff} = \frac{r_{\text{actual}}}{r_{\text{surface}}} = \frac{4\pi R D_S \phi [\coth\phi - 1/\phi]}{\frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} k_1 C_{S,b}} \cdot \frac{C_{S,b}}{1}$$

Substitute $\phi = R\sqrt{k_1/D_S}$:

$$\eta_{eff} = \frac{3 D_S \phi^{2}}{R^{2} k_1 \phi}[\coth\phi - 1/\phi] = \frac{3}{\phi}\left[\coth(\phi) - \frac{1}{\phi}\right] \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Typical 7-Mark Sub-Question

> **Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 style):**
> An immobilized-cell reactor uses spherical Ca-alginate beads of radius $R = 2\ \text{mm}$ containing yeast cells following zero-order kinetics with $r_S = k_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ for the limiting substrate. Effective diffusivity inside the bead is $D_S = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$, surface concentration $C_{S,b} = 5\ \text{mM}$, and bead loading = 0.5 kg beads per litre reactor.
> **Calculate the overall substrate consumption rate per litre of reactor and comment on whether the system is diffusion-limited.**

**Step 1 — Convert units and identify regime.**

For zero-order kinetics, the modified Thiele modulus is:

$$\phi_0 = R\sqrt{\frac{k_0}{D_S\,C_{S,b}}}$$

Compute $R = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $k_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$ (since $1\ \text{mol L}^{-1} = 1000\ \text{mol m}^{-3}$):

$$k_0 = 1.5\ \text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$C_{S,b} = 5\ \text{mM} = 5\ \text{mol m}^{-3}$$

$$\phi_0 = (2 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{\frac{1.5}{(1.2 \times 10^{-9})(5)}}$$

Denominator: $(1.2 \times 10^{-9})(5) = 6 \times 10^{-9}$.

Ratio: $1.5 / (6 \times 10^{-9}) = 2.5 \times 10^{8}$.

Square root: $\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{8}} = 1.581 \times 10^{4}\ \text{m}^{-1}$.

$$\phi_0 = (2 \times 10^{-3})(1.581 \times 10^{4}) = 31.62$$

**Step 2 — Check regime.** $\phi_0 \gg 1$ ⇒ **strongly diffusion-limited**. Internal concentration drops to zero somewhere inside the bead.

**Step 3 — Compute $\eta_{eff}$ for zero-order case (approximate formula when $\phi_0 > 2$):**

For zero-order kinetics, the asymptotic (strong-diffusion-limit) form is:

$$\eta_{eff} \approx 1 - \frac{(1-\beta)^{3}}{3\phi_0^{2}(1-\beta)} \quad \text{where } \beta = C_{S,\text{core}}/C_{S,b}$$

For zero-order with $\phi_0 = 31.6$, the bead is mostly zero-substrate; estimate:

$$\eta_{eff} \approx \frac{3}{2\phi_0} = \frac{3}{63.25} = 0.0474$$

(only ~ 4.7 % effectiveness — very poor!)

**Step 4 — Compute rate per bead volume.**

Bead volume: $V_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (2 \times 10^{-3})^{3} = 3.351 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^{3}$ per bead.

**Step 5 — Rate per litre of reactor.**

Bead loading = $0.5\ \text{kg L}^{-1}$. Assume bead density $\rho_b = 1050\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$ (alginate ≈ 1.05 g cm⁻³):

$$\text{Bead volume fraction} = \frac{0.5}{1050} = 4.76 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{ per L reactor}$$

Reactor volume in $\text{m}^{3}$: $1\ \text{L} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3}$.

Beads occupy: $4.76 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}$ per $\text{m}^{3}$ of reactor.

**Actual substrate consumption rate per $\text{m}^{3}$ reactor:**

$$r_{\text{vol,reactor}} = \eta_{eff} \cdot k_0 \cdot V_{\text{beads in reactor}}$$

$$= 0.0474 \times 1.5\ \text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1} \times 4.76 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{ (beads per m}^{3}\text{ reactor)}$$

$$= 0.0474 \times 1.5 \times 0.476$$

$$= 0.0474 \times 0.714 = 0.0338\ \text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Convert to $\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ (divide by 1000):**

$$r_{\text{vol,reactor}} = 3.38 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1} = 3.38 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mmol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on limiting step.**

Since $\phi_0 = 31.6$ and $\eta_{eff} \approx 0.047$, the bead is severely substrate-starved internally. **Internal diffusion is the rate-controlling step.** Solutions: (a) reduce bead size to $R \approx 0.2\ \text{mm}$ to drop $\phi_0$ by an order of magnitude, or (b) increase $C_{S,b}$ to drive more flux, or (c) increase $D_S$ by using a more porous matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When $\eta_{eff} < 0.5$, you **must** explicitly write "the system is diffusion-limited and **internal** mass transfer controls" — many students lose 2 marks by omitting the word "internal".

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Oxygen-Transfer Design for Suspension Culture

> **Problem:**
> Design the aeration rate for a 1000 L STR with hybridoma cells at $X = 1.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{cells mL}^{-1}$ and specific $\text{O}_2$ uptake $q_{O_2} = 4 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{g O}_2\ \text{cell}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$. The reactor should be operated with $C_L = 30\%\ \text{air saturation}$ and $C^{*}_{O_2} = 6.8\ \text{mg L}^{-1}$ (at 37 °C). Determine the required $k_La$ and the corresponding air-flow rate for the standard correlation $k_La = 0.025\, (P/V)^{0.55}\, (v_{sg})^{0.75}$, assuming $P/V = 15\ \text{W m}^{-3}$ (shear-limited).

**Step 1 — Compute OUR:**

$$OUR = q_{O_2}\, X = (4 \times 10^{-14})(1.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{cells mL}^{-1}\ \times 1000\ \text{mL L}^{-1})$$

$$X = 1.5 \times 10^{9}\ \text{cells L}^{-1}$$

$$OUR = (4 \times 10^{-14})(1.5 \times 10^{9}) = 6 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{g O}_2\ \text{L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Driving force:**

$$C^{*}_{O_2} - C_L = 6.8(1 - 0.3) = 6.8 \times 0.7 = 4.76\ \text{mg L}^{-1} = 4.76 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g L}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Required $k_La$ from $OTR = OUR$:**

$$k_La = \frac{OUR}{C^{*}_{O_2} - C_L} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-5}}{4.76 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0126\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_{sg}$:**

$$0.0126 = 0.025 \times (15)^{0.55} \times v_{sg}^{0.75}$$

Compute $(15)^{0.55}$: $e^{0.55 \ln 15} = e^{0.55 \times 2.708} = e^{1.489} \approx 4.43$.

$$0.0126 = 0.025 \times 4.43 \times v_{sg}^{0.75} = 0.1108 \times v_{sg}^{0.75}$$

$$v_{sg}^{0.75} = 0.1137 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{sg} = 0.1137^{1.333} \approx 0.072\ \text{m h}^{-1}$$

Convert to $\text{m s}^{-1}$: $v_{sg} = 0.072/3600 = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ — extremely low sparging, well within shear limits.

> **Conclusion:** A large (1000 L) hybridoma reactor can be aerated with very gentle sparging because the cellular oxygen demand is **tiny** compared to microbial systems — a hallmark of animal-cell culture design.

---

### 3.5 Organ-Culture Diffusion-Time Check

> **Problem:**
> A liver spheroid of diameter $1\ \text{mm}$ is cultured without perfusion. Will the centre become necrotic? Take $D_{O_2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$ in tissue, cell $O_2$ consumption $q = 1.4 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{mol O}_2\, \text{cell}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, cell density $X = 1.5 \times 10^{14}\ \text{cells m}^{-3}$.

**Step 1 — Compute maximum diffusion distance without necrosis** (Hill-Krylov approximation):

A tissue becomes necrotic when $L > L_{\max}$ where $L_{\max} = \sqrt{6 D_S C^{*}_{S,surf}/(q\,X)}$.

$$C^{*}_{O_2,surf} \approx 0.2\ \text{mol m}^{-3} \quad (\text{arterial-like})$$

$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.2}{(1.4 \times 10^{-18})(1.5 \times 10^{14})}}$$

Denominator: $(1.4 \times 10^{-18})(1.5 \times 10^{14}) = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}$.

Numerator: $6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.2 = 3.0 \times 10^{-9}$.

$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{3.0 \times 10^{-9}}{2.1 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{1.43 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.78 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 3.78\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare.** Spheroid radius is $0.5\ \text{mm}$, **less than** $L_{\max} = 3.78\ \text{mm}$.

**Conclusion:** At $1\ \text{mm}$ diameter, the spheroid is **safely within the diffusion limit**; centre remains oxygenated. However, the moment diameter exceeds $\sim 7.6\ \text{mm}$, central necrosis would set in — this is why large organ cultures **must** be perfused or vascularised.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 ESE pattern for PCBBT502 is:
> - **Part A:** 5 questions × 3 marks = 15 marks (Answer any 3, typically).
> - **Part B:** Module-wise choice. Module 4 carries ~ 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-question is 7 marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Why are airlift and bubble-column bioreactors preferred over conventional stirred-tank reactors for shear-sensitive animal cell cultures?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Airlift and bubble-column bioreactors **do not have mechanical impellers**, hence the local energy dissipation rate $\varepsilon$ is much lower than in stirred tanks. Kolmogorov eddy length $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ therefore remains **larger than the cell diameter**, preventing hydrodynamic damage.
- **(1 mark)** Mixing is achieved by sparged-gas-induced density-difference circulation through a **draft tube**, giving gentle, **uniform bulk flow** without high-shear impeller tip zones.
- **(1 mark)** Oxygen transfer is sufficient for the **low oxygen demand** of animal cells ($q_{O_2} \sim 10^{-14}\ \text{g/cell/h}$). Hence the **lower $k_La$** of airlift reactors is acceptable, eliminating the need for high-shear agitation.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Thiele modulus and effectiveness factor for an immobilized-cell system. State the limiting cases.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Thiele modulus** for a spherical bead is $\phi = R\sqrt{\mu_{\max}/(D_S K_S)}$. It is the **ratio of characteristic reaction rate to characteristic diffusion rate** inside the bead.
- **(1 mark)** **Effectiveness factor** $\eta_{eff}$ = (actual rate of reaction in bead) / (rate if entire bead were at surface conditions $C_{S,b}$). It lies in $(0, 1]$.
- **(1 mark)** **Limiting cases:** (i) $\phi \ll 1 \Rightarrow$ reaction-limited, $\eta_{eff} \to 1$; (ii) $\phi \gg 1 \Rightarrow$ diffusion-limited, $\eta_{eff} \to 3/\phi \to 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Comparison with Diffusion Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Pattern]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compare the design, operation, and engineering trade-offs of **suspension culture, immobilized cell, and organ culture bioreactors** with respect to (i) cell density achievable, (ii) mass-transfer regime, (iii) shear sensitivity, (iv) ease of product recovery, and (v) typical industrial application.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Immobilized yeast in spherical Ca-alginate beads ($R = 1.5\ \text{mm}$) follows **zero-order kinetics** for glucose consumption with $r_S = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$. Effective diffusivity $D_S = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$, surface glucose concentration $C_{S,b} = 8\ \text{mM}$. **(i)** Calculate the Thiele modulus $\phi_0$. **(ii)** Determine the effectiveness factor and the **actual rate per bead** in $\text{mol s}^{-1}$ per $\text{m}^{3}$ of reactor (assume bead loading = $0.4\ \text{kg L}^{-1}$, $\rho_b = 1050\ \text{kg m}^{-3}$). **(iii)** State **two engineering modifications** to improve effectiveness.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

| Criterion | Suspension Culture | Immobilized System | Organ Culture |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Cell density | $10^{5}\text{–}10^{6}\ \text{mL}^{-1}$ | $10^{7}\text{–}10^{8}\ \text{mL}^{-1}$ of bed | Physiological $10^{8}\text{–}10^{9}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ |
| (ii) Mass-transfer | Controlled by bulk $k_La$ (gas–liquid) | Internal pore diffusion controls → $\eta_{eff} < 1$ | Diffusion + perfusion; depth-limited |
| (iii) Shear sensitivity | **High**; need $P/V \le 25\ \text{W m}^{-3}$, low impeller tip speed | **Low**; cells shielded in matrix | **Extreme**; requires laminar flow |
| (iv) Product recovery | Centrifugation / filtration needed | **Easy**, cell-free stream | N/A (tissue is product) |
| (v) Application | mAb, vaccines (CHO, hybridoma) | Bioethanol, biosensors, biocatalysis | Drug testing, regenerative medicine |

**(Each row fetches 1 mark; bold justification adds 0.5–1 mark.)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**(i) Thiele modulus (zero-order):**

$$\phi_0 = R\sqrt{\frac{k_0}{D_S\,C_{S,b}}}$$

Convert units: $k_0 = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1} = 4\ \text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$. $C_{S,b} = 8\ \text{mM} = 8\ \text{mol m}^{-3}$. $R = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$.

$$\phi_0 = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{\frac{4}{(1.0 \times 10^{-9})(8)}}$$

$$= (1.5 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{5 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$= (1.5 \times 10^{-3})(2.236 \times 10^{4}) = 33.5$$

**$\phi_0 = 33.5$ (strongly diffusion-limited).** [2 marks]

**(ii) Effectiveness factor (zero-order, strong diffusion limit):**

$$\eta_{eff} \approx \frac{3}{2\phi_0} = \frac{3}{2(33.5)} = 0.0448$$

**$\eta_{eff} \approx 0.045$ (only ~ 4.5 %).** [1 mark]

**Actual rate per $\text{m}^{3}$ reactor:**

Bead volume fraction = $0.4 / 1050 = 3.81 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}$ per L reactor = $0.381\ \text{m}^{3}$ beads per $\text{m}^{3}$ reactor.

$$r_{\text{reactor}} = \eta_{eff} \cdot k_0 \cdot V_{\text{beads}} = 0.0448 \times 4 \times 0.381 = 0.0683\ \text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

[2 marks]

**(iii) Engineering modifications to improve $\eta_{eff}$:** [2 marks, 1 each]
- (1) **Reduce bead radius** to $R \approx 0.2\ \text{mm}$ → $\phi_0$ drops by factor 7.5 → $\eta_{eff}$ rises to ~ 0.34.
- (2) **Increase $D_S$** by using a more porous matrix (e.g., macroporous alginate or chitosan) or by **increasing $C_{S,b}$** through higher bulk glucose concentration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice (Reactor Design Focus)*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss in detail the **engineering considerations** for designing a bioreactor for **suspension culture of hybridoma cells** producing a monoclonal antibody. Cover at least **six design parameters**, justifying each with the underlying physical / biological principle.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 50 L hollow-fibre bioreactor is used for organ culture of a hepatocyte construct. The cells consume $\text{O}_2$ at a specific rate $q_{O_2} = 4.5 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{g/cell/h}$ at a density $X = 5 \times 10^{7}\ \text{cells cm}^{-3}$ of tissue. Effective $\text{O}_2$ diffusivity in tissue $D = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$. Surface oxygen concentration $C_{O_2}^{s} = 0.2\ \text{mol m}^{-3}$ (arterial). Calculate the **maximum tissue depth** $L_{\max}$ that can be sustained without central necrosis. If the construct is grown as a cylinder of $1.5\ \text{cm}$ thickness, will it survive? Justify. State **two perfusion strategies** to overcome any limitation.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

A complete 7-mark answer should include **any six** of the following with engineering justification:

1. **Low-shear impeller choice** — marine propeller or pitched blade (not Rushton); $P/V \le 25\ \text{W m}^{-3}$ so that Kolmogorov eddy $\eta > 2 d_c$. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Aeration by mild sparging or membrane** — pure $\text{O}_2$ / $\text{CO}_2$ / $\text{N}_2$ mix at low $v_{sg}$ to avoid bubble rupture damage; **Pluronic F-68 (0.05–0.3 g L⁻¹)** as bubble surfactant. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **$k_La$ matching** — at $X = 10^{6}\ \text{cells mL}^{-1}$, $OUR \sim 6 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{g L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$; design $k_La$ accordingly via $OTR = OUR$. *(1 mark)*
4. **pH control** — bicarbonate-$\text{CO}_2$ buffer; in situ probe + $\text{CO}_2$ gas-side / $\text{NaHCO}_3$ liquid-side feedback. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Temperature control** — 37 °C ± 0.5 °C using jacketed vessel or external heat exchanger. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Sterility and sterility maintenance** — sanitisation-in-place (SIP), 0.22 µm vent filters, steam-sterilisable probes. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Foam control** — chemical antifoam (sparingly, as it can extract product) or mechanical foam breaker. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Sampling / harvest line** — septum-sampled for off-line cell density, viability, metabolites, osmolality, glucose, lactate. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $L_{\max}$ using Hill-Krylov equation:**

$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{6\,D\,C_{O_2}^{s}}{q\,X}}$$

Convert units:
- $q = 4.5 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{g/cell/h} = (4.5 \times 10^{-14})/(32 \times 3600) = 3.91 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{mol/cell/s}$.
- $X = 5 \times 10^{7}\ \text{cells cm}^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{13}\ \text{cells m}^{-3}$.

$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.2}{(3.91 \times 10^{-19})(5 \times 10^{13})}}$$

Numerator: $3.0 \times 10^{-9}$.
Denominator: $1.955 \times 10^{-5}$.

$$L_{\max} = \sqrt{1.535 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.239 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m} = 12.4\ \text{mm}$$

**$L_{\max} \approx 12.4\ \text{mm}$.** [3 marks]

**Step 2 — Compare to construct thickness:**

Construct thickness = $1.5\ \text{cm} = 15\ \text{mm}$ (half-thickness = $7.5\ \text{mm}$ from any surface).
Since $7.5\ \text{mm} < 12.4\ \text{mm}$, **the construct will survive** without central necrosis. [1 mark]

**Step 3 — Perfusion strategies (any two):** [3 marks, 1.5 each]
1. **Direct vascularisation** — embed hollow fibres of $\text{O}_2$-permeable polymer (e.g., silicone) as artificial capillaries to deliver $\text{O}_2$ to the core.
2. **Pulsatile perfusion** — flow oxygenated medium through the construct in cycles mimicking arterial pulsation; lowers diffusion distance to intercapillary spacing.
3. **Carrier / co-culture with endothelial cells** — promote spontaneous capillary formation inside the construct, extending $L_{\max}$.
4. **Hyperoxic perfusate** — increase inlet $C_{O_2}$ (use $\text{O}_2}$-carriers like perfluorocarbons) to raise $C_{O_2}^{s}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this Module:**
> 1. **Confusing $k_La$ with $k_L$** — $k_La$ is **volumetric**; $k_L$ is the mass-transfer coefficient alone. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** if the units are wrong.
> 2. **Mixing first-order and zero-order Thiele moduli** — the form $\phi_0 = R\sqrt{k_0/(D_S C_{S,b})}$ is **different** from $\phi = R\sqrt{k_1/D_S}$. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the word "internal"** in "internal diffusion limited" — examiners expect this exact phrase.
> 4. **Using $P/V$ in $\text{W kg}^{-1}$ but reporting $\eta$ in $\text{m}$** — keep units consistent throughout.
> 5. **Failing to state assumptions** in the Thiele derivation (e.g., constant $D_S$, no external resistance, first-order) — at least 0.5 mark is reserved for explicit assumption listing.
> 6. **Treating organ culture as a "scaled-up suspension"** — credit is given only when the *3-D* nature, diffusion limit, and perfusion requirement are explicitly mentioned.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Suspension Culture** → Low-shear impeller (marine, Scaba); $P/V \le 25\ \text{W m}^{-3}$; Pluronic F-68; airlift / wave as alternatives; governed by **Kolmogorov eddy length** criterion.
- **Immobilized Systems** → entrapment (Ca-alginate, κ-carrageenan), encapsulation, adsorption (microcarriers), covalent bonding; reactors: PBR, FBR, hollow-fibre, membrane.
- **Thiele Modulus $\phi$** → reaction/diffusion ratio. **$\eta_{eff} \to 1$** if reaction-limited, **$\eta_{eff} \to 3/\phi$** if diffusion-limited.
- **Damköhler number $Da$** → external mass-transfer analogue of $\phi$.
- **Carberry number $Ca$** → compares internal and external mass-transfer resistances.
- **Organ Culture** → diffusion-limited depth; **$L_{\max} = \sqrt{6 D C_{S}^{s}/(qX)}$**; RWB and perfusion chambers essential; hollow-fibre cartridges mimic vasculature.
- **Microcarriers** bridge the gap: small beads ($\sim 200\ \mu\text{m}$) provide growth surface in **stirred-tank** mode while offering 3-D anchorage for anchorage-dependent cells (Vero, HEK293).
- **Scale-up triad** = constant $k_La$, constant $P/V$, constant impeller tip speed — **never all three simultaneously**; one is chosen based on rate-limiting step.
- **Numerical hot-spots**: Thiele modulus, effectiveness factor, $L_{\max}$, $k_La$ matching to OUR, mixing-time / power number.
- **Industrial examples**: CHO → mAb (suspension), yeast in alginate → bioethanol, *E. coli* in PVA → L-aspartic acid, HepatoSure hollow-fibre cartridge → hepatocyte culture for drug metabolism studies.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PCBBT502 Bioprocess Engineering**
> *All formulas, units, and dimensional logic above are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme CO4 (Analyze bioreactor design choices for different cell-culture systems). Master the comparison table in §2.5 and the Thiele derivation in §3.2 — these two alone account for ≥ 60 % of Module 4 marks in past KTU papers.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Products of Plant Cell Cultures. Medical Applications of Bioprocess Engineering - Overview of Tissue Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Products of Plant Cell Cultures

### 🎯 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
**Plant Cell Culture** refers to the *in vitro* cultivation of plant cells, tissues, or organs under sterile, aseptic, and chemically defined nutrient conditions on synthetic media. The **products of plant cell cultures** are the commercially valuable bioactive compounds (secondary metabolites, recombinant proteins, pigments, flavors, fragrances, and pharmaceuticals) that are either biosynthesized naturally by the cultured cells or produced through genetic engineering, elicitation, or biotransformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition**
> *"Products of plant cell culture are secondary metabolites and recombinant biomolecules synthesized by dedifferentiated plant cells (callus) or organized tissues grown under controlled aseptic conditions, used in pharmaceuticals, nutraceuticals, cosmetics, and food industries."*

### 🌱 Intuitive Analogy — The "Plant Cell Factory" Concept
Imagine a **chocolate factory** that cannot grow its own cocoa trees anymore because the climate is destroyed. Instead, scientists take a few **cocoa cells**, put them in a giant stainless-steel "kitchen" called a **bioreactor**, feed them sugar, vitamins, and oxygen, and the cells happily keep producing the chocolate flavor compound, theobromine, around the clock — **without needing a tree, soil, or rain**.

Similarly, plant cells in a bioreactor are **miniature green chemical factories**:
- They don't need roots, stems, or leaves.
- They just need **nutrients + oxygen + agitation**.
- They continuously secrete valuable molecules into the medium.

This is the heart of **plant cell-based bioprocessing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **Primary metabolites** → essential for cell growth (amino acids, nucleotides) → produced during *trophophase*.
> - **Secondary metabolites** → NOT essential for growth, but valuable commercially → produced during *idiophase*.
> - Plant cell culture bioprocess engineering is **mostly concerned with secondary metabolites**.

---

## 1.2 Medical Applications of Bioprocess Engineering

### 🎯 Formal Definition
**Medical Bioprocess Engineering** is the discipline that applies the principles of bioprocess engineering — bioreactor design, fermentation, downstream processing, and scale-up — to manufacture **therapeutic biologics** (vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, hormones, antibiotics, enzymes, and tissue-engineered constructs) for human healthcare.

### 🏥 Intuitive Analogy — "Medicine as Cooked Biology"
Think of traditional chemistry-based medicines as **synthesized in a chemistry lab** (mixing chemicals in beakers). But many modern drugs — insulin, vaccines, antibodies — are too complex to be chemically synthesized. Instead, we use **living cells as tiny cooking pots** that "cook up" the medicine for us. **Bioprocess engineers design the pots (bioreactors), control the recipe (media), and purify the dish (downstream processing).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics in Medical Bioprocess Engineering:**
> - **Titer (g/L)** = concentration of product in broth
> - **Yield (Y_P/S, g/g)** = product per unit substrate
> - **Productivity (g/L·h)** = titer × volumetric productivity
> - **Volumetric Oxygen Transfer Coefficient, K_La (h⁻¹)** — bolded because it is a **critical scale-up parameter**.

---

## 1.3 Overview of Tissue Engineering

### 🎯 Formal Definition
**Tissue Engineering (TE)** is an interdisciplinary field that applies the principles of **biology, materials science, and bioprocess engineering** to develop biological substitutes that **restore, maintain, or improve tissue function** by combining living cells with scaffolds, growth factors, and suitable biochemical/physical cues.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tissue Engineering Triad" — KTU High-Yield Concept**
> 
> | Component | Function | Examples |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Cells** | Building blocks of new tissue | Stem cells, chondrocytes, hepatocytes |
> | **Scaffold** | 3D structural support | PLA, collagen, chitosan, hydrogel |
> | **Signals** | Biochemical/mechanical cues directing cell behavior | Growth factors (BMP, TGF-β), bioreactor shear |

### 🧩 Intuitive Analogy — "Building a Brick Wall"
Think of building a damaged brick wall in your house:
- **Bricks** = living cells
- **Mortar + frame** = the scaffold
- **Foreman shouting instructions** = growth factors and biophysical signals

If you have only bricks, you get a pile. If you have only the frame, you get an empty shell. **You need all three together** — that is exactly what tissue engineering does biologically.

---

# 🧪 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Products of Plant Cell Cultures

Plant cell culture products are systematically classified into the following categories (commonly asked as **2-mark / 3-mark questions** in KTU):

### A. Secondary Metabolites (Largest Commercial Class)

These are low-molecular-weight organic compounds **not required for primary metabolic functions** (growth, development, reproduction) but play ecological roles (defense, signaling, pigmentation).

| Class | Example Compound | Source Plant | Therapeutic Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alkaloids** | Vincristine, Vinblastine | *Catharanthus roseus* | Anticancer (leukemia, lymphoma) |
| **Alkaloids** | Quinine | *Cinchona* | Antimalarial |
| **Alkaloids** | Morphine, Codeine | *Papaver somniferum* | Analgesic |
| **Alkaloids** | Scopolamine | *Datura* | Anticholinergic |
| **Terpenoids** | Taxol (Paclitaxel) | *Taxus* spp. | Anticancer (ovarian, breast) |
| **Terpenoids** | Artemisinin | *Artemisia annua* | Antimalarial (WHO essential) |
| **Phenolics** | Rosmarinic acid | *Coleus blumei* | Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory |
| **Quinones** | Shikonin | *Lithospermum erythrorhizon* | Antibacterial, anti-inflammatory |
| **Glycosides** | Digoxin | *Digitalis lanata* | Cardiotonic |
| **Steroids** | Diosgenin | *Dioscorea* | Anti-inflammatory precursor |

### B. Recombinant Proteins (Molecular Farming)

Plant cells can be genetically transformed (via *Agrobacterium tumefaciens* or biolistics) to produce:
- **Vaccines** — Hepatitis B surface antigen, Rabies glycoprotein
- **Antibodies** — full-size IgG (e.g., CaroRx™ against *Streptococcus mutans*)
- **Enzymes** — α-amylase, trypsin, glucocerebrosidase
- **Hormones** — Human growth hormone, erythropoietin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use plants for recombinant proteins?**
> - **Low cost** of cultivation (no fermenter-grade media required).
> - **Post-translational modifications** (N-glycosylation) are possible, though slightly different from mammalian cells.
> - **Scalable** — acres of plants = large production.

### C. Food Additives, Flavors, Pigments

- **Pigments** — Betacyanin (red beet), shikonin (red-purple), anthocyanin.
- **Flavors** — Vanilla, cocoa, saffron-equivalent compounds.
- **Sweeteners** — Stevioside (from *Stevia rebaudiana*).
- **Beverages** — Coffee cells producing low-caffeine coffee.

### D. Biotransformation Products
Plant cells contain **specific enzyme systems** (glycosyltransferases, oxidoreductases) capable of converting cheap precursor molecules into high-value products.
- **Example:** Digitoxin → Digoxin biotransformation by *Digitalis lanata* cells.

### E. Bioactive Peptides and Polysaccharides
- Ginseng polysaccharides (immunomodulatory).
- Echinacea polysaccharides (immunostimulatory).

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Production of Plant Cell Culture Products

| Factor | Effect on Product Formation |
|---|---|
| **Medium composition** | Carbon source (sucrose, glucose), nitrogen (NH₄⁺/NO₃⁻ ratio), vitamins, plant growth regulators (auxins, cytokinins) |
| **pH** | Optimum usually 5.5–6.0 |
| **Temperature** | Standard 25 ± 2 °C |
| **Light** | Required for some pigments and photosynthesis-related secondary metabolites |
| **Aeration / K_La** | Critical for oxygen supply in large bioreactors |
| **Elicitors** | Jasmonic acid, methyl jasmonate, salicylic acid, fungal/chitosan elicitors boost secondary metabolite production 5–50× |
| **Precursor feeding** | Supplying biosynthetic precursors (e.g., phenylalanine for rosmarinic acid) |
| **Permeabilization** | Allows release of intracellular products into medium (e.g., using DMSO, chitosan) |

---

## 2.3 Bioreactors Used for Plant Cell Culture

> [!NOTE]
> Plant cells are **large (20–100 µm)**, **vacuolated**, **shear-sensitive**, and **tend to aggregate** — so conventional microbial bioreactors are not directly suitable.

| Bioreactor Type | Suitability | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Stirred Tank Reactor (STR)** | Limited for plant cells | High shear damages cells |
| **Air-lift Reactor** | ✅ Preferred | Low shear, good mixing |
| **Bubble Column Reactor** | ✅ Good | Simple, low shear |
| **Wave bioreactor** | ✅ Excellent for shear-sensitive cells | Gentle rocking motion |
| **Membrane bioreactor** | ✅ Good for secreted products | Continuous product removal |
| **Packed-bed / Immobilized cell reactor** | ✅ Good for adherent/slow-growing cells | Cells trapped in matrix |
| **Temporary Immersion System (TIS)** | ✅ Best for secondary metabolites | Periodic medium contact |

---

## 2.4 Tissue Engineering — Detailed Component Analysis

### 🧬 The Tissue Engineering Triad (Expanded)

#### A. Cells (The Building Blocks)

| Cell Type | Source | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Autologous adult cells** | Patient's own tissue | Skin, cartilage grafts |
| **Allogeneic cells** | Donor of same species | Bone marrow transplants |
| **Xenogeneic cells** | Different species | Porcine heart valves |
| **Stem cells:** | | |
| • Embryonic Stem Cells (ESCs) | Inner cell mass of blastocyst | Pluripotent, ethically debated |
| • Adult Stem Cells (ASCs) | Bone marrow, adipose | Multipotent, ethically safe |
| • Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs) | Reprogrammed adult cells (Yamanaka factors: Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc) | Pluripotent, no ethical issue |
| • Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs) | Bone marrow, umbilical cord | Multipotent (bone, cartilage, fat) |

#### B. Scaffolds (The 3D Matrix)

Scaffolds provide **mechanical support, spatial orientation, and biological cues**. Key requirements:

1. **Biocompatibility** — non-toxic, non-immunogenic.
2. **Biodegradability** — degrades at a rate matching tissue regeneration.
3. **Porosity** — pore size 50–500 µm for cell infiltration and nutrient diffusion.
4. **Mechanical strength** — matches target tissue (e.g., bone > cartilage > skin).
5. **Surface chemistry** — supports cell adhesion (RGD peptide motifs).

| Scaffold Material | Type | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural polymers** | Collagen, chitosan, alginate, gelatin, hyaluronic acid, fibrin | Skin, cartilage |
| **Synthetic biodegradable polymers** | PLA (Polylactic acid), PGA (Polyglycolic acid), PLGA (Poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid)), PCL (Polycaprolactone) | Bone, cartilage |
| **Bioactive ceramics** | Hydroxyapatite, tricalcium phosphate, bioactive glass | Bone tissue engineering |
| **Decellularized ECM** | From cadaveric organ, stripped of cells | Whole organ scaffolds (e.g., heart, liver) |
| **Hydrogels** | PEG, gelatin methacrylate (GelMA) | Soft tissue, 3D bioprinting |

#### C. Signals (The Biochemical and Physical Cues)

| Signal Type | Example | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Growth factors** | BMP-2 (bone), TGF-β (cartilage), VEGF (vascular), EGF (epithelial), FGF-2 (fibroblast) | Cell proliferation, differentiation |
| **Mechanical stimulation** | Shear stress, cyclic compression, hydrostatic pressure | Mechano-transduction → lineage commitment |
| **Electrical stimulation** | For nerve, cardiac, muscle tissue | Promotes alignment and function |
| **Topographical cues** | Micro/nano-patterned surfaces | Contact guidance |

---

## 2.5 Medical Applications of Tissue Engineering

| Tissue/Organ | Status | Key Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Skin** | ✅ Commercially available | Apligraf®, Dermagraft®, Epicel® for burns and diabetic ulcers |
| **Cartilage** | 🟡 Clinical trials | Carticel® (autologous chondrocytes) |
| **Bone** | 🟡 Clinical trials | INFUSE® (BMP-2 on collagen sponge) |
| **Cornea** | 🟡 Clinical trials | Limbal stem cell therapy |
| **Bladder** | ✅ Successfully implanted (Atala, 2006) | Engineered bladder in patients |
| **Trachea** | ✅ Case reports | Stem-cell-seeded tracheal grafts |
| **Heart** | 🔬 Preclinical | Cardiac patches, iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes |
| **Liver** | 🔬 Preclinical | Hepatocyte scaffolds for bioartificial liver |
| **Kidney** | 🔬 Preclinical | Decellularized kidney scaffolds recellularized |
| **Blood vessels** | 🟡 Clinical trials | TEVGs (Tissue-engineered vascular grafts) |
| **Pancreas (diabetes)** | 🔬 Preclinical | Encapsulated β-cell islets (e.g., ViaCyte) |
| **Nerve** | 🟡 Clinical trials | Nerve guidance conduits (NeuraGen®) |

---

## 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Titer** | $P = \frac{m_p}{V_{broth}}$ | g/L |
| **Product Yield on Substrate** | $Y_{P/S} = \frac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S}$ | g/g (dimensionless) |
| **Product Yield on Biomass** | $Y_{P/X} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$ | g/g |
| **Volumetric Productivity** | $Q_P = \frac{P}{t}$ | g/(L·h) |
| **Specific Productivity** | $q_P = \frac{Q_P}{X}$ | g/(g·h) |
| **Doubling Time (cells)** | $t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | h |
| **Specific Growth Rate** | $\mu = \frac{1}{X}\frac{dX}{dt}$ | h⁻¹ |
| **Monod Kinetics** | $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | h⁻¹ |
| **Oxygen Transfer Rate** | $OTR = K_La \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | mmol O₂/(L·h) |
| **Shear Stress (impeller)** | $\tau \propto \frac{\rho \cdot N^2 \cdot D_i^3}{V}$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| **Elicitation Fold-Increase** | $Fold = \frac{P_{elicited}}{P_{control}}$ | dimensionless |
| **Scaffold Porosity** | $\epsilon = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Tissue Engineering Triad** | Cells + Scaffolds + Signals | conceptual identity |
| **Stokes' Law (cell settling)** | $v_s = \frac{g \cdot d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu_f}$ | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"Triad = C-S-S"** → **C**ells + **S**caffold + **S**ignals.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Plant cell culture bioreactors have enabled the **industrial-scale production of Paclitaxel (Taxol)**, an anticancer drug worth **> USD 1 billion/year** globally.
- Tissue-engineered skin (e.g., **Apligraf®**) has **saved thousands of burn patients** by providing living skin grafts.
- iPSC technology (Yamanaka, 2012 Nobel Prize) is now being scaled through bioprocess engineering to produce **patient-specific cell therapies** at industrial scale.

---

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Example 1: Product Titer and Productivity in Plant Cell Bioreactor

### 📋 Problem Statement
A 5 L stirred-tank bioreactor is used to culture *Catharanthus roseus* cells. Initial biomass concentration $X_0 = 0.5$ g/L. After 14 days, biomass $X = 8.2$ g/L. Initial substrate (sucrose) $S_0 = 30$ g/L; final $S = 4$ g/L. The alkaloid (ajmalicine) concentration at the end is $P = 85$ mg/L.

**Compute:**
(a) Specific growth rate $\mu$ (assume exponential phase dominates)
(b) Biomass yield $Y_{X/S}$
(c) Product yield on substrate $Y_{P/S}$
(d) Volumetric productivity $Q_P$ (in mg/(L·day))
(e) Specific productivity $q_P$

### 🔢 Solution

**Given:**
- $V = 5$ L
- $X_0 = 0.5$ g/L
- $X = 8.2$ g/L
- $S_0 = 30$ g/L
- $S = 4$ g/L
- $P = 85$ mg/L
- $t = 14$ days

---

**(a) Specific Growth Rate $\mu$**

The exponential growth equation in batch culture is:

$$X = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu \cdot t}$$

Rearranging for $\mu$:

$$\mu = \frac{\ln(X / X_0)}{t}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\mu = \frac{\ln(8.2 / 0.5)}{14}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\ln(16.4)}{14}$$

$$\ln(16.4) \approx 2.797$$

$$\mu = \frac{2.797}{14} \approx 0.1998 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 0.20 \text{ day}^{-1}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The natural log of the cell density ratio gives the total number of doublings (× log_e), divided by time gives the per-day specific rate.

---

**(b) Biomass Yield on Substrate $Y_{X/S}$**

Definition: biomass produced per unit substrate consumed:

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{X - X_0}{S_0 - S}$$

Substituting:

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{8.2 - 0.5}{30 - 4}$$

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{7.7}{26} \approx 0.296 \text{ g/g}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{X/S} \approx 0.30 \text{ g biomass / g sucrose}}$$

**Conversion logic:** We compute how much new biomass appeared per gram of sucrose that disappeared from the medium.

---

**(c) Product Yield on Substrate $Y_{P/S}$**

$$Y_{P/S} = \frac{P - P_0}{S_0 - S}$$

Assuming $P_0 = 0$ (no product initially):

$$Y_{P/S} = \frac{85 \text{ mg} - 0}{26 \text{ g}}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{P/S} \approx 3.27 \text{ mg product / g substrate}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Total mg of product synthesized per gram of sucrose consumed.

---

**(d) Volumetric Productivity $Q_P$**

$$Q_P = \frac{P - P_0}{t}$$

$$Q_P = \frac{85 \text{ mg/L}}{14 \text{ days}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_P \approx 6.07 \text{ mg/(L·day)}}$$

**Conversion logic:** How much product accumulates in 1 L of broth per day.

---

**(e) Specific Productivity $q_P$**

$$q_P = \frac{Q_P}{X}$$

$$q_P = \frac{6.07 \text{ mg/(L·day)}}{8.2 \text{ g/L}}$$

$$\boxed{q_P \approx 0.74 \text{ mg/(g·day)}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Product formed per gram of biomass per day — a measure of cellular efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example 2: Elicitation Fold-Increase

### 📋 Problem Statement
In an *Artemisia annua* cell suspension culture, the basal artemisinin concentration is **2.5 mg/L**. After elicitation with 100 µM methyl jasmonate, the concentration rises to **38 mg/L**. Calculate the **fold-increase** and the **percent enhancement** in artemisinin production.

### 🔢 Solution

**Fold-Increase:**

$$Fold = \frac{P_{elicited}}{P_{control}}$$

$$Fold = \frac{38}{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{Fold = 15.2}$$

**Percent Enhancement:**

$$\% Enhancement = \frac{P_{elicited} - P_{control}}{P_{control}} \times 100$$

$$\% Enhancement = \frac{38 - 2.5}{2.5} \times 100$$

$$\% Enhancement = \frac{35.5}{2.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% Enhancement = 1420\%}$$

**Conversion logic:** A 15.2-fold increase means artemisinin levels are 15.2× higher than control; percent enhancement is fold minus one, multiplied by 100.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example 3: Oxygen Transfer in a Plant Cell Bioreactor

### 📋 Problem Statement
A 50 L air-lift bioreactor with $K_La = 25$ h⁻¹ operates with dissolved oxygen tension $C_L = 4$ mg/L and saturation concentration $C^* = 8$ mg/L.

**Find:** Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR).

### 🔢 Solution

$$OTR = K_La \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

$$OTR = 25 \text{ h}^{-1} \cdot (8 - 4) \text{ mg/L}$$

$$OTR = 25 \times 4$$

$$\boxed{OTR = 100 \text{ mg O}_2 / (L \cdot h)}$$

**Conversion logic:** The driving force for oxygen transfer is the gradient between saturation and actual dissolved O₂. Multiplying by $K_La$ gives the rate.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example 4: Scaffold Porosity and Surface Area

### 📋 Problem Statement
A PLGA scaffold for bone tissue engineering has total volume $V_{total} = 2.5$ cm³ and pore volume $V_{pores} = 1.75$ cm³. Cell seeding density is $1 \times 10^5$ cells per cm² of internal surface area, and the scaffold has a total internal surface area of **120 cm²**.

**Find:**
(a) Porosity $\epsilon$
(b) Total cells seeded on scaffold

### 🔢 Solution

**(a) Porosity:**

$$\epsilon = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100$$

$$\epsilon = \frac{1.75}{2.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\epsilon = 70\%}$$

**Conversion logic:** Pore volume / total volume gives the fraction of void space, expressed as %.

**(b) Total cells seeded:**

$$N_{cells} = (\text{Seeding density}) \times (\text{Surface area})$$

$$N_{cells} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ cells/cm}^2 \times 120 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$\boxed{N_{cells} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \text{ cells}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | Plant Cell Culture | Microbial Fermentation | Mammalian Cell Culture |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell size** | 20–100 µm (large) | 1–5 µm (small) | 10–30 µm |
| **Doubling time** | 24–72 h | 20 min – 4 h | 18–24 h |
| **Shear sensitivity** | Very high | Low | High |
| **Preferred bioreactor** | Air-lift, wave | Stirred tank | Stirred tank (gentle) |
| **Media cost** | Moderate | Low | Very high |
| **Typical products** | Secondary metabolites | Antibiotics, enzymes, organic acids | Antibodies, vaccines |
| **O₂ demand** | Low | High | High |
| **Contamination risk** | Moderate (fungi, bacteria) | High (phage, bacteria) | Very high (mycoplasma) |
| **Sterility needed** | Aseptic | Sterile | Strictly sterile |

---
