# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### ❓ Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define plant cell culture. List any four commercially important products obtained from plant cell cultures with one example each.*

### ✅ Model Answer
**Plant cell culture** is the *in vitro* cultivation of plant cells, tissues, or organs on chemically defined nutrient media under sterile conditions.

**Four commercially important products:**

| Product | Example Compound | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Alkaloid** | Vincristine | *Catharanthus roseus* |
| **Terpenoid** | Taxol (Paclitaxel) | *Taxus chinensis* |
| **Pigment** | Shikonin | *Lithospermum erythrorhizon* |
| **Recombinant Protein** | Hepatitis B surface antigen | Transgenic *Nicotiana* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 4 products with example (2 marks, 0.5 each).

---

### ❓ Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
*What is the "Tissue Engineering Triad"? Briefly explain each component.*

### ✅ Model Answer
The **Tissue Engineering Triad** consists of three essential components:

1. **Cells** — Living units (stem cells, primary cells, iPSCs) that synthesize new tissue matrix.
2. **Scaffold** — A 3D biodegradable matrix (collagen, PLGA, hydrogel) that provides structural support, mechanical integrity, and a template for tissue growth.
3. **Signals** — Biochemical (growth factors like BMP, TGF-β) and biophysical (shear, compression, electrical) cues that direct cell differentiation, proliferation, and matrix deposition.

> **Mnemonic:** "**C-S-S**" = **C**ells, **S**caffold, **S**ignals.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### 📝 Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **categories of secondary metabolites** produced by plant cell cultures. Give **two examples** with their source plant and therapeutic application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain in detail the **bioreactor configurations** used for plant cell culture. Why are conventional stirred-tank reactors often unsuitable? *(7 marks)*

### ✅ Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Categories of Secondary Metabolites

Secondary metabolites are classified based on their **chemical structure and biosynthetic pathway**:

**1. Alkaloids (Nitrogen-containing)**
- Biosynthesized from amino acids (tryptophan, tyrosine, ornithine).
- **Examples:**
  - *Vincristine* from *Catharanthus roseus* — anticancer (Hodgkin's lymphoma, leukemia).
  - *Quinine* from *Cinchona* — antimalarial.

**2. Terpenoids / Terpenes (Isoprenoid derivatives)**
- Built from C5 isoprene units.
- **Examples:**
  - *Paclitaxel (Taxol)* from *Taxus* spp. — anticancer (ovarian, breast cancer).
  - *Artemisinin* from *Artemisia annua* — antimalarial (WHO essential medicine).

**3. Phenolic Compounds (Shikimate / Acetate pathways)**
- Aromatic ring with hydroxyl groups.
- **Examples:**
  - *Rosmarinic acid* from *Coleus blumei* — antioxidant, anti-inflammatory.
  - *Shikonin* from *Lithospermum erythrorhizon* — antimicrobial.

**4. Glycosides (Sugar + non-sugar moiety)**
- **Examples:** Digoxin (cardiotonic), stevioside (natural sweetener).

**5. Steroids & Saponins**
- **Example:** Diosgenin from *Dioscorea* — precursor for corticosteroid drugs.

> **Significance:** Secondary metabolites are usually produced in the **idiophase** (after the trophophase) and are regulated by elicitors, precursors, and stress signals.

---

#### Part (b) — Bioreactor Configurations

**Why stirred-tank reactors (STRs) are often unsuitable:**
- Plant cells are **large (20–100 µm)**, **vacuolated**, and have **rigid cell walls**, but they are **highly shear-sensitive**.
- High impeller speeds in STR generate **hydrodynamic shear** that ruptures cell membranes, reducing viability and product formation.
- Plant cells also tend to **aggregate into clumps**, causing wall-growth and impeller fouling.

**Preferred bioreactor types for plant cells:**

| Bioreactor | Key Feature | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Air-lift Reactor** | Draft tube; circulation by aeration only | Low shear, good oxygen transfer |
| **Bubble Column** | Simple cylindrical vessel; sparged air | No mechanical agitation, low cost |
| **Wave Bioreactor** | Rocking platform; flexible bag | Very gentle, single-use |
| **Membrane Bioreactor** | Cells retained by membrane | Continuous product removal, high cell density |
| **Temporary Immersion System (TIS)** | Periodic flooding of cells with medium | Excellent for shoot cultures and secondary metabolites |
| **Packed-bed / Immobilized Cell Reactor** | Cells entrapped in matrix (alginate, polyurethane foam) | Protects from shear, allows perfusion |
| **Stirred-tank with low-shear impellers (e.g., marine, pitched-blade)** | Modified STR | Used with care at low RPM |

**Critical scale-up parameter:** The **volumetric oxygen transfer coefficient K_La** must be maintained between lab and production scale to ensure consistent growth and product formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks distribution:**
> - Categories of secondary metabolites (4 marks: 3 categories × 1 + 2 examples × 0.5)
> - Bioreactor configurations (4 marks)
> - Why STRs unsuitable (3 marks)

---

### 📝 Question B-B (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe the **tissue engineering triad** in detail. Compare **natural vs. synthetic scaffolds** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What are the **medical applications of bioprocess engineering**? Discuss the production of **monoclonal antibodies** and **tissue-engineered skin** as case studies. *(7 marks)*

### ✅ Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Tissue Engineering Triad & Scaffold Comparison

**The Tissue Engineering Triad:**

1. **Cells** — Provide biological function.
   - Types: *Autologous, allogeneic, stem cells (ESCs, iPSCs, MSCs), primary cells*.
   - iPSCs (induced by **Yamanaka factors**: Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc) avoid ethical issues of ESCs.

2. **Scaffolds** — Provide 3D architecture.
   - Should mimic **extracellular matrix (ECM)**.
   - Required properties: **biocompatibility, biodegradability, porosity (50–500 µm), mechanical strength, surface chemistry (RGD motifs).**

3. **Signals** — Direct cell behavior.
   - Biochemical: **Growth factors (BMP, TGF-β, VEGF, EGF, FGF)**.
   - Physical: **Shear stress, compression, electrical stimulation, topographical cues.**

**Natural vs. Synthetic Scaffolds:**

| Property | Natural Scaffolds | Synthetic Scaffolds |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Animal/plant-derived | Chemically synthesized |
| **Biocompatibility** | High (cell-friendly) | Variable (depends on polymer) |
| **Biodegradability** | Fast (enzymatic) | Tunable |
| **Mechanical strength** | Low | High, tunable |
| **Reproducibility** | Poor (batch variation) | Excellent |
| **Immunogenicity** | Possible | Low (if pure) |
| **Examples** | Collagen, chitosan, alginate, fibrin, gelatin, hyaluronic acid | PLA, PGA, PLGA, PCL, PEG, PVA |
| **Application** | Skin, wound healing, soft tissue | Bone, load-bearing, controlled release |

**Key Insight:** Modern TE uses **composite/hybrid scaffolds** (e.g., collagen-PLGA) to combine biological cues of natural materials with mechanical robustness of synthetics.

---

#### Part (b) — Medical Applications of Bioprocess Engineering

**Broad Categories of Medical Applications:**

1. **Therapeutic proteins** (insulin, EPO, Factor VIII, hormones).
2. **Vaccines** (recombinant, mRNA, viral vector).
3. **Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)**.
4. **Cell and gene therapies.**
5. **Tissue-engineered constructs.**
6. **Diagnostic reagents.**

**Case Study 1: Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs)**

mAbs are produced by **hybridoma cells** (fused B-lymphocyte + myeloma cell) or by **recombinant CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary) cells**.

**Process steps:**
1. **Antigen injection** into mouse → B-cell activation.
2. **B-cell + myeloma fusion** using polyethylene glycol (PEG) → hybridoma.
3. **Screening** for desired antibody specificity.
4. **Cell line development** (master cell bank, working cell bank).
5. **Bioreactor cultivation** in stirred-tank bioreactor (fed-batch mode, 10,000 L scale).
6. **Downstream processing:** centrifugation → Protein A affinity chromatography → ion exchange → viral clearance → formulation.

**Examples:** Trastuzumab (breast cancer), Adalimumab (rheumatoid arthritis), Rituximab (lymphoma).

**Critical bioprocess parameters:** pH 7.0, dissolved O₂ ~50% saturation, temperature 37 °C, fed-batch with glucose/glu­tamine feeds, **K_La > 4 h⁻¹**.

**Case Study 2: Tissue-Engineered Skin (e.g., Apligraf®, Dermagraft®)**

- **Apligraf®** — Bilayered skin construct:
  - **Upper layer:** Allogeneic keratinocytes (epidermal analog).
  - **Lower layer:** Bovine collagen matrix + allogeneic fibroblasts (dermal analog).
  - Used for **chronic diabetic foot ulcers, venous leg ulcers, burns**.
- **Dermagraft®** — Fibroblasts seeded on a bioabsorbable polyglactin mesh; cryopreserved.
  - Used for diabetic ulcers.

**Process flow:**
1. **Cell isolation** from neonatal foreskin.
2. **Cell expansion** in tissue culture flasks.
3. **Seeding onto scaffold.**
4. **Maturation** in air-liquid interface (for keratinocyte layer formation).
5. **Quality control:** Sterility, cell viability, mechanical integrity.
6. **Packaging and storage** (4 °C or cryopreserved).

> **Engineering challenges:** Vascularization, scale-up, regulatory compliance (FDA, EMA), shelf life, cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing "Trophophase" and "Idiophase"** — Trophophase = primary metabolite production (growth phase); Idiophase = secondary metabolite production (stationary phase). Examiners deduct 1 mark if mixed.
> 2. **Calling K_La just "oxygen transfer"** — It is the **volumetric oxygen transfer coefficient**, units **h⁻¹**. Missing units = −0.5 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting that plant cells need LOW shear bioreactors** — Writing "STR with high RPM" is wrong. Use air-lift, bubble column, or wave.
> 4. **In tissue engineering, students often list "cells" and "scaffold" but forget "signals"** — Triad has **THREE** components, not two.
> 5. **Mixing up mAb platforms** — Hybridoma (mouse) is older; modern production uses **CHO cells** in suspension. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting to write the source plant in plant cell culture questions** — Vincristine alone is incomplete; "Vincristine from *Catharanthus roseus*" gets full marks.
> 7. **Not specifying units** in numerical problems (e.g., productivity in mg/(L·h) vs mg/(L·day)). Always include units.
> 8. **Writing "iPSC" without expanding** — First mention: "induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)" — gives better presentation marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📋 **One-Page Quick Recap:**

- [x] **Plant Cell Culture Products** = Secondary metabolites, recombinant proteins, pigments, flavors, biotransformation products.
- [x] **Secondary metabolites** are NOT essential for cell growth but commercially valuable.
- [x] **Trophophase = primary metabolism; Idiophase = secondary metabolism.**
- [x] **Elicitors** (methyl jasmonate, salicylic acid, chitosan) boost secondary metabolite yield 5–50×.
- [x] **Vincristine** (anticancer), **Taxol/Paclitaxel** (anticancer), **Artemisinin** (antimalarial), **Shikonin** (antimicrobial pigment) are the **"Big Four"** high-yield examples.
- [x] Plant cells are **large, vacuolated, shear-sensitive** → prefer **air-lift, bubble column, wave, or TIS** bioreactors.
- [x] **K_La** is the **critical scale-up parameter** for plant cell bioreactors.
- [x] **Medical Bioprocess Engineering** covers biologics, vaccines, mAbs, cell/gene therapy, tissue engineering.
- [x] **Tissue Engineering Triad = Cells + Scaffold + Signals** (mnemonic: "C-S-S").
- [x] **Cells:** Autologous, allogeneic, ESC, MSC, **iPSC** (Yamanaka factors: Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc).
- [x] **Scaffolds:** Natural (collagen, chitosan) vs Synthetic (PLGA, PLA, PCL). Porosity 50–500 µm.
- [x] **Signals:** Growth factors (BMP, TGF-β, VEGF) + Mechanical (shear, compression) + Electrical.
- [x] **Apligraf®** = bilayered engineered skin (keratinocytes + fibroblasts in collagen).
- [x] **mAbs** are produced in **CHO cells** in fed-batch STR bioreactors, purified via **Protein A chromatography**.
- [x] **mRNA vaccines** (e.g., COVID-19) are made by **in vitro transcription** — a bioprocess, not a fermentation.
- [x] **Paclitaxel** is the most valuable plant-cell-culture-derived drug (>$1B/year market).
- [x] **Yamanaka factors** = Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc (Nobel 2012).
- [x] Always include **units** in numerical answers (mg/L, h⁻¹, g/g, etc.).
- [x] **Decellularized ECM scaffolds** are an emerging frontier in whole-organ tissue engineering.

---

> ✅ **End of Module 4 Notes — Plant Cell Culture Products & Tissue Engineering Overview**
> *These notes are aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education (NEP 2020), and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate).*

topics.title: Commercial Tissue Culture Processes, Gene Therapy Using Viral Vectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Commercial Tissue Culture Processes, Gene Therapy Using Viral Vectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Commercial Tissue Culture Processes — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercial Tissue Culture** refers to the **large-scale, industrial application of in vitro cell and tissue culture techniques** to mass-produce genetically identical, disease-free plants (Plant Tissue Culture / PTC), high-value secondary metabolites, vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, and other bioproducts under sterile, controlled, and economically viable conditions using bioreactors and aseptic engineering principles.

In the KTU 2024 Bioprocess Engineering context, this topic unites two parallel industries:

| Domain | Commercial Output | Key Organism |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant Tissue Culture (PTC)** | Micropropagated plantlets, disease-free germplasm, secondary metabolites (e.g., shikonin, taxol) | Banana, sugarcane, orchid, *Catharanthus roseus* |
| **Animal Cell Culture (ACC)** | Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), viral vaccines, interferons, recombinant proteins | CHO cells, hybridomas, Vero, HeLa |
| **Insect Cell Culture** | Recombinant proteins via Baculovirus Expression Vector System (BEVS) | *Spodoptera frugiperda* (Sf9, Sf21) |

## 1.2 Gene Therapy Using Viral Vectors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gene Therapy** is the therapeutic delivery of functional genetic material (cDNA, mRNA, siRNA, CRISPR cassettes) into a patient's somatic or germ-line cells to **correct, replace, regulate, or silence a defective gene** responsible for a disease. **Viral Vectors** are engineered, replication-deficient viruses that exploit natural viral infection machinery to deliver therapeutic transgenes efficiently into target cells.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Tissue Culture as a "Bioclone Factory":**
> Imagine you have a single perfect mango tree that yields extraordinary fruit. Instead of waiting 5 years for seeds to grow, you take a tiny leaf cutting, place it into a sterile "magic gel jar" with nutrients and hormones, and within weeks the cutting multiplies into **thousands of genetically identical plantlets** — all clones of the original super-tree. This is micropropagation: a **biological photocopy machine** running at industrial scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Viral Vector as a "Trojan Horse Mailman":**
> A virus is naturally a delivery machine — it injects its genetic payload into cells. Scientists **remove the virus's harmful genes** and stuff a **therapeutic gene** into the empty viral capsid (the "Trojan Horse"). The modified virus still rings the doorbell (binds receptors) and enters the cell, but instead of causing disease, it **deposits the cure** in the form of a healthy gene copy.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics (Standard Industrial Benchmarks)

- **Micropropagation multiplication rate:** typically **3× to 10× per subculture cycle** of **3–6 weeks**
- **Plating efficiency (PE)** of explant tissue: **40–90%** for meristems, **20–60%** for callus
- **Specific growth rate (μ)** of plant cell suspensions: **0.15–0.30 day⁻¹**
- **Doubling time (td)** of hybridoma cells: **18–24 hours**
- **AAV vector titer:** **10¹² – 10¹³ viral genomes (vg)/mL** in clinical-grade preparations
- **Lentiviral titer:** **10⁷ – 10⁹ TU/mL** (TU = Transducing Units)
- **Bioreactor working volume for commercial PTC:** **10 L – 500 L** (temporary immersion systems like RITA®)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plant Tissue Culture (PTC) — Stages of Commercial Micropropagation

A KTU-favorite topic. The **Five-Stage Protocol** is universally tested.

### Stage 0 — Mother Plant Selection (Pre-initiation)
- Select **elite, disease-indexed, true-to-type** mother plants.
- Maintain under controlled greenhouse conditions for **2–3 months**.
- Purpose: Provide physiologically uniform, pathogen-free explant source.

### Stage I — Inoculation & Initiation
- **Explant** (meristem, axillary bud, leaf, node) is excised.
- **Surface sterilization** sequence:
$$\text{Tap water} \rightarrow 70\% \text{ EtOH (30 s)} \rightarrow 0.1\% \text{ HgCl}_2 \text{ (2–5 min)} \rightarrow 5\times \text{ sterile water rinse}$$
- Inoculated on **Murashige & Skoog (MS) medium** with auxins (IAA, NAA, 2,4-D) and cytokinins (BAP, kinetin).
- Critical parameters: **Temperature 25 ± 2 °C**, **pH 5.6–5.8**, **Photoperiod 16 h light / 8 h dark**, **PPFD 40–60 μmol m⁻² s⁻¹**.

### Stage II — Multiplication (Shoot Proliferation)
- Repeated subculture on **high-cytokinin medium** (BAP 1–5 mg/L).
- Each explant produces **3–10 axillary shoots** per cycle.
- **Multiplication factor formula** (KTU High-Yield):
$$M = R^n$$
where **M** = total number of plantlets produced, **R** = multiplication rate per cycle, **n** = number of subculture cycles.

### Stage III — Rooting (In Vitro)
- Individual shoots transferred to **low-auxin / auxin-free medium** (½ MS + IAA 0.5 mg/L).
- Roots develop in **2–4 weeks**.

### Stage IV — Hardening / Acclimatization
- Plantlets transferred to **greenhouse, greenhouse-grade substrate** (peat:perlite:vermiculite = 1:1:1).
- **Critical step**: gradual humidity reduction from **100% → 60%** over 2–3 weeks.
- Survival rate: **70–95%** for well-hardened plants.

## 2.2 Somaclonal Variation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *somaclonal variation* (genetic) with *epigenetic variation*. Only **heritable, genetic** changes qualify as true somaclonal variation.

- Spontaneous genetic/epigenetic variation arising in **somatic cell-derived callus and suspension cultures**.
- **Larkin & Scowcroft (1981)** coined the term.
- Useful for: **crop improvement, novel trait selection** (e.g., salt-tolerant rice lines).
- Detrimental for: **clonal propagation requiring genetic fidelity** — detected using **isozyme, RAPD, SSR, ISSR markers**.

## 2.3 Secondary Metabolite Production in Plant Cell Cultures

- **Strategy 1: Callus/Cell Suspension Culture** — e.g., shikonin (*Lithospermum erythrorhizon*), berberine (*Coptis japonica*).
- **Strategy 2: Hairy Root Culture** — *Agrobacterium rhizogenes*-induced; produces **stable, high-yielding roots** with secondary metabolites (e.g., ajmalicine, ginsenosides).
- **Strategy 3: Elicitation** — treatment with methyl jasmonate (MeJA), chitosan, or fungal elicitors to **upregulate** defense-related biosynthetic pathways.

## 2.4 Animal Cell Culture Bioprocess

### Hybridoma Technology for Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs)
1. **Immunize** mouse with antigen (3–4 booster doses over 2–4 weeks).
2. **Harvest splenocytes** (B-lymphocytes producing specific Ab).
3. **Fuse** with **myeloma cells** (e.g., SP2/0, NS0) using **PEG-1450 (50% w/v)** or **electrofusion**.
4. **HAT selection** medium: Hypoxanthine-Aminopterin-Thymidine — kills unfused myeloma (HGPRT⁻) and unfused B-cells; only **hybridomas survive**.
5. **Screening** by ELISA; **single-cell cloning** by limiting dilution.
6. Scale up: **static culture → roller bottle → stirred-tank bioreactor with microcarriers**.

### Bioreactors for Animal Cells
- **Stirred-tank** (with low-shear marine impellers), **Airlift**, **Hollow-fiber**, **Packed-bed** (e.g., Cytopilot™), **Wave-type** (GE WAVE Bioreactor™).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear-sensitive organisms**: Animal cells lack a rigid cell wall. Operational tip speed must be kept **< 25 cm/s** to avoid hydrodynamic stress damage.

## 2.5 Gene Therapy — Classification

### By Target Cell Type
| Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Somatic gene therapy** | Therapeutic gene into body (somatic) cells — non-heritable | ADA-SCID, Leber's congenital amaurosis |
| **Germ-line gene therapy** | Into sperm/egg/zygote — heritable | Ethically restricted in humans |

### By Delivery Route
- **Ex vivo:** Cells harvested → transduced in vitro → returned to patient (e.g., hematopoietic stem cell gene therapy for ADA-SCID).
- **In vivo:** Vector injected directly (intratumoral, intravenous, intrathecal) (e.g., AAV-RPE65 for blindness).

## 2.6 Viral Vectors — Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Retrovirus (γ-RV) | Lentivirus | Adenovirus (Ad) | AAV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Genome** | ssRNA | ssRNA | dsDNA | ssDNA |
| **Capacity (kb)** | 7–8 | 7–10 | **up to 36** | **4.7** (max) |
| **Integration** | **Yes** (random) | **Yes** (random) | **No** (episomal) | **No/minimal** |
| **Tropism** | Dividing cells only | Dividing + non-dividing | Broad | Depends on serotype |
| **Expression duration** | Long-term | Long-term | Transient (1–2 wk) | Long-term (years) |
| **Immunogenicity** | Low | Low | **High** | Very low |
| **Key safety concern** | Insertional mutagenesis | Insertional mutagenesis | Strong innate immunity | Capsid immunogenicity |
| **Clinical example** | ADA-SCID (first trial) | CAR-T cell therapy | Gendicine® (p53, China) | Luxturna® (RPE65) |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **one-page cheat sheet** for viva and numerical problems. Memorize before the exam.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = R^n$ | Micropropagation total plantlets | R = rate, n = subculture cycles |
| 2 | $td = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | Cell doubling time | hours |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t}$ | Specific growth rate | h⁻¹ or day⁻¹ |
| 4 | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$ | Exponential cell growth | N = cell density |
| 5 | $PE = \dfrac{\text{No. of explants forming callus/shoots}}{\text{Total explants inoculated}} \times 100$ | Plating efficiency | % |
| 6 | $PD = 3.322 \times \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_f}{N_0}\right)$ | Population doubling level | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_d = \dfrac{N_0 \cdot V_0}{N_t}$ | Dilution volume in passaging | mL |
| 8 | $\text{TU/mL} = \dfrac{\text{No. transduced cells}}{\text{Volume of vector stock}}$ | Viral titer (functional) | TU = transducing units |
| 9 | $\text{MOI} = \dfrac{\text{PFU (or TU) added}}{\text{Number of target cells}}$ | Multiplicity of infection | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{1/2}^{\text{expression}} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{decay}}}$ | Vector expression half-life | days |

## 2.8 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Commercial PTC industry:** global market **> USD 600 million** (2024); major hubs in **India (KF Bioplants, Mahabaleshwar), Netherlands, Israel**.
- **mAb industry:** Top 5 selling mAbs (Humira®, Keytruda®, Eylea®, Stelara®, Opdivo®) collectively earn **> USD 80 billion/year** (2023 sales).
- **Gene therapy market:** ~USD 7 billion (2024); projected **> USD 30 billion by 2030**.
- **Approved gene therapies:** Luxturna® (AAV2-RPE65), Zolgensma® (AAV9-SMN1), Kymriah® (lentiviral CAR-T), Yescarta® (retroviral CAR-T), Strimvelis® (γ-RV ADA), Hemgenix® (AAV5-Factor IX).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Micropropagation Total Plantlet Yield (Multiplication Geometry)

**Problem Statement:** A commercial banana tissue culture lab uses an explant with a multiplication rate (R) of 4 per subculture. The lab performs 8 subculture cycles per year. If the lab starts with **5,000 explants**, how many hardened plantlets are produced in 1 year, assuming a **75% hardening survival rate**?

### Step 1 — Identify governing equation

The governing micropropagation growth equation is the geometric progression form:

$$M = N_0 \cdot R^n$$

where:
- $M$ = total number of plantlets generated at end of stage II
- $N_0$ = initial number of explants
- $R$ = multiplication rate per subculture cycle
- $n$ = number of subculture cycles

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$M = 5000 \times 4^8$$

### Step 3 — Compute the exponential term

We compute $4^8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4^1 &= 4 \\
4^2 &= 16 \\
4^3 &= 64 \\
4^4 &= 256 \\
4^5 &= 1024 \\
4^6 &= 4096 \\
4^7 &= 16384 \\
4^8 &= 65536
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$M = 5000 \times 65536 = 3.2768 \times 10^{8} \text{ plantlets}$$

### Step 4 — Apply hardening survival correction

$$M_{\text{hardened}} = M \times S_{\text{hardening}} = 3.2768 \times 10^{8} \times 0.75$$

$$M_{\text{hardened}} = 2.4576 \times 10^{8} \approx \mathbf{2.46 \times 10^{8} \text{ hardened plantlets}}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

> A single 5,000-explant inoculum can theoretically produce **~246 million hardened banana plantlets per year** — illustrating why PTC is the method of choice for commercial clonal propagation of vegetatively propagated crops.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hybridoma Doubling Time from Growth Curve

**Problem Statement:** A hybridoma cell line (used for anti-HER2 mAb production) has a cell density of $1.0 \times 10^5$ cells/mL at $t = 0$ h and $8.0 \times 10^5$ cells/mL at $t = 24$ h in exponential phase. Calculate the **specific growth rate (μ)** and **doubling time (td)**.

### Step 1 — Apply exponential growth law

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$$

Rearranging for μ:

$$\mu = \dfrac{\ln(N_t / N_0)}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Compute ratio and logarithm

$$N_t / N_0 = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^5} = 8.0$$

$$\ln(8.0) = \ln(2^3) = 3 \ln 2 = 3 \times 0.6931 = 2.0794$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into μ equation

$$\mu = \dfrac{2.0794}{24 \text{ h}} = 0.0866 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate doubling time

$$t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.6931}{0.0866}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_d &= \dfrac{0.6931}{0.0866} \\
    &= 8.00 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

> A doubling time of **8 hours** indicates a **fast-growing hybridoma**. Industrially, μ falls between **0.025–0.05 h⁻¹** (td = 14–28 h) for most CHO/hybridoma production strains. Faster μ strains are preferred but must be screened for **productivity (qp)** — high-growth rate doesn't always mean high mAb titer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Viral Vector MOI and Vector Volume Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A gene therapy laboratory wishes to transduce **5 × 10⁶ HEK-293T target cells** at an **MOI = 10** using a lentiviral stock with a titer of **1 × 10⁸ TU/mL**. Calculate the **volume of viral stock required**.

### Step 1 — Define MOI

$$\text{MOI} = \dfrac{\text{Number of viral particles (TU) added}}{\text{Number of target cells}}$$

Rearranging for the required total TU:

$$TU_{\text{required}} = \text{MOI} \times N_{\text{cells}}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate total TU required

$$TU_{\text{required}} = 10 \times 5 \times 10^6 = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ TU}$$

### Step 3 — Convert TU to volume using stock titer

$$V_{\text{stock}} = \dfrac{TU_{\text{required}}}{\text{Titer}_{\text{stock}}}$$

$$V_{\text{stock}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{7} \text{ TU}}{1 \times 10^{8} \text{ TU/mL}} = 0.5 \text{ mL}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation

> **0.5 mL of stock** is required. In practice, scientists add **polybrene (8 μg/mL)** to enhance lentiviral adsorption and incubate for **6–18 h** before media change. For in vivo dosing, MOI translates to **vg/kg body weight** (e.g., **1 × 10¹⁴ vg/kg** for systemic AAV).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Population Doubling Level (PDL) in Cell Line Ageing

**Problem Statement:** A primary cell culture (e.g., MEF — mouse embryonic fibroblasts) is passaged from 1 × 10⁶ cells to 1 × 10¹⁰ cells in a serial expansion. Calculate the **Population Doubling Level (PDL)**.

### Step 1 — Apply the PDL equation

$$PDL = 3.322 \times \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_f}{N_0}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the ratio

$$N_f / N_0 = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{10}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 10^4$$

### Step 3 — Take log₁₀

$$\log_{10}(10^4) = 4$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by 3.322

$$PDL = 3.322 \times 4 = 13.29$$

> **~13 population doublings** occurred. MEFs typically undergo **Hayflick limit** of **~15–20 PDL** before senescence, making this calculation critical for **cell line qualification** in bioprocess engineering.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Vector for a Target Disease

| Disease | Target Tissue | Ideal Vector | Rationale (Why) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ADA-SCID** | Hematopoietic stem cells (ex vivo) | **γ-Retrovirus** | Stable integration, long-term expression in dividing HSCs |
| **Hemophilia B** | Hepatocytes (in vivo) | **AAV8/AAV5** | Liver tropism, episomal persistence, low immunogenicity |
| **Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)** | Motor neurons | **AAV9** | Crosses blood-brain barrier (BBB) |
| **CAR-T (CD19 leukemia)** | T-cells (ex vivo) | **Lentivirus** | Transduces non-dividing T-cells, no silencing |
| **Cystic Fibrosis** | Airway epithelium | **AAV** (non-integrating) | Episomal, avoids insertional risk in airway cells |
| **Solid tumors (oncolysis)** | Tumor cells | **Adenovirus** | Large cargo capacity, strong immunogenic lysis |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Commercial Micropropagation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 0: Mother Plant Selection and Indexing] --> B[Stage I: Explant Excision and Surface Sterilization]
    B --> C[Stage II: Shoot Multiplication on MS Plus BAP Medium]
    C --> D{Cycle n Times at R fold Rate}
    D --> C
    D --> E[Stage III: Rooting on Half MS Plus IAA]
    E --> F[Stage IV: Primary Hardening in Greenhouse]
    F --> G[Stage V: Secondary Hardening in Shade Net]
    G --> H[Field Transplant and Commercial Distribution]
    B --> I[Quality Control: PCR and ELISA Pathogen Indexing]
    C --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J[Rejection if Contamination Detected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop `D → C → D` represents the geometric multiplication of shoots. The parallel `→ I → J` path enforces **Quality Control (QC)** at every stage — a regulatory requirement in commercial PTC (DUS testing, virus indexing via **RT-PCR, ELISA, NASH**).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Gene Therapy Workflow with Viral Vector

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C[Co transfect Packaging Cell Line HEK 293T with Helper Plasmids]
    C --> D[Harvest Replication Deficient Viral Vector Particles]
    D --> E{Purification Method}
    E --> E1[Ultracentrifugation Sucrose CsCl Gradient]
    E --> E2[Iodixanol Density Gradient]
    E --> E3[Affinity Chromatography]
    E1 --> F[Titer Determination qPCR and Functional Assay]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Delivery Route}
    G --> G1[Ex vivo Patient Cell Harvest Transduce Reinfuse]
    G --> G2[In vivo Direct Intravenous Intrathecal Intratumoral Injection]
    G1 --> H[Patient Monitoring Expression and Clinical Outcomes]
    G2 --> H
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Hybridoma Monoclonal Antibody Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Immunize Mouse with Antigen 3 to 4 Boosters] --> B[Harvest Splenocytes B Lymphocytes]
    B --> C[Fuse with Myeloma Cells using PEG 1450]
    C --> D[Culture in HAT Selection Medium]
    D --> E[Hybridoma Colonies Survive HGPRT Positive]
    E --> F[Screen Supernatant by ELISA]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — General for Module 4:**
> 1. Confusing **AAV** (Parvoviridae) with **Adenovirus** (Adenoviridae) — they are **not** related despite both starting with "Ad-".
> 2. Mixing up **ex vivo** and **in vivo** gene therapy — students often write about viral injection when asked about HSCs.
> 3. Forgetting that **retrovirus** transduces only **dividing cells** — a frequently marked error.
> 4. Skipping the **HAT selection** explanation in hybridoma questions.
> 5. Confusing **doubling time (td)** and **generation time** — they are the same in microbiology, but students may wrongly use μ in place of td.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between Plant Tissue Culture (PTC) and Animal Cell Culture (ACC) with respect to: (i) medium composition, (ii) subculture method, and (iii) shear sensitivity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~150 words):**

| Feature | Plant Tissue Culture (PTC) | Animal Cell Culture (ACC) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Medium** | **MS medium** with sucrose, inorganics, vitamins, **plant growth regulators** (auxins + cytokinins). Solidified with **0.8% agar**. | **DMEM/RPMI-1640** with glucose, amino acids, **5–10% FBS**. No cell wall-compatible gelling agent (uses **agarose-coated plates**). |
| **(ii) Subculture** | **Excision and transfer of shoot/apex** onto fresh medium (3–6 weeks); multiplication is **organized** (shoots, somatic embryos). | **Trypsin-EDTA dissociation** of monolayer; or **dilution passaging** of suspension. Multiplication is **cell-level**. |
| **(iii) Shear sensitivity** | **Moderate** — plant cells have a cell wall, tolerating up to **~50 cm/s** tip speed. | **Very high** — no cell wall, must keep tip speed **< 25 cm/s** to prevent cell lysis. |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2022]**
**What is a viral vector? Name any TWO viral vectors used in gene therapy and state ONE advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~120 words):**

A **viral vector** is a genetically engineered, **replication-deficient virus** in which the disease-causing viral genes are removed and replaced with a **therapeutic transgene**, while the viral capsid and entry machinery are retained to deliver the gene into target cells.

**Two examples:**

1. **Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV):**
   *Advantage:* **Low immunogenicity** and **long-term episomal expression** (1–5 years), with excellent safety profile — used in Luxturna® and Zolgensma®.

2. **Lentivirus (based on HIV-1):**
   *Advantage:* **Transduces both dividing and non-dividing cells** (e.g., resting T-cells, neurons, HSCs) and integrates stably for permanent correction — used in Kymriah® CAR-T therapy.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **one** full question from the choice provided. Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **stages of commercial plant tissue culture (micropropagation)** with a labeled flow diagram. Highlight the critical parameters at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **somaclonal variation**? Explain its **causes, detection methods, and applications** in crop improvement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Commercial micropropagation is the clonal multiplication of plants under sterile in vitro conditions. It follows the universally accepted **5-stage (or 4-stage) protocol** standardized for crops like banana, sugarcane, orchids, and strawberry.

**Stage 0 — Pre-initiation (1 mark):**
- Selection of elite, true-to-type, disease-free mother plant.
- Maintenance under controlled greenhouse for 2–3 months.
- Aim: provide physiologically uniform explant source.

**Stage I — Initiation (1 mark):**
- Excise meristem/axillary bud.
- Surface sterilization with **0.1% HgCl₂ (2–5 min)** after **70% EtOH (30 s)**.
- Inoculate on **MS medium + 3% sucrose + 0.8% agar**, pH **5.6–5.8**, BAP 1 mg/L.
- Conditions: 25 ± 2 °C, 16 h photoperiod, 40–60 μmol m⁻² s⁻¹ PPFD.

**Stage II — Multiplication (1.5 marks):**
- Repeated subculture on **high-cytokinin medium** (BAP 2–5 mg/L).
- Geometric growth: $M = N_0 \cdot R^n$
- Each cycle: 3–6 weeks; 3–10× multiplication per cycle.

**Stage III — Rooting (1 mark):**
- Transfer individual shoots to **½ MS + IAA 0.5 mg/L** (low auxin).
- Roots develop in 2–4 weeks.

**Stage IV — Hardening (1 mark):**
- Transfer to greenhouse, **gradually reduce humidity 100% → 60%** over 2–3 weeks.
- Substrate: **peat:perlite:vermiculite (1:1:1)**.
- Survival: 70–95%.

**Labeled Flow Diagram (0.5 mark):** as shown in Section 4.1 above.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Somaclonal variation** (Larkin & Scowcroft, 1981) is the heritable genetic variation that arises spontaneously in plants regenerated from **somatic cell cultures** (callus, cell suspension, protoplast, meristem).

**Causes (2 marks):**
- **Chromosomal aberrations** (aneuploidy, polyploidy, translocations) during mitosis in undifferentiated callus.
- **Point mutations** and **rearrangements** activated by culture stress (2,4-D, high BAP).
- **Transposable element activation** (e.g., Ac/Ds in maize).
- **Epigenetic changes** — DNA methylation alterations (cytosine hyper/hypomethylation).
- **Mitotic recombination** during prolonged subculture.

**Detection Methods (2 marks):**
- **Morphological markers** — leaf shape, flower color, plant height.
- **Cytological analysis** — chromosome counting, karyotyping.
- **Isozyme profiling** (peroxidase, esterase).
- **Molecular markers** — **RAPD, ISSR, AFLP, SSR, SCAR**.
- **Flow cytometry** for ploidy.

**Applications (2 marks):**
- Development of **disease-resistant** lines (e.g., sugarcane — eye spot disease resistance).
- **Stress-tolerant** lines (salt-tolerant rice — IRRI somaclones).
- **Improved agronomic traits** — yield, quality, earliness.
- Source of novel alleles for **breeding programs**.
- **Germplasm conservation** of rare species.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Q-A:** Students often write "variation in tissue culture is *always* undesirable" — **wrong**. Somaclonal variation is a **double-edged sword**: detrimental for clonal fidelity programs, but **a valuable tool for crop improvement**. Cover both sides to get full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is **hybridoma technology**? Outline the complete process for the production of **monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)**, including the principle of **HAT selection**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four main types of **viral vectors** used in gene therapy — **retrovirus, lentivirus, adenovirus, and AAV** — comparing their genome, payload capacity, integration, and key safety concerns. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Hybridoma technology** (Köhler & Milstein, 1975; Nobel Prize 1984) is a method to produce **monoclonal antibodies** of defined specificity by fusing antibody-producing **B-lymphocytes** (from immunized mouse spleen) with **immortal myeloma cells** to create a stable hybrid cell line — the **hybridoma**.

**Process Steps (4.5 marks):**

1. **Immunization (1 mark):**
   Inject mouse with antigen + adjuvant (CFA/IFA) — primary dose, then 3–4 boosters over 2–4 weeks. Test antibody titer by ELISA.

2. **Cell Fusion (1.5 marks):**
   - Harvest splenocytes (B-cells with desired Ab specificity).
   - Mix with **myeloma cells** (HGPRT-deficient, e.g., SP2/0, NS0, P3X63Ag8) in **5:1 to 10:1 ratio**.
   - Fuse using **PEG-1450 (50% w/v, MW 1450 Da)** for 1 min, or by **electrofusion** (1500 V/cm, 50 μs).

3. **HAT Selection (1.5 marks):**
   - Culture in **HAT medium** = **Hypoxanthine + Aminopterin + Thymidine**.
   - **Aminopterin** blocks the *de novo* nucleotide synthesis pathway.
   - Cells must use the **salvage pathway** (HGPRT enzyme + hypoxanthine + thymidine).
   - **Unfused myeloma (HGPRT⁻):** die (no salvage, no de novo).
   - **Unfused B-cells:** die naturally (limited lifespan, ~7–10 days).
   - **Unfused myeloma-myeloma fusions:** die (HGPRT⁻).
   - **B-B fusions:** die.
   - **Hybridomas (B-cell × myeloma):** **SURVIVE** — they inherit HGPRT from B-cell + immortality from myeloma.

4. **Screening + Cloning (0.5 mark):**
   - Screen hybridoma supernatants by **ELISA** for desired specificity.
   - **Single-cell cloning** by **limiting dilution** (0.3 cells/well) or **FACS** or **soft agar**.

5. **Scale-up + Purification (0.5 mark):**
   - Expand clone in **static culture → roller bottle → stirred-tank bioreactor** (with microcarriers like Cytodex-1) or **in vivo ascites**.
   - Purify via **Protein A/G affinity chromatography** → formulation → QC release.

**Significance (0.5 mark):**
- mAbs are used in **cancer therapy** (trastuzumab, rituximab), **autoimmune diseases** (adalimumab, infliximab), and **diagnostics** (pregnancy tests, HIV ELISA).

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Gene therapy requires a **safe, efficient, target-specific delivery vehicle** for therapeutic DNA. Viral vectors exploit the natural ability of viruses to enter cells and deliver genetic material, with disease-causing genes removed.

**Vector-by-Vector Comparison (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):**

**(i) γ-Retrovirus (Moloney MLV-based):**
- **Genome:** ssRNA, ~7–8 kb payload capacity.
- **Integration:** **Yes** — randomly into host genome (requires cell division for nuclear entry, as it depends on nuclear membrane breakdown during mitosis).
- **Safety concern:** **Insertional mutagenesis** — 5 patients in the French X-SCID trial developed leukemia due to LMO2 proto-oncogene activation.
- **Clinical use:** Strimvelis® (ADA-SCID).

**(ii) Lentivirus (HIV-1-derived):**
- **Genome:** ssRNA, ~7–10 kb payload.
- **Integration:** **Yes** — random, but uses an **integrase + nuclear localization signal** to enter the nucleus of **non-dividing cells** (e.g., neurons, resting T-cells, macrophages).
- **Safety concern:** Insertional mutagenesis (lower than γ-RV due to improved SIN — self-inactivation — design), risk of RCL (replication-competent lentivirus).
- **Clinical use:** Kymriah® (CD19 CAR-T), Zynteglo® (β-thalassemia).

**(iii) Adenovirus (Ad5, Ad2):**
- **Genome:** dsDNA, **up to 36 kb** — the **largest** capacity among common vectors.
- **Integration:** **No** — remains episomal; transient expression (1–2 weeks).
- **Safety concern:** **High innate immune response**; pre-existing antibodies in 50–80% of population. The 1999 **Jesse Gelsinger** death (Ornithine Transcarbamylase trial) due to innate immune over-activation is a key historical case.
- **Clinical use:** Gendicine® (recombinant p53, head-and-neck cancer, China 2003 — first approved gene therapy).

**(iv) Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV):**
- **Genome:** ssDNA, **4.7 kb** (max), small but high-efficiency.
- **Integration:** **No/minimal** — site-specific integration into **AAVS1** on chromosome 19 in wild-type, but recombinant AAV remains episomal.
- **Safety concern:** **Capsid immunogenicity**, pre-existing neutralizing antibodies (30–70% of population seropositive to AAV2), and **dose-dependent hepatotoxicity** at high systemic doses (> 1 × 10¹⁴ vg/kg).
- **Clinical use:** **Luxturna® (AAV2-RPE65, Leber's congenital amaurosis)**, **Zolgensma® (AAV9-SMN1, SMA)**.

**Comparative Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Vector | Capacity | Integration | Tropism | Duration | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| γ-RV | 7–8 kb | Yes (random) | Dividing | Long-term | Strimvelis® |
| Lentivirus | 7–10 kb | Yes (random) | All | Long-term | Kymriah® |
| Adenovirus | Up to 36 kb | No | Broad | Transient | Gendicine® |
| AAV | 4.7 kb | No (episomal) | Serotype-dependent | Long-term | Luxturna®, Zolgensma® |

**Concluding Remark (0.5 mark):**
Choice of vector depends on **payload size, target cell division status, required expression duration, immunogenicity tolerance, and risk-benefit profile** for the specific disease.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Q-B:** Many students forget to mention **self-inactivating (SIN) design** of modern γ-retrovirus and lentiviral vectors — a critical safety modification that deletes the U3 promoter region of the LTR to prevent transactivation of host oncogenes. Mentioning SIN design can earn **bonus thinking marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and read it the night before the exam — high-yield condensed facts.**

### **A. Commercial Plant Tissue Culture**

- ☐ The 5 stages are: **0 (Mother), I (Initiation), II (Multiplication), III (Rooting), IV (Hardening)**.
- ☐ **MS medium** = **Murashige & Skoog (1962)** — the universal PTC medium.
- ☐ **Auxins** (IAA, NAA, 2,4-D) promote **rooting/callus**; **cytokinins** (BAP, kinetin) promote **shoot/bud**.
- ☐ Surface sterilant: **0.1% HgCl₂** (highly effective but toxic waste) — alternative: **1% NaOCl + 0.1% Tween-20**.
- ☐ **Micropropagation equation:** $M = N_0 \cdot R^n$ — appears in **almost every KTU numerical**.
- ☐ **Somaclonal variation** = Larkin & Scowcroft (1981); detected by RAPD/ISSR; both useful (crop improvement) and harmful (loss of clonal fidelity).
- ☐ **Hairy root cultures** = *Agrobacterium rhizogenes* with **Ri plasmid** — stable, fast-growing, secondary metabolite production.
- ☐ **Elicitors** (MeJA, chitosan, fungal) enhance secondary metabolite yield by **2–20×**.
- ☐ **Bioreactors for PTC:** stirred-tank (with low-shear), bubble column, airlift, **Temporary Immersion System (TIS)** like RITA®, SETIS™.

### **B. Animal Cell Culture & Hybridoma**

- ☐ **Hybridoma** = **B-cell (Ab producer) + Myeloma (immortal)** fused with **PEG-1450**.
- ☐ **HAT selection:** only hybridomas survive. **H**ypoxanthine + **A**minopterin + **T**hymidine.
- ☐ **Myeloma cells must be HGPRT⁻ (e.g., SP2/0, NS0)** — to enable HAT killing of unfused myeloma.
- ☐ mAb purification = **Protein A affinity chromatography** (binds Fc region of IgG).
- ☐ **CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary)** is the **workhorse** industrial cell line for therapeutic mAbs.
- ☐ Shear sensitivity: animal cells need **tip speed < 25 cm/s**; use **marine/pitched-blade impellers**.
- ☐ **Monoclonal = single epitope specificity**; **Polyclonal = multiple epitopes**.

### **C. Viral Vectors in Gene Therapy**

- ☐ **Retrovirus (γ-RV)** = integrating, **dividing cells only**, 7–8 kb — Strimvelis®, first ex vivo trial (ADA-SCID, 1990, French Anderson).
- ☐ **Lentivirus** = integrating, **all cells (dividing + non-dividing)**, 7–10 kb — CAR-T, Kymriah®, Zynteglo®, Hemgenix® uses AAV5.
- ☐ **Adenovirus** = **non-integrating**, **largest cargo (36 kb)**, **transient + immunogenic** — Gendicine® (first approved GT, China 2003).
- ☐ **AAV** = **non-integrating (episomal)**, 4.7 kb, **long-term expression (years)**, low immunogenicity — Luxturna® (AAV2-RPE65), Zolgensma® (AAV9-SMN1), Hemgenix® (AAV5-Factor IX).
- ☐ **Ex vivo** = cells removed, transduced, reinfused (CAR-T, ADA-SCID). **In vivo** = direct vector injection (Luxturna subretinal, Zolgensma IV).
- ☐ **Key safety events:** (1) Jesse Gelsinger death 1999 (Adenovirus, OTC), (2) French X-SCID leukemia 2002–2005 (γ-RV, LMO2 insertional activation), (3) Zolgensma hepatotoxicity 2019 (AAV, high dose).
- ☐ **MOI** = TU per cell; clinical AAV doses are often expressed in **vg/kg body weight** (~1 × 10¹⁴ vg/kg for systemic).
- ☐ **Helper plasmids** (gag-pol, rev, vsv-g env) used in 3-plasmid or 4-plasmid systems to package lentivirus in HEK-293T cells.

### **D. Numericals to Master**

- ☐ $M = N_0 \cdot R^n$ (micropropagation yield)
- ☐ $\mu = \dfrac{\ln(N_t/N_0)}{t}$ and $t_d = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ (growth kinetics)
- ☐ $PDL = 3.322 \times \log_{10}(N_f/N_0)$ (cell line age)
- ☐ $V_{\text{vector}} = \dfrac{\text{MOI} \times N_{\text{cells}}}{\text{Titer}}$ (viral dosing)
- ☐ $PE = \dfrac{\text{Successful explants}}{\text{Total}} \times 100$ (tissue culture efficiency)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Bioreactors as Artificial Hybrid Organs and for Mass Production of Cells for Transplantation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT502 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Use of Bioreactors as Artificial Hybrid Organs and for Mass Production of Cells for Transplantation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bioartificial (Hybrid) Artificial Organs

A **Bioartificial Organ** (also termed a **Hybrid Artificial Organ** or **Biohybrid System**) is a medical device that integrates **living biological components** (such as isolated mammalian cells, tissue slices, or cell clusters) with **synthetic, semi-permeable, or polymeric biomaterials** to partially or fully replace the physiological function of a failed native organ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** A hybrid organ is a closed-loop extracorporeal or implantable device in which a patient's blood/plasma is perfused through a bioreactor cartridge containing metabolically active cells, retained behind a semi-permeable membrane that allows selective two-way transport of oxygen, nutrients, toxins, and therapeutic metabolites but **prevents direct cell–patient contact (immunoisolation)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples of Bioartificial Organs Studied under KTU Module 4**
> - **Bioartificial Liver (BAL)** – porcine or human hepatocytes in hollow-fibre cartridges.
> - **Bioartificial Pancreas** – pancreatic islets of Langerhans within immuno-isolating capsules.
> - **Bioartificial Kidney** – renal tubular cells seeded on haemofiltration membranes.

### 1.2 Mass Production of Cells for Transplantation

Cellular therapies (e.g., haematopoietic stem cell transplantation, chondrocyte grafts, induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived therapies, and CAR-T cell products) require the **ex-vivo expansion** of primary or stem cells under Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) conditions to clinically relevant numbers — typically **10⁹ to 10¹¹ cells per dose**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** Mass cell production for transplantation is the controlled, scalable in-vitro expansion of anchorage-dependent or suspension mammalian cells inside GMP-compliant bioreactor systems, achieving high cell density (≥ 10⁶ cells/mL), high viability (≥ 90%), and preserved differentiated phenotype, suitable for re-implantation into a human recipient.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English)**
> - Think of a **fish-tank with a very fine net** stretched across it. You drop live fish (cells) on one side of the net, and dirty aquarium water (patient's plasma) is gently pumped across the other side. The fish cannot escape (immunoisolation), but dissolved waste and oxygen pass freely through the mesh. The fish "clean" the dirty water exactly the way liver cells would detoxify a patient's blood. That is essentially how a **bioartificial liver** works.
> - For **mass cell production**, imagine scaling up a small kitchen garden (T-flask) into a commercial greenhouse (a stirred-tank bioreactor with microcarriers). The same plant (cell) is grown on thousands of tiny soil beads (microcarriers), so you harvest millions instead of dozens.

### 1.4 Key Quantitative Benchmarks

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Membrane MW cut-off | **10 – 100 kDa** | Excludes antibodies/cells, allows albumin & toxins |
| Hepatocyte density (BAL) | **~10⁷ cells/mL** | Mimics in-vivo liver cellularity |
| Islet encapsulation size | **300 – 800 µm** | Optimal diffusion / minimum fibrotic overgrowth |
| Perfusion dilution rate (D) | **0.5 – 2.0 h⁻¹** | Maintains high viability in stem cell expansion |
| Clinical cell dose | **10⁹ – 10¹¹ cells/patient** | Drives scale-up requirement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Bioreactors? — The Engineering Logic

Native tissues survive because of an extraordinarily dense capillary network. To replicate this artificially, bioreactors must simultaneously provide:

1. **High surface area-to-volume ratio** for mass transfer (achieved by hollow fibres, microcarriers, or porous scaffolds).
2. **Controlled hydrodynamic environment** (low shear to protect shear-sensitive mammalian cells: critical shear ≤ **1–5 dyn/cm²**).
3. **Immunoisolation** via a semi-permeable membrane.
4. **Scalable, sterilizable, GMP-compliant** operation.
5. **Sustained metabolic activity** (cells must remain differentiated — a hepatocyte must keep producing urea, albumin, and cytochrome-P450 enzymes for weeks).

### 2.2 Classification of Bioreactors Used

**A. For Artificial Hybrid Organs (Patient-Facing Devices)**
- **Hollow-Fibre Bioreactor** — most common; cells seeded in the extra-capillary space (ECS); patient's plasma flows through fibre lumens.
- **Flat-Sheet Membrane Bioreactor** — cells between two membranes (e.g., HepatAssist / ELAD systems).
- **Packed-Bed / Fluidized-Bed Bioreactor** — cells on porous microcarriers within a column.

**B. For Mass Cell Production (Ex-vivo Expansion)**
- **Stirred-Tank Bioreactor with Microcarriers** (Cytodex-1, Cytodex-3).
- **Wave / Rocking Bioreactor** (low shear, single-use bags).
- **Perfusion / Hollow-Fibre Cell Expansion System** (Terumo Quantum, Miltenyi Prodigy).
- **Fixed-Bed / Packed-Bed Bioreactor** for anchorage-dependent cells.

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' — Operational Logic Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Cell Sourcing**
> Allogeneic (donor), autologous (patient's own), or xenogeneic (e.g., porcine) cells. For BAL, primary porcine hepatocytes are widely used because of physiological similarity to human liver.
>
> **Step 2 — Immobilisation**
> Cells are seeded either (i) within a 3-D matrix (alginate, collagen, Matrigel), (ii) on microcarriers, or (iii) behind a hollow-fibre membrane. Immobilisation prevents washout and provides 3-D architecture essential for differentiated function.
>
> **Step 3 — Perfusion / Mass Transfer**
> Plasma or culture medium is perfused to deliver O₂ (critical pO₂ > 30 mmHg for hepatocytes) and remove toxins/metabolic waste.
>
> **Step 4 — Immunoisolation**
> The membrane's molecular weight cut-off is chosen so that immunoglobulins (IgG ≈ 150 kDa) and immune cells are excluded, but smaller proteins (albumin 66 kDa) and ammonia/urea pass freely.
>
> **Step 5 — Monitoring & Control**
> Online sensors for pH, DO, glucose, lactate, and ammonia keep the system within physiological set-points — identical in philosophy to a microbial bioreactor.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical constants used below in the derivations are **bold-marked** for examiner visibility.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{\max}\,S}{K_{s}+S}$ | Monod growth kinetics | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | $X(t) = X_{0}\,e^{\mu t}$ | Exponential cell growth | cells/mL |
| 3 | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Doubling time | h |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{F}{V}$ | Dilution / perfusion rate | h⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{D}$ | Mean residence time | h |
| 6 | $\text{OTR} = k_{L}a\,(C^{*}-C)$ | Oxygen transfer rate | mmol O₂ / (L·h) |
| 7 | $\text{OUR} = q_{O_{2}}\,X$ | Oxygen uptake rate | mmol O₂ / (L·h) |
| 8 | $\text{Steady-state: } k_{L}a\,(C^{*}-C) = q_{O_{2}}\,X$ | O₂ balance in bioreactor | mmol O₂ / (L·h) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P}{V} = \rho\,N_{p}\,N^{3}\,D_{i}^{5}$ | Power number correlation (impeller) | W/m³ |
| 10 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho\,v\,D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Sc} = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | Schmidt number (diffusion) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Sh} = \dfrac{k_{L}\,D}{D_{AB}} = a + b\,\text{Re}^{c}\,\text{Sc}^{d}$ | Sherwood correlation | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\text{Damköhler} = \dfrac{k\,L^{2}}{D_{AB}}$ | Reaction vs diffusion in tissue | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\text{Productivity } P_{r} = \dfrac{N_{cells,\,final}}{V_{reactor}\,t}$ | Cells per L per h | cells / (L·h) |
| 15 | $C_{crit,O_{2}} \approx 0.03\,\text{mM}$ | Critical DO for hepatocytes | mM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are used by engineers to size a hollow-fibre cartridge (cross-sectional area, fibre count, length) for a clinical BAL, and to determine the perfusion flow rate needed to keep hepatocytes oxygenated and detoxifying efficiently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Hollow-Fibre Surface Area for a Bioartificial Liver

A BAL cartridge contains $N$ hollow fibres of inner diameter $d_{i}$ and length $L$. Plasma flows through the lumen; hepatocytes reside in the ECS. We need enough luminal surface to (i) deliver O₂ and (ii) accept the ammonia (NH₃) load generated by the patient.

**Mass balance on ammonia across the cartridge**

$$
\text{NH}_{3,\text{removed}} = Q_{plasma}\,(C_{NH_{3},in} - C_{NH_{3},out}) = V_{ECS}\,v_{max,NH_{3}}\,\dfrac{C_{NH_{3},in}}{K_{m,NH_{3}}+C_{NH_{3},in}}
$$

**Total luminal surface area**

$$
A = N\,\pi\,d_{i}\,L
$$

**Mass-transfer-limited regime (clinically realistic, $C_{NH_{3}} \ll K_{m}$)**

$$
\text{NH}_{3,\text{removed}} \approx V_{ECS}\,\dfrac{v_{max,NH_{3}}}{K_{m,NH_{3}}}\,C_{NH_{3},in}
$$

**Effective first-order clearance constant**

$$
K = \dfrac{V_{ECS}\,v_{max,NH_{3}}}{K_{m,NH_{3}}}
$$

**Equate and solve for $N$**

$$
N = \dfrac{Q_{plasma}\,(C_{in}-C_{out})}{\pi\,d_{i}\,L\,\Delta C_{NH_{3}}\,\eta}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The number of fibres required scales linearly with plasma flow rate and ammonia removal demand, and inversely with fibre diameter, length, and mass-transfer efficiency ($\eta$). Smaller-diameter fibres ⇒ more surface per unit volume, but at the cost of higher pressure drop.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Oxygen Mass Balance in a Perfusion Cell-Expansion Bioreactor

In a mammalian-cell perfusion bioreactor, dissolved oxygen (DO) is consumed by cells and replenished by sparging/headspace transfer.

**Dynamic DO balance**

$$
\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C) - q_{O_{2}}\,X
$$

**At steady state, $dC/dt = 0$**

$$
k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C) = q_{O_{2}}\,X
$$

**Solve for the maximum sustainable cell density $X_{max}$** (when $C \to C_{crit,O_{2}}$)

$$
X_{max} = \dfrac{k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C_{crit,O_{2}})}{q_{O_{2}}}
$$

**Insert typical values for hepatocytes**

- $k_{L}a = 10 \;\text{h}^{-1}$ (sparged bioreactor)
- $C^{*} = 0.21 \;\text{mM}$ (air, 37 °C)
- $C_{crit,O_{2}} = 0.03 \;\text{mM}$
- $q_{O_{2}} = 0.20 \;\text{nmol}/(\text{cell} \cdot \text{h}) = 2 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{mmol}/(\text{cell} \cdot \text{h})$

$$
X_{max} = \dfrac{10 \times (0.21 - 0.03)}{2 \times 10^{-10}} = \dfrac{1.8}{2 \times 10^{-10}} = 9 \times 10^{9}\;\text{cells/L}
$$

$$
\boxed{X_{max} \approx 9 \times 10^{6}\;\text{cells/mL}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** For hepatocytes with a relatively high $q_{O_{2}}$, the sparged stirred-tank design alone cannot reach the **~10⁷ cells/mL** density required for a clinically effective BAL. This is precisely why **hollow-fibre / membrane bioreactors** (with internal membrane oxygenation and a 3-D matrix) are used: they effectively multiply $k_{L}a$ by providing direct cell–membrane contact.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Perfusion Rate Required to Maintain Steady-State Glucose

For an anchorage-dependent cell expansion on microcarriers, glucose is the limiting substrate. Mass balance over the perfusion bioreactor at steady state gives:

$$
D\,(S_{in} - S) = \dfrac{\mu\,X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

**Using Monod kinetics** $\mu = \mu_{max}\,S/(K_{s}+S)$:

$$
D\,(S_{in} - S) = \dfrac{\mu_{max}\,S}{K_{s}+S}\,\dfrac{X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

**At typical operating point for stem cells** ($S \gg K_{s}$, so $\mu \to \mu_{max}$):

$$
D_{min} = \dfrac{\mu_{max}\,X}{Y_{X/S}\,(S_{in} - S)}
$$

**Numerical example** — mesenchymal stem cells (MSC):

- $\mu_{max} = 0.035 \;\text{h}^{-1}$ ($\;t_{d} \approx 20\;\text{h}$)
- $Y_{X/S} = 1.5 \times 10^{8}\;\text{cells/g\;glucose}$
- $X = 1 \times 10^{6}\;\text{cells/mL}$
- $S_{in} = 25 \;\text{mM}$ (4.5 g/L); $S = 5 \;\text{mM}$ (set-point)

$$
D_{min} = \dfrac{0.035 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{8} \times (25-5)\times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 10^{4}}{3 \times 10^{6}} = 0.0117\;\text{h}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{min} \approx 0.012\;\text{h}^{-1} \;\; \text{(equivalent perfusion flow } F = D\,V\text{)}}
$$

> **Note:** This very low dilution rate (≈ 1.2 % reactor volume per hour) demonstrates why **perfusion** is superior to fed-batch for cell-therapy manufacturing: the same culture can be perfused for 10–14 days while maintaining viability and phenotype, yielding multi-billion-cell batches.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bioreactor Selection

| Selection Criterion | Hollow-Fibre (Hybrid Organ) | Stirred-Tank + Microcarriers (Cell Production) | Wave Bioreactor (Cell Production) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum cell density | **10⁷ – 10⁸ cells/mL** (in ECS) | 2 – 5 × 10⁶ cells/mL | 0.5 – 1 × 10⁶ cells/mL |
| Scale-up | Limited (extracorporeal) | Linear to 2000 L | Limited to ~ 500 L |
| Shear stress | Low | Moderate (microcarrier design dependent) | Very low |
| GMP compatibility | Difficult (multi-use) | High (single-use) | Highest |
| Cell types | Primary hepatocytes, islets | MSC, iPSC, Vero, HEK | MSC, suspension cells |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Low–medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bioartificial Liver Support System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Bloodstream] -->|Plasma separated| B[Plasma Filter]
    B -->|Purified plasma| C[Sterile Reservoir]
    C -->|Peristaltic pump| D[Hollow-Fibre Bioreactor]
    D -->|Cell compartment| E[Primary Hepatocytes in 3-D Matrix]
    D -->|O2 in / CO2 out| F[Membrane Oxygenator]
    D -->|Return detoxified plasma| G[Plasma Re-combiner]
    G -->|Re-infusion| A
    D -.Online monitoring.-> H[DO, pH, NH3, Glucose Sensors]
    H -->|Feedback| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The patient is connected extracorporeally. Blood is drawn, plasma is separated, perfused through the hollow-fibre cartridge containing the hepatocytes, oxygenated via an internal membrane, monitored in-line, and the detoxified plasma is recombined with blood cells and returned. The membrane around the cells provides **immunoisolation**.

### 4.2 Process Flow — Mass Cell Production for Transplantation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Tissue / Apheresis Sample] --> S2[Cell Isolation and Enrichment]
    S2 --> S3[Seed Expansion - T-flasks]
    S3 --> S4[Inoculation into Perfusion Bioreactor]
    S4 --> S5{Anchorage Dependent?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Microcarrier Beads Added]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Suspension Perfusion Mode]
    S6 --> S8[Growth Phase - Log Expansion]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Harvest - Enzymatic or Mechanical Detachment]
    S9 --> S10[Wash, Formulate, Vial]
    S10 --> S11[Cryopreservation - LN2 Storage]
    S11 --> S12[Release Testing - GMP QC]
    S12 --> S13[Clinical Infusion to Patient]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering take-away:** Steps 4 → 8 are the *bioreactor stage* where bioprocess engineering principles (kLa, μ, D, OUR) are applied. Steps 9 → 12 belong to downstream processing and are not part of the bioreactor design itself.

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Bioreactor Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1[Hollow-Fibre Configuration]
        HF1[Plasma In] --> HF2[Fibre Lumen]
        HF2 -->|Diffusion through pores| HF3[ECS - Cells Here]
        HF3 --> HF4[Plasma Out]
    end
    subgraph A2[Microcarrier Configuration]
        MC1[Impeller] --> MC2[Microcarriers with Adhered Cells]
        MC3[Medium with Dissolved O2] --> MC2
        MC2 --> MC4[Harvest Filter]
    end
    subgraph A3[Encapsulation Configuration]
        EN1[Cell Suspension] --> EN2[Droplet Generator]
        EN2 --> EN3[Alginate Bead with Cells]
        EN3 --> EN4[Culture / Implantation]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
> Define a *bioartificial organ*. List the two essential functional components without which such a device cannot operate.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **bioartificial organ** is a medical device combining living cells (or tissue) with a synthetic/polymeric support to substitute the function of a failed native organ.
The two essential components are:
1. **A viable, metabolically active cell population** (e.g., hepatocytes, islets) that performs the physiological function. **(1 mark)**
2. **A semi-permeable membrane / encapsulation matrix** that provides **immunoisolation** — permitting diffusion of nutrients, gases, and waste while blocking antibodies and immune cells. **(1 mark)**
Additionally, a controlled perfusion / mass-transfer system is implicit. **(1 mark)**

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
> State the role of **microcarriers** in the mass production of anchorage-dependent mammalian cells. Give two examples of commercial microcarriers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Microcarriers are small (≈ 100 – 300 µm), typically dextran-based, spherical beads that provide a **high surface-area-to-volume ratio** for anchorage-dependent cells to grow on while remaining in suspension under gentle stirring. This allows stirred-tank bioreactors to achieve cell densities (1 – 5 × 10⁶ cells/mL) that are impossible in 2-D T-flasks. **(2 marks)**
Examples: **Cytodex-1** (DEAE-dextran, positively charged), **Cytodex-3** (collagen-coated dextran), **Cultispher-G** (gelatin), **SoloHill Fact III**. **(1 mark for two examples)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Describe the design and working of a **bioartificial liver (BAL)** using a hollow-fibre bioreactor. Discuss how immunoisolation and oxygen delivery are engineered into the device. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the **hollow-fibre** and **stirred-tank with microcarriers** bioreactor configurations with respect to (i) achievable cell density, (ii) shear stress, (iii) scale-up potential, and (iv) suitability for clinical cell-therapy manufacturing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design and working of a Bioartificial Liver (BAL) — 7 marks**

The BAL is an **extracorporeal device** intended as a "bridge to transplant" for patients in acute liver failure.

1. **Cell compartment** — Primary porcine or human hepatocytes (≈ 10¹⁰ cells) are seeded into the **extra-capillary space (ECS)** of a hollow-fibre cartridge. The cells are often embedded in a collagen or Matrigel matrix to maintain polarity and differentiated function. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Hollow fibres** — Typically several thousand polyethersulfone or cellulose-acetate fibres of inner diameter ≈ 200 µm and MW cut-off **≈ 70 – 100 kDa**. Patient plasma is pumped through the fibre lumens; the membrane excludes IgG (150 kDa) and immune cells, providing **immunoisolation**. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Mass transfer** — Two-way diffusion of O₂, CO₂, glucose, ammonia, bilirubin, albumin, and drugs occurs across the membrane pores. **(1 mark)**
4. **Oxygen delivery** — A second set of gas-permeable silicone hollow fibres runs through the ECS, supplying O₂ directly to the cells; this overcomes the low solubility of O₂ in plasma and maintains hepatocyte pO₂ > 30 mmHg. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Perfusion circuit** — Patient blood → plasma separator → reservoir → peristaltic pump → BAL cartridge (with online DO/pH/NH₃ sensors) → plasma recombiner → return to patient. Heparin anticoagulation is used. **(1 mark)**
6. **Clinical examples** — HepatAssist (Circe Biomedical), ELAD (Vital Therapies), and AMC-BAL. **(0.5 marks)**

**(b) Comparative analysis — 7 marks**

| Parameter | Hollow-Fibre (HF) | Stirred-Tank + Microcarriers (ST-MC) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Achievable cell density** | 10⁷ – 10⁸ cells/mL (in ECS) **(1)** | 2 – 5 × 10⁶ cells/mL **(1)** |
| **(ii) Shear stress** | Very low — cells are protected inside the ECS **(1)** | Moderate; depends on impeller speed & bead density; critical shear ≤ 1–5 dyn/cm² for MSCs **(1)** |
| **(iii) Scale-up potential** | Limited to extracorporeal cartridges; fibre count ↑ ⇒ pressure drop ↑ **(1)** | Linear from 1 L to 2000 L; classical stirred-tank scale-up criteria (constant P/V, kLa) apply **(1)** |
| **(iv) Cell-therapy suitability** | Excellent for *primary organ-support* but not for *harvestable cell expansion* **(1)** | Industry workhorse for MSC, iPSC, HEK293, Vero expansion under GMP **(1)** |
| **Final synthesis** | HF excels in organ support (function retention) | ST-MC excels in cell mass production (yield and GMP) **(1)** |

> **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **ex-vivo expansion of stem cells** for transplantation. With the help of a suitable bioreactor, describe how a target dose of 10¹⁰ mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) can be produced starting from a 10 mL bone-marrow aspirate. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the relationship between **maximum sustainable cell density (Xₘₐₓ)** and the **volumetric oxygen-transfer coefficient (kLa)** in a perfusion mammalian-cell bioreactor. Use the result to explain why hollow-fibre / membrane oxygenation is essential for bioartificial organ bioreactors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ex-vivo MSC expansion — 7 marks**

1. **Concept** — MSCs represent ≈ 0.001 – 0.01 % of nucleated cells in bone marrow. To deliver a therapeutic dose (≥ 10⁸ – 10¹⁰ cells/patient), an *ex-vivo* expansion of ~ 1000-fold to 1 000 000-fold is required. **(1 mark)**
2. **Starting material** — 10 mL aspirate ≈ 5 × 10⁷ nucleated cells ≈ 5 × 10⁴ MSCs. **(0.5 marks)**
3. **Step 1 – Primary culture in T-flasks** — Mononuclear fraction isolated by density-gradient centrifugation (Ficoll), plated in T-175 flasks with DMEM + 10 % FBS; non-adherent cells removed after 48 h. Passaged at 80 % confluence. **(1 mark)**
4. **Step 2 – Expansion in a perfusion/stirred bioreactor with microcarriers** — Cytodex-1 microcarriers (3 g/L) are inoculated with P1 – P2 MSCs at 5 × 10³ cells/cm²; the culture is perfused at $D \approx 0.01 - 0.05\;\text{h}^{-1}$ with medium containing 4.5 g/L glucose. Over 10 – 14 days, $X$ reaches ≈ 5 × 10⁵ – 1 × 10⁶ cells/mL. **(2 marks)**
5. **Step 3 – Scale-up to 5 – 10 L bioreactor** — With ≈ 10⁶ cells/mL in a 10 L reactor, harvest ≈ 10¹⁰ cells. **(1 mark)**
6. **Step 4 – Harvest, formulate, cryopreserve** — Detachment (TrypLE), washing, formulation in CryoStor CS10, controlled-rate freezing, storage in liquid nitrogen, and release testing (sterility, mycoplasma, viability, karyotype, trilineage differentiation potency). **(1 mark)**
7. **Quality & regulatory** — Entire process done in GMP-graded clean rooms, single-use disposables, validated aseptic process. **(0.5 marks)**

**(b) Derivation of $X_{max}$ in terms of $k_{L}a$ — 7 marks**

> **Step 1 – Write the dynamic DO balance** (1 mark)

$$
\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C) - q_{O_{2}}\,X
$$

> **Step 2 – Apply steady-state $dC/dt = 0$** (1 mark)

$$
k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C) = q_{O_{2}}\,X
$$

> **Step 3 – Solve for X** (1 mark)

$$
X = \dfrac{k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C)}{q_{O_{2}}}
$$

> **Step 4 – Recognise the ceiling: as $C \to C_{crit,O_{2}}$** the cells become oxygen-limited (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{X_{max} = \dfrac{k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C_{crit,O_{2}})}{q_{O_{2}}}}
$$

> **Step 5 – Numerical evaluation for hepatocytes** ($k_{L}a = 10\;\text{h}^{-1}$, $C^{*}=0.21\;\text{mM}$, $C_{crit}=0.03\;\text{mM}$, $q_{O_{2}}=2\times10^{-10}\;\text{mmol/cell/h}$) (1 mark)

$$
X_{max} = \dfrac{10 \times 0.18}{2\times 10^{-10}} = 9 \times 10^{9}\;\text{cells/L} = 9 \times 10^{6}\;\text{cells/mL}
$$

> **Step 6 – Interpretation and engineering consequence** (2 marks)
> In a conventional sparged stirred-tank, $k_{L}a$ is limited to ≈ 5 – 15 h⁻¹ by bubble coalescence and shear. Furthermore, hepatocytes are very large (~ 25 µm) and have a high $q_{O_{2}}$. Hence a simple stirred tank can at best support ≈ 10⁶ cells/mL, whereas bioartificial organs require ≈ 10⁷ – 10⁸ cells/mL. To raise the *effective* $k_{L}a$ by 10 – 100×, designers embed **gas-permeable hollow fibres** inside the cell compartment, so each cell is in millimetric proximity to an O₂ source. This is the fundamental reason **membrane oxygenation is indispensable** for hybrid-organ bioreactors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating "bioartificial organ" with "prosthetic/implant".** A titanium hip is *not* a bioartificial organ — there are no living cells. Always mention the **cell component** and the **membrane / scaffold**.
> 2. **Confusing oxygen transfer coefficient ($k_{L}a$) with permeability (k).** $k_{L}a$ has units of **h⁻¹**; $k$ has units of m/s. Examiners deduct a full mark for unit errors.
> 3. **Skipping the steady-state assumption** in the OUR = OTR balance. The phrase "at steady state, $dC/dt = 0$" must be written explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring immunoisolation** in a 14-mark answer on artificial organs. It is the single concept that distinguishes a *hybrid* organ from a plain cell-culture vessel.
> 5. **Forgetting scale-up** — always conclude a mass-production answer with a numerical target (e.g., "10 L bioreactor → 10¹⁰ cells") to demonstrate engineering competence.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
> - **Bioartificial organ = living cells + semi-permeable membrane + perfusion + immunoisolation.** Always list all four.
> - The two most clinically investigated hybrid organs are the **bioartificial liver (hepatocytes)** and the **bioartificial pancreas (islets)**.
> - **Hollow-fibre cartridge** is the dominant architecture for hybrid organs; cells reside in the **ECS**, plasma flows through the lumen.
> - **MW cut-off ≈ 70 – 100 kDa** for liver-support membranes — excludes IgG, permits albumin and toxins.
> - Critical **dissolved-oxygen level** for hepatocytes: **pO₂ > 30 mmHg** (≈ 0.03 mM).
> - Mass transfer in tissue-engineered constructs is governed by the **Damköhler number** — when Da ≫ 1, cells in the construct core become necrotic.
> - Mass cell production for transplantation is driven by **exponential growth**: $X(t) = X_0\,e^{\mu t}$; with MSC doubling time ≈ 20 h, ~ 10 – 12 doublings are needed to go from 10⁴ to 10⁸ cells.
> - **Microcarriers** (Cytodex-1/3) enable anchorage-dependent cells to be grown in stirred-tank bioreactors at **2 – 5 × 10⁶ cells/mL**.
> - **Perfusion** (continuous medium replacement at dilution rate D) is preferred over fed-batch for cell-therapy manufacturing because it keeps lactate and ammonia low over long runs.
> - **Maximum sustainable cell density** is set by oxygen transfer: $X_{max} = k_{L}a\,(C^{*} - C_{crit})/q_{O_{2}}$. For hepatocytes, this mandates **membrane oxygenation** inside the cartridge.
> - **Shear sensitivity**: mammalian cells tolerate ≤ 1 – 5 dyn/cm²; hence wave/rocking and low-shear impellers (marine, pitched-blade) are preferred.
> - **Regulatory**: cell-therapy products must be manufactured in **GMP-graded facilities** with full traceability, mycoplasma testing, karyotyping, and potency assays.
> - **Clinical benchmarks**: BAL cartridges contain ≈ 10¹⁰ hepatocytes; clinical MSC doses are 10⁸ – 10⁹ cells/patient; CAR-T doses are 10⁸ – 10⁹ cells/patient.
> - The **Sherwood–Reynolds–Schmidt** correlations (Sh = a + b Reᶜ Scᵈ) are used to estimate the external mass-transfer coefficient $k_{L}$ around microcarriers.
> - For **scale-up** of cell-expansion bioreactors, maintain **constant P/V** (typically 10 – 50 W/m³) and **constant kLa**, not constant impeller tip speed alone.
> - The **key engineering advantage** of bioreactors over static flasks is the **reproducible, monitored, scalable** environment — exactly what regulatory agencies demand for clinical translation.
